subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the differential equation: (1 + x2) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 2xy - 4x2 = 0, subject to the initial condition y(0) = 0.
question_text: Solve the differential equation: (1 + x 2 ) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 2xy - 4x 2 = 0, subject to the initial condition y(0) = 0.
explanation: Given: {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y-4 x^{2}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2 x y}{1+x^{2}}-\frac{4 x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2 x y}{1+x^{2}}=\frac{4 x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} Here, {tex}P=\frac{2 x }{1+x^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}Q=\frac{4 x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{I.F}=e^{\int P d x}=e^{\int \frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}} d x}{/tex} {tex}\text { I.F. }=e^{\log \left|1+x^{2}\right|}{/tex} I.F. = 1 + x 2 {tex}\left[\because e^{\log x}=x\right]{/tex} Solution of given differential equation is {tex}y(\mathrm{I.F})=\int(\mathrm{I} \cdot \mathrm{F} \times \mathrm{Q}) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\int\left(1+x^{2}\right)\left(\frac{4 x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\frac{4 x^{3}}{3}+C{/tex} ...(i) Now, putting x = 0, y = 0 in (i), we get 0 = 0 + C {tex}\therefore{/tex} C = 0 Substitute the value of C in (i), we get {tex}y\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\frac{4 x^{3}}{3}{/tex} {tex}y=\frac{4 x^{3}}{3\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex}
solution: Given: {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y-4 x^{2}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2 x y}{1+x^{2}}-\frac{4 x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2 x y}{1+x^{2}}=\frac{4 x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} Here, {tex}P=\frac{2 x }{1+x^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}Q=\frac{4 x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{I.F}=e^{\int P d x}=e^{\int \frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}} d x}{/tex} {tex}\text { I.F. }=e^{\log \left|1+x^{2}\right|}{/tex} I.F. = 1 + x 2 {tex}\left[\because e^{\log x}=x\right]{/tex} Solution of given differential equation is {tex}y(\mathrm{I.F})=\int(\mathrm{I} \cdot \mathrm{F} \times \mathrm{Q}) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\int\left(1+x^{2}\right)\left(\frac{4 x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\frac{4 x^{3}}{3}+C{/tex} ...(i) Now, putting x = 0, y = 0 in (i), we get 0 = 0 + C {tex}\therefore{/tex} C = 0 Substitute the value of C in (i), we get {tex}y\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\frac{4 x^{3}}{3}{/tex} {tex}y=\frac{4 x^{3}}{3\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y-4 x^{2}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2 x y}{1+x^{2}}-\frac{4 x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2 x y}{1+x^{2}}=\frac{4 x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}
Here, {tex}P=\frac{2 x }{1+x^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}Q=\frac{4 x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{I.F}=e^{\int P d x}=e^{\int \frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}} d x}{/tex}
{tex}\text { I.F. }=e^{\log \left|1+x^{2}\right|}{/tex}
I.F. = 1 + x2 {tex}\left[\because e^{\log x}=x\right]{/tex}
Solution of given differential equation is
{tex}y(\mathrm{I.F})=\int(\mathrm{I} \cdot \mathrm{F} \times \mathrm{Q}) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\int\left(1+x^{2}\right)\left(\frac{4 x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\frac{4 x^{3}}{3}+C{/tex} ...(i)
Now, putting x = 0, y = 0 in (i), we get
0 = 0 + C
{tex}\therefore{/tex} C = 0
Substitute the value of C in (i), we get
{tex}y\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\frac{4 x^{3}}{3}{/tex}
{tex}y=\frac{4 x^{3}}{3\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex}

question_html: The integrating factor of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+y=\frac{1+y}{x}{/tex} is:
question_text: The integrating factor of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+y=\frac{1+y}{x}{/tex} is:

options.explanation: We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+y=\frac{1+y}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{x}+\frac{y(1-x)}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-\left(\frac{1-x}{x}\right) y=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} This is a linear differential equation. On comparing it with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} , we get {tex}P=\frac{-(1-x)}{x}, Q=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} I.F. = {tex}e^{\int P d x}=e^{-\int \frac{1-x}{x} d x}{/tex} {tex}=e^{x-\log x}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{e^{x}}{x}{/tex}

explanation: We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+y=\frac{1+y}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{x}+\frac{y(1-x)}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-\left(\frac{1-x}{x}\right) y=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} This is a linear differential equation. On comparing it with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} , we get {tex}P=\frac{-(1-x)}{x}, Q=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} I.F. = {tex}e^{\int P d x}=e^{-\int \frac{1-x}{x} d x}{/tex} {tex}=e^{x-\log x}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{e^{x}}{x}{/tex}

question_html:
Find the particular solution of the differential equation
{tex}\left[x \sin ^{2}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-y\right] d x+x d y=0{/tex}
given that {tex}{y}=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}, when {tex}{x}=1{/tex}.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\left[x \sin ^{2}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-y\right] d x+x d y=0{/tex} given that {tex}{y}=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} , when {tex}{x}=1{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}{\left[x \cdot \sin ^{2} \frac{y}{x}-y\right] \cdot d x+x \cdot d y=0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\sin ^{2} \frac{y}{x}{/tex} Put {tex}y=v x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=x \frac{d v}{d x}+v{/tex} {tex}\therefore x \frac{d v}{d x}+v=v-\sin ^{2} v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\int \operatorname{cosec}^{2} {v} {d v}=\int \frac{{d x}}{{x}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cot {v}=\log |{x}|+{c}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cot \frac{{y}}{{x}}=\log |{x}|+{c}{/tex} {tex}{x}=1, {y}=\frac{\pi}{4} \Rightarrow {c}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cot \frac{{y}}{{x}}=\log |{x}|+1{/tex}
solution: {tex}{\left[x \cdot \sin ^{2} \frac{y}{x}-y\right] \cdot d x+x \cdot d y=0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\sin ^{2} \frac{y}{x}{/tex} Put {tex}y=v x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=x \frac{d v}{d x}+v{/tex} {tex}\therefore x \frac{d v}{d x}+v=v-\sin ^{2} v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\int \operatorname{cosec}^{2} {v} {d v}=\int \frac{{d x}}{{x}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cot {v}=\log |{x}|+{c}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cot \frac{{y}}{{x}}=\log |{x}|+{c}{/tex} {tex}{x}=1, {y}=\frac{\pi}{4} \Rightarrow {c}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cot \frac{{y}}{{x}}=\log |{x}|+1{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}{\left[x \cdot \sin ^{2} \frac{y}{x}-y\right] \cdot d x+x \cdot d y=0}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\sin ^{2} \frac{y}{x}{/tex}
Put {tex}y=v x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=x \frac{d v}{d x}+v{/tex}
{tex}\therefore x \frac{d v}{d x}+v=v-\sin ^{2} v{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-\int \operatorname{cosec}^{2} {v} {d v}=\int \frac{{d x}}{{x}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \cot {v}=\log |{x}|+{c}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \cot \frac{{y}}{{x}}=\log |{x}|+{c}{/tex}
{tex}{x}=1, {y}=\frac{\pi}{4} \Rightarrow {c}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \cot \frac{{y}}{{x}}=\log |{x}|+1{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Solve the following differential equation:
{tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y=4 x^{2} .{/tex}
question_text: Solve the following differential equation: {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y=4 x^{2} .{/tex}
explanation: Given differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}} y=\frac{4 x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} , which is linear in y. I.F. {tex}=e^{\int \frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}} d x}=e^{\log \left(1+x^{2}\right)}=1+x^{2}{/tex} The solution is given by {tex}y\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\int 4 x^{2} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\frac{4}{3} x^{3}+C{/tex} or {tex}y=\frac{4 x^{3}}{3\left(1+x^{2}\right)}+C \frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex} , which is the required general solution.
solution: Given differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}} y=\frac{4 x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} , which is linear in y. I.F. {tex}=e^{\int \frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}} d x}=e^{\log \left(1+x^{2}\right)}=1+x^{2}{/tex} The solution is given by {tex}y\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\int 4 x^{2} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\frac{4}{3} x^{3}+C{/tex} or {tex}y=\frac{4 x^{3}}{3\left(1+x^{2}\right)}+C \frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex} , which is the required general solution.
solution_html:
Given differential equation can be written as
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}} y=\frac{4 x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}, which is linear in y.
I.F. {tex}=e^{\int \frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}} d x}=e^{\log \left(1+x^{2}\right)}=1+x^{2}{/tex}
The solution is given by
{tex}y\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\int 4 x^{2} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\frac{4}{3} x^{3}+C{/tex}
or {tex}y=\frac{4 x^{3}}{3\left(1+x^{2}\right)}+C \frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex}, which is the required general solution.

question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex}2({y}+3)-x {y} \frac{{dy}}{{d} x}=0{/tex}; given {tex}{y}(1)=-2{/tex}.
question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex}2({y}+3)-x {y} \frac{{dy}}{{d} x}=0{/tex} ; given {tex}{y}(1)=-2{/tex} .
explanation: Given differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{y}{y+3} d y=\frac{2}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int\left(1-\frac{3}{y+3}\right) d y=2 \int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y-3 \log |y+3|=2 \log |x|+C{/tex} {tex}{y}=-2{/tex} , when {tex}{x}=1 \Rightarrow C=-2{/tex} Hence, the required particular solution is {tex}\Rightarrow y-3 \log |y+3|=2 \log |x|-2{/tex}
solution: Given differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{y}{y+3} d y=\frac{2}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int\left(1-\frac{3}{y+3}\right) d y=2 \int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y-3 \log |y+3|=2 \log |x|+C{/tex} {tex}{y}=-2{/tex} , when {tex}{x}=1 \Rightarrow C=-2{/tex} Hence, the required particular solution is {tex}\Rightarrow y-3 \log |y+3|=2 \log |x|-2{/tex}
solution_html:
Given differential equation can be written as
{tex}\frac{y}{y+3} d y=\frac{2}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int\left(1-\frac{3}{y+3}\right) d y=2 \int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y-3 \log |y+3|=2 \log |x|+C{/tex}
{tex}{y}=-2{/tex}, when {tex}{x}=1 \Rightarrow C=-2{/tex}
Hence, the required particular solution is
{tex}\Rightarrow y-3 \log |y+3|=2 \log |x|-2{/tex}

question_html:
Solve the differential equation
{tex}{x} \cos \left(\frac{{y}}{{x}}\right) \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}={y} \cos \left(\frac{{y}}{{x}}\right)+{x}{/tex}.
question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex}{x} \cos \left(\frac{{y}}{{x}}\right) \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}={y} \cos \left(\frac{{y}}{{x}}\right)+{x}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}+\sec \frac{y}{x}{/tex} Put {tex}{y}={v x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}={v}+{x} \frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {v}+{x} \frac{{d v}}{{d x}}={v}+\boldsymbol{\operatorname { s e c }} {v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \cos {v} {d v}=\int \frac{{d x}}{{x}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \boldsymbol{\operatorname { s i n }} {v}=\boldsymbol{\operatorname { l o g }}|{x}|+{c}{/tex} {tex}\Longrightarrow \boldsymbol{\operatorname { s i n }} \frac{{y}}{{x}}=\boldsymbol{\operatorname { l o g }}|{x}|+{c}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}+\sec \frac{y}{x}{/tex} Put {tex}{y}={v x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}={v}+{x} \frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {v}+{x} \frac{{d v}}{{d x}}={v}+\boldsymbol{\operatorname { s e c }} {v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \cos {v} {d v}=\int \frac{{d x}}{{x}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \boldsymbol{\operatorname { s i n }} {v}=\boldsymbol{\operatorname { l o g }}|{x}|+{c}{/tex} {tex}\Longrightarrow \boldsymbol{\operatorname { s i n }} \frac{{y}}{{x}}=\boldsymbol{\operatorname { l o g }}|{x}|+{c}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}+\sec \frac{y}{x}{/tex}
Put {tex}{y}={v x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}={v}+{x} \frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {v}+{x} \frac{{d v}}{{d x}}={v}+\boldsymbol{\operatorname { s e c }} {v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \cos {v} {d v}=\int \frac{{d x}}{{x}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \boldsymbol{\operatorname { s i n }} {v}=\boldsymbol{\operatorname { l o g }}|{x}|+{c}{/tex}
{tex}\Longrightarrow \boldsymbol{\operatorname { s i n }} \frac{{y}}{{x}}=\boldsymbol{\operatorname { l o g }}|{x}|+{c}{/tex}

question_html:
Find the particular solution of the differential equation
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-3 y \cot x=\sin 2 x, {/tex}{tex}\text { given that } y=2 \text { when } x=\frac{\pi}{2} .{/tex}
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-3 y \cot x=\sin 2 x, {/tex} {tex}\text { given that } y=2 \text { when } x=\frac{\pi}{2} .{/tex}
explanation: I.F. {tex}=e^{\int-3 \cot x d x}=\sin ^{-3} {x}{/tex} Solution is y. {tex}\sin ^{-3} {x}=\int \sin 2 x \cdot \sin ^{-3} {x} d x+{C}{/tex} y. {tex}\sin ^{-3} x=2 \int \operatorname{cosec} x \cdot \cot x d x+C{/tex} y. {tex}\sin ^{-3} x=-2 \operatorname{cosec} x+C{/tex} putting {tex}y=2, x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} gives {tex}C=4{/tex} Particular solution is {tex}{y} \cdot \sin ^{-3} {x}=-2 \operatorname{cosec} x+4{/tex} or {tex}{y}=-2 \sin ^{2} x+4 \sin ^{3} {x}{/tex}
solution: I.F. {tex}=e^{\int-3 \cot x d x}=\sin ^{-3} {x}{/tex} Solution is y. {tex}\sin ^{-3} {x}=\int \sin 2 x \cdot \sin ^{-3} {x} d x+{C}{/tex} y. {tex}\sin ^{-3} x=2 \int \operatorname{cosec} x \cdot \cot x d x+C{/tex} y. {tex}\sin ^{-3} x=-2 \operatorname{cosec} x+C{/tex} putting {tex}y=2, x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} gives {tex}C=4{/tex} Particular solution is {tex}{y} \cdot \sin ^{-3} {x}=-2 \operatorname{cosec} x+4{/tex} or {tex}{y}=-2 \sin ^{2} x+4 \sin ^{3} {x}{/tex}
solution_html:
I.F. {tex}=e^{\int-3 \cot x d x}=\sin ^{-3} {x}{/tex}
Solution is
y. {tex}\sin ^{-3} {x}=\int \sin 2 x \cdot \sin ^{-3} {x} d x+{C}{/tex}
y. {tex}\sin ^{-3} x=2 \int \operatorname{cosec} x \cdot \cot x d x+C{/tex}
y. {tex}\sin ^{-3} x=-2 \operatorname{cosec} x+C{/tex}
putting {tex}y=2, x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} gives {tex}C=4{/tex}
Particular solution is {tex}{y} \cdot \sin ^{-3} {x}=-2 \operatorname{cosec} x+4{/tex} or {tex}{y}=-2 \sin ^{2} x+4 \sin ^{3} {x}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}x^{2} \frac{d y}{d x}=x^{2}+x y+y^{2} .{/tex}
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}x^{2} \frac{d y}{d x}=x^{2}+x y+y^{2} .{/tex}
explanation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}+\frac{y}{x}+1{/tex} Put {tex}\frac{y}{x}=v{/tex} i.e. {tex}y=v x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} Equation becomes {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=v^{2}+{v}+1{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v^{2}+1} d v=\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} Integrating, we get {tex}\tan ^{-1} {v}=\log |{x}|+{C} \Longrightarrow \tan ^{-1} \frac{y}{x}=\log |{x}|{/tex} + C
solution: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}+\frac{y}{x}+1{/tex} Put {tex}\frac{y}{x}=v{/tex} i.e. {tex}y=v x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} Equation becomes {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=v^{2}+{v}+1{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v^{2}+1} d v=\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} Integrating, we get {tex}\tan ^{-1} {v}=\log |{x}|+{C} \Longrightarrow \tan ^{-1} \frac{y}{x}=\log |{x}|{/tex} + C
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}+\frac{y}{x}+1{/tex}
Put {tex}\frac{y}{x}=v{/tex} i.e. {tex}y=v x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
Equation becomes
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=v^{2}+{v}+1{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{v^{2}+1} d v=\frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
Integrating, we get
{tex}\tan ^{-1} {v}=\log |{x}|+{C} \Longrightarrow \tan ^{-1} \frac{y}{x}=\log |{x}|{/tex} + C

question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex}(x-\sin y) d y+(\tan y) d x=0{/tex}, given {tex}{y}(0)=0{/tex}.
question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex}(x-\sin y) d y+(\tan y) d x=0{/tex} , given {tex}{y}(0)=0{/tex} .
explanation: The differential equation can be written as: {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\cot y \cdot x=\cos y{/tex} , which is a linear order differential equation Here, {tex}P=\cot y, Q=\cos y{/tex} , I.F. (Integrating Factor) {tex}=e^{\int \cot y d y}=e^{\log \sin y}=\sin y{/tex} The solution is, {tex}x(\sin y)=\int \cos y \cdot \sin y d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {x}(\sin {y})=\frac{(\sin {y})^{2}}{2}+{C}{/tex} , For {tex}{x}={0}, {y}={0}, {C}={0}{/tex} . {tex}\therefore{/tex} The Particular solution is: {tex}x \sin y=\frac{\sin ^{2} y}{2}{/tex} or {tex}\sin y=2 x{/tex} or {tex}y=\sin ^{-1} 2 x{/tex}
solution: The differential equation can be written as: {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\cot y \cdot x=\cos y{/tex} , which is a linear order differential equation Here, {tex}P=\cot y, Q=\cos y{/tex} , I.F. (Integrating Factor) {tex}=e^{\int \cot y d y}=e^{\log \sin y}=\sin y{/tex} The solution is, {tex}x(\sin y)=\int \cos y \cdot \sin y d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {x}(\sin {y})=\frac{(\sin {y})^{2}}{2}+{C}{/tex} , For {tex}{x}={0}, {y}={0}, {C}={0}{/tex} . {tex}\therefore{/tex} The Particular solution is: {tex}x \sin y=\frac{\sin ^{2} y}{2}{/tex} or {tex}\sin y=2 x{/tex} or {tex}y=\sin ^{-1} 2 x{/tex}
solution_html:
The differential equation can be written as:
{tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\cot y \cdot x=\cos y{/tex}, which is a linear order differential equation
Here, {tex}P=\cot y, Q=\cos y{/tex}, I.F. (Integrating Factor) {tex}=e^{\int \cot y d y}=e^{\log \sin y}=\sin y{/tex}
The solution is, {tex}x(\sin y)=\int \cos y \cdot \sin y d y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {x}(\sin {y})=\frac{(\sin {y})^{2}}{2}+{C}{/tex}, For {tex}{x}={0}, {y}={0}, {C}={0}{/tex}.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The Particular solution is: {tex}x \sin y=\frac{\sin ^{2} y}{2}{/tex} or {tex}\sin y=2 x{/tex} or {tex}y=\sin ^{-1} 2 x{/tex}

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}-\frac{{y}}{{x}}+\operatorname{cosec}\left(\frac{{y}}{{x}}\right)=0{/tex}; given that {tex}{y}=0{/tex}, when {tex}{x}=1{/tex}.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}-\frac{{y}}{{x}}+\operatorname{cosec}\left(\frac{{y}}{{x}}\right)=0{/tex} ; given that {tex}{y}=0{/tex} , when {tex}{x}=1{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\operatorname{cosec} \frac{y}{x}{/tex} Put {tex}{y}={v x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}={v}+{x} \frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {v}+{x} \frac{{d v}}{{d x}}={v}-\operatorname{cosec} {v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\int \sin {v} {d} {v}=\int \frac{{d} {x}}{{x}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos v=\log |x|+c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \frac{y}{x}=\log |x|+c{/tex} {tex}{x}={1}, {y}={0} \Rightarrow {c}={1}{/tex} {tex}\Longrightarrow \cos \frac{y}{x}=\log |x|+1{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\operatorname{cosec} \frac{y}{x}{/tex} Put {tex}{y}={v x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}={v}+{x} \frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {v}+{x} \frac{{d v}}{{d x}}={v}-\operatorname{cosec} {v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\int \sin {v} {d} {v}=\int \frac{{d} {x}}{{x}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos v=\log |x|+c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \frac{y}{x}=\log |x|+c{/tex} {tex}{x}={1}, {y}={0} \Rightarrow {c}={1}{/tex} {tex}\Longrightarrow \cos \frac{y}{x}=\log |x|+1{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\operatorname{cosec} \frac{y}{x}{/tex}
Put {tex}{y}={v x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}={v}+{x} \frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {v}+{x} \frac{{d v}}{{d x}}={v}-\operatorname{cosec} {v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-\int \sin {v} {d} {v}=\int \frac{{d} {x}}{{x}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \cos v=\log |x|+c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \cos \frac{y}{x}=\log |x|+c{/tex}
{tex}{x}={1}, {y}={0} \Rightarrow {c}={1}{/tex}
{tex}\Longrightarrow \cos \frac{y}{x}=\log |x|+1{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex}\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right) d x+x y d y=0, y(1)=1{/tex}.
question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex}\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right) d x+x y d y=0, y(1)=1{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{x y}= -\frac{1+\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}}{\frac{y}{x}}, {/tex} {tex}\text { Put } y=v x, \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{1+v^{2}}{v} \Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{1+2 v^{2}}{v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{4} \int \frac{4 v}{1+2 v^{2}} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{4} \log \left(1+2 v^{2}\right)=-\log |x|+\log C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left(1+2 v^{2}\right)=\log \frac{C^{4}}{x^{4}} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1+2\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}=\frac{D}{x^{4}}, D=C^{4}{/tex} {tex}\text { For } x=1, y=1, D=3{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The solution of the differential equation is, {tex}\left(2 {y}^{2}+{x}^{2}\right) {x}^{2}={3}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{x y}= -\frac{1+\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}}{\frac{y}{x}}, {/tex} {tex}\text { Put } y=v x, \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{1+v^{2}}{v} \Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{1+2 v^{2}}{v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{4} \int \frac{4 v}{1+2 v^{2}} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{4} \log \left(1+2 v^{2}\right)=-\log |x|+\log C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left(1+2 v^{2}\right)=\log \frac{C^{4}}{x^{4}} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1+2\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}=\frac{D}{x^{4}}, D=C^{4}{/tex} {tex}\text { For } x=1, y=1, D=3{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The solution of the differential equation is, {tex}\left(2 {y}^{2}+{x}^{2}\right) {x}^{2}={3}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{x y}= -\frac{1+\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}}{\frac{y}{x}}, {/tex}
{tex}\text { Put } y=v x, \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{1+v^{2}}{v} \Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{1+2 v^{2}}{v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{4} \int \frac{4 v}{1+2 v^{2}} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{4} \log \left(1+2 v^{2}\right)=-\log |x|+\log C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left(1+2 v^{2}\right)=\log \frac{C^{4}}{x^{4}} {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 1+2\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}=\frac{D}{x^{4}}, D=C^{4}{/tex}
{tex}\text { For } x=1, y=1, D=3{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The solution of the differential equation is, {tex}\left(2 {y}^{2}+{x}^{2}\right) {x}^{2}={3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
During a heavy gaming session, the temperature of a student's laptop processor increases significantly. After the session, the processor begins to cool down, and the rate of cooling is proportional to the difference between the processor's temperature and the room temperature ({tex}25^{\circ} {C}{/tex}). Initially the processor's temperature is {tex}85^{\circ} {C}{/tex}. The rate of cooling is defined by the equation {tex}\frac{{d}}{{dt}}({T}({t}))=-{k}({T}({t})-25){/tex},
where {tex}{T}({t}){/tex} represents the temperature of the processor at time t (in minutes) and k is a constant.

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Find the expression for temperature of processor, {tex}{T}({t}){/tex} given that {tex}{T}(0)=85^{\circ} {C}{/tex}. (2)
  2. How long will it take for the processor's temperature to reach {tex}40^{\circ} {C}{/tex}? Given that {tex}{k}=0 \cdot 03, \log _{{e}} 4=1 \cdot 3863{/tex}. (2)
question_text: During a heavy gaming session, the temperature of a student's laptop processor increases significantly. After the session, the processor begins to cool down, and the rate of cooling is proportional to the difference between the processor's temperature and the room temperature ( {tex}25^{\circ} {C}{/tex} ). Initially the processor's temperature is {tex}85^{\circ} {C}{/tex} . The rate of cooling is defined by the equation {tex}\frac{{d}}{{dt}}({T}({t}))=-{k}({T}({t})-25){/tex} , where {tex}{T}({t}){/tex} represents the temperature of the processor at time t (in minutes) and k is a constant. Based on the above information, answer the following questions: Find the expression for temperature of processor, {tex}{T}({t}){/tex} given that {tex}{T}(0)=85^{\circ} {C}{/tex} . (2) How long will it take for the processor's temperature to reach {tex}40^{\circ} {C}{/tex} ? Given that {tex}{k}=0 \cdot 03, \log _{{e}} 4=1 \cdot 3863{/tex} . (2)
explanation: {tex}\frac{d T}{d t}=-k(T-25){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d T}{T-25}=-k d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d T}{T-25}=-k \int d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log |T-25|=-k t+C{/tex} When {tex}t=0, T=85{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log 60=C{/tex} Using in equation {tex}(a), \log |T-25|=-k t+\log 60{/tex} ...(b) When {tex}k=0.03, \log |T-25|=-0.03 t+\log 60{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left|\frac{T-25}{60}\right|=-0.03 t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow T-25=60 . e^{-0.03 t}{/tex} When {tex}T=40, t=t_{1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{15}{60}=e^{-0.03 t_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{-0.03 t_{1}}=\frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow-0.03 t_{1}=-\log 4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow t_{1}=\frac{\log 4}{0.03}=\frac{1.3863}{0.03}=46.21 {~m}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{d T}{d t}=-k(T-25){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d T}{T-25}=-k d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d T}{T-25}=-k \int d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log |T-25|=-k t+C{/tex} When {tex}t=0, T=85{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log 60=C{/tex} Using in equation {tex}(a), \log |T-25|=-k t+\log 60{/tex} ...(b) When {tex}k=0.03, \log |T-25|=-0.03 t+\log 60{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left|\frac{T-25}{60}\right|=-0.03 t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow T-25=60 . e^{-0.03 t}{/tex} When {tex}T=40, t=t_{1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{15}{60}=e^{-0.03 t_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{-0.03 t_{1}}=\frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow-0.03 t_{1}=-\log 4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow t_{1}=\frac{\log 4}{0.03}=\frac{1.3863}{0.03}=46.21 {~m}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}\frac{d T}{d t}=-k(T-25){/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d T}{T-25}=-k d t{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d T}{T-25}=-k \int d t{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \log |T-25|=-k t+C{/tex}
    When {tex}t=0, T=85{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \log 60=C{/tex}
    Using in equation {tex}(a), \log |T-25|=-k t+\log 60{/tex} ...(b)
  2. When {tex}k=0.03, \log |T-25|=-0.03 t+\log 60{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left|\frac{T-25}{60}\right|=-0.03 t{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow T-25=60 . e^{-0.03 t}{/tex}
    When {tex}T=40, t=t_{1}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{15}{60}=e^{-0.03 t_{1}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow e^{-0.03 t_{1}}=\frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow-0.03 t_{1}=-\log 4{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow t_{1}=\frac{\log 4}{0.03}=\frac{1.3863}{0.03}=46.21 {~m}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the differential equation: {tex}x^{2} y d x-\left(x^{3}+y^{3}\right) d y=0{/tex}
question_text: Solve the differential equation: {tex}x^{2} y d x-\left(x^{3}+y^{3}\right) d y=0{/tex}
explanation: Given differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y x^{2}}{x^{3}+y^{3}}{/tex} Put {tex}y=v x{/tex} , so {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v x^{3}}{x^{3}+v^{3} x^{3}}=\frac{v}{1+v^{3}}{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-v^{4}}{1+v^{3}}{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{1}{v^{4}}+\frac{1}{v}\right) d v=\frac{-d x}{x}{/tex} Integrating we get {tex}\frac{-1}{3 v^{3}}+\log |v|=-\log |x|+C{/tex} {tex}\frac{-{x}^{3}}{3 {y}^{3}}+\log |y|={C}{/tex}
solution: Given differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y x^{2}}{x^{3}+y^{3}}{/tex} Put {tex}y=v x{/tex} , so {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v x^{3}}{x^{3}+v^{3} x^{3}}=\frac{v}{1+v^{3}}{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-v^{4}}{1+v^{3}}{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{1}{v^{4}}+\frac{1}{v}\right) d v=\frac{-d x}{x}{/tex} Integrating we get {tex}\frac{-1}{3 v^{3}}+\log |v|=-\log |x|+C{/tex} {tex}\frac{-{x}^{3}}{3 {y}^{3}}+\log |y|={C}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y x^{2}}{x^{3}+y^{3}}{/tex}
Put {tex}y=v x{/tex}, so {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v x^{3}}{x^{3}+v^{3} x^{3}}=\frac{v}{1+v^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-v^{4}}{1+v^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\left(\frac{1}{v^{4}}+\frac{1}{v}\right) d v=\frac{-d x}{x}{/tex}
Integrating we get
{tex}\frac{-1}{3 v^{3}}+\log |v|=-\log |x|+C{/tex}
{tex}\frac{-{x}^{3}}{3 {y}^{3}}+\log |y|={C}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Find the general solution of the differential equation
{tex}\left(2 x^{2}+y\right) d x=x d y .{/tex}
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\left(2 x^{2}+y\right) d x=x d y .{/tex}
explanation: {tex}\left(2 x^{2}+y\right) d x=x d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{1}{x} \cdot y=2 x{/tex} {tex}\text { I.F. }=e^{-\int \frac{1}{x} d x}=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} Solution is given by, {tex}y \cdot\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)=\int 2 x \cdot \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{x}=2 x+C \text { or } y=2 x^{2}+C x{/tex}
solution: {tex}\left(2 x^{2}+y\right) d x=x d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{1}{x} \cdot y=2 x{/tex} {tex}\text { I.F. }=e^{-\int \frac{1}{x} d x}=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} Solution is given by, {tex}y \cdot\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)=\int 2 x \cdot \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{x}=2 x+C \text { or } y=2 x^{2}+C x{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\left(2 x^{2}+y\right) d x=x d y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{1}{x} \cdot y=2 x{/tex}
{tex}\text { I.F. }=e^{-\int \frac{1}{x} d x}=\frac{1}{x}{/tex}
Solution is given by,
{tex}y \cdot\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)=\int 2 x \cdot \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{x}=2 x+C \text { or } y=2 x^{2}+C x{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Camphor is a waxy, colourless solid with strong aroma that evaporates through the process of sublimation, if left in the open at room temperature.

A cylindrical camphor tablet whose height is equal to its radius (r) evaporates when exposed to air such that the rate of reduction of its volume is proportional to its total surface area. Thus, {tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dt}}={kS}{/tex} is the differential equation, where {tex}V{/tex} is the volume, {tex}S{/tex} is the surface area and t is the time in hours.
Based upon the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Write the order and degree of the given differential equation. (1)
  2. Substituting {tex}{V}=\pi {r}^3{/tex} and {tex}{S}=2 \pi {r}^2{/tex}, we get the differential equation {tex}\frac{{dr}}{{dt}}=\frac{2}{3} {k}{/tex}. Solve it, given that {tex}{r}(0)=5 {~mm}{/tex}. (1)
    1. If it is given that {tex}r=3 {~mm}{/tex} when {tex}t=1{/tex} hour, find the value of k. Hence, find {tex}t{/tex} for {tex}r=0 {~mm}{/tex}. (2)

      OR

    2. If it is given that {tex}r=1 {~mm}{/tex} when {tex}t=1{/tex} hour, find the value of k. Hence, find {tex}t{/tex} for {tex}r=0 {~mm}{/tex}. (2)
question_text: Camphor is a waxy, colourless solid with strong aroma that evaporates through the process of sublimation, if left in the open at room temperature. A cylindrical camphor tablet whose height is equal to its radius (r) evaporates when exposed to air such that the rate of reduction of its volume is proportional to its total surface area. Thus, {tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dt}}={kS}{/tex} is the differential equation, where {tex}V{/tex} is the volume, {tex}S{/tex} is the surface area and t is the time in hours. Based upon the above information, answer the following questions: Write the order and degree of the given differential equation. (1) Substituting {tex}{V}=\pi {r}^3{/tex} and {tex}{S}=2 \pi {r}^2{/tex} , we get the differential equation {tex}\frac{{dr}}{{dt}}=\frac{2}{3} {k}{/tex} . Solve it, given that {tex}{r}(0)=5 {~mm}{/tex} . (1) If it is given that {tex}r=3 {~mm}{/tex} when {tex}t=1{/tex} hour, find the value of k. Hence, find {tex}t{/tex} for {tex}r=0 {~mm}{/tex} . (2) OR If it is given that {tex}r=1 {~mm}{/tex} when {tex}t=1{/tex} hour, find the value of k. Hence, find {tex}t{/tex} for {tex}r=0 {~mm}{/tex} . (2)
explanation: Order = 1, Degree = 1 Separating the variable and integrating, {tex}\int d r=\frac{2 k}{3} \int d t \Rightarrow r=\frac{2}{3} k t+C{/tex} + Putting {tex}{t}=0, r=5{/tex} , we get {tex}{C}=5{/tex} {tex}r=\frac{2}{3} k t+5{/tex} Putting {tex}{r}=3, {t}=1,3=\frac{2}{3} {k}(1)+5{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {k}=-3 {r}=-{2 t}+{5}{/tex} , For {tex}{r}={0}, {t}=\frac{{5}}{{2}}{/tex} hours or {tex}{2 . 5}{/tex} hours OR Putting {tex}r=1, t=1,1=\frac{2}{3} k+5 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow k=-6 \therefore r=-4 t+5{/tex} , For {tex}r=0, t=\frac{5}{4}{/tex} hours or 1.25 hours
solution: Order = 1, Degree = 1 Separating the variable and integrating, {tex}\int d r=\frac{2 k}{3} \int d t \Rightarrow r=\frac{2}{3} k t+C{/tex} + Putting {tex}{t}=0, r=5{/tex} , we get {tex}{C}=5{/tex} {tex}r=\frac{2}{3} k t+5{/tex} Putting {tex}{r}=3, {t}=1,3=\frac{2}{3} {k}(1)+5{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {k}=-3 {r}=-{2 t}+{5}{/tex} , For {tex}{r}={0}, {t}=\frac{{5}}{{2}}{/tex} hours or {tex}{2 . 5}{/tex} hours OR Putting {tex}r=1, t=1,1=\frac{2}{3} k+5 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow k=-6 \therefore r=-4 t+5{/tex} , For {tex}r=0, t=\frac{5}{4}{/tex} hours or 1.25 hours
solution_html:
1. Order = 1, Degree = 1
  2. Separating the variable and integrating, {tex}\int d r=\frac{2 k}{3} \int d t \Rightarrow r=\frac{2}{3} k t+C{/tex} +
    Putting {tex}{t}=0, r=5{/tex}, we get {tex}{C}=5{/tex}

question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\cos x-2 y{/tex}.
question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\cos x-2 y{/tex} .
explanation: The given differential equation can be written as: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+2 y=\cos x, \text { Taking } P=2, Q=\cos x{/tex} Integrating factor is given by, {tex}I=e^{\int 2 d x}=e^{2 x}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The solution is, {tex}y \cdot e^{2 x}=\int e^{2 x} \cos x d x{/tex} {tex}\text { Let, }{/tex} {tex}I_{1} =\int \cos x \cdot e^{2 x} d x{/tex} {tex}=\cos x \frac{e^{2 x}}{2}-\int(-\sin x) \frac{e^{2 x}}{2} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{e^{2 x} \cos x}{2}+\frac{1}{2}\left[\sin x \cdot \frac{e^{2 x}}{2}-\int \cos x \cdot \frac{e^{2 x}}{2} d x\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I_{1} =\frac{e^{2 x} \cos x}{2}+\frac{e^{2 x} \sin x}{4}-\frac{1}{4} I_{1}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow I_{1}=\frac{e^{2 x}}{5}(2 \cos x+\sin x){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The solution of the differential equation is {tex}y \cdot e^{2 x}=\frac{e^{2 x}}{5}(2 \cos x+\sin x)+C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{5}(2 \cos x+\sin x)+C e^{-2 x}{/tex}
solution: The given differential equation can be written as: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+2 y=\cos x, \text { Taking } P=2, Q=\cos x{/tex} Integrating factor is given by, {tex}I=e^{\int 2 d x}=e^{2 x}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The solution is, {tex}y \cdot e^{2 x}=\int e^{2 x} \cos x d x{/tex} {tex}\text { Let, }{/tex} {tex}I_{1} =\int \cos x \cdot e^{2 x} d x{/tex} {tex}=\cos x \frac{e^{2 x}}{2}-\int(-\sin x) \frac{e^{2 x}}{2} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{e^{2 x} \cos x}{2}+\frac{1}{2}\left[\sin x \cdot \frac{e^{2 x}}{2}-\int \cos x \cdot \frac{e^{2 x}}{2} d x\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I_{1} =\frac{e^{2 x} \cos x}{2}+\frac{e^{2 x} \sin x}{4}-\frac{1}{4} I_{1}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow I_{1}=\frac{e^{2 x}}{5}(2 \cos x+\sin x){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The solution of the differential equation is {tex}y \cdot e^{2 x}=\frac{e^{2 x}}{5}(2 \cos x+\sin x)+C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{5}(2 \cos x+\sin x)+C e^{-2 x}{/tex}
solution_html:
The given differential equation can be written as:
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+2 y=\cos x, \text { Taking } P=2, Q=\cos x{/tex}
Integrating factor is given by, {tex}I=e^{\int 2 d x}=e^{2 x}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The solution is, {tex}y \cdot e^{2 x}=\int e^{2 x} \cos x d x{/tex}
{tex}\text { Let, }{/tex}{tex}I_{1} =\int \cos x \cdot e^{2 x} d x{/tex} {tex}=\cos x \frac{e^{2 x}}{2}-\int(-\sin x) \frac{e^{2 x}}{2} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{e^{2 x} \cos x}{2}+\frac{1}{2}\left[\sin x \cdot \frac{e^{2 x}}{2}-\int \cos x \cdot \frac{e^{2 x}}{2} d x\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I_{1} =\frac{e^{2 x} \cos x}{2}+\frac{e^{2 x} \sin x}{4}-\frac{1}{4} I_{1}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow I_{1}=\frac{e^{2 x}}{5}(2 \cos x+\sin x){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The solution of the differential equation is
{tex}y \cdot e^{2 x}=\frac{e^{2 x}}{5}(2 \cos x+\sin x)+C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{5}(2 \cos x+\sin x)+C e^{-2 x}{/tex}

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation given by {tex} x^2 \frac{d y}{d x}-x y=x^2 \cos ^2\left(\frac{y}{2 x}\right){/tex}, given that when x = 1, y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation given by {tex} x^2 \frac{d y}{d x}-x y=x^2 \cos ^2\left(\frac{y}{2 x}\right){/tex} , given that when x = 1, y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}+\cos ^2\left(\frac{y}{2 x}\right){/tex} Put y = vx so that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {v}+{x} \frac{d v}{d x}=v+\cos ^2\left(\frac{v}{2}\right) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \int \sec ^2\left(\frac{v}{2}\right) d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2 \tan \left(\frac{v}{2}\right)=\log |x|+C {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2 \tan \left(\frac{y}{2 x}\right)=\log |x|+C {/tex} {tex} 2 \tan \frac{\pi}{4}=\log 1+C \Rightarrow C=2 \Rightarrow 2 \tan \left(\frac{y}{2 x}\right)=\log |x|+2{/tex}
solution: {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}+\cos ^2\left(\frac{y}{2 x}\right){/tex} Put y = vx so that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {v}+{x} \frac{d v}{d x}=v+\cos ^2\left(\frac{v}{2}\right) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \int \sec ^2\left(\frac{v}{2}\right) d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2 \tan \left(\frac{v}{2}\right)=\log |x|+C {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2 \tan \left(\frac{y}{2 x}\right)=\log |x|+C {/tex} {tex} 2 \tan \frac{\pi}{4}=\log 1+C \Rightarrow C=2 \Rightarrow 2 \tan \left(\frac{y}{2 x}\right)=\log |x|+2{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}+\cos ^2\left(\frac{y}{2 x}\right){/tex}
Put y = vx so that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {v}+{x} \frac{d v}{d x}=v+\cos ^2\left(\frac{v}{2}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \int \sec ^2\left(\frac{v}{2}\right) d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 2 \tan \left(\frac{v}{2}\right)=\log |x|+C {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 2 \tan \left(\frac{y}{2 x}\right)=\log |x|+C {/tex}
{tex} 2 \tan \frac{\pi}{4}=\log 1+C \Rightarrow C=2 \Rightarrow 2 \tan \left(\frac{y}{2 x}\right)=\log |x|+2{/tex}

question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex}\left(\tan ^{-1} y-x\right) d y=\left(1+y^{2}\right) d x{/tex}
question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex}\left(\tan ^{-1} y-x\right) d y=\left(1+y^{2}\right) d x{/tex}
explanation: The given differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\frac{x}{1+y^{2}}=\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} .......(i) Now (i) is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\mathrm{P}_{1} x=\mathrm{Q}_{1}{/tex} where, {tex}P_{1}=\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}Q_{1}=\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} Therefore, I.F = {tex}e^{\int \frac{1}{1+y^{2}} d y}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is {tex}x e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\int\left(\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}\right) e^{\tan ^{-1} y} d y+C{/tex} ......(ii) Let {tex}\mathrm{I}=\int\left(\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}\right) e^{\tan ^{-1} y} d y{/tex} Substituting tan -1 y = t so that {tex}\left(\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}\right) d y=d t{/tex} , we get {tex}\mathrm{I}=\int t e^{t} d t=t e^{t}-\int 1 . e^{t} d t=t e^{t}-e^{t}=e^{t}(t-1){/tex} or {tex}\mathrm{I}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right){/tex} Substituting the value of I in equation (ii), we get {tex}x \cdot e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right)+C{/tex} or {tex}x=\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right)+C e^{-\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} which is the general solution of the given differential equation.
solution: The given differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\frac{x}{1+y^{2}}=\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} .......(i) Now (i) is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\mathrm{P}_{1} x=\mathrm{Q}_{1}{/tex} where, {tex}P_{1}=\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}Q_{1}=\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} Therefore, I.F = {tex}e^{\int \frac{1}{1+y^{2}} d y}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is {tex}x e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\int\left(\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}\right) e^{\tan ^{-1} y} d y+C{/tex} ......(ii) Let {tex}\mathrm{I}=\int\left(\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}\right) e^{\tan ^{-1} y} d y{/tex} Substituting tan -1 y = t so that {tex}\left(\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}\right) d y=d t{/tex} , we get {tex}\mathrm{I}=\int t e^{t} d t=t e^{t}-\int 1 . e^{t} d t=t e^{t}-e^{t}=e^{t}(t-1){/tex} or {tex}\mathrm{I}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right){/tex} Substituting the value of I in equation (ii), we get {tex}x \cdot e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right)+C{/tex} or {tex}x=\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right)+C e^{-\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} which is the general solution of the given differential equation.
solution_html:
The given differential equation can be written as
{tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\frac{x}{1+y^{2}}=\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} .......(i)
Now (i) is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\mathrm{P}_{1} x=\mathrm{Q}_{1}{/tex} 
where, {tex}P_{1}=\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}Q_{1}=\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} 
Therefore, I.F = {tex}e^{\int \frac{1}{1+y^{2}} d y}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} 
Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is
{tex}x e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\int\left(\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}\right) e^{\tan ^{-1} y} d y+C{/tex} ......(ii)
Let {tex}\mathrm{I}=\int\left(\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}\right) e^{\tan ^{-1} y} d y{/tex} 
Substituting tan-1 y = t so that {tex}\left(\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}\right) d y=d t{/tex}, we get
{tex}\mathrm{I}=\int t e^{t} d t=t e^{t}-\int 1 . e^{t} d t=t e^{t}-e^{t}=e^{t}(t-1){/tex} 
or {tex}\mathrm{I}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right){/tex} 
Substituting the value of I in equation (ii), we get 
{tex}x \cdot e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right)+C{/tex} 
or {tex}x=\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right)+C e^{-\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} 
which is the general solution of the given differential equation.

question_html: The solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\log y}{/tex} is:
question_text: The solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\log y}{/tex} is:

options.text: y log y - y = x + c
options.html: y log y - y = x + c
options.explanation: The given differential equation is: {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\log y}{/tex} To solve this, we can separate the variables: {tex} \log y d y=d x{/tex} Integrating both sides: {tex} \int \log y d y=\int d x{/tex} The integral on the left side can be solved by integration by parts: {tex} \int \log y d y=y \log y-\int y \cdot \frac{1}{y} d y {/tex} {tex} \int \log y d y=y \log y-\int d y {/tex} {tex} \int \log y d y=y \log y-y+C {/tex} Therefore, the solution to the differential equation is: {tex} y \log y-y=x+C{/tex}

options.text: y log y + y = x + c
options.html: y log y + y = x + c

correct_option_text: y log y - y = x + c
explanation: The given differential equation is: {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\log y}{/tex} To solve this, we can separate the variables: {tex} \log y d y=d x{/tex} Integrating both sides: {tex} \int \log y d y=\int d x{/tex} The integral on the left side can be solved by integration by parts: {tex} \int \log y d y=y \log y-\int y \cdot \frac{1}{y} d y {/tex} {tex} \int \log y d y=y \log y-\int d y {/tex} {tex} \int \log y d y=y \log y-y+C {/tex} Therefore, the solution to the differential equation is: {tex} y \log y-y=x+C{/tex}

question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x^2+y^2}{2 x y}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x^2+y^2}{2 x y}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} \text { Put } y=v x \text { so that } \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x} {/tex} {tex} {v}+{x} \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{x^2+v^2 x^2}{2 x v x} {/tex} {tex} \int \frac{1}{x} d x=\int \frac{2 v}{1-v^2} d v {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log |x|=-\log \left|1-v^2\right|+\log C {/tex} {tex} \log \left|{x}\left(1-v^2\right)\right|=\log {C} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x\left(1-\frac{y^2}{x^2}\right)=C \text { or } x^2-y^2=C x {/tex}
solution: {tex} \text { Put } y=v x \text { so that } \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x} {/tex} {tex} {v}+{x} \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{x^2+v^2 x^2}{2 x v x} {/tex} {tex} \int \frac{1}{x} d x=\int \frac{2 v}{1-v^2} d v {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log |x|=-\log \left|1-v^2\right|+\log C {/tex} {tex} \log \left|{x}\left(1-v^2\right)\right|=\log {C} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x\left(1-\frac{y^2}{x^2}\right)=C \text { or } x^2-y^2=C x {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \text { Put } y=v x \text { so that } \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x} {/tex}
{tex} {v}+{x} \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{x^2+v^2 x^2}{2 x v x} {/tex}
{tex} \int \frac{1}{x} d x=\int \frac{2 v}{1-v^2} d v {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \log |x|=-\log \left|1-v^2\right|+\log C {/tex}
{tex} \log \left|{x}\left(1-v^2\right)\right|=\log {C} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x\left(1-\frac{y^2}{x^2}\right)=C \text { or } x^2-y^2=C x {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation given by {tex}2 x y+y^2-2 x^2 \frac{d y}{d x}=0 ; y=2, \text { when } x=1{/tex}.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation given by {tex}2 x y+y^2-2 x^2 \frac{d y}{d x}=0 ; y=2, \text { when } x=1{/tex} .
explanation: Given differential equation can be written as {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 x y+y^2}{2 x^2}=\frac{y}{x}+\frac{y^2}{2 x^2}{/tex} Let {tex}y=v x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} The equation becomes {tex} x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{2} v^2 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{v^2}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{d x}{x} {/tex} Integrating both sides, we get {tex} \frac{-1}{v}=\frac{1}{2} \log |x|+C {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow-\frac{x}{y}=\frac{1}{2} \log |x|+C {/tex} {tex} y=2, x=1 \text { gives } C=-\frac{1}{2} {/tex} The particular solution is {tex} -\frac{x}{y}=\frac{1}{2} \log |x|-\frac{1}{2} \text { or, } y=\frac{2 x}{1-\log |x|}{/tex}
solution: Given differential equation can be written as {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 x y+y^2}{2 x^2}=\frac{y}{x}+\frac{y^2}{2 x^2}{/tex} Let {tex}y=v x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} The equation becomes {tex} x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{2} v^2 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{v^2}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{d x}{x} {/tex} Integrating both sides, we get {tex} \frac{-1}{v}=\frac{1}{2} \log |x|+C {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow-\frac{x}{y}=\frac{1}{2} \log |x|+C {/tex} {tex} y=2, x=1 \text { gives } C=-\frac{1}{2} {/tex} The particular solution is {tex} -\frac{x}{y}=\frac{1}{2} \log |x|-\frac{1}{2} \text { or, } y=\frac{2 x}{1-\log |x|}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given differential equation can be written as
{tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 x y+y^2}{2 x^2}=\frac{y}{x}+\frac{y^2}{2 x^2}{/tex}
Let {tex}y=v x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
The equation becomes
{tex} x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{2} v^2 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{v^2}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{d x}{x} {/tex}
Integrating both sides, we get
{tex} \frac{-1}{v}=\frac{1}{2} \log |x|+C {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow-\frac{x}{y}=\frac{1}{2} \log |x|+C {/tex}
{tex} y=2, x=1 \text { gives } C=-\frac{1}{2} {/tex}
The particular solution is
{tex} -\frac{x}{y}=\frac{1}{2} \log |x|-\frac{1}{2} \text { or, } y=\frac{2 x}{1-\log |x|}{/tex}

question_text: The number of solutions of differential equation {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}-y=1{/tex} , given that y(0) = 1, is:

options.explanation: The differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-y=1{/tex} is a first-order linear equation. Its general solution is {tex}y(x)=C e^x+e^x-1{/tex} . Given the initial condition {tex}y(0)=1{/tex} , we find {tex}C=0{/tex} . Thus, there is exactly one solution: {tex}y(x)=e^x-1{/tex} . The answer is 1 .

question_text: The integrating factor of the differential equation {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=x^4-3 x{/tex} is:

options.explanation: {tex} x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=x^4-3 x {/tex} {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}=\frac{x\left(x^3-3\right)}{x} {/tex} {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}=x^3-3 {/tex} Compare with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} . {tex} P =-\frac{1}{x} {/tex} {tex} I F =e^{\int P d x} {/tex} {tex} =e^{\int-\frac{1}{x} d x} {/tex} {tex} =e^{-\log x} {/tex} {tex} =e^{\log -1} {/tex} {tex} =x^{-1} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{x} {/tex}

question_html: The differential equation {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{F}(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}){/tex} will not be a homogeneous differential equation, if F(x, y) is:
question_text: The differential equation {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{F}(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}){/tex} will not be a homogeneous differential equation, if F(x, y) is:

options.explanation: (i.) {tex}f(x, y)=\cos x-\sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} Replace {tex}x \rightarrow \lambda x y \rightarrow \lambda y{/tex} . {tex} f(\lambda x, \lambda y) =\cos \lambda x-\sin \left(\frac{\lambda y}{\lambda x}\right) {/tex} {tex} =\cos \lambda x-\sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right) {/tex} {tex} \neq \lambda^n f(\Delta x, y) {/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Not Homogenous. (ii) {tex} f(x, y)=\frac{y}{x} {/tex} {tex} f(\lambda x, \lambda y)=\frac{\lambda y}{\lambda x}=\lambda^0 \frac{y}{x}=\lambda^0 f(x, y) {/tex} Hence it is homog. {tex} \text { (iii) } f(x, y)=\frac{x^2+y^2}{x y} {/tex} {tex} f(\lambda x, \lambda y)=\frac{\lambda^2 x^2+\lambda^2 y^2}{\lambda x \lambda y} {/tex} {tex} =\lambda^0 \frac{x^2+y^2}{x y}=\lambda^0 f(x, y) {/tex} {tex} \text { Hence homog. } {/tex} {tex} \text { (r) } \cos ^2\left(\frac{x}{y}\right) {/tex} {tex} f(\lambda x, \lambda y)=\cos ^2\left(\frac{\lambda x}{\lambda y}\right)=\lambda^0 \cos ^2\left(\frac{x}{y}\right) {/tex} Hence homogeneous

explanation: (i.) {tex}f(x, y)=\cos x-\sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} Replace {tex}x \rightarrow \lambda x y \rightarrow \lambda y{/tex} . {tex} f(\lambda x, \lambda y) =\cos \lambda x-\sin \left(\frac{\lambda y}{\lambda x}\right) {/tex} {tex} =\cos \lambda x-\sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right) {/tex} {tex} \neq \lambda^n f(\Delta x, y) {/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Not Homogenous. (ii) {tex} f(x, y)=\frac{y}{x} {/tex} {tex} f(\lambda x, \lambda y)=\frac{\lambda y}{\lambda x}=\lambda^0 \frac{y}{x}=\lambda^0 f(x, y) {/tex} Hence it is homog. {tex} \text { (iii) } f(x, y)=\frac{x^2+y^2}{x y} {/tex} {tex} f(\lambda x, \lambda y)=\frac{\lambda^2 x^2+\lambda^2 y^2}{\lambda x \lambda y} {/tex} {tex} =\lambda^0 \frac{x^2+y^2}{x y}=\lambda^0 f(x, y) {/tex} {tex} \text { Hence homog. } {/tex} {tex} \text { (r) } \cos ^2\left(\frac{x}{y}\right) {/tex} {tex} f(\lambda x, \lambda y)=\cos ^2\left(\frac{\lambda x}{\lambda y}\right)=\lambda^0 \cos ^2\left(\frac{x}{y}\right) {/tex} Hence homogeneous

question_html:
A bacteria sample of certain number of bacteria is observed to grow exponentially in a given amount of time. Using exponential growth model, the rate of growth of this sample of bacteria is calculated.

The differential equation representing the growth of bacteria is given as: {tex}\frac{d P}{d t}=k P{/tex}, where {tex}P{/tex} is the population of bacteria at any time '{tex}t{/tex}'.
Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Obtain the general solution of the given differential equation and express it as an exponential function of 't'. (2)
  2. If population of bacteria is 1000 at {tex}{t}=0{/tex}, and 2000 at {tex}{t}=1{/tex}, find the value of k. (2)
question_text: A bacteria sample of certain number of bacteria is observed to grow exponentially in a given amount of time. Using exponential growth model, the rate of growth of this sample of bacteria is calculated. The differential equation representing the growth of bacteria is given as: {tex}\frac{d P}{d t}=k P{/tex} , where {tex}P{/tex} is the population of bacteria at any time ' {tex}t{/tex} '. Based on the above information, answer the following questions: Obtain the general solution of the given differential equation and express it as an exponential function of 't'. (2) If population of bacteria is 1000 at {tex}{t}=0{/tex} , and 2000 at {tex}{t}=1{/tex} , find the value of k. (2)
explanation: {tex}\frac{d P}{d t}=k P \Rightarrow \int \frac{d P}{P}=\int k d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log P=k t+C \text { or } P=e^{k t+C}{/tex} {tex}\log P=k t+C{/tex} {tex}\text { when } t=0, P= 1000{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow C=\log 1000{/tex} {tex}\text { when } t=1, P=2000{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log 2000=k+\log 1000{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow k=\log 2{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{d P}{d t}=k P \Rightarrow \int \frac{d P}{P}=\int k d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log P=k t+C \text { or } P=e^{k t+C}{/tex} {tex}\log P=k t+C{/tex} {tex}\text { when } t=0, P= 1000{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow C=\log 1000{/tex} {tex}\text { when } t=1, P=2000{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log 2000=k+\log 1000{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow k=\log 2{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}\frac{d P}{d t}=k P \Rightarrow \int \frac{d P}{P}=\int k d t{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \log P=k t+C \text { or } P=e^{k t+C}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\log P=k t+C{/tex}
    {tex}\text { when } t=0, P= 1000{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow C=\log 1000{/tex}
    {tex}\text { when } t=1, P=2000{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log 2000=k+\log 1000{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow k=\log 2{/tex}

question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=x y \ \log x \ \log y{/tex}.
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=x y \ \log x \ \log y{/tex} .
explanation: Separating the variables and integrating, {tex}\int \frac{d y}{y \log y}=\int x \log x d x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log |\log y|{/tex} {tex}=\log x\left(\frac{x^2}{2}\right)-\int \frac{1}{x} \cdot \frac{x^2}{2} d x+c {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log |\log y|=\frac{x^2}{2} \log x-\frac{x^2}{4}+c {/tex}
solution: Separating the variables and integrating, {tex}\int \frac{d y}{y \log y}=\int x \log x d x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log |\log y|{/tex} {tex}=\log x\left(\frac{x^2}{2}\right)-\int \frac{1}{x} \cdot \frac{x^2}{2} d x+c {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log |\log y|=\frac{x^2}{2} \log x-\frac{x^2}{4}+c {/tex}
solution_html:
Separating the variables and integrating, {tex}\int \frac{d y}{y \log y}=\int x \log x d x{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \log |\log y|{/tex}{tex}=\log x\left(\frac{x^2}{2}\right)-\int \frac{1}{x} \cdot \frac{x^2}{2} d x+c {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \log |\log y|=\frac{x^2}{2} \log x-\frac{x^2}{4}+c {/tex}

question_html:
Find the particular solution of the differential equation:
{tex}{x} \cos {y} {dy}=\left({x} {e}^{{x}} \log {x}+{e}^{{x}}\right) {dx} {/tex}{tex}\text { given that } {y}=\frac{\pi}{2} \text { when } {x}=1{/tex}
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation: {tex}{x} \cos {y} {dy}=\left({x} {e}^{{x}} \log {x}+{e}^{{x}}\right) {dx} {/tex} {tex}\text { given that } {y}=\frac{\pi}{2} \text { when } {x}=1{/tex}
explanation: Separating the variables and integrating: {tex} \int \cos y d y=\int e^x\left(\log x+\frac{1}{x}\right) d x {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \sin y=e^x \log x+c {/tex} Using {tex}y=\frac{\pi}{2}, x=1{/tex} , we get {tex}c=1{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} the particular solution is, {tex}\sin y=1+e^x \log x{/tex}
solution: Separating the variables and integrating: {tex} \int \cos y d y=\int e^x\left(\log x+\frac{1}{x}\right) d x {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \sin y=e^x \log x+c {/tex} Using {tex}y=\frac{\pi}{2}, x=1{/tex} , we get {tex}c=1{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} the particular solution is, {tex}\sin y=1+e^x \log x{/tex}
solution_html:
Separating the variables and integrating:
{tex} \int \cos y d y=\int e^x\left(\log x+\frac{1}{x}\right) d x {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \sin y=e^x \log x+c {/tex}
Using {tex}y=\frac{\pi}{2}, x=1{/tex}, we get {tex}c=1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} the particular solution is, {tex}\sin y=1+e^x \log x{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Solve the following differential equation :
{tex}x^{2} d y+y(x+y) d x=0{/tex}
question_text: Solve the following differential equation : {tex}x^{2} d y+y(x+y) d x=0{/tex}
explanation: {tex}x^2 d y+y(x+y) d x=0 \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{y}{x}-\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^2{/tex} Putting {tex}\frac{y}{x}=v \Rightarrow y=v x, \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=-v-v^2,{/tex} separating the variable and integrating {tex}\int \frac{1}{v^2+2 v} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{(v+1)^2-1} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \log \left|\frac{v}{v+2}\right|=\log \left|\frac{C}{x}\right|{/tex} The solution of the differential equation is, {tex}\left\|\frac{y}{y+2 x}\right\|=\frac{C^{2}}{x^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}x^{2} y=k(y+2 x){/tex}
solution: {tex}x^2 d y+y(x+y) d x=0 \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{y}{x}-\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^2{/tex} Putting {tex}\frac{y}{x}=v \Rightarrow y=v x, \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=-v-v^2,{/tex} separating the variable and integrating {tex}\int \frac{1}{v^2+2 v} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{(v+1)^2-1} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \log \left|\frac{v}{v+2}\right|=\log \left|\frac{C}{x}\right|{/tex} The solution of the differential equation is, {tex}\left\|\frac{y}{y+2 x}\right\|=\frac{C^{2}}{x^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}x^{2} y=k(y+2 x){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}x^2 d y+y(x+y) d x=0 \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{y}{x}-\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^2{/tex}
Putting {tex}\frac{y}{x}=v \Rightarrow y=v x, \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=-v-v^2,{/tex}
separating the variable and integrating
{tex}\int \frac{1}{v^2+2 v} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{(v+1)^2-1} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \log \left|\frac{v}{v+2}\right|=\log \left|\frac{C}{x}\right|{/tex}
The solution of the differential equation is, {tex}\left\|\frac{y}{y+2 x}\right\|=\frac{C^{2}}{x^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}x^{2} y=k(y+2 x){/tex}

question_html:
Find the particular solution of the differential equation
{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}-2 x {y}=3 x^{2} {e}^{x^{2}} ; {y}(0)=5{/tex}
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}-2 x {y}=3 x^{2} {e}^{x^{2}} ; {y}(0)=5{/tex}
explanation: Given differential equation is a linear order differential equation with: {tex}P=-2 x, Q=3 x^2 e^{x^2}{/tex} {tex}\text { Integrating Factor }=e^{\int-2 x d x}=e^{-x^2}{/tex} The general solution is: {tex}y \cdot e^{-x^2}=\int e^{-x^2} \cdot 3 x^2 e^{x^2} d x+C \Rightarrow y \cdot e^{-x^2}=x^3+C{/tex} Putting {tex}x=0, y=5{/tex} , we get, {tex}C=5{/tex} ∴ The Particular solution is: {tex}{y} \cdot {e}^{-{x}^{{2}}}={x}^{{3}}+5{/tex} or {tex}{y}=\left({x}^{{3}}+5\right) {e}^{{x}^{{2}}}{/tex}
solution: Given differential equation is a linear order differential equation with: {tex}P=-2 x, Q=3 x^2 e^{x^2}{/tex} {tex}\text { Integrating Factor }=e^{\int-2 x d x}=e^{-x^2}{/tex} The general solution is: {tex}y \cdot e^{-x^2}=\int e^{-x^2} \cdot 3 x^2 e^{x^2} d x+C \Rightarrow y \cdot e^{-x^2}=x^3+C{/tex} Putting {tex}x=0, y=5{/tex} , we get, {tex}C=5{/tex} ∴ The Particular solution is: {tex}{y} \cdot {e}^{-{x}^{{2}}}={x}^{{3}}+5{/tex} or {tex}{y}=\left({x}^{{3}}+5\right) {e}^{{x}^{{2}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given differential equation is a linear order differential equation with:
{tex}P=-2 x, Q=3 x^2 e^{x^2}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Integrating Factor }=e^{\int-2 x d x}=e^{-x^2}{/tex}
The general solution is: {tex}y \cdot e^{-x^2}=\int e^{-x^2} \cdot 3 x^2 e^{x^2} d x+C \Rightarrow y \cdot e^{-x^2}=x^3+C{/tex}
Putting {tex}x=0, y=5{/tex}, we get, {tex}C=5{/tex}
∴ The Particular solution is: {tex}{y} \cdot {e}^{-{x}^{{2}}}={x}^{{3}}+5{/tex} or {tex}{y}=\left({x}^{{3}}+5\right) {e}^{{x}^{{2}}}{/tex}

{tex}\left(x {e}^{\frac{{y}}{x}}+{y}\right) {d} x{/tex} = x dy, given that y = 1 when x = 1
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\left(x {e}^{\frac{{y}}{x}}+{y}\right) {d} x{/tex} = x dy, given that y = 1 when x = 1
explanation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=e^{\frac{y}{x}}+\frac{y}{x}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} so, its a homogeneous differential equation Let {tex}y=v x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} Now, {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=e^{v}+v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int e^{-v} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-e^{-v}=\log |x|+c {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-e^{\frac{-y}{x}}=\log |x|+c{/tex} ...(1) Now, {tex}x=1, y=1{/tex} , gives {tex}c=-e^{-1}{/tex} Thus, {tex}\log |x |+e^{\frac{-y}{x}}=e^{-1}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=e^{\frac{y}{x}}+\frac{y}{x}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} so, its a homogeneous differential equation Let {tex}y=v x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} Now, {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=e^{v}+v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int e^{-v} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-e^{-v}=\log |x|+c {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-e^{\frac{-y}{x}}=\log |x|+c{/tex} ...(1) Now, {tex}x=1, y=1{/tex} , gives {tex}c=-e^{-1}{/tex} Thus, {tex}\log |x |+e^{\frac{-y}{x}}=e^{-1}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=e^{\frac{y}{x}}+\frac{y}{x}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} so, its a homogeneous differential equation
Let {tex}y=v x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
Now, {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=e^{v}+v{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int e^{-v} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-e^{-v}=\log |x|+c {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-e^{\frac{-y}{x}}=\log |x|+c{/tex}...(1)
Now, {tex}x=1, y=1{/tex}, gives {tex}c=-e^{-1}{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\log |x |+e^{\frac{-y}{x}}=e^{-1}{/tex}

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}={y} \cot 2 x{/tex}, given that {tex}{y}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=2{/tex}.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}={y} \cot 2 x{/tex} , given that {tex}{y}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=2{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=y \cot 2 x \Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{y}=\int \cot 2 x\ d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log|y|=\frac{1}{2} \log |\sin 2 x|+\log c{/tex} {tex}y=c . \sqrt{\sin 2 x}{/tex} {tex}\text { when } y\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=2, \text { gives } c=2{/tex} {tex}\therefore y=2 \sqrt{\sin 2 x}{/tex} is the required Particular solution of given D.E.
solution: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=y \cot 2 x \Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{y}=\int \cot 2 x\ d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log|y|=\frac{1}{2} \log |\sin 2 x|+\log c{/tex} {tex}y=c . \sqrt{\sin 2 x}{/tex} {tex}\text { when } y\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=2, \text { gives } c=2{/tex} {tex}\therefore y=2 \sqrt{\sin 2 x}{/tex} is the required Particular solution of given D.E.
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=y \cot 2 x \Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{y}=\int \cot 2 x\ d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log|y|=\frac{1}{2} \log |\sin 2 x|+\log c{/tex}
{tex}y=c . \sqrt{\sin 2 x}{/tex}
{tex}\text { when } y\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=2, \text { gives } c=2{/tex}
{tex}\therefore y=2 \sqrt{\sin 2 x}{/tex} is the required Particular solution of given D.E.

question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}y d x-x d y+(x \log x) d x=0{/tex}
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}y d x-x d y+(x \log x) d x=0{/tex}
explanation: The given differential equation can be written as, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{1}{x} y=\log x{/tex} {tex} \therefore P=-\frac{1}{x}, Q=\log x {/tex} {tex} \text { I.F. }=e^{\int-\frac{1}{x} d x}{/tex} {tex}=e^{-\log x}=e^{\log \frac{1}{x}}=\frac{1}{x} {/tex} The solution of the differential equation is: {tex} y \cdot \frac{1}{x}=\int \log x \cdot \frac{1}{x} d x {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{y}{x}=\frac{(\log x)^2}{2}+c, \text { or } y=\frac{1}{2} x \cdot(\log x)^2+c x {/tex}
solution: The given differential equation can be written as, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{1}{x} y=\log x{/tex} {tex} \therefore P=-\frac{1}{x}, Q=\log x {/tex} {tex} \text { I.F. }=e^{\int-\frac{1}{x} d x}{/tex} {tex}=e^{-\log x}=e^{\log \frac{1}{x}}=\frac{1}{x} {/tex} The solution of the differential equation is: {tex} y \cdot \frac{1}{x}=\int \log x \cdot \frac{1}{x} d x {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{y}{x}=\frac{(\log x)^2}{2}+c, \text { or } y=\frac{1}{2} x \cdot(\log x)^2+c x {/tex}
solution_html:
The given differential equation can be written as, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{1}{x} y=\log x{/tex}
{tex} \therefore P=-\frac{1}{x}, Q=\log x {/tex}
{tex} \text { I.F. }=e^{\int-\frac{1}{x} d x}{/tex} {tex}=e^{-\log x}=e^{\log \frac{1}{x}}=\frac{1}{x} {/tex}
The solution of the differential equation is:
{tex} y \cdot \frac{1}{x}=\int \log x \cdot \frac{1}{x} d x {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{y}{x}=\frac{(\log x)^2}{2}+c, \text { or } y=\frac{1}{2} x \cdot(\log x)^2+c x {/tex}

explanation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=e^{\frac{y}{x}}+\frac{y}{x}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} so, its a homogeneous differential equation Let {tex}y=v x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} Now, {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=e^{v}+v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int e^{-v} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-e^{-v}=\log |x|+c \Rightarrow-e^{\frac{-y}{x}}=\log |x|+c \ldots{/tex} (1) Now, {tex}x=1, y=1{/tex} , gives {tex}c=-e^{-1}{/tex} Thus, {tex}\log |x|+e^{\frac{-y}{x}}=e^{-1}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=e^{\frac{y}{x}}+\frac{y}{x}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} so, its a homogeneous differential equation Let {tex}y=v x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} Now, {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=e^{v}+v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int e^{-v} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-e^{-v}=\log |x|+c \Rightarrow-e^{\frac{-y}{x}}=\log |x|+c \ldots{/tex} (1) Now, {tex}x=1, y=1{/tex} , gives {tex}c=-e^{-1}{/tex} Thus, {tex}\log |x|+e^{\frac{-y}{x}}=e^{-1}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=e^{\frac{y}{x}}+\frac{y}{x}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} so, its a homogeneous differential equation
Let {tex}y=v x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
Now, {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=e^{v}+v{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int e^{-v} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-e^{-v}=\log |x|+c \Rightarrow-e^{\frac{-y}{x}}=\log |x|+c \ldots{/tex}(1)
Now, {tex}x=1, y=1{/tex}, gives {tex}c=-e^{-1}{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\log |x|+e^{\frac{-y}{x}}=e^{-1}{/tex}

question_html:
Find the general solution of the differential equation
ex tan y dx + (1 - ex)sec2y dy = 0.
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation e x tan y dx + (1 - e x )sec 2 y dy = 0.
explanation: According to the question,The given differential equation is e x tan y dx + (1 - e x )sec 2 y dy = 0 On separating the variables, we get {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { e ^ { x } } { e ^ { x } - 1 } d x = \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } y } { \tan y } d y{/tex} Therefore,on integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac { e ^ { x } } { e ^ { x } - 1 } d x = \int \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } y } { \tan y } d y{/tex} Therefore,on putting e x - 1 = t and tan y = z, we get, {tex}\Rightarrow e ^ { x } d x = d t \text { and } \sec ^ { 2 } y d y = d z{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \int \frac { d t } { t } = \int \frac { d z } { z }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log | t | = \log | z | + \log C \left[ \because \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x = \log | x | \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| e ^ { x } - 1 \right| = \log | \tan y | + \log C{/tex} {tex}[ \because \log m + \log n = \log m n ]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad e ^ { x } - 1 = C \tan y{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \tan y = \frac { e ^ { x } - 1 } { C }{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad y = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { e ^ { x } - 1 } { C } \right){/tex} which is the required solution.
solution: According to the question,The given differential equation is e x tan y dx + (1 - e x )sec 2 y dy = 0 On separating the variables, we get {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { e ^ { x } } { e ^ { x } - 1 } d x = \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } y } { \tan y } d y{/tex} Therefore,on integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac { e ^ { x } } { e ^ { x } - 1 } d x = \int \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } y } { \tan y } d y{/tex} Therefore,on putting e x - 1 = t and tan y = z, we get, {tex}\Rightarrow e ^ { x } d x = d t \text { and } \sec ^ { 2 } y d y = d z{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \int \frac { d t } { t } = \int \frac { d z } { z }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log | t | = \log | z | + \log C \left[ \because \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x = \log | x | \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| e ^ { x } - 1 \right| = \log | \tan y | + \log C{/tex} {tex}[ \because \log m + \log n = \log m n ]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad e ^ { x } - 1 = C \tan y{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \tan y = \frac { e ^ { x } - 1 } { C }{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad y = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { e ^ { x } - 1 } { C } \right){/tex} which is the required solution.
solution_html:
According to the question,The given differential equation is
ex tan y dx + (1 - ex)sec2y dy = 0
On separating the variables, we get
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { e ^ { x } } { e ^ { x } - 1 } d x = \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } y } { \tan y } d y{/tex}
Therefore,on integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int \frac { e ^ { x } } { e ^ { x } - 1 } d x = \int \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } y } { \tan y } d y{/tex}
Therefore,on putting ex - 1 = t and tan y = z, we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow e ^ { x } d x = d t \text { and } \sec ^ { 2 } y d y = d z{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \int \frac { d t } { t } = \int \frac { d z } { z }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log | t | = \log | z | + \log C \left[ \because \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x = \log | x | \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| e ^ { x } - 1 \right| = \log | \tan y | + \log C{/tex} {tex}[ \because \log m + \log n = \log m n ]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad e ^ { x } - 1 = C \tan y{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \tan y = \frac { e ^ { x } - 1 } { C }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad y = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { e ^ { x } - 1 } { C } \right){/tex}
which is the required solution.

Solve the differential equation given by
x dy - y dx - {tex}\sqrt {\text x^2 + \text y^2}{/tex} dx = 0.
question_text: Solve the differential equation given by x dy - y dx - {tex}\sqrt {\text x^2 + \text y^2}{/tex} dx = 0.
explanation: Given differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text y}{\text x}+\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{\text y}{\text x}\right)^2}{/tex} ...(i) Let y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text {dv}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = v + x {tex}\frac{\text {dv}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} substituting in (i) We get v + x {tex}\frac{\text {dv}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = v + {tex}\sqrt{1+\text v^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text {dv}}{\sqrt{1+\text v^2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text {dx}}{\text x}{/tex} Integrating both sides, we get log | {tex}\sqrt{1+\text v^2}+\text v{/tex} | = log |x| + log C y + {tex}\sqrt{\text x^2+\text y^2}{/tex} = C x 2
solution: Given differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text y}{\text x}+\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{\text y}{\text x}\right)^2}{/tex} ...(i) Let y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text {dv}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = v + x {tex}\frac{\text {dv}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} substituting in (i) We get v + x {tex}\frac{\text {dv}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = v + {tex}\sqrt{1+\text v^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text {dv}}{\sqrt{1+\text v^2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text {dx}}{\text x}{/tex} Integrating both sides, we get log | {tex}\sqrt{1+\text v^2}+\text v{/tex} | = log |x| + log C y + {tex}\sqrt{\text x^2+\text y^2}{/tex} = C x 2
solution_html:
Given differential equation can be written as
{tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text y}{\text x}+\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{\text y}{\text x}\right)^2}{/tex} ...(i)
Let y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text {dv}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = v + x {tex}\frac{\text {dv}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} substituting in (i)
We get v + x {tex}\frac{\text {dv}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = v + {tex}\sqrt{1+\text v^2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text {dv}}{\sqrt{1+\text v^2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text {dx}}{\text x}{/tex}
Integrating both sides, we get
log |{tex}\sqrt{1+\text v^2}+\text v{/tex}| = log |x| + log C
y + {tex}\sqrt{\text x^2+\text y^2}{/tex} = C x2

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac {\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} + sec2 x{tex}\cdot{/tex}y = tan x{tex}\cdot{/tex}sec2x, given that y(0) = 0.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac {\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} + sec 2 x {tex}\cdot{/tex} y = tan x {tex}\cdot{/tex} sec 2 x, given that y(0) = 0.
explanation: Integrating factor = {tex}\text e^{\int \sec ^2 \text x\; \text {dx}}{/tex} = e tan x Solution is ye tan x = {tex}\int{/tex} tan x sec 2 x e tan x dx + C Let tan x = t sec 2 x dx = dt {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} e tan x tan x sec 2 x dx = {tex}\int{/tex} e t t dt = e t (t - 1) {tex}\therefore{/tex} ye tan x = e tan x (tan x - 1) + C y(0) = 0 gives C = 1 Particular solution is ye tan x = e tan x (tan x - 1) + 1 or y = tanx - 1 + e -tanx ////////////////////
solution: Integrating factor = {tex}\text e^{\int \sec ^2 \text x\; \text {dx}}{/tex} = e tan x Solution is ye tan x = {tex}\int{/tex} tan x sec 2 x e tan x dx + C Let tan x = t sec 2 x dx = dt {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} e tan x tan x sec 2 x dx = {tex}\int{/tex} e t t dt = e t (t - 1) {tex}\therefore{/tex} ye tan x = e tan x (tan x - 1) + C y(0) = 0 gives C = 1 Particular solution is ye tan x = e tan x (tan x - 1) + 1 or y = tanx - 1 + e -tanx ////////////////////
solution_html:
Integrating factor = {tex}\text e^{\int \sec ^2 \text x\; \text {dx}}{/tex} = etan x
Solution is yetan x = {tex}\int{/tex} tan x sec2 x etan x dx + C
Let tan x = t sec2 x dx = dt
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} etan x tan x sec2 x dx = {tex}\int{/tex} et t dt = et(t - 1)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} yetan x = etan x (tan x - 1) + C
y(0) = 0 gives C = 1
Particular solution is yetan x = etan x (tan x - 1) + 1 or y = tanx - 1 + e-tanx
////////////////////
