question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation: {tex}e^{\frac{d y} {d x}}{/tex} = x2.
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation: {tex}e^{\frac{d y} {d x}}{/tex} = x 2 .
explanation: {tex}e^{\frac{d y} {d x}}{/tex} = x 2 {tex}\frac {dy}{dx}{/tex} = log x 2 = 2log x {tex}\int d y=2 \int \log x d x{/tex} y= 2[log x {tex}\times{/tex} x - {tex}\int 1 d x{/tex} ] y = 2[x log x - x] + C
solution: {tex}e^{\frac{d y} {d x}}{/tex} = x 2 {tex}\frac {dy}{dx}{/tex} = log x 2 = 2log x {tex}\int d y=2 \int \log x d x{/tex} y= 2[log x {tex}\times{/tex} x - {tex}\int 1 d x{/tex} ] y = 2[x log x - x] + C
solution_html:
{tex}e^{\frac{d y} {d x}}{/tex} = x2
{tex}\frac {dy}{dx}{/tex} = log x2 = 2log x
{tex}\int d y=2 \int \log x d x{/tex}
y= 2[log x {tex}\times{/tex} x - {tex}\int 1 d x{/tex}]
y = 2[x log x - x] + C

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation: ydx + (x - y2) dy = 0
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation: ydx + (x - y 2 ) dy = 0
explanation: The given differential equation is, ydx+ (x - y 2 ) dy = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ydx= (y 2 - x) dy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{y^{2}-x}{y}=y-\frac{x}{y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\frac{x}{y}=y{/tex} This is a linear differential equation of the form: {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}{/tex} + px = Q (where P = {tex}\frac{1}{y}{/tex} and Q = y) Now, I.F = {tex}e^{\int \ p\ d y}=e^{\int \frac{1}{y} d y}{/tex} = e log y = y The general solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation, x(I.F.) = {tex}\int {/tex} (Q {tex}\times{/tex} I.F.) dy + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy = {tex}\int {/tex} (y . y) dy + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy = {tex}\int {/tex} y 2 dy + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy = {tex}\frac{y^{3}}{3}{/tex} + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{y^{2}}{3}+\frac{C}{y}{/tex}
solution: The given differential equation is, ydx+ (x - y 2 ) dy = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ydx= (y 2 - x) dy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{y^{2}-x}{y}=y-\frac{x}{y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\frac{x}{y}=y{/tex} This is a linear differential equation of the form: {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}{/tex} + px = Q (where P = {tex}\frac{1}{y}{/tex} and Q = y) Now, I.F = {tex}e^{\int \ p\ d y}=e^{\int \frac{1}{y} d y}{/tex} = e log y = y The general solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation, x(I.F.) = {tex}\int {/tex} (Q {tex}\times{/tex} I.F.) dy + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy = {tex}\int {/tex} (y . y) dy + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy = {tex}\int {/tex} y 2 dy + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy = {tex}\frac{y^{3}}{3}{/tex} + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{y^{2}}{3}+\frac{C}{y}{/tex}
solution_html:
The given differential equation is,
ydx+ (x - y2) dy = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ydx= (y2 - x) dy
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{y^{2}-x}{y}=y-\frac{x}{y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\frac{x}{y}=y{/tex}
This is a linear differential equation of the form:
{tex}\frac{d x}{d y}{/tex} + px = Q (where P = {tex}\frac{1}{y}{/tex} and Q = y)
Now, I.F = {tex}e^{\int \ p\ d y}=e^{\int \frac{1}{y} d y}{/tex} = elog y = y
The general solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation,
x(I.F.) = {tex}\int {/tex} (Q {tex}\times{/tex} I.F.) dy + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy = {tex}\int {/tex} (y . y) dy + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy = {tex}\int {/tex}y2dy + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy = {tex}\frac{y^{3}}{3}{/tex} + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{y^{2}}{3}+\frac{C}{y}{/tex}

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation x{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - y = x2{tex}\cdot{/tex} ex, given y(1) = 0.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - y = x 2 {tex}\cdot{/tex} e x , given y(1) = 0.
explanation: Given differential equation is {tex}x\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} -y = x 2 .e x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = xe x , which is of the form {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} + Py = Q Here, P = {tex}-\frac{1}{x}{/tex} and Q = xe x I.F. = {tex}e^{\int pdx}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\int \frac{-1}{x}dx}{/tex} = e -logx = {tex}e^{log\frac{1}{x}}{/tex} The solution is given by y.I.F. = {tex}\int{/tex} Q {tex}\times{/tex} I.F.dx + C y. {tex}\frac 1 x{/tex} = {tex}\int xe^x\times \frac{1}{x}dx + C{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = e x + C {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = e x + C ...(i) Given y = 0 when x = 1 From eq (i), we get 0 = 1.e x + C.1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = -e {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = e x - e
solution: Given differential equation is {tex}x\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} -y = x 2 .e x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = xe x , which is of the form {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} + Py = Q Here, P = {tex}-\frac{1}{x}{/tex} and Q = xe x I.F. = {tex}e^{\int pdx}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\int \frac{-1}{x}dx}{/tex} = e -logx = {tex}e^{log\frac{1}{x}}{/tex} The solution is given by y.I.F. = {tex}\int{/tex} Q {tex}\times{/tex} I.F.dx + C y. {tex}\frac 1 x{/tex} = {tex}\int xe^x\times \frac{1}{x}dx + C{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = e x + C {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = e x + C ...(i) Given y = 0 when x = 1 From eq (i), we get 0 = 1.e x + C.1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = -e {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = e x - e
solution_html:
Given differential equation is
{tex}x\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}-y = x2.ex
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = xex, which is of the form
{tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} + Py = Q
Here, P = {tex}-\frac{1}{x}{/tex} and Q = xex
I.F. = {tex}e^{\int pdx}{/tex}
= {tex}e^{\int \frac{-1}{x}dx}{/tex}
= e-logx
= {tex}e^{log\frac{1}{x}}{/tex}
The solution is given by
y.I.F. = {tex}\int{/tex}Q {tex}\times{/tex} I.F.dx + C
y.{tex}\frac 1 x{/tex} = {tex}\int xe^x\times \frac{1}{x}dx + C{/tex}
{tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = ex + C
{tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = ex + C ...(i)
Given y = 0 when x = 1
From eq (i), we get
0 = 1.ex + C.1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = -e
 {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = ex - e

question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation: {tex}\left(x^3+y^3\right){/tex}dy = x2y dx
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation: {tex}\left(x^3+y^3\right){/tex} dy = x 2 y dx
explanation: Given differential equation is (x 3 + y 3 ) dy = x 2 y dx {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x^3+y^3}{x^2 y}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = vy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}{/tex} = v + y {tex}\frac{d v}{d y}{/tex} from eq. (i), we have v + y {tex}\frac{d v}{d y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(v y)^3+y^3}{(v y)^2 y}{/tex} v + y {tex}\frac{d v}{d y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v^3 y^3+y^3}{v^2 y^3}{/tex} v + y {tex}\frac{d v}{d y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v^3+1}{v^2}{/tex} y {tex}\frac{d v}{d y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v^3+1}{v^2}{/tex} - v y {tex}\frac{d v}{d y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{v^2}{/tex} v 2 dv = {tex}\frac{d y}{y}{/tex} (variable separation method) Integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int{/tex} v 2 dv = {tex}\int{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{y}{/tex} {tex}\frac{v^3}{3}{/tex} = log y + C Putting v = {tex}\frac{x}{y}{/tex} , we get {tex}\frac{x^3}{3 y^3}{/tex} = log y + c
solution: Given differential equation is (x 3 + y 3 ) dy = x 2 y dx {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x^3+y^3}{x^2 y}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = vy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}{/tex} = v + y {tex}\frac{d v}{d y}{/tex} from eq. (i), we have v + y {tex}\frac{d v}{d y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(v y)^3+y^3}{(v y)^2 y}{/tex} v + y {tex}\frac{d v}{d y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v^3 y^3+y^3}{v^2 y^3}{/tex} v + y {tex}\frac{d v}{d y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v^3+1}{v^2}{/tex} y {tex}\frac{d v}{d y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v^3+1}{v^2}{/tex} - v y {tex}\frac{d v}{d y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{v^2}{/tex} v 2 dv = {tex}\frac{d y}{y}{/tex} (variable separation method) Integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int{/tex} v 2 dv = {tex}\int{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{y}{/tex} {tex}\frac{v^3}{3}{/tex} = log y + C Putting v = {tex}\frac{x}{y}{/tex} , we get {tex}\frac{x^3}{3 y^3}{/tex} = log y + c
solution_html:
Given differential equation is
(x3 + y3) dy = x2y dx
{tex}\frac{d x}{d y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x^3+y^3}{x^2 y}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = vy
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}{/tex} = v + y{tex}\frac{d v}{d y}{/tex}
from eq. (i), we have
v + y{tex}\frac{d v}{d y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(v y)^3+y^3}{(v y)^2 y}{/tex}
v + y{tex}\frac{d v}{d y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v^3 y^3+y^3}{v^2 y^3}{/tex}
v + y{tex}\frac{d v}{d y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v^3+1}{v^2}{/tex}
y{tex}\frac{d v}{d y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v^3+1}{v^2}{/tex} - v
y{tex}\frac{d v}{d y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{v^2}{/tex}
v2dv = {tex}\frac{d y}{y}{/tex}
(variable separation method)
Integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int{/tex}v2dv = {tex}\int{/tex}{tex}\frac{d y}{y}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{v^3}{3}{/tex} = log y + C
Putting v = {tex}\frac{x}{y}{/tex}, we get
{tex}\frac{x^3}{3 y^3}{/tex} = log y + c

question_html: Find the value of (2a - 3b), if a and b represent respectively the order and the degree of the differential equation {tex}x\left[y\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)^3+x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2-\frac{y}{x} \frac{d y}{d x}\right]=0{/tex}.

explanation: Order (a) of differential equation = 2 Degree (b) of differential equation = 3 So, (2a - 3b) = 2 × 2 - 3 × 3 = 4 - 9 = -5
solution: Order (a) of differential equation = 2 Degree (b) of differential equation = 3 So, (2a - 3b) = 2 × 2 - 3 × 3 = 4 - 9 = -5
solution_html:
Order (a) of differential equation = 2
Degree (b) of differential equation = 3
So, (2a - 3b) = 2 × 2 - 3 × 3 = 4 - 9 = -5

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the integrating factor of {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + (1 + x cot x) y = x.
question_text: Find the integrating factor of {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + (1 + x cot x) y = x.
explanation: The differential equation can be written as: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}+\cot \mathrm{x}}\right){/tex} y = 1 Integrating factor = {tex}e^{\int\left(\frac{1}{x}+\cot x\right) d x}{/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{(\log x+\log \sin x)}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\log (x \sin x)}{/tex} = x.sinx
solution: The differential equation can be written as: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}+\cot \mathrm{x}}\right){/tex} y = 1 Integrating factor = {tex}e^{\int\left(\frac{1}{x}+\cot x\right) d x}{/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{(\log x+\log \sin x)}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\log (x \sin x)}{/tex} = x.sinx
solution_html:
The differential equation can be written as: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}+\cot \mathrm{x}}\right){/tex}y = 1
Integrating factor = {tex}e^{\int\left(\frac{1}{x}+\cot x\right) d x}{/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{(\log x+\log \sin x)}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\log (x \sin x)}{/tex} = x.sinx

question_html: If the solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{2 x y-y^2}{2 x^2}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{\mathrm{ax}}{\mathrm{y}}{/tex} = b log |x| + C, find the value of a and b.
question_text: If the solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{2 x y-y^2}{2 x^2}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{\mathrm{ax}}{\mathrm{y}}{/tex} = b log |x| + C, find the value of a and b.
explanation: The given differential equation can be written as. {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y}{x}-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^2{/tex} Put y = vx, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} , we get v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = v - {tex}\frac{v^2}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v^2} \mathrm{dv}{/tex} = {tex}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}{/tex} , integrating both sides {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{v}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log | x | + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-\frac{x}{y}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log | x | + c, a = -1, b = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} or {tex} \frac{x}{y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log | x | + c, a = 1, b = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
solution: The given differential equation can be written as. {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y}{x}-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^2{/tex} Put y = vx, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} , we get v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = v - {tex}\frac{v^2}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v^2} \mathrm{dv}{/tex} = {tex}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}{/tex} , integrating both sides {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{v}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log | x | + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-\frac{x}{y}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log | x | + c, a = -1, b = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} or {tex} \frac{x}{y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log | x | + c, a = 1, b = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
The given differential equation can be written as.
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y}{x}-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^2{/tex}
Put y = vx, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}, we get
v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = v - {tex}\frac{v^2}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v^2} \mathrm{dv}{/tex} = {tex}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}{/tex}, integrating both sides
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{v}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log | x | + c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-\frac{x}{y}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log | x | + c, a = -1, b = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
or {tex} \frac{x}{y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log | x | + c, a = 1, b = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find a particular solution of the differential equation {tex}(x+1) \frac{d y}{d x}=2 e^{-y}-1{/tex} given that y = 0 when x = 0.
question_text: Find a particular solution of the differential equation {tex}(x+1) \frac{d y}{d x}=2 e^{-y}-1{/tex} given that y = 0 when x = 0.
explanation: It is given that {tex}(x+1) \frac{d y}{d x}=2 e^{-y}-1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{2 e^{-y}-1}=\frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{e^{y} d y}{2-e^{y}}=\frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get, {tex}\int \frac{e^{y} d y}{2-e^{y}}=\log |x+1|+\log C{/tex} .....(i) Let {tex}2-e^{y}=t{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d}{d y}\left(2-e^{y}\right)=\frac{d t}{d y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{dy}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e y dy = -dt Substituting value in equation (i), we get, {tex}\int \frac{-\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}}=\log |\mathrm{x}+1|+\log \mathrm{c}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log|t| = log|C(x+1)| {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log|2 - e y | = log|C(x + 1)| {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}}=\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{x}+1){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} ......(ii) Now, at x = 0 and y = 0, equation (ii) becomes, {tex}\Rightarrow 2-1=\frac{1}{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1 Now, substituting the value of C in equation (ii), we get, {tex}\Rightarrow 2-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=2-\frac{1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+2-1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\log \left|\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+1}{\mathrm{x}+1}\right|,(\mathrm{x} \neq-1){/tex} Therefore, the required particular solution of the given differential equation is {tex}y=\log \left|\frac{2 x+1}{x+1}\right|,(x \neq-1){/tex}
solution: It is given that {tex}(x+1) \frac{d y}{d x}=2 e^{-y}-1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{2 e^{-y}-1}=\frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{e^{y} d y}{2-e^{y}}=\frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get, {tex}\int \frac{e^{y} d y}{2-e^{y}}=\log |x+1|+\log C{/tex} .....(i) Let {tex}2-e^{y}=t{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d}{d y}\left(2-e^{y}\right)=\frac{d t}{d y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{dy}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e y dy = -dt Substituting value in equation (i), we get, {tex}\int \frac{-\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}}=\log |\mathrm{x}+1|+\log \mathrm{c}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log|t| = log|C(x+1)| {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log|2 - e y | = log|C(x + 1)| {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}}=\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{x}+1){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} ......(ii) Now, at x = 0 and y = 0, equation (ii) becomes, {tex}\Rightarrow 2-1=\frac{1}{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1 Now, substituting the value of C in equation (ii), we get, {tex}\Rightarrow 2-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=2-\frac{1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+2-1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\log \left|\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+1}{\mathrm{x}+1}\right|,(\mathrm{x} \neq-1){/tex} Therefore, the required particular solution of the given differential equation is {tex}y=\log \left|\frac{2 x+1}{x+1}\right|,(x \neq-1){/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that {tex}(x+1) \frac{d y}{d x}=2 e^{-y}-1{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{2 e^{-y}-1}=\frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{e^{y} d y}{2-e^{y}}=\frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex} 
On integrating both sides, we get,
{tex}\int \frac{e^{y} d y}{2-e^{y}}=\log |x+1|+\log C{/tex} .....(i)
Let {tex}2-e^{y}=t{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore \frac{d}{d y}\left(2-e^{y}\right)=\frac{d t}{d y}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{dy}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} eydy = -dt
Substituting value in equation (i), we get,
{tex}\int \frac{-\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}}=\log |\mathrm{x}+1|+\log \mathrm{c}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log|t| = log|C(x+1)|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log|2 - ey| = log|C(x + 1)|
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}}=\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{x}+1){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow 2-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} ......(ii)
Now, at x = 0 and y = 0, equation (ii) becomes,
{tex}\Rightarrow 2-1=\frac{1}{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1
Now, substituting the value of C in equation (ii), we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow 2-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=2-\frac{1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+2-1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\log \left|\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+1}{\mathrm{x}+1}\right|,(\mathrm{x} \neq-1){/tex} 
Therefore, the required particular solution of the given differential equation is
{tex}y=\log \left|\frac{2 x+1}{x+1}\right|,(x \neq-1){/tex}

question_html:
Find the general solution of the differential equation,
{tex}\text {ye}^{\frac {\text x}{\text y}}{/tex} dx = ({tex}\text {xe}^{\frac {\text x}{\text y}}{/tex} + y2) dy, y {tex}\neq{/tex} 0
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation, {tex}\text {ye}^{\frac {\text x}{\text y}}{/tex} dx = ( {tex}\text {xe}^{\frac {\text x}{\text y}}{/tex} + y 2 ) dy, y {tex}\neq{/tex} 0
explanation: {tex}\text y{\text e^{\frac {\text x}{\text y}}}\text {dx} = (\text x{e^{\frac {\text x}{\text y}}} + {\text y^2})\text {dy}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{x{e^{x/y}} + {y^2}}}{{y{e^{x/y}}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{x}{y} + y{e^{ - x/y}}{/tex} ...(i) let x = vy, we get, {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = v + y\frac{{dv}}{{dy}}{/tex} Put {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}}{/tex} in eq (1), we get, {tex}v + y\frac{{dv}}{{dy}} = v + y{e^{ - v}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}e^vdv=dy{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int {{e^v}dv = \int {dy} } {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e v = y + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e x/y = y + c
solution: {tex}\text y{\text e^{\frac {\text x}{\text y}}}\text {dx} = (\text x{e^{\frac {\text x}{\text y}}} + {\text y^2})\text {dy}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{x{e^{x/y}} + {y^2}}}{{y{e^{x/y}}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{x}{y} + y{e^{ - x/y}}{/tex} ...(i) let x = vy, we get, {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = v + y\frac{{dv}}{{dy}}{/tex} Put {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}}{/tex} in eq (1), we get, {tex}v + y\frac{{dv}}{{dy}} = v + y{e^{ - v}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}e^vdv=dy{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int {{e^v}dv = \int {dy} } {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e v = y + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e x/y = y + c
solution_html:
{tex}\text y{\text e^{\frac {\text x}{\text y}}}\text {dx} = (\text x{e^{\frac {\text x}{\text y}}} + {\text y^2})\text {dy}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{x{e^{x/y}} + {y^2}}}{{y{e^{x/y}}}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{x}{y} + y{e^{ - x/y}}{/tex} ...(i)
let x = vy, we get,
{tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = v + y\frac{{dv}}{{dy}}{/tex}
Put {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}}{/tex} in eq (1), we get,
{tex}v + y\frac{{dv}}{{dy}} = v + y{e^{ - v}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}e^vdv=dy{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\int {{e^v}dv = \int {dy} } {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ev = y + c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ex/y = y + c

question_html: The integrating factor of the differential equation {tex}x \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} x}+2 \mathrm{y}=x^{2}{/tex} is ________.
question_text: The integrating factor of the differential equation {tex}x \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} x}+2 \mathrm{y}=x^{2}{/tex} is ________.
explanation: x 2 {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}+2 y=x^{2}{/tex} Dividing both sides by x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2 y}{x}=x{/tex} Differential equation is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} Where P = {tex}2 \over x{/tex} and Q = x {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\text { IF }=e^{\int \frac{2}{x}} d x{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{IF}=e^{2 \log x}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{IF}=e^{\log x^{2}}{/tex} (As a logx = logx a ) IF = x 2
solution: x 2 {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}+2 y=x^{2}{/tex} Dividing both sides by x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2 y}{x}=x{/tex} Differential equation is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} Where P = {tex}2 \over x{/tex} and Q = x {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\text { IF }=e^{\int \frac{2}{x}} d x{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{IF}=e^{2 \log x}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{IF}=e^{\log x^{2}}{/tex} (As a logx = logx a ) IF = x 2
solution_html:
x2
{tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}+2 y=x^{2}{/tex}
Dividing both sides by x
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2 y}{x}=x{/tex}
Differential equation is of the form
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex}
Where P = {tex}2 \over x{/tex} and Q = x
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\text { IF }=e^{\int \frac{2}{x}} d x{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{IF}=e^{2 \log x}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{IF}=e^{\log x^{2}}{/tex} (As a logx = logxa)
IF = x2

question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex}y e^{\frac{x}{y}} d x=\left(x e^{\frac{x}{y}}+y^{2}\right) d y(y \neq 0){/tex}
question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex}y e^{\frac{x}{y}} d x=\left(x e^{\frac{x}{y}}+y^{2}\right) d y(y \neq 0){/tex}
explanation: It is given that {tex}\text { ye }^{\frac{x}{y}} d x=\left(x e^{\frac{x}{y}}+y^{2}\right) d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \text { ye }^{\frac{x}{y}} \frac{d x}{d y}=x e^{\frac{x}{y}}+y^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\frac{x}{y}}\left[y \cdot \frac{d x}{d y}-x\right]=y^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{\frac{x}{y}} \cdot \frac{\left[y \cdot \frac{d x}{d y}-x\right]}{y^{2}}=1{/tex} .......(i) Let {tex}e^{\frac{x}{y}}=z{/tex} Differentiating it w.r.t. y, we get, {tex}\frac{d}{d y}\left(e^{\frac{x}{y}}\right)=\frac{d z}{d y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{\frac{x}{y}} \cdot \frac{d}{d y}\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)=\frac{d z}{d y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{\frac{x}{y}} \cdot\left[\frac{y \cdot \frac{d x}{d y}-x}{y^{2}}\right]=\frac{d z}{d y}{/tex} ......(ii) From equation (i) and equation (ii), we get, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{z}}{\mathrm{dy}}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dz = dy On integrating both sides, we get, z = y + C {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}}}=\mathrm{y}+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
solution: It is given that {tex}\text { ye }^{\frac{x}{y}} d x=\left(x e^{\frac{x}{y}}+y^{2}\right) d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \text { ye }^{\frac{x}{y}} \frac{d x}{d y}=x e^{\frac{x}{y}}+y^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\frac{x}{y}}\left[y \cdot \frac{d x}{d y}-x\right]=y^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{\frac{x}{y}} \cdot \frac{\left[y \cdot \frac{d x}{d y}-x\right]}{y^{2}}=1{/tex} .......(i) Let {tex}e^{\frac{x}{y}}=z{/tex} Differentiating it w.r.t. y, we get, {tex}\frac{d}{d y}\left(e^{\frac{x}{y}}\right)=\frac{d z}{d y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{\frac{x}{y}} \cdot \frac{d}{d y}\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)=\frac{d z}{d y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{\frac{x}{y}} \cdot\left[\frac{y \cdot \frac{d x}{d y}-x}{y^{2}}\right]=\frac{d z}{d y}{/tex} ......(ii) From equation (i) and equation (ii), we get, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{z}}{\mathrm{dy}}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dz = dy On integrating both sides, we get, z = y + C {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}}}=\mathrm{y}+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that {tex}\text { ye }^{\frac{x}{y}} d x=\left(x e^{\frac{x}{y}}+y^{2}\right) d y{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \text { ye }^{\frac{x}{y}} \frac{d x}{d y}=x e^{\frac{x}{y}}+y^{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\frac{x}{y}}\left[y \cdot \frac{d x}{d y}-x\right]=y^{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow e^{\frac{x}{y}} \cdot \frac{\left[y \cdot \frac{d x}{d y}-x\right]}{y^{2}}=1{/tex} .......(i)
Let {tex}e^{\frac{x}{y}}=z{/tex} 
Differentiating it w.r.t. y, we get,
{tex}\frac{d}{d y}\left(e^{\frac{x}{y}}\right)=\frac{d z}{d y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow e^{\frac{x}{y}} \cdot \frac{d}{d y}\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)=\frac{d z}{d y}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow e^{\frac{x}{y}} \cdot\left[\frac{y \cdot \frac{d x}{d y}-x}{y^{2}}\right]=\frac{d z}{d y}{/tex} ......(ii)
From equation (i) and equation (ii), we get,
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{z}}{\mathrm{dy}}=1{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dz = dy
On integrating both sides, we get,
z = y + C
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}}}=\mathrm{y}+\mathrm{C}{/tex}

question_html: For what value of n is the following a homogeneous differential equation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x^3-y^n}{x^2 y+x y^2}{/tex}
question_text: For what value of n is the following a homogeneous differential equation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x^3-y^n}{x^2 y+x y^2}{/tex}
explanation: Homogeneous differential equation must have same degree in both numerator and denominator it means, {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x^3-y^n}{x^2y+xy^2}{/tex} so, n = 3
solution: Homogeneous differential equation must have same degree in both numerator and denominator it means, {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x^3-y^n}{x^2y+xy^2}{/tex} so, n = 3

{tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x^3-y^n}{x^2y+xy^2}{/tex}
so, n = 3

question_html:
Solve the differential equation:
x sin{tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + x - y sin {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = 0
Given that x = 1 when y = {tex}\frac{\neq}{2}{/tex}.
question_text: Solve the differential equation: x sin {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + x - y sin {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = 0 Given that x = 1 when y = {tex}\frac{\neq}{2}{/tex} .
explanation: Given differential equation gives {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-x}{x \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{y x}{/tex} = v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v \sin v-1}{\sin v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{\sin v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} sin vdv = {tex}\int {/tex} {tex}\frac{-1}{x}{/tex} dx {tex}\therefore{/tex} -cos v = -log|x| + C or cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = log |x| - C Given x = 1 when y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = log |x| is the required solution.
solution: Given differential equation gives {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-x}{x \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{y x}{/tex} = v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v \sin v-1}{\sin v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{\sin v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} sin vdv = {tex}\int {/tex} {tex}\frac{-1}{x}{/tex} dx {tex}\therefore{/tex} -cos v = -log|x| + C or cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = log |x| - C Given x = 1 when y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = log |x| is the required solution.
solution_html:
Given differential equation gives
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-x}{x \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = v
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = vx
and {tex}\frac{d y}{y x}{/tex} = v + x{tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} v + x{tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v \sin v-1}{\sin v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x{tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{\sin v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex}sin vdv = {tex}\int {/tex}{tex}\frac{-1}{x}{/tex}dx
{tex}\therefore{/tex} -cos v = -log|x| + C
or cos{tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = log |x| - C
Given x = 1
when y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 0 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = log |x| is the required solution.

given that y = {tex}\frac {\pi}{4}{/tex} when x = 0.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation cos y dx + (1 + e -x ) sin y dy = 0 given that y = {tex}\frac {\pi}{4}{/tex} when x = 0.
explanation: {tex}\frac{\text {dx}}{1+\text e^{-\text x}}=-\frac{\sin \text y}{\cos \text y} \text {dy}{/tex} (Separating variables) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}}{1+\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}} \mathrm{dx}{/tex} = {tex}-\int{/tex} tan y dy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log|e x + 1| = log|cos y| + c whern x = 0, y = {tex}\frac \pi 4{/tex} log 2 = {tex}\log \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = {tex}\frac {3}{2}{/tex} log2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} log|e x + 1| = log|cos y| + {tex}\frac {3}{2}{/tex} log 2 or y = cos -1 {tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}+1}{2 \sqrt{2}}\right){/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{\text {dx}}{1+\text e^{-\text x}}=-\frac{\sin \text y}{\cos \text y} \text {dy}{/tex} (Separating variables) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}}{1+\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}} \mathrm{dx}{/tex} = {tex}-\int{/tex} tan y dy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log|e x + 1| = log|cos y| + c whern x = 0, y = {tex}\frac \pi 4{/tex} log 2 = {tex}\log \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = {tex}\frac {3}{2}{/tex} log2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} log|e x + 1| = log|cos y| + {tex}\frac {3}{2}{/tex} log 2 or y = cos -1 {tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}+1}{2 \sqrt{2}}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{\text {dx}}{1+\text e^{-\text x}}=-\frac{\sin \text y}{\cos \text y} \text {dy}{/tex} (Separating variables)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}}{1+\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}} \mathrm{dx}{/tex} = {tex}-\int{/tex} tan y dy
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log|ex + 1| = log|cos y| + c
whern x = 0, y = {tex}\frac \pi 4{/tex}

question_html:
Find the particular solution of the differential equation
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} + y sec x = tan x, where x {tex}\in\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}
given that y = 1, when x = {tex}\frac \pi 4{/tex}.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} + y sec x = tan x, where x {tex}\in\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} given that y = 1, when x = {tex}\frac \pi 4{/tex} .
explanation: I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \sec \text {xdx}}{/tex} = e log|sec x + tan x| = sec x + tan x {tex}\therefore{/tex} y {tex}\cdot{/tex} (sec x + tan x) = {tex}\int{/tex} tan x(sec x + tan x)dx = sec x + tan x - x + c When x = {tex}\frac \pi 4{/tex} , y = 1 we get c = {tex}\frac \pi 4{/tex} y(sec x + tan x) = sec x + tan x - x + {tex}\frac \pi 4{/tex}
solution: I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \sec \text {xdx}}{/tex} = e log|sec x + tan x| = sec x + tan x {tex}\therefore{/tex} y {tex}\cdot{/tex} (sec x + tan x) = {tex}\int{/tex} tan x(sec x + tan x)dx = sec x + tan x - x + c When x = {tex}\frac \pi 4{/tex} , y = 1 we get c = {tex}\frac \pi 4{/tex} y(sec x + tan x) = sec x + tan x - x + {tex}\frac \pi 4{/tex}
solution_html:
I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \sec \text {xdx}}{/tex} = elog|sec x + tan x| = sec x + tan x
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y{tex}\cdot{/tex}(sec x + tan x) = {tex}\int{/tex} tan x(sec x + tan x)dx
= sec x + tan x - x + c
When x = {tex}\frac \pi 4{/tex}, y = 1 we get c = {tex}\frac \pi 4{/tex}

question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\frac{1}{\text x}=\frac{\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}}{\text x}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\frac{1}{\text x}=\frac{\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}}{\text x}{/tex} .
explanation: Given differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}=\frac{1}{\text x}\left(\text e^\text y-1\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text e^\text y-1}=\frac{\text {dx}}{\text x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{\text {dy}}{\text e^\text y-1}=\int \frac{\text {dx}}{\text x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{\text e^{-\text y}}{1-\text e^{-\text y}} \text {dy}=\int \frac{\text {dx}}{\text x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |1 - e -y | = log |x| + log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 - e -y = Cx
solution: Given differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}=\frac{1}{\text x}\left(\text e^\text y-1\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text e^\text y-1}=\frac{\text {dx}}{\text x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{\text {dy}}{\text e^\text y-1}=\int \frac{\text {dx}}{\text x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{\text e^{-\text y}}{1-\text e^{-\text y}} \text {dy}=\int \frac{\text {dx}}{\text x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |1 - e -y | = log |x| + log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 - e -y = Cx
solution_html:
Given differential equation can be written as
{tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}=\frac{1}{\text x}\left(\text e^\text y-1\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text e^\text y-1}=\frac{\text {dx}}{\text x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{\text {dy}}{\text e^\text y-1}=\int \frac{\text {dx}}{\text x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{\text e^{-\text y}}{1-\text e^{-\text y}} \text {dy}=\int \frac{\text {dx}}{\text x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |1 - e-y| = log |x| + log C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 - e-y = Cx

question_text: The degree of the differential equation {tex}\text x^2 \frac{\text d^2 \text y}{\text {dx}^2}=\left(\text x \frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}-\text y\right)^3{/tex} is

options.explanation: {tex} x^2 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=\left(x \frac{d y}{d x}-y\right)^3 {/tex} {tex} x^3 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=x^3\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^3-y^3-3 x^2 y\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2+3 y^2 x \frac{d y}{d x} {/tex} {tex} x^3 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-x^3\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^3+3 x^2 y\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2+3 x y^2 \frac{d y}{d x}+y^3=0 {/tex} {tex} \text { Order }=2 {/tex} {tex} \text { degree }=1 {/tex}

explanation: {tex} x^2 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=\left(x \frac{d y}{d x}-y\right)^3 {/tex} {tex} x^3 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=x^3\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^3-y^3-3 x^2 y\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2+3 y^2 x \frac{d y}{d x} {/tex} {tex} x^3 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-x^3\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^3+3 x^2 y\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2+3 x y^2 \frac{d y}{d x}+y^3=0 {/tex} {tex} \text { Order }=2 {/tex} {tex} \text { degree }=1 {/tex}

question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation y ey dx = (y3 + 2xey) dy.
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation y e y dx = (y 3 + 2xe y ) dy.
explanation: {tex} {ye}^{{y}} {dx}=\left({y}^3+2 {xe}^y\right) {dy}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{y^3+2 x^y}{y^y}{/tex} by dividing the above equation by {tex}{ye}^y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}-\frac{2}{y} x=y^2 e^{-y}{/tex} by seperating the variables {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}-\frac{2}{y} x=y^2 e^{-y}{/tex} is of the form {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+P x=Q{/tex} where {tex}P=\frac{2}{y}{/tex} and {tex}Q=y^2 e^{-y}{/tex} Integrating Factor {tex}=\text { I.F. }={e}^{\int {P}({x}) . {dx}}{/tex} Now, {tex} x \times I . F=\int Q \times I . F d y+c {/tex} {tex} \frac{x}{y^2}=\int y^2 e^{-y} \times \frac{1}{y^2} d y+c {/tex} {tex} =-e^{-y}+c \text { where } \int e^{-x} d x=-e^{-x}+c {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{y^2}=-e^{-y}+c {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x=-y^2 e^{-y}+c y^2 {/tex}
solution: {tex} {ye}^{{y}} {dx}=\left({y}^3+2 {xe}^y\right) {dy}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{y^3+2 x^y}{y^y}{/tex} by dividing the above equation by {tex}{ye}^y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}-\frac{2}{y} x=y^2 e^{-y}{/tex} by seperating the variables {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}-\frac{2}{y} x=y^2 e^{-y}{/tex} is of the form {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+P x=Q{/tex} where {tex}P=\frac{2}{y}{/tex} and {tex}Q=y^2 e^{-y}{/tex} Integrating Factor {tex}=\text { I.F. }={e}^{\int {P}({x}) . {dx}}{/tex} Now, {tex} x \times I . F=\int Q \times I . F d y+c {/tex} {tex} \frac{x}{y^2}=\int y^2 e^{-y} \times \frac{1}{y^2} d y+c {/tex} {tex} =-e^{-y}+c \text { where } \int e^{-x} d x=-e^{-x}+c {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{y^2}=-e^{-y}+c {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x=-y^2 e^{-y}+c y^2 {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} {ye}^{{y}} {dx}=\left({y}^3+2 {xe}^y\right) {dy}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{y^3+2 x^y}{y^y}{/tex} by dividing the above equation by {tex}{ye}^y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}-\frac{2}{y} x=y^2 e^{-y}{/tex} by seperating the variables
{tex}\frac{d x}{d y}-\frac{2}{y} x=y^2 e^{-y}{/tex} is of the form {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+P x=Q{/tex}
where {tex}P=\frac{2}{y}{/tex} and {tex}Q=y^2 e^{-y}{/tex}
Integrating Factor {tex}=\text { I.F. }={e}^{\int {P}({x}) . {dx}}{/tex}
Now,
{tex} x \times I . F=\int Q \times I . F d y+c {/tex}
{tex} \frac{x}{y^2}=\int y^2 e^{-y} \times \frac{1}{y^2} d y+c {/tex}
{tex} =-e^{-y}+c \text { where } \int e^{-x} d x=-e^{-x}+c {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{y^2}=-e^{-y}+c {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x=-y^2 e^{-y}+c y^2 {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Solve the following differential equation:
{tex}\left(1+e^{\frac{y}{x}}\right) d y+e^{\frac{y}{x}}\left(1-\frac{y}{x}\right) d x{/tex} = 0 (x {tex}\ne{/tex} 0)
question_text: Solve the following differential equation: {tex}\left(1+e^{\frac{y}{x}}\right) d y+e^{\frac{y}{x}}\left(1-\frac{y}{x}\right) d x{/tex} = 0 (x {tex}\ne{/tex} 0)
explanation: The given differential equation is: {tex} \left(1+e^{\frac{y}{x}}\right) d y+e^{\frac{y}{x}}\left(1-\frac{y}{x}\right) d x=0 (x \neq 0){/tex} First, we recognize that this is a homogeneous differential equation. We use the substitution {tex}v=\frac{y}{x}{/tex} , which implies {tex}y=v x{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} . Substituting these into the differential equation, we get: {tex} v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-e^v(1-v)}{1+e^v}{/tex} Rearranging terms, we have: {tex} x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-e^v(1-v)-v\left(1+e^v\right)}{1+e^v}{/tex} Simplifying the numerator: {tex} -e^v(1-v)-v\left(1+e^v\right)=-e^v+v e^v-v-v e^v=-e^v-v{/tex} Thus, the equation becomes: {tex} x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-e^v-v}{1+e^v}{/tex} Separating variables: {tex} \frac{1+e^v}{-e^v-v} d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} Integrating both sides, we recognize that the left integral can be simplified by noting that the numerator is the derivative of the denominator: {tex} -\int \frac{d\left(e^v+v\right)}{e^v+v}=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} This results in: {tex} -\ln \left|e^v+v\right|=\ln |x|+C{/tex} Exponentiating both sides, we get: {tex} \frac{1}{x\left(e^v+v\right)}=e^C{/tex} Rewriting in terms of {tex}y{/tex} and {tex}x{/tex} : {tex} x\left(e^{\frac{y}{x}}+\frac{y}{x}\right)=K{/tex} Simplifying, we obtain the solution: {tex} x e^{\frac{y}{x}}+y=C{/tex} Thus, the solution to the differential equation is: {tex} x e^{\frac{y}{x}}+y=C{/tex}
solution: The given differential equation is: {tex} \left(1+e^{\frac{y}{x}}\right) d y+e^{\frac{y}{x}}\left(1-\frac{y}{x}\right) d x=0 (x \neq 0){/tex} First, we recognize that this is a homogeneous differential equation. We use the substitution {tex}v=\frac{y}{x}{/tex} , which implies {tex}y=v x{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} . Substituting these into the differential equation, we get: {tex} v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-e^v(1-v)}{1+e^v}{/tex} Rearranging terms, we have: {tex} x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-e^v(1-v)-v\left(1+e^v\right)}{1+e^v}{/tex} Simplifying the numerator: {tex} -e^v(1-v)-v\left(1+e^v\right)=-e^v+v e^v-v-v e^v=-e^v-v{/tex} Thus, the equation becomes: {tex} x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-e^v-v}{1+e^v}{/tex} Separating variables: {tex} \frac{1+e^v}{-e^v-v} d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} Integrating both sides, we recognize that the left integral can be simplified by noting that the numerator is the derivative of the denominator: {tex} -\int \frac{d\left(e^v+v\right)}{e^v+v}=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} This results in: {tex} -\ln \left|e^v+v\right|=\ln |x|+C{/tex} Exponentiating both sides, we get: {tex} \frac{1}{x\left(e^v+v\right)}=e^C{/tex} Rewriting in terms of {tex}y{/tex} and {tex}x{/tex} : {tex} x\left(e^{\frac{y}{x}}+\frac{y}{x}\right)=K{/tex} Simplifying, we obtain the solution: {tex} x e^{\frac{y}{x}}+y=C{/tex} Thus, the solution to the differential equation is: {tex} x e^{\frac{y}{x}}+y=C{/tex}
solution_html:
The given differential equation is:
{tex} \left(1+e^{\frac{y}{x}}\right) d y+e^{\frac{y}{x}}\left(1-\frac{y}{x}\right) d x=0 (x \neq 0){/tex}
First, we recognize that this is a homogeneous differential equation. We use the substitution {tex}v=\frac{y}{x}{/tex} , which implies {tex}y=v x{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}. Substituting these into the differential equation, we get:
{tex} v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-e^v(1-v)}{1+e^v}{/tex}
Rearranging terms, we have:
{tex} x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-e^v(1-v)-v\left(1+e^v\right)}{1+e^v}{/tex}
Simplifying the numerator:
{tex} -e^v(1-v)-v\left(1+e^v\right)=-e^v+v e^v-v-v e^v=-e^v-v{/tex}
Thus, the equation becomes:
{tex} x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-e^v-v}{1+e^v}{/tex}
Separating variables:
{tex} \frac{1+e^v}{-e^v-v} d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
Integrating both sides, we recognize that the left integral can be simplified by noting that the numerator is the derivative of the denominator:
{tex} -\int \frac{d\left(e^v+v\right)}{e^v+v}=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
This results in:
{tex} -\ln \left|e^v+v\right|=\ln |x|+C{/tex}
Exponentiating both sides, we get:
{tex} \frac{1}{x\left(e^v+v\right)}=e^C{/tex}
Rewriting in terms of {tex}y{/tex} and {tex}x{/tex} :
{tex} x\left(e^{\frac{y}{x}}+\frac{y}{x}\right)=K{/tex}
Simplifying, we obtain the solution:
{tex} x e^{\frac{y}{x}}+y=C{/tex}
Thus, the solution to the differential equation is:
{tex} x e^{\frac{y}{x}}+y=C{/tex}

question_html:
Solve the following differential equation.
{tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } = y - x \tan \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex}
question_text: Solve the following differential equation. {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } = y - x \tan \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex}
explanation: According to the question,we have to solve the following differential equation, {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } = y - x \tan \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} Now,the given Equation can be rewritten as, {tex}{/tex} {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y - x \tan \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) } { x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } - \tan \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} ............(i) which is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} . Therefore, on putting y = vx {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get, {tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = v - \tan v \Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = - \tan v{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d v } { \tan v } = - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \cot v d v = - \frac { d x } { x } \quad \left[ \because \frac { 1 } { \tan v } = \cot v \right]{/tex} Therefore,on integrating both sides, we get, {tex} \int \cot v d v = - \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} log|sin v| = -log|x| + C {tex} \left[ \because \int \cot v d v = \log | \sin v | \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} log |sin v| + log |x| = C {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} log |x sin v| = C [ {tex} \because{/tex} log m + log n =log mn] {tex} \therefore \; \log \left| x \sin \frac { y } { x } \right| = C \quad \left[ \operatorname { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x \sin \frac { y } { x } = e ^ { C }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x \sin \frac { y } { x } = A \quad \left[ \because e ^ { C } = A \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \sin \frac { y } { x } = \frac { A } { x } \Rightarrow y = x \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { A } { x } \right){/tex} which is the required solution.
solution: According to the question,we have to solve the following differential equation, {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } = y - x \tan \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} Now,the given Equation can be rewritten as, {tex}{/tex} {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y - x \tan \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) } { x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } - \tan \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} ............(i) which is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} . Therefore, on putting y = vx {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get, {tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = v - \tan v \Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = - \tan v{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d v } { \tan v } = - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \cot v d v = - \frac { d x } { x } \quad \left[ \because \frac { 1 } { \tan v } = \cot v \right]{/tex} Therefore,on integrating both sides, we get, {tex} \int \cot v d v = - \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} log|sin v| = -log|x| + C {tex} \left[ \because \int \cot v d v = \log | \sin v | \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} log |sin v| + log |x| = C {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} log |x sin v| = C [ {tex} \because{/tex} log m + log n =log mn] {tex} \therefore \; \log \left| x \sin \frac { y } { x } \right| = C \quad \left[ \operatorname { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x \sin \frac { y } { x } = e ^ { C }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x \sin \frac { y } { x } = A \quad \left[ \because e ^ { C } = A \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \sin \frac { y } { x } = \frac { A } { x } \Rightarrow y = x \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { A } { x } \right){/tex} which is the required solution.
solution_html:
According to the question,we have to solve the following differential equation,

 {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } = y - x \tan \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex}

Now,the given Equation can be rewritten as,
{tex}{/tex}{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y - x \tan \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) } { x }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } - \tan \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex}............(i)
which is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex}.
Therefore, on putting y = vx {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} in Eq. (i),
we get,
{tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = v - \tan v \Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = - \tan v{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d v } { \tan v } = - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \cot v d v = - \frac { d x } { x } \quad \left[ \because \frac { 1 } { \tan v } = \cot v \right]{/tex}
Therefore,on integrating both sides, we get,
{tex} \int \cot v d v = - \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} log|sin v| = -log|x| + C {tex} \left[ \because \int \cot v d v = \log | \sin v | \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} log |sin v| + log |x| = C
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} log |x sin v| = C [{tex} \because{/tex} log m + log n =log mn]
{tex} \therefore \; \log \left| x \sin \frac { y } { x } \right| = C \quad \left[ \operatorname { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x \sin \frac { y } { x } = e ^ { C }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x \sin \frac { y } { x } = A \quad \left[ \because e ^ { C } = A \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \sin \frac { y } { x } = \frac { A } { x } \Rightarrow y = x \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { A } { x } \right){/tex}
which is the required solution.

question_html:
Solve the following differential equation.
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + y = \cos x - \sin x{/tex}
question_text: Solve the following differential equation. {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + y = \cos x - \sin x{/tex}
explanation: Given differential equation is {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + y = \cos x - \sin x{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} Here, P = 1 and Q = cos x - sinx {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d { x } } = e ^ { \int 1 d x } = e ^ { x }{/tex} We know that the solution of linear differential equation is given by {tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \text { IF } ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y e ^ { x } = \int e ^ { x } ( \cos x - \sin x ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { x } = e ^ { x } \cos x + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int e ^ { x } \left( f ( x ) + f ^ { \prime } ( x ) \right) d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C\right.{/tex} where {tex}f ( x ) = \cos x ; f ^ { \prime } ( x ) = - \sin x ]{/tex} Therefore,on dividing both sides by e x , we get, y = cos x + Ce -x which is the required solution.
solution: Given differential equation is {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + y = \cos x - \sin x{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} Here, P = 1 and Q = cos x - sinx {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d { x } } = e ^ { \int 1 d x } = e ^ { x }{/tex} We know that the solution of linear differential equation is given by {tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \text { IF } ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y e ^ { x } = \int e ^ { x } ( \cos x - \sin x ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { x } = e ^ { x } \cos x + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int e ^ { x } \left( f ( x ) + f ^ { \prime } ( x ) \right) d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C\right.{/tex} where {tex}f ( x ) = \cos x ; f ^ { \prime } ( x ) = - \sin x ]{/tex} Therefore,on dividing both sides by e x , we get, y = cos x + Ce -x which is the required solution.
solution_html:
Given differential equation is
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + y = \cos x - \sin x{/tex}
which is a linear differential equation of the form
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex}
Here, P = 1 and Q = cos x - sinx
{tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d { x } } = e ^ { \int 1 d x } = e ^ { x }{/tex}
We  know that the solution of linear differential equation is given by
{tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \text { IF } ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad y e ^ { x } = \int e ^ { x } ( \cos x - \sin x ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { x } = e ^ { x } \cos x + C{/tex}
{tex}\left[ \because \int e ^ { x } \left( f ( x ) + f ^ { \prime } ( x ) \right) d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C\right.{/tex} where {tex}f ( x ) = \cos x ; f ^ { \prime } ( x ) = - \sin x ]{/tex}
Therefore,on dividing both sides by ex, we get,
y = cos x + Ce-x
which is the required solution.

question_html:
Solve the following differential equation.
{tex} \left( y + 3 x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d x } { d y } = x{/tex}
question_text: Solve the following differential equation. {tex} \left( y + 3 x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d x } { d y } = x{/tex}
explanation: According to the question,we have to solve the differential equation , {tex} \left( y + 3 x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d x } { d y } = x \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } + 3 x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } - \frac { y } { x } = 3 x{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} . Here, {tex} P = \frac { - 1 } { x }{/tex} and Q = 3x {tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int - \frac { 1 } { x } d x } = e ^ { - \log | x | } = e ^ { \log x ^ { - 1 } } = x ^ { - 1 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { IF } = x ^ { - 1 } = \frac { 1 } { x }{/tex} The solution of linear differential equation is given by {tex} y \times I F = \int ( Q \times I F ) d x + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \times \frac { 1 } { x } = \int \left( 3 x \times \frac { 1 } { x } \right) d x+C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { y } { x } = \int 3 d x+C \Rightarrow \frac { y } { x } = 3 x + C{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad y = 3 x ^ { 2 } + C x{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution: According to the question,we have to solve the differential equation , {tex} \left( y + 3 x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d x } { d y } = x \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } + 3 x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } - \frac { y } { x } = 3 x{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} . Here, {tex} P = \frac { - 1 } { x }{/tex} and Q = 3x {tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int - \frac { 1 } { x } d x } = e ^ { - \log | x | } = e ^ { \log x ^ { - 1 } } = x ^ { - 1 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { IF } = x ^ { - 1 } = \frac { 1 } { x }{/tex} The solution of linear differential equation is given by {tex} y \times I F = \int ( Q \times I F ) d x + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \times \frac { 1 } { x } = \int \left( 3 x \times \frac { 1 } { x } \right) d x+C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { y } { x } = \int 3 d x+C \Rightarrow \frac { y } { x } = 3 x + C{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad y = 3 x ^ { 2 } + C x{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution_html:
According to the question,we have to solve the differential equation ,
{tex} \left( y + 3 x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d x } { d y } = x \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } + 3 x{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } - \frac { y } { x } = 3 x{/tex}
which is a linear differential equation of the form
{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex}.
Here, {tex} P = \frac { - 1 } { x }{/tex}and Q = 3x
{tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int - \frac { 1 } { x } d x } = e ^ { - \log | x | } = e ^ { \log x ^ { - 1 } } = x ^ { - 1 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { IF } = x ^ { - 1 } = \frac { 1 } { x }{/tex}
The solution of linear differential equation is given by
{tex} y \times I F = \int ( Q \times I F ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \times \frac { 1 } { x } = \int \left( 3 x \times \frac { 1 } { x } \right) d x+C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { y } { x } = \int 3 d x+C \Rightarrow \frac { y } { x } = 3 x + C{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad y = 3 x ^ { 2 } + C x{/tex}
which is the required solution.

question_html: Solve the differential equation: (1 + x2) dy + 2xy dx = cot x dx
question_text: Solve the differential equation: (1 + x 2 ) dy + 2xy dx = cot x dx
explanation: The given differential equation is (1 + x 2 ) dy + 2xy dx = cot x dx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\frac{2 \mathrm{x}}{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}} \times \mathrm{y}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\cot x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} This equation is a linear differential equation of the form: {tex}\left.\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q \text { (where } P=\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}} \text { and } Q=\frac{\cot x}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex} I.F. {tex}=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{2 \mathrm{x}}{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}} \mathrm{dx}}{/tex} {tex}=\mathrm{e}^{\log \left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}=\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Solution is given by: {tex}y \cdot\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\int \frac{(\cot x)}{1+x^{2}}\left(1+x^{2}\right) d x{/tex} y(1 + x 2 ) {tex}=\int \cot x d x{/tex} {tex}y \cdot\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\log (\sin x)+C{/tex}
solution: The given differential equation is (1 + x 2 ) dy + 2xy dx = cot x dx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\frac{2 \mathrm{x}}{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}} \times \mathrm{y}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\cot x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} This equation is a linear differential equation of the form: {tex}\left.\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q \text { (where } P=\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}} \text { and } Q=\frac{\cot x}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex} I.F. {tex}=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{2 \mathrm{x}}{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}} \mathrm{dx}}{/tex} {tex}=\mathrm{e}^{\log \left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}=\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Solution is given by: {tex}y \cdot\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\int \frac{(\cot x)}{1+x^{2}}\left(1+x^{2}\right) d x{/tex} y(1 + x 2 ) {tex}=\int \cot x d x{/tex} {tex}y \cdot\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\log (\sin x)+C{/tex}
solution_html:
The given differential equation is
(1 + x2) dy + 2xy dx = cot x dx
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\frac{2 \mathrm{x}}{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}} \times \mathrm{y}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{\cot x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}
This equation is a linear differential equation of the form:
{tex}\left.\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q \text { (where } P=\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}} \text { and } Q=\frac{\cot x}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex}
I.F. {tex}=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{2 \mathrm{x}}{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}} \mathrm{dx}}{/tex}
{tex}=\mathrm{e}^{\log \left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}=\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Solution is given by:
{tex}y \cdot\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\int \frac{(\cot x)}{1+x^{2}}\left(1+x^{2}\right) d x{/tex}
y(1 + x2) {tex}=\int \cot x d x{/tex}
{tex}y \cdot\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\log (\sin x)+C{/tex}

question_html: Show that the differential equation {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) + x - y \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = 0{/tex} is homogeneous. Find the particular solution of this differential equation, given that x = 1, when y={tex} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}.
question_text: Show that the differential equation {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) + x - y \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = 0{/tex} is homogeneous. Find the particular solution of this differential equation, given that x = 1, when y= {tex} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question , Given, Differential equation is {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = y \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - x{/tex} On Dividing both sides by {tex} x \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} , we get {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } - \frac { 1 } { \sin \frac { y } { x } }{/tex} ......(i) Let F(x, y) = {tex} \frac { y } { x } - \frac { 1 } { \sin \frac { y } { x } }{/tex} On replacing x by {tex} \lambda{/tex} x and y by {tex} \lambda{/tex} y , we get {tex} F ( \lambda x , \lambda y ) = \frac { \lambda y } { \lambda x } - \frac { 1 } { \sin \frac { \lambda y } { \lambda x } } {/tex} {tex} = \lambda ^ { 0 } \left( \frac { y } { x } - \frac { 1 } { \sin \frac { y } { x } } \right) = \lambda ^ { 0 } F ( x , y ){/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} given differential equation is homogeneous. On putting {tex} y = vx{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} in Eq(i), we get {tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = v - \frac { 1 } { \sin v }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = - \frac { 1 } { \sin v }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \sin v d v = - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex} \int \sin v d v = - \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad - \cos v = - \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad - \cos \frac { y } { x } = - \log | x | + C {/tex} [Put v = {tex} \frac yx{/tex} ] ..........(ii) On putting x = 1 and y = {tex} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} , we get {tex} - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = - \log | 1 | + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} - 0 = -0 + C {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} C = 0 On putting the value of C in Eq.(ii), we get {tex} \cos \frac { y } { x } = \log | x |{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution: According to the question , Given, Differential equation is {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = y \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - x{/tex} On Dividing both sides by {tex} x \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} , we get {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } - \frac { 1 } { \sin \frac { y } { x } }{/tex} ......(i) Let F(x, y) = {tex} \frac { y } { x } - \frac { 1 } { \sin \frac { y } { x } }{/tex} On replacing x by {tex} \lambda{/tex} x and y by {tex} \lambda{/tex} y , we get {tex} F ( \lambda x , \lambda y ) = \frac { \lambda y } { \lambda x } - \frac { 1 } { \sin \frac { \lambda y } { \lambda x } } {/tex} {tex} = \lambda ^ { 0 } \left( \frac { y } { x } - \frac { 1 } { \sin \frac { y } { x } } \right) = \lambda ^ { 0 } F ( x , y ){/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} given differential equation is homogeneous. On putting {tex} y = vx{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} in Eq(i), we get {tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = v - \frac { 1 } { \sin v }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = - \frac { 1 } { \sin v }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \sin v d v = - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex} \int \sin v d v = - \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad - \cos v = - \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad - \cos \frac { y } { x } = - \log | x | + C {/tex} [Put v = {tex} \frac yx{/tex} ] ..........(ii) On putting x = 1 and y = {tex} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} , we get {tex} - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = - \log | 1 | + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} - 0 = -0 + C {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} C = 0 On putting the value of C in Eq.(ii), we get {tex} \cos \frac { y } { x } = \log | x |{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution_html:
According to the question ,

Given, Differential equation is

{tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = y \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - x{/tex}

On Dividing both sides by {tex} x \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} , we get 
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } - \frac { 1 } { \sin \frac { y } { x } }{/tex}......(i)
Let F(x, y) = {tex} \frac { y } { x } - \frac { 1 } { \sin \frac { y } { x } }{/tex}
On replacing x by {tex} \lambda{/tex}x and y by {tex} \lambda{/tex}y , we get
{tex} F ( \lambda x , \lambda y ) = \frac { \lambda y } { \lambda x } - \frac { 1 } { \sin \frac { \lambda y } { \lambda x } } {/tex}{tex} = \lambda ^ { 0 } \left( \frac { y } { x } - \frac { 1 } { \sin \frac { y } { x } } \right) = \lambda ^ { 0 } F ( x , y ){/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} given differential equation is homogeneous.
 On putting {tex} y = vx{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} in Eq(i), we get
{tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = v - \frac { 1 } { \sin v }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = - \frac { 1 } { \sin v }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \sin v d v = - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
 On integrating both sides, we get
 {tex} \int \sin v d v = - \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad - \cos v = - \log | x | + C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad - \cos \frac { y } { x } = - \log | x | + C {/tex} [Put v ={tex} \frac yx{/tex}] ..........(ii)
On putting x = 1 and y = {tex} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}, we get
{tex} - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = - \log | 1 | + C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} - 0 = -0 + C 

{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} C = 0
On putting the value of C in Eq.(ii), we get
{tex} \cos \frac { y } { x } = \log | x |{/tex} which is the required solution.

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x y } { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } {/tex}, given that y = 1 when x = 0.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x y } { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } {/tex} , given that y = 1 when x = 0.
explanation: Given differential equation is {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x y } { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \frac { y } { x } } { 1 + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} .....(i) Which is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} . Now, put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} ,then from Eq.(i), we get {tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v } { 1 + v ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v } { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } - v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v - v - v ^ { 3 } } { 1 + v ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = - \frac { v ^ { 3 } } { 1 + v ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } { v ^ { 3 } } d v = - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \left( \frac { 1 } { v ^ { 3 } } + \frac { 1 } { v } \right) d v = - \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { 1 } { 2 v ^ { 2 } } + \log | v | = - \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 y ^ { 2 } } + \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| = - \log | x | + C \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 y ^ { 2 } } + \log | y | - \log | x | = - \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log \frac { m } { n } = \log m - \log n \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 y ^ { 2 } } + \log | y | = C{/tex} ...(ii) Also, it is given that y = 1, when x = 0. From Eq. (ii), we have 0 + log|1| = C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 0 On putting C = 0 in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}- \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 y ^ { 2 } } + \log | y | = 0 \Rightarrow \log | y | = \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 y ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore y = e ^ { \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 y ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution: Given differential equation is {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x y } { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \frac { y } { x } } { 1 + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} .....(i) Which is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} . Now, put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} ,then from Eq.(i), we get {tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v } { 1 + v ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v } { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } - v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v - v - v ^ { 3 } } { 1 + v ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = - \frac { v ^ { 3 } } { 1 + v ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } { v ^ { 3 } } d v = - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \left( \frac { 1 } { v ^ { 3 } } + \frac { 1 } { v } \right) d v = - \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { 1 } { 2 v ^ { 2 } } + \log | v | = - \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 y ^ { 2 } } + \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| = - \log | x | + C \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 y ^ { 2 } } + \log | y | - \log | x | = - \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log \frac { m } { n } = \log m - \log n \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 y ^ { 2 } } + \log | y | = C{/tex} ...(ii) Also, it is given that y = 1, when x = 0. From Eq. (ii), we have 0 + log|1| = C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 0 On putting C = 0 in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}- \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 y ^ { 2 } } + \log | y | = 0 \Rightarrow \log | y | = \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 y ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore y = e ^ { \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 y ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution_html:
Given differential equation is
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x y } { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \frac { y } { x } } { 1 + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}  .....(i)
Which is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex}.
Now, put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex},then  from Eq.(i), we get
{tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v } { 1 + v ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v } { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } - v{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v - v - v ^ { 3 } } { 1 + v ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = - \frac { v ^ { 3 } } { 1 + v ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } { v ^ { 3 } } d v = - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int \left( \frac { 1 } { v ^ { 3 } } + \frac { 1 } { v } \right) d v = - \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { 1 } { 2 v ^ { 2 } } + \log | v | = - \log | x | + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 y ^ { 2 } } + \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| = - \log | x | + C \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 y ^ { 2 } } + \log | y | - \log | x | = - \log | x | + C{/tex}
{tex}\left[ \because \log \frac { m } { n } = \log m - \log n \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 y ^ { 2 } } + \log | y | = C{/tex} ...(ii)
Also, it is given that y = 1, when x = 0.
From Eq. (ii), we have
0 + log|1| = C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 0
On putting C = 0 in Eq. (ii), we get
{tex}- \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 y ^ { 2 } } + \log | y | = 0 \Rightarrow \log | y | = \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 y ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore y = e ^ { \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 y ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
which is the required solution.

question_html: Find the order and degree (if defined) of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}=2 x^{2} \log \left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right){/tex}
question_text: Find the order and degree (if defined) of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}=2 x^{2} \log \left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right){/tex}
explanation: Given differential equation is {tex}\frac{d x}{d x}+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2{/tex} = 2x 2 log {tex}\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right) {/tex} The highest order derivative present in the differential equation is {tex}\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}{/tex} . So, it is of order 2. Clearly, the differential equation is not expressible as polynomial in {tex}\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}{/tex} . So, its degree is not defined. Hence, order =2, degree = not defined
solution: Given differential equation is {tex}\frac{d x}{d x}+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2{/tex} = 2x 2 log {tex}\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right) {/tex} The highest order derivative present in the differential equation is {tex}\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}{/tex} . So, it is of order 2. Clearly, the differential equation is not expressible as polynomial in {tex}\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}{/tex} . So, its degree is not defined. Hence, order =2, degree = not defined
solution_html:
Given differential equation is {tex}\frac{d x}{d x}+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2{/tex}= 2x2 log {tex}\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right) {/tex}
The highest order derivative present in the differential equation is {tex}\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}{/tex}. So, it is of order 2.
Clearly, the differential equation is not expressible as polynomial in {tex}\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}{/tex}. So, its degree is not defined.
Hence, order =2, degree = not defined

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the differential equation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+y}{x-y}{/tex}
question_text: Solve the differential equation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+y}{x-y}{/tex}
explanation: The given differential equation is: {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+y}{x-y}{/tex} ...(i) Let F(x, y) = {tex}\frac{x+y}{x-y}{/tex} {tex}\therefore F(\lambda x, \lambda y)=\frac{\lambda x+\lambda y}{\lambda x-\lambda y}=\frac{x+y}{x-y}=\lambda^{0} \cdot F(x, y){/tex} Thus, the given differential equation is a homogeneous equation. To solve it, we make the substitution as: y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{d x}(y)=\frac{d}{d x}(v x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} Substituting the values of y and {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} in equation (i), we get {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{x+v x}{x-v x}=\frac{1+v}{1-v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1+v}{1-v}-v=\frac{1+v-v(1-v)}{1-v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1+v^{2}}{1-v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{1-v}{1+v^{2}}\right) d v=\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{1}{1+v^{2}}-\frac{v}{1+v^{2}}\right) d v=\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} Integrating both sides, we get: {tex}\tan ^{-1} v-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(1+v^{2}\right)=\log x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left[1+\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}\right]=\log x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right)=\log x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left[\log \left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)-\log x^{2}\right]=\log x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\frac{1}{2} \log \left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)-logx+\mathrm{C}{/tex} This is a required solution of the given differential equation.
solution: The given differential equation is: {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+y}{x-y}{/tex} ...(i) Let F(x, y) = {tex}\frac{x+y}{x-y}{/tex} {tex}\therefore F(\lambda x, \lambda y)=\frac{\lambda x+\lambda y}{\lambda x-\lambda y}=\frac{x+y}{x-y}=\lambda^{0} \cdot F(x, y){/tex} Thus, the given differential equation is a homogeneous equation. To solve it, we make the substitution as: y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{d x}(y)=\frac{d}{d x}(v x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} Substituting the values of y and {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} in equation (i), we get {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{x+v x}{x-v x}=\frac{1+v}{1-v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1+v}{1-v}-v=\frac{1+v-v(1-v)}{1-v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1+v^{2}}{1-v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{1-v}{1+v^{2}}\right) d v=\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{1}{1+v^{2}}-\frac{v}{1+v^{2}}\right) d v=\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} Integrating both sides, we get: {tex}\tan ^{-1} v-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(1+v^{2}\right)=\log x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left[1+\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}\right]=\log x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right)=\log x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left[\log \left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)-\log x^{2}\right]=\log x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\frac{1}{2} \log \left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)-logx+\mathrm{C}{/tex} This is a required solution of the given differential equation.
solution_html:
The given differential equation is:
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+y}{x-y}{/tex} ...(i)
Let F(x, y) = {tex}\frac{x+y}{x-y}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore F(\lambda x, \lambda y)=\frac{\lambda x+\lambda y}{\lambda x-\lambda y}=\frac{x+y}{x-y}=\lambda^{0} \cdot F(x, y){/tex}
Thus, the given differential equation is a homogeneous equation. To solve it, we make the substitution as:
y = vx
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{d x}(y)=\frac{d}{d x}(v x){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
Substituting the values of y and {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}in equation (i), we get
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{x+v x}{x-v x}=\frac{1+v}{1-v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1+v}{1-v}-v=\frac{1+v-v(1-v)}{1-v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1+v^{2}}{1-v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{1-v}{1+v^{2}}\right) d v=\frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{1}{1+v^{2}}-\frac{v}{1+v^{2}}\right) d v=\frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
Integrating both sides, we get:
{tex}\tan ^{-1} v-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(1+v^{2}\right)=\log x+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left[1+\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}\right]=\log x+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right)=\log x+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left[\log \left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)-\log x^{2}\right]=\log x+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\frac{1}{2} \log \left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)-logx+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
This is a required solution of the given differential equation.
