question_html: Solve the differential equation : xdy - ydx = {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}} d x{/tex}, given that y = 0 when x = 1
question_text: Solve the differential equation : xdy - ydx = {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}} d x{/tex} , given that y = 0 when x = 1
explanation: Given: xdy - ydx = {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x d y=(\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}+y) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}+y}{x}{/tex} Put y = vx {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} So, {tex}v+x \cdot \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{\sqrt{x^{2}+v^{2} x^{2}}+v x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\sqrt{1+v^{2}}+v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \cdot \frac{x v}{d x}=\sqrt{1+v^{2}}{/tex} Inegrating both sides, we get {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \int \frac{d v}{\sqrt{1+v^{2}}}=\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\log |v+\sqrt{1+v^{2}}|{/tex} = logx + logc {tex}\log |v+\sqrt{1+v^{2}}|{/tex} = log(xc) {tex}v+\sqrt{1+x^{2}}{/tex} = xc {tex}\frac{y}{x}+\sqrt{1+\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}}=x \int c{/tex} {tex}\frac{y+\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}}{x}=x c{/tex} Now, Given y = 0 when x = 1 From equation (i), 0 + 1 = c c = 1 Substitute the value of c in (i), we get {tex}y+\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}=x^{2}{/tex}
solution: Given: xdy - ydx = {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x d y=(\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}+y) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}+y}{x}{/tex} Put y = vx {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} So, {tex}v+x \cdot \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{\sqrt{x^{2}+v^{2} x^{2}}+v x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\sqrt{1+v^{2}}+v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \cdot \frac{x v}{d x}=\sqrt{1+v^{2}}{/tex} Inegrating both sides, we get {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \int \frac{d v}{\sqrt{1+v^{2}}}=\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\log |v+\sqrt{1+v^{2}}|{/tex} = logx + logc {tex}\log |v+\sqrt{1+v^{2}}|{/tex} = log(xc) {tex}v+\sqrt{1+x^{2}}{/tex} = xc {tex}\frac{y}{x}+\sqrt{1+\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}}=x \int c{/tex} {tex}\frac{y+\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}}{x}=x c{/tex} Now, Given y = 0 when x = 1 From equation (i), 0 + 1 = c c = 1 Substitute the value of c in (i), we get {tex}y+\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}=x^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: xdy - ydx = {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x d y=(\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}+y) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}+y}{x}{/tex}
Put y = vx
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
So, {tex}v+x \cdot \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{\sqrt{x^{2}+v^{2} x^{2}}+v x}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\sqrt{1+v^{2}}+v{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \cdot \frac{x v}{d x}=\sqrt{1+v^{2}}{/tex}
Inegrating both sides, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \int \frac{d v}{\sqrt{1+v^{2}}}=\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\log |v+\sqrt{1+v^{2}}|{/tex} = logx + logc
{tex}\log |v+\sqrt{1+v^{2}}|{/tex} = log(xc)
{tex}v+\sqrt{1+x^{2}}{/tex} = xc
{tex}\frac{y}{x}+\sqrt{1+\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}}=x \int c{/tex}
{tex}\frac{y+\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}}{x}=x c{/tex}
Now, Given y = 0 when x = 1
From equation (i),
0 + 1 = c
c = 1
Substitute the value of c in (i), we get
{tex}y+\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}=x^{2}{/tex}

question_html: Solve the differential equation: {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}=y-x \tan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex}
question_text: Solve the differential equation: {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}=y-x \tan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex}
explanation: The given differential equation can be written as: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\tan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} Put {tex}\frac{y}{x}=v \text { and } \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} , to get {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=v-\tan v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\tan v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cot vdv = {tex}-\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} , Integrating both sides we get, log |sin v| = -log |x| + log c {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log |\sin v|=\log \left|\frac{c}{x}\right|{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Solution of differential equation is {tex}\sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\frac{c}{x} \Rightarrow x \cdot \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=c{/tex} The given differential equation can be written as: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x} \cdot y=\frac{-x}{1+\sin x}{/tex} ; I.F. = {tex}e^{\int \frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x} d x}=e^{\log (1+\sin x)}=1+\sin x{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Solution of the given differential equation is: {tex}y(1+\sin x)=\int \frac{-x}{1+\sin x} \times(1+\sin x) d x+c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y(1+\sin x)=\frac{-x^{2}}{2}+c{/tex}
solution: The given differential equation can be written as: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\tan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} Put {tex}\frac{y}{x}=v \text { and } \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} , to get {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=v-\tan v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\tan v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cot vdv = {tex}-\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} , Integrating both sides we get, log |sin v| = -log |x| + log c {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log |\sin v|=\log \left|\frac{c}{x}\right|{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Solution of differential equation is {tex}\sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\frac{c}{x} \Rightarrow x \cdot \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=c{/tex} The given differential equation can be written as: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x} \cdot y=\frac{-x}{1+\sin x}{/tex} ; I.F. = {tex}e^{\int \frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x} d x}=e^{\log (1+\sin x)}=1+\sin x{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Solution of the given differential equation is: {tex}y(1+\sin x)=\int \frac{-x}{1+\sin x} \times(1+\sin x) d x+c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y(1+\sin x)=\frac{-x^{2}}{2}+c{/tex}
solution_html:
The given differential equation can be written as: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\tan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex}
Put {tex}\frac{y}{x}=v \text { and } \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}, to get
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=v-\tan v{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\tan v{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cot vdv = {tex}-\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}, Integrating both sides we get, log |sin v| = -log |x| + log c
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log |\sin v|=\log \left|\frac{c}{x}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Solution of differential equation is
{tex}\sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\frac{c}{x} \Rightarrow x \cdot \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=c{/tex}
The given differential equation can be written as:
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x} \cdot y=\frac{-x}{1+\sin x}{/tex};
I.F. = {tex}e^{\int \frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x} d x}=e^{\log (1+\sin x)}=1+\sin x{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Solution of the given differential equation is:
{tex}y(1+\sin x)=\int \frac{-x}{1+\sin x} \times(1+\sin x) d x+c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y(1+\sin x)=\frac{-x^{2}}{2}+c{/tex}

question_html: Determine the order and the degree of the differential equation {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}+2 y \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=0{/tex}
question_text: Determine the order and the degree of the differential equation {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}+2 y \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=0{/tex}
explanation: The given differential equation is, {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}+2 y \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=0{/tex} Here,highest order derivative appearing in this equation is 2 and it's power is 1.So,given differential equation is second order and of degree 1
solution: The given differential equation is, {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}+2 y \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=0{/tex} Here,highest order derivative appearing in this equation is 2 and it's power is 1.So,given differential equation is second order and of degree 1

{tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}+2 y \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=0{/tex}

Here,highest order derivative appearing in this equation is 2 and it's power is 1.So,given differential equation is second order and of degree 1

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = 1 + x2 + y2 + x2y2, given that y = 1 when x = 0.
question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = 1 + x 2 + y 2 + x 2 y 2 , given that y = 1 when x = 0.
explanation: Given differential equation may be rewritten as, {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = (1 + x 2 )(1 + y 2 ) {tex}\int \frac{d y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\int\left(x^{2}+1\right) d x{/tex} tan -1 y = {tex}\frac{x^{3}}{3}{/tex} + x + C x = 0, y = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} Therefore, particular solution is, tan -1 y = {tex}\frac{x^{3}}{3}{/tex} + x + {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
solution: Given differential equation may be rewritten as, {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = (1 + x 2 )(1 + y 2 ) {tex}\int \frac{d y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\int\left(x^{2}+1\right) d x{/tex} tan -1 y = {tex}\frac{x^{3}}{3}{/tex} + x + C x = 0, y = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} Therefore, particular solution is, tan -1 y = {tex}\frac{x^{3}}{3}{/tex} + x + {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given differential equation may be rewritten as,
{tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = (1 + x2)(1 + y2)
{tex}\int \frac{d y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\int\left(x^{2}+1\right) d x{/tex}
tan-1 y = {tex}\frac{x^{3}}{3}{/tex} + x + C
x = 0, y = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
Therefore, particular solution is, tan-1 y = {tex}\frac{x^{3}}{3}{/tex} + x + {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If y = 5e7x + 6e-7x, show that {tex}\frac{{d}^{2} {y}}{{dx}^{2}}{/tex} = 49y
question_text: If y = 5e 7x + 6e -7x , show that {tex}\frac{{d}^{2} {y}}{{dx}^{2}}{/tex} = 49y
explanation: Given : y = 5e 7x + 6e -7x Then, we have {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}5 \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{7 x}\right){/tex} + {tex}6 \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{-7 x}\right){/tex} = {tex}5 \cdot e^{7 x} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(7 x){/tex} + {tex}6 \cdot e^{-7 x} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(-7 x){/tex} = 35e 7x - 42e -7x {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}35 \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{7 x}\right){/tex} - {tex}42 \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{-7 x}\right){/tex} = {tex}35 \cdot e^{7} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(7 x){/tex} - {tex}42 \cdot e^{-7 x} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(-7 x){/tex} = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 35 e 7x + 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 42 e -7x = 49 {tex}\times{/tex} 5e 7x + 49 {tex}\times{/tex} 6e -7x = 49(5 e 7x + 6 e -7x ) = 49y Henced proved
solution: Given : y = 5e 7x + 6e -7x Then, we have {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}5 \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{7 x}\right){/tex} + {tex}6 \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{-7 x}\right){/tex} = {tex}5 \cdot e^{7 x} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(7 x){/tex} + {tex}6 \cdot e^{-7 x} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(-7 x){/tex} = 35e 7x - 42e -7x {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}35 \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{7 x}\right){/tex} - {tex}42 \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{-7 x}\right){/tex} = {tex}35 \cdot e^{7} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(7 x){/tex} - {tex}42 \cdot e^{-7 x} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(-7 x){/tex} = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 35 e 7x + 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 42 e -7x = 49 {tex}\times{/tex} 5e 7x + 49 {tex}\times{/tex} 6e -7x = 49(5 e 7x + 6 e -7x ) = 49y Henced proved
solution_html:
Given : y = 5e7x + 6e-7x 
Then, we have
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}5 \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{7 x}\right){/tex} + {tex}6 \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{-7 x}\right){/tex} 
= {tex}5 \cdot e^{7 x} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(7 x){/tex} + {tex}6 \cdot e^{-7 x} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(-7 x){/tex} 
= 35e7x - 42e-7x 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}35 \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{7 x}\right){/tex} - {tex}42 \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{-7 x}\right){/tex} 
= {tex}35 \cdot e^{7} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(7 x){/tex} - {tex}42 \cdot e^{-7 x} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(-7 x){/tex} 
= 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 35 e7x + 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 42 e-7x 
= 49 {tex}\times{/tex} 5e7x + 49 {tex}\times{/tex} 6e-7x 
= 49(5 e7x + 6 e-7x)
= 49y
Henced proved

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the order of the differential equation of the family of circles of radius 3 units.
question_text: Find the order of the differential equation of the family of circles of radius 3 units.
explanation: The equation of the family of circles having centre (0, b) on y -axis and radius 3 units is x 2 + (y - b) 2 = 9 .....(i) Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get 2x + 2(y - b) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 0 or y - b = {tex}-\frac{x}{\frac{d y}{d x}}{/tex} Putting this value of y - b in (i), we get {tex}x^{2}+\frac{x^{2}}{\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}}{/tex} = 9 or {tex}\left(x^{2}-9\right)+\frac{x^{2}}{\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}}=0{/tex} or {tex}\left(x^{2}-9\right)\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}+x^{2}{/tex} = 0 The order of the differential equation is 2.
solution: The equation of the family of circles having centre (0, b) on y -axis and radius 3 units is x 2 + (y - b) 2 = 9 .....(i) Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get 2x + 2(y - b) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 0 or y - b = {tex}-\frac{x}{\frac{d y}{d x}}{/tex} Putting this value of y - b in (i), we get {tex}x^{2}+\frac{x^{2}}{\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}}{/tex} = 9 or {tex}\left(x^{2}-9\right)+\frac{x^{2}}{\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}}=0{/tex} or {tex}\left(x^{2}-9\right)\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}+x^{2}{/tex} = 0 The order of the differential equation is 2.
solution_html:
The equation of the family of circles having centre (0, b) on y -axis and radius 3 units is
x2 + (y - b)2 = 9 .....(i)
Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
2x + 2(y - b){tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 0 or y - b = {tex}-\frac{x}{\frac{d y}{d x}}{/tex} 
Putting this value of y - b in (i), we get
{tex}x^{2}+\frac{x^{2}}{\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}}{/tex} = 9
or {tex}\left(x^{2}-9\right)+\frac{x^{2}}{\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}}=0{/tex} 
or {tex}\left(x^{2}-9\right)\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}+x^{2}{/tex} = 0
The order of the differential equation is 2.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation xdx - yey {tex}\sqrt{1+x^{2}}{/tex} dy = 0, given that y = 1 when x  = 0.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation xdx - ye y {tex}\sqrt{1+x^{2}}{/tex} dy = 0, given that y = 1 when x = 0.
explanation: Here we have, xdx - ye y {tex}\sqrt{1+x^{2}}{/tex} dy = 0, Given equation can be written as x dx = {tex}y e^{y} \sqrt{1+x^{2}} d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int y \cdot e^{y} d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{1+x^{2}}{/tex} = e y (y – 1) + C when y = 1, x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required solution is {tex}\sqrt{1+x^{2}}{/tex} = e y (y - 1) + 1
solution: Here we have, xdx - ye y {tex}\sqrt{1+x^{2}}{/tex} dy = 0, Given equation can be written as x dx = {tex}y e^{y} \sqrt{1+x^{2}} d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int y \cdot e^{y} d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{1+x^{2}}{/tex} = e y (y – 1) + C when y = 1, x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required solution is {tex}\sqrt{1+x^{2}}{/tex} = e y (y - 1) + 1
solution_html:
Here we have, xdx - yey {tex}\sqrt{1+x^{2}}{/tex} dy = 0,
Given equation can be written as
x dx = {tex}y e^{y} \sqrt{1+x^{2}} d y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int y \cdot e^{y} d y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{1+x^{2}}{/tex} = ey (y – 1) + C
when y = 1, x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required solution is {tex}\sqrt{1+x^{2}}{/tex} = ey (y - 1) + 1

question_html: If xpyq = (x + y)p+q, prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=0{/tex}.
question_text: If x p y q = (x + y) p+q , prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=0{/tex} .
explanation: x p y p = (x + y) p+q taking log both the sides and applying property of log {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p log x + q log y = (p + q) log(x + y) Differentiating w.r.t x, {tex}\frac{p}{x}+\frac{q}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{p+q}{x+y}\left(1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{q}{y}-\frac{p+q}{x+y}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{p+q}{x+y}-\frac{p}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{q x-p y}{y(x+y)}\right] \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q x-p y}{x(x+y)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}{/tex} Differentiating again w.r.t x {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-y}{x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=0{/tex}
solution: x p y p = (x + y) p+q taking log both the sides and applying property of log {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p log x + q log y = (p + q) log(x + y) Differentiating w.r.t x, {tex}\frac{p}{x}+\frac{q}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{p+q}{x+y}\left(1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{q}{y}-\frac{p+q}{x+y}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{p+q}{x+y}-\frac{p}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{q x-p y}{y(x+y)}\right] \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q x-p y}{x(x+y)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}{/tex} Differentiating again w.r.t x {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-y}{x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=0{/tex}
solution_html:
xpyp = (x + y)p+q
taking log both the sides and applying property of log
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p log x + q log y = (p + q) log(x + y)
Differentiating w.r.t x, {tex}\frac{p}{x}+\frac{q}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}={tex}\frac{p+q}{x+y}\left(1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{q}{y}-\frac{p+q}{x+y}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}={tex}\frac{p+q}{x+y}-\frac{p}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{q x-p y}{y(x+y)}\right] \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}={tex}\frac{q x-p y}{x(x+y)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}{/tex}
Differentiating again w.r.t x
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}={tex}\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}={tex}\frac{x\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-y}{x^{2}}{/tex}{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=0{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}\sqrt{1-x^2}+\sqrt{1-y^2}{/tex} = a (x - y), |x| < 1, |y| < 1, show that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\sqrt{\frac{1-y^2}{1-x^2}}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}\sqrt{1-x^2}+\sqrt{1-y^2}{/tex} = a (x - y), |x| < 1, |y| < 1, show that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\sqrt{\frac{1-y^2}{1-x^2}}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}}+\sqrt{1-y^{2}}{/tex} = a(x - y) Substitate x = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} and y = sin {tex}\phi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}{/tex} + {tex}\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}{/tex} = a(sin {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\phi{/tex} ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = a {tex}\left(2 \cos \left(\frac{\theta+d}{2}\right) \cdot \sin \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right)\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 \cos \left(\frac{\theta+d}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}a \cdot 2 \cos \left(\frac{\theta+\phi}{2}\right) \cdot \sin \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right){/tex} [ cos a + cos b = {tex}2 \cos \left(a+\frac{b}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{a-b}{2}\right){/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\cos \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}a \sin \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\cot \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}{/tex} = cot-1 a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta-\phi{/tex} = 2cot -1 x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin -1 x - sin -1 y = 2 cot -1 x [x = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = sin -1 x and y = sin {tex}\phi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\phi{/tex} = sin -1 y] Now differentiating above equation with respect to x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-y^{2}}} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}2 x \frac{-1}{1+a^{2}} \frac{d a}{d x}{/tex} [ {tex}\frac{d}{d x} \sin ^{-1} x=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} ] [ {tex}\frac{d}{d x} \sin ^{-1} y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-y^{2}}} \frac{d y}{dx}{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-y^{2}}} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 0 [ {tex}\frac{d a}{d x}{/tex} = 0] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-y^{2}}} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{1-y^{2}}{1-x^{2}}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}}+\sqrt{1-y^{2}}{/tex} = a(x - y) Substitate x = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} and y = sin {tex}\phi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}{/tex} + {tex}\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}{/tex} = a(sin {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\phi{/tex} ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = a {tex}\left(2 \cos \left(\frac{\theta+d}{2}\right) \cdot \sin \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right)\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 \cos \left(\frac{\theta+d}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}a \cdot 2 \cos \left(\frac{\theta+\phi}{2}\right) \cdot \sin \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right){/tex} [ cos a + cos b = {tex}2 \cos \left(a+\frac{b}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{a-b}{2}\right){/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\cos \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}a \sin \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\cot \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}{/tex} = cot-1 a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta-\phi{/tex} = 2cot -1 x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin -1 x - sin -1 y = 2 cot -1 x [x = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = sin -1 x and y = sin {tex}\phi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\phi{/tex} = sin -1 y] Now differentiating above equation with respect to x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-y^{2}}} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}2 x \frac{-1}{1+a^{2}} \frac{d a}{d x}{/tex} [ {tex}\frac{d}{d x} \sin ^{-1} x=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} ] [ {tex}\frac{d}{d x} \sin ^{-1} y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-y^{2}}} \frac{d y}{dx}{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-y^{2}}} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 0 [ {tex}\frac{d a}{d x}{/tex} = 0] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-y^{2}}} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{1-y^{2}}{1-x^{2}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}}+\sqrt{1-y^{2}}{/tex} = a(x - y)
Substitate x = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} and y = sin {tex}\phi{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}{/tex} + {tex}\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}{/tex} = a(sin {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\phi{/tex})
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = a{tex}\left(2 \cos \left(\frac{\theta+d}{2}\right) \cdot \sin \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right)\right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 \cos \left(\frac{\theta+d}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}a \cdot 2 \cos \left(\frac{\theta+\phi}{2}\right) \cdot \sin \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right){/tex} 
[ cos a + cos b = {tex}2 \cos \left(a+\frac{b}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{a-b}{2}\right){/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\cos \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}a \sin \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\cot \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}{/tex} = cot-1 a
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta-\phi{/tex} = 2cot-1 x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin-1 x - sin-1 y = 2 cot-1 x [x = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = sin-1 x and y = sin {tex}\phi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\phi{/tex} = sin-1 y] 
Now differentiating above equation with respect to x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-y^{2}}} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}2 x \frac{-1}{1+a^{2}} \frac{d a}{d x}{/tex} 
[ {tex}\frac{d}{d x} \sin ^{-1} x=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex}] [ {tex}\frac{d}{d x} \sin ^{-1} y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-y^{2}}} \frac{d y}{dx}{/tex} ]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-y^{2}}} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 0  [{tex}\frac{d a}{d x}{/tex} = 0]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-y^{2}}} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{1-y^{2}}{1-x^{2}}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation: (1 + e2x) dy + (1 + y2) ex dx = 0, given that y(0) = 1.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation: (1 + e 2x ) dy + (1 + y 2 ) e x dx = 0, given that y(0) = 1.
explanation: (1 + e 2x ) dy + (1 + y 2 )e x dx = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{1+y^{2}}+\frac{e^{2} d x}{1+e^{2 x}}{/tex} = 0 Integrating both sides {tex}\int \frac{d y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} + {tex}\int \frac{e^{x}}{1+e^{2 x}} \cdot d x{/tex} = c e x = t , e x dx = dt tan -1 y + {tex}\int \frac{d t}{1+t^{2}}{/tex} = c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan -1 y + tan -1 t = c , x = 0; y = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} + tan -1 (1) = c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} tan -1 (e x ) + tan -1 y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
solution: (1 + e 2x ) dy + (1 + y 2 )e x dx = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{1+y^{2}}+\frac{e^{2} d x}{1+e^{2 x}}{/tex} = 0 Integrating both sides {tex}\int \frac{d y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} + {tex}\int \frac{e^{x}}{1+e^{2 x}} \cdot d x{/tex} = c e x = t , e x dx = dt tan -1 y + {tex}\int \frac{d t}{1+t^{2}}{/tex} = c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan -1 y + tan -1 t = c , x = 0; y = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} + tan -1 (1) = c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} tan -1 (e x ) + tan -1 y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
(1 + e2x) dy + (1 + y2)ex dx = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{1+y^{2}}+\frac{e^{2} d x}{1+e^{2 x}}{/tex} = 0
Integrating both sides
{tex}\int \frac{d y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} + {tex}\int \frac{e^{x}}{1+e^{2 x}} \cdot d x{/tex} = c
ex = t , ex dx = dt
tan-1 y + {tex}\int \frac{d t}{1+t^{2}}{/tex} = c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan-1 y + tan-1 t = c , x = 0; y = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} + tan-1 (1) = c  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} 
tan-1 (ex) + tan-1 y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}

question_html: Write the order and the degree of the differential equation: {tex}\left(\frac{d^{4} y}{d x^{4}}\right)^{2}=\left[x+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}\right]^{3}{/tex}
question_text: Write the order and the degree of the differential equation: {tex}\left(\frac{d^{4} y}{d x^{4}}\right)^{2}=\left[x+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}\right]^{3}{/tex}
explanation: {tex}\left(\frac{d^{4} y}{d x^{4}}\right)^{2}=\left[x+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}\right]^{3}{/tex} Order = 4, because highest order derivative is 4th order. And its power is 2 , So Degree = 2.
solution: {tex}\left(\frac{d^{4} y}{d x^{4}}\right)^{2}=\left[x+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}\right]^{3}{/tex} Order = 4, because highest order derivative is 4th order. And its power is 2 , So Degree = 2.
solution_html:
{tex}\left(\frac{d^{4} y}{d x^{4}}\right)^{2}=\left[x+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}\right]^{3}{/tex}
Order  = 4, because highest order derivative is 4th order. And its power is 2 ,
So Degree = 2.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the differential equation: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y} \cos \mathrm{x}}{1+\sin \mathrm{x}}\right]{/tex}
question_text: Solve the differential equation: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y} \cos \mathrm{x}}{1+\sin \mathrm{x}}\right]{/tex}
explanation: {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}-\left(\frac{x+y \cos x}{1+\sin x}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-x}{1+\sin x}-\frac{y \cos x}{1+\sin x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x} y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-x}{1+\sin x}{/tex} Comparing with linear Differential Equation form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q P = {tex}\frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x}{/tex} , Q = {tex}\frac{-x}{1+\sin x}{/tex} Integrating factor, I.F. = e = {tex}e^{\int p \, d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\int \frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x} d x}{/tex} = e log(1 + sin x) = 1 + sin x General solution y.(I.F.) {tex}\int(Q .(1 . F .)) d x{/tex} + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y (1 + sin x) = {tex}\int \frac{-x}{1+sinx}(1+sinx) d x{/tex} + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(1 + sin x) = {tex}\frac{-x^{2}}{2}{/tex} + C
solution: {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}-\left(\frac{x+y \cos x}{1+\sin x}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-x}{1+\sin x}-\frac{y \cos x}{1+\sin x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x} y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-x}{1+\sin x}{/tex} Comparing with linear Differential Equation form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q P = {tex}\frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x}{/tex} , Q = {tex}\frac{-x}{1+\sin x}{/tex} Integrating factor, I.F. = e = {tex}e^{\int p \, d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\int \frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x} d x}{/tex} = e log(1 + sin x) = 1 + sin x General solution y.(I.F.) {tex}\int(Q .(1 . F .)) d x{/tex} + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y (1 + sin x) = {tex}\int \frac{-x}{1+sinx}(1+sinx) d x{/tex} + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(1 + sin x) = {tex}\frac{-x^{2}}{2}{/tex} + C
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}-\left(\frac{x+y \cos x}{1+\sin x}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-x}{1+\sin x}-\frac{y \cos x}{1+\sin x}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x} y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-x}{1+\sin x}{/tex} 
Comparing with linear Differential Equation form  {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q
P = {tex}\frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x}{/tex}, Q = {tex}\frac{-x}{1+\sin x}{/tex} 
Integrating factor, I.F. = e = {tex}e^{\int p \, d x}{/tex} 
= {tex}e^{\int \frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x} d x}{/tex} = elog(1 + sin x) = 1 + sin x
General solution
y.(I.F.) {tex}\int(Q .(1 . F .)) d x{/tex} + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y (1 + sin x) = {tex}\int \frac{-x}{1+sinx}(1+sinx) d x{/tex} + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(1 + sin x) = {tex}\frac{-x^{2}}{2}{/tex} + C

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the differential equation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}{/tex}y = x2 + 2
question_text: Solve the differential equation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}{/tex} y = x 2 + 2
explanation: Given differential equation is: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}\right){/tex} y = x 2 + 2 On comparing the given differential equation with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q, we get P = {tex}-\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}{/tex} , Q = x 2 + 2 I.F. = {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\int \frac{-2 x}{1+x^2} d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{-\log \left(1+x^2\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^2}{/tex}
solution: Given differential equation is: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}\right){/tex} y = x 2 + 2 On comparing the given differential equation with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q, we get P = {tex}-\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}{/tex} , Q = x 2 + 2 I.F. = {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\int \frac{-2 x}{1+x^2} d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{-\log \left(1+x^2\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given differential equation is:
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}\right){/tex}y = x2 + 2
On comparing the given differential equation with
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q, we get
P = {tex}-\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}{/tex}, Q = x2 + 2
I.F. = {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex}
= {tex}e^{\int \frac{-2 x}{1+x^2} d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{-\log \left(1+x^2\right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the differential equation: x dy - y dx = {tex}\sqrt{x^2+y^2}{/tex} dx, given that y = 0 when x = 1.
question_text: Solve the differential equation: x dy - y dx = {tex}\sqrt{x^2+y^2}{/tex} dx, given that y = 0 when x = 1.
explanation: Writing {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y+\sqrt{x^2+y^2}}{x}=\frac{y}{x}+\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^2}{/tex} Put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} Differential equation becomes {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=v+\sqrt{1+v^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d v}{\sqrt{1+v^2}}=\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left|v+\sqrt{1+v^2}\right|{/tex} = log | x | log c {tex}\Rightarrow v+\sqrt{1+v^2}{/tex} = cx {tex}\Rightarrow y+\sqrt{x^2+y^2}{/tex} = cx 2 when x = 1, y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} y + {tex}\sqrt{x^2+y^2}{/tex} = x 2
solution: Writing {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y+\sqrt{x^2+y^2}}{x}=\frac{y}{x}+\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^2}{/tex} Put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} Differential equation becomes {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=v+\sqrt{1+v^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d v}{\sqrt{1+v^2}}=\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left|v+\sqrt{1+v^2}\right|{/tex} = log | x | log c {tex}\Rightarrow v+\sqrt{1+v^2}{/tex} = cx {tex}\Rightarrow y+\sqrt{x^2+y^2}{/tex} = cx 2 when x = 1, y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} y + {tex}\sqrt{x^2+y^2}{/tex} = x 2
solution_html:
Writing {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y+\sqrt{x^2+y^2}}{x}=\frac{y}{x}+\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^2}{/tex}
Put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
Differential equation becomes {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=v+\sqrt{1+v^2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d v}{\sqrt{1+v^2}}=\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left|v+\sqrt{1+v^2}\right|{/tex} = log | x | log c
{tex}\Rightarrow v+\sqrt{1+v^2}{/tex} = cx {tex}\Rightarrow y+\sqrt{x^2+y^2}{/tex} = cx2
when x = 1, y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y + {tex}\sqrt{x^2+y^2}{/tex} = x2

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the differential equation: (x + 1){tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} x}{/tex} = 2e-y - 1; y(0) = 0.
question_text: Solve the differential equation: (x + 1) {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} x}{/tex} = 2e -y - 1; y(0) = 0.
explanation: Given equation can be written as {tex}\int \frac{d y}{2 e^{-y}-1}=\int \frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{e^y}{2-e^y} d y=\int \frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log | 2 - e y | + log c = log | x + 1 | {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2 - e y ) (x + 1) = c When x = 0, y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Solution is (2 - e y ) (x + 1) = 1
solution: Given equation can be written as {tex}\int \frac{d y}{2 e^{-y}-1}=\int \frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{e^y}{2-e^y} d y=\int \frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log | 2 - e y | + log c = log | x + 1 | {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2 - e y ) (x + 1) = c When x = 0, y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Solution is (2 - e y ) (x + 1) = 1
solution_html:
Given equation can be written as
{tex}\int \frac{d y}{2 e^{-y}-1}=\int \frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{e^y}{2-e^y} d y=\int \frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log | 2 - ey | + log c = log | x + 1 |
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2 - ey) (x + 1) = c
When x = 0, y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Solution is (2 - ey) (x + 1) = 1

question_html: Find the integrating factor of the differential equation {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}-2 y=2 x^2{/tex}.
question_text: Find the integrating factor of the differential equation {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}-2 y=2 x^2{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}-\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}} \cdot \mathrm{y}{/tex} = 2x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I.F. = e -2log x = {tex}\frac{1}{x^2}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}-\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}} \cdot \mathrm{y}{/tex} = 2x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I.F. = e -2log x = {tex}\frac{1}{x^2}{/tex}
solution_html: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}-\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}} \cdot \mathrm{y}{/tex} = 2x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I.F. = e-2log x = {tex}\frac{1}{x^2}{/tex}

question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = ex + y.
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = e x + y .
explanation: Given differential equation can be written as: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = e x {tex}\cdot{/tex} e y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e -y dy e x dx Integrating both sides, we get -e -y = e x + c
solution: Given differential equation can be written as: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = e x {tex}\cdot{/tex} e y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e -y dy e x dx Integrating both sides, we get -e -y = e x + c
solution_html:
Given differential equation can be written as:
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = ex {tex}\cdot{/tex} ey {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e-y dy ex dx
Integrating both sides, we get
-e-y = ex + c

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the differential equation: {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = y - x tan {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex}
question_text: Solve the differential equation: {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = y - x tan {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex}
explanation: The given differential equation can be written as: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\tan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} Put {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = v and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} , to get {tex}\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = v - tan v {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = -tan v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cot v dv = {tex}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}{/tex} , Integrating both sides we get, log |sin v| = -log |x| + log c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |sin v| = {tex}\log \left|\frac{c}{x}\right|{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Solution of differential equation is {tex}\sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\frac{c}{x} \text { or } x \cdot \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = c
solution: The given differential equation can be written as: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\tan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} Put {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = v and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} , to get {tex}\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = v - tan v {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = -tan v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cot v dv = {tex}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}{/tex} , Integrating both sides we get, log |sin v| = -log |x| + log c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |sin v| = {tex}\log \left|\frac{c}{x}\right|{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Solution of differential equation is {tex}\sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\frac{c}{x} \text { or } x \cdot \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = c
solution_html:
The given differential equation can be written as:
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\tan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex}
Put {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = v and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}, to get
{tex}\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = v - tan v {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = -tan v
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cot v dv = {tex}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}{/tex},
Integrating both sides we get,
log |sin v| = -log |x| + log c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |sin v| = {tex}\log \left|\frac{c}{x}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Solution of differential equation is
{tex}\sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\frac{c}{x} \text { or } x \cdot \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = c

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the differential equation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\left[\frac{x+y \cos x}{1+\sin x}\right]{/tex}
question_text: Solve the differential equation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\left[\frac{x+y \cos x}{1+\sin x}\right]{/tex}
explanation: The given differential equation can be written as: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x} \cdot y=\frac{-x}{1+\sin x}{/tex} ; I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{\cos \mathrm{x}}{1+\sin \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\log (1+\sin \mathrm{x})}{/tex} = 1 + sin x {tex}\therefore{/tex} Solution of the given differential equation is: y(1 + sin x) = {tex}\int \frac{-x}{1+\sin x} \times{/tex} (1 + sin x)dx + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(1 + sin x) = {tex}\frac{-x^2}{2}+c{/tex} or y = {tex}\frac{-x^2}{2(1+\sin x)}+\frac{c}{1+\sin x}{/tex}
solution: The given differential equation can be written as: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x} \cdot y=\frac{-x}{1+\sin x}{/tex} ; I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{\cos \mathrm{x}}{1+\sin \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\log (1+\sin \mathrm{x})}{/tex} = 1 + sin x {tex}\therefore{/tex} Solution of the given differential equation is: y(1 + sin x) = {tex}\int \frac{-x}{1+\sin x} \times{/tex} (1 + sin x)dx + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(1 + sin x) = {tex}\frac{-x^2}{2}+c{/tex} or y = {tex}\frac{-x^2}{2(1+\sin x)}+\frac{c}{1+\sin x}{/tex}
solution_html:
The given differential equation can be written as:
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x} \cdot y=\frac{-x}{1+\sin x}{/tex};
I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{\cos \mathrm{x}}{1+\sin \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\log (1+\sin \mathrm{x})}{/tex} = 1 + sin x
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Solution of the given differential equation is:
y(1 + sin x) = {tex}\int \frac{-x}{1+\sin x} \times{/tex} (1 + sin x)dx + c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(1 + sin x) = {tex}\frac{-x^2}{2}+c{/tex} or y = {tex}\frac{-x^2}{2(1+\sin x)}+\frac{c}{1+\sin x}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the differential equation: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} x}-\frac{2 x}{1+x^2} \mathrm{y}{/tex} = x2 + 2
question_text: Solve the differential equation: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} x}-\frac{2 x}{1+x^2} \mathrm{y}{/tex} = x 2 + 2
explanation: I.F. = {tex}e^{-\int \frac{2 x}{1+x^2} d x}=\frac{1}{1+x^2}{/tex} Solution is given by {tex}y \cdot\left(\frac{1}{1+x^2}\right)=\int \frac{x^2+2}{1+x^2} d x{/tex} {tex}y \cdot \frac{1}{1+x^2}=\int\left(1+\frac{1}{1+x^2}\right) d x{/tex} = x + tan -1 x + c or y = (1 + x 2 ) (x + tan -1 x + c)
solution: I.F. = {tex}e^{-\int \frac{2 x}{1+x^2} d x}=\frac{1}{1+x^2}{/tex} Solution is given by {tex}y \cdot\left(\frac{1}{1+x^2}\right)=\int \frac{x^2+2}{1+x^2} d x{/tex} {tex}y \cdot \frac{1}{1+x^2}=\int\left(1+\frac{1}{1+x^2}\right) d x{/tex} = x + tan -1 x + c or y = (1 + x 2 ) (x + tan -1 x + c)
solution_html:
I.F. = {tex}e^{-\int \frac{2 x}{1+x^2} d x}=\frac{1}{1+x^2}{/tex}
Solution is given by
{tex}y \cdot\left(\frac{1}{1+x^2}\right)=\int \frac{x^2+2}{1+x^2} d x{/tex}
{tex}y \cdot \frac{1}{1+x^2}=\int\left(1+\frac{1}{1+x^2}\right) d x{/tex} = x + tan-1 x + c
or y = (1 + x2) (x + tan-1x + c)

question_html: Solve the following differential equation: {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+x=\left(\tan y+\sec ^2 y\right){/tex}
question_text: Solve the following differential equation: {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+x=\left(\tan y+\sec ^2 y\right){/tex}
explanation: I.F. = {tex}e^{\int 1 d y}=e^y{/tex} Solution is {tex}x \cdot e^y=\int e^y\left(\tan y+\sec ^2 y\right) d y+C{/tex} x {tex}\cdot{/tex} e y = e y tan y + C x = tan y + ce -y
solution: I.F. = {tex}e^{\int 1 d y}=e^y{/tex} Solution is {tex}x \cdot e^y=\int e^y\left(\tan y+\sec ^2 y\right) d y+C{/tex} x {tex}\cdot{/tex} e y = e y tan y + C x = tan y + ce -y
solution_html:
I.F. = {tex}e^{\int 1 d y}=e^y{/tex}
Solution is
{tex}x \cdot e^y=\int e^y\left(\tan y+\sec ^2 y\right) d y+C{/tex}
x{tex}\cdot{/tex}ey = ey tan y + C

x = tan y + ce-y

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation: {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x} \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+x-y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=0{/tex}, given that {tex}y(1)=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation: {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x} \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+x-y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=0{/tex} , given that {tex}y(1)=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x} \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+x-y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\frac{1}{\sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{/tex} ...(i) Put {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = v i.e., y = vx in (i) so that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=v-\frac{1}{\sin v}{/tex} {tex}\int \sin \mathrm{vdv}=\int-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -cos v = -log|x| + C {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = log|x| + C ...(ii) Substituting {tex}\mathrm{y}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} when x = 1 in (ii) {tex}\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} = log1 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 0 Required solution is {tex}\cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\log |x|{/tex}
solution: {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x} \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+x-y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\frac{1}{\sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{/tex} ...(i) Put {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = v i.e., y = vx in (i) so that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=v-\frac{1}{\sin v}{/tex} {tex}\int \sin \mathrm{vdv}=\int-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -cos v = -log|x| + C {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = log|x| + C ...(ii) Substituting {tex}\mathrm{y}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} when x = 1 in (ii) {tex}\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} = log1 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 0 Required solution is {tex}\cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\log |x|{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}x \frac{d y}{d x} \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+x-y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\frac{1}{\sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{/tex} ...(i)
Put {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = v i.e., y = vx in (i) so that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=v-\frac{1}{\sin v}{/tex}
{tex}\int \sin \mathrm{vdv}=\int-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -cos v = -log|x| + C {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = log|x| + C ...(ii)
Substituting {tex}\mathrm{y}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} when x = 1 in (ii)
{tex}\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} = log1 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 0
Required solution is {tex}\cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\log |x|{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If y = a cos (log x) + b sin (log x), show that {tex}x^2 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=0{/tex}.
question_text: If y = a cos (log x) + b sin (log x), show that {tex}x^2 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=0{/tex} .
explanation: We have, y = a cos (log x) + b sin (log x) .......(i) Differentiating equation (i) with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{a \sin (\log x)}{x}+\frac{b \cos (\log x)}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = -a sin (log x) + b cos (log x) Differentiating both sides with respect to x, we obtain {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{a \cos (\log x)}{x}-\frac{b \sin (\log x)}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = -[a cos (log x) + b sin (log x)] {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}=-y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=0{/tex} , which is the same as the given differential equation. Hence, y = a cos (log x) + b sin (log x) is a solution of the given differential equation.
solution: We have, y = a cos (log x) + b sin (log x) .......(i) Differentiating equation (i) with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{a \sin (\log x)}{x}+\frac{b \cos (\log x)}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = -a sin (log x) + b cos (log x) Differentiating both sides with respect to x, we obtain {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{a \cos (\log x)}{x}-\frac{b \sin (\log x)}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = -[a cos (log x) + b sin (log x)] {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}=-y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=0{/tex} , which is the same as the given differential equation. Hence, y = a cos (log x) + b sin (log x) is a solution of the given differential equation.
solution_html:
We have, y = a cos (log x) + b sin (log x) .......(i)
Differentiating equation (i) with respect to x, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{a \sin (\log x)}{x}+\frac{b \cos (\log x)}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = -a sin (log x) + b cos (log x)
Differentiating both sides with respect to x, we obtain
{tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{a \cos (\log x)}{x}-\frac{b \sin (\log x)}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = -[a cos (log x) + b sin (log x)]
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}=-y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=0{/tex}, which is the same as the given differential equation.
Hence, y = a cos (log x) + b sin (log x) is a solution of the given differential equation.

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Find the particular solution of the differential equation x dx - y ey{tex}\sqrt{1+x^2}{/tex} dy = 0, given that y = 1 when x = 0.
OR
Solve the differential equation x cos {tex} \left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = y cos {tex} \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+x{/tex}.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation x dx - y e y {tex}\sqrt{1+x^2}{/tex} dy = 0, given that y = 1 when x = 0. OR Solve the differential equation x cos {tex} \left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = y cos {tex} \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+x{/tex} .
explanation: Given equation can be written as {tex} x d x=y e^y \sqrt{1+x^2} d y {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \int \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^2}} \mathrm{dx}=\int \mathrm{y} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{dy} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \sqrt{1+x^2}=e^y(y-1)+C {/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required solution is {tex} \sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^2}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}(\mathrm{y}-1)+1 {/tex}
solution: Given equation can be written as {tex} x d x=y e^y \sqrt{1+x^2} d y {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \int \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^2}} \mathrm{dx}=\int \mathrm{y} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{dy} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \sqrt{1+x^2}=e^y(y-1)+C {/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required solution is {tex} \sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^2}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}(\mathrm{y}-1)+1 {/tex}
solution_html:
Given equation can be written as
{tex} x d x=y e^y \sqrt{1+x^2} d y {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \int \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^2}} \mathrm{dx}=\int \mathrm{y} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{dy} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \sqrt{1+x^2}=e^y(y-1)+C {/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required solution is {tex} \sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^2}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}(\mathrm{y}-1)+1 {/tex}

question_html: Find the integrating factor of the differential equation {tex} y \frac{d x}{d y}-2 x=y^3 e^{-y} {/tex}.
question_text: Find the integrating factor of the differential equation {tex} y \frac{d x}{d y}-2 x=y^3 e^{-y} {/tex} .
explanation: {tex} \frac{d x}{d y}-\frac{2}{y} \cdot x=y^2 e^{-y} {/tex} I.F. {tex}=\mathrm{e}^{\int-\frac{2}{y} d y}=e^{-2 \log y}=\frac{1}{y^2} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \frac{d x}{d y}-\frac{2}{y} \cdot x=y^2 e^{-y} {/tex} I.F. {tex}=\mathrm{e}^{\int-\frac{2}{y} d y}=e^{-2 \log y}=\frac{1}{y^2} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \frac{d x}{d y}-\frac{2}{y} \cdot x=y^2 e^{-y} {/tex}
I.F. {tex}=\mathrm{e}^{\int-\frac{2}{y} d y}=e^{-2 \log y}=\frac{1}{y^2} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex} x \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \frac{d y}{d x} {/tex} {tex} =y \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+x {/tex}.
question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex} x \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \frac{d y}{d x} {/tex} {tex} =y \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+x {/tex} .
explanation: Given differential equation can be written as {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}+\frac{1}{\cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)} {/tex} {tex} \frac{y}{x}{/tex} = v i.e.y = vx {tex} \Rightarrow\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \cdot \frac{d v}{d x} {/tex} Given equation becomes {tex} v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=v+\frac{1}{\cos v} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow\int \cos v d v=\int \frac{d x}{x} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow\sin v=\log |x|+c {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow\sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\log |x|+c {/tex}
solution: Given differential equation can be written as {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}+\frac{1}{\cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)} {/tex} {tex} \frac{y}{x}{/tex} = v i.e.y = vx {tex} \Rightarrow\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \cdot \frac{d v}{d x} {/tex} Given equation becomes {tex} v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=v+\frac{1}{\cos v} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow\int \cos v d v=\int \frac{d x}{x} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow\sin v=\log |x|+c {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow\sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\log |x|+c {/tex}
solution_html:
Given differential equation can be written as
{tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}+\frac{1}{\cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{y}{x}{/tex} = v i.e.y = vx 
{tex} \Rightarrow\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \cdot \frac{d v}{d x} {/tex}
Given equation becomes
{tex} v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=v+\frac{1}{\cos v} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow\int \cos v d v=\int \frac{d x}{x} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow\sin v=\log |x|+c {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow\sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\log |x|+c {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 x y = \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}, given that y = 0 when x = 1.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 x y = \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} , given that y = 0 when x = 1.
explanation: {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} Divide both sides by 1 + x 2 {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2 x y}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right) \cdot\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{2 x }{1+x^{2}}\right) y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^2}{/tex} Comparing with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q, P = {tex}\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} & Q = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} Finding Integrating factor: IF = {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex} IF = {tex}e^{\int \frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}} d x{/tex} Let 1 + x 2 = t Diff. w.r.t. x 2x = {tex}\frac{dt}{d x} {/tex} dx = {tex}\frac{d t}{2 x}{/tex} Thus, IF = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{2 x}{t} \frac{d t}{2 x}}{/tex} IF = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{d t}{t}}{/tex} IF = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\log |t|}{/tex} IF = t IF = 1 + x 2 Solution of the differential equation: y {tex}\times{/tex} I.F. = {tex}\int {Q\, \times \,I.F\,dx} {/tex} Putting values, y {tex}\times{/tex} (1 + x 2 ) = {tex}{\int {\frac{1}{{(1 + {x^2})^2}}} (1 + {x^2})dx} {/tex} y (1 + x 2 ) = {tex}\int{\frac{1}{{(1 + {x^2})}}} {/tex} dx y (1 + x 2 ) = tan -1 x + C ...(1) Putting that y = 0 and x = 1, 0(1 + 1 2 ) = tan -1 (1) + C 0 = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} + C C = - {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} Putting value of C in eq(1), y(1 + x 2 ) = tan -1 x + C y(1 + x 2 ) = tan -1 x - {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} Divide both sides by 1 + x 2 {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2 x y}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right) \cdot\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{2 x }{1+x^{2}}\right) y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^2}{/tex} Comparing with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q, P = {tex}\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} & Q = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} Finding Integrating factor: IF = {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex} IF = {tex}e^{\int \frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}} d x{/tex} Let 1 + x 2 = t Diff. w.r.t. x 2x = {tex}\frac{dt}{d x} {/tex} dx = {tex}\frac{d t}{2 x}{/tex} Thus, IF = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{2 x}{t} \frac{d t}{2 x}}{/tex} IF = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{d t}{t}}{/tex} IF = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\log |t|}{/tex} IF = t IF = 1 + x 2 Solution of the differential equation: y {tex}\times{/tex} I.F. = {tex}\int {Q\, \times \,I.F\,dx} {/tex} Putting values, y {tex}\times{/tex} (1 + x 2 ) = {tex}{\int {\frac{1}{{(1 + {x^2})^2}}} (1 + {x^2})dx} {/tex} y (1 + x 2 ) = {tex}\int{\frac{1}{{(1 + {x^2})}}} {/tex} dx y (1 + x 2 ) = tan -1 x + C ...(1) Putting that y = 0 and x = 1, 0(1 + 1 2 ) = tan -1 (1) + C 0 = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} + C C = - {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} Putting value of C in eq(1), y(1 + x 2 ) = tan -1 x + C y(1 + x 2 ) = tan -1 x - {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}
Divide both sides by 1 + x2
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2 x y}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right) \cdot\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{2 x }{1+x^{2}}\right) y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^2}{/tex}
Comparing with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q,
P = {tex}\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} & Q = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
Finding Integrating factor:
IF = {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex}
IF = {tex}e^{\int \frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}} d x{/tex}
Let 1 + x2 = t
Diff. w.r.t. x
2x = {tex}\frac{dt}{d x} {/tex}
dx = {tex}\frac{d t}{2 x}{/tex}
Thus, IF = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{2 x}{t} \frac{d t}{2 x}}{/tex}
IF = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{d t}{t}}{/tex}
IF = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\log |t|}{/tex}
IF = t
IF = 1 + x2
Solution of the differential equation:
y {tex}\times{/tex} I.F. = {tex}\int {Q\, \times \,I.F\,dx} {/tex}
Putting values,
y {tex}\times{/tex} (1 + x2) = {tex}{\int {\frac{1}{{(1 + {x^2})^2}}} (1 + {x^2})dx} {/tex} 
y (1 + x2) = {tex}\int{\frac{1}{{(1 + {x^2})}}} {/tex} dx
y (1 + x2) = tan-1x + C ...(1)
Putting that y = 0 and x = 1,
0(1 + 12) = tan-1(1) + C
0 = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} + C
C = -{tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
Putting value of C in eq(1),
y(1 + x2) = tan-1x + C
y(1 + x2) = tan-1x - {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation ex tany dx + (2 – ex) sec2y dy = 0, given that y ={tex}\frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} when x = 0.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation e x tany dx + (2 – e x ) sec 2 y dy = 0, given that y = {tex}\frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} when x = 0.
explanation: According to the question, e x tan y dx + (2 – e x ) sec 2 y dy = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} e x tany dx = (e x - 2) sec 2 y dy {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } y } { \tan y } d y = \frac { e ^ { x } } { e ^ { x } - 2 } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log | \tan y | = \log \left| e ^ { x } - 2 \right| + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { f ^ { \prime } ( x ) } { f ( x ) } d x = \log | f ( x ) | + C \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log | \tan y | - \log | e ^ { x } - 2| = C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left| \frac { \tan y } { e ^ { x } - 2 } \right| = C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log m - \log n = \log \left( \frac { m } { n } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \tan y } { e ^ { x } - 2 } = e ^ { C }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log_e m = n \Rightarrow m = e ^ { n } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan y = e C (e x - 2) .....(i) According to the given question, y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} when x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } = e ^ { C } \left( e ^ { 0 } - 2 \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1 = e ^ { C} ( 1 - 2 ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e ^ { C } = - 1{/tex} ....(ii) Substitute eq.(ii) in eq.(i), we get {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan y =-1(e x - 2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tany = 2 - e x Thus, the particular solution of the given differential equation is tan y = 2 - e x .
solution: According to the question, e x tan y dx + (2 – e x ) sec 2 y dy = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} e x tany dx = (e x - 2) sec 2 y dy {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } y } { \tan y } d y = \frac { e ^ { x } } { e ^ { x } - 2 } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log | \tan y | = \log \left| e ^ { x } - 2 \right| + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { f ^ { \prime } ( x ) } { f ( x ) } d x = \log | f ( x ) | + C \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log | \tan y | - \log | e ^ { x } - 2| = C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left| \frac { \tan y } { e ^ { x } - 2 } \right| = C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log m - \log n = \log \left( \frac { m } { n } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \tan y } { e ^ { x } - 2 } = e ^ { C }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log_e m = n \Rightarrow m = e ^ { n } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan y = e C (e x - 2) .....(i) According to the given question, y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} when x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } = e ^ { C } \left( e ^ { 0 } - 2 \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1 = e ^ { C} ( 1 - 2 ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e ^ { C } = - 1{/tex} ....(ii) Substitute eq.(ii) in eq.(i), we get {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan y =-1(e x - 2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tany = 2 - e x Thus, the particular solution of the given differential equation is tan y = 2 - e x .
solution_html:
According to the question, ex tan y dx + (2 – ex) sec2y dy = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex}ex tany dx = (ex - 2) sec2y dy
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } y } { \tan y } d y = \frac { e ^ { x } } { e ^ { x } - 2 } d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log | \tan y | = \log \left| e ^ { x } - 2 \right| + C{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { f ^ { \prime } ( x ) } { f ( x ) } d x = \log | f ( x ) | + C \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log | \tan y | - \log | e ^ { x } - 2| = C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left| \frac { \tan y } { e ^ { x } - 2 } \right| = C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log m - \log n = \log \left( \frac { m } { n } \right) \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \tan y } { e ^ { x } - 2 } = e ^ { C }{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \log_e m = n \Rightarrow m = e ^ { n } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}tan y = eC(ex - 2) .....(i)
According to the given question, y ={tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} when x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } = e ^ { C } \left( e ^ { 0 } - 2 \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 1 = e ^ { C} ( 1 - 2 ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow e ^ { C } = - 1{/tex}....(ii)
Substitute eq.(ii) in eq.(i), we get
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}tan y =-1(ex - 2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}tany = 2 - ex
Thus, the particular solution of the given differential equation is tan y = 2 - ex.

question_html: Show that it is homogeneous and solve it: (x cos {tex}\frac y x{/tex}){tex}\frac {dy}{dx}{/tex} = (x cos {tex}\frac y x{/tex}) + x
question_text: Show that it is homogeneous and solve it: (x cos {tex}\frac y x{/tex} ) {tex}\frac {dy}{dx}{/tex} = (x cos {tex}\frac y x{/tex} ) + x
explanation: {tex}x\cos \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = y\cos \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right) + x{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{y\cos \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right) + x}}{{x\cos \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right)}}{/tex} ...(i) let y = vx {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = v.1 + x.\frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex} ...(ii) Put {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} in eq ...(i) {tex}v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{{vx\cos v + x}}{{x\cos v}}{/tex} {tex}v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{{v\cos v + 1}}{{\cos v}}{/tex} {tex}x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{{v\cos v - 1}}{{\cos v}} - v{/tex} {tex}x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{{v\cos v + 1 - v\cos v}}{{\cos v}}{/tex} {tex}x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{\cos v}}{/tex} {tex}\int {\cos vdv} = \int {\frac{{dx}}{x}} {/tex} {tex}\sin v = \log x + \log c{/tex} sin v = log x + log c {tex}\sin v = \log \left| {cx} \right|{/tex} sin v = log|cx| {tex}\left[ {\because y = vx} \right]{/tex} [ y = vx ] {tex}\sin \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right) = \log \left| {cx} \right|{/tex}
solution: {tex}x\cos \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = y\cos \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right) + x{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{y\cos \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right) + x}}{{x\cos \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right)}}{/tex} ...(i) let y = vx {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = v.1 + x.\frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex} ...(ii) Put {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} in eq ...(i) {tex}v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{{vx\cos v + x}}{{x\cos v}}{/tex} {tex}v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{{v\cos v + 1}}{{\cos v}}{/tex} {tex}x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{{v\cos v - 1}}{{\cos v}} - v{/tex} {tex}x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{{v\cos v + 1 - v\cos v}}{{\cos v}}{/tex} {tex}x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{\cos v}}{/tex} {tex}\int {\cos vdv} = \int {\frac{{dx}}{x}} {/tex} {tex}\sin v = \log x + \log c{/tex} sin v = log x + log c {tex}\sin v = \log \left| {cx} \right|{/tex} sin v = log|cx| {tex}\left[ {\because y = vx} \right]{/tex} [ y = vx ] {tex}\sin \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right) = \log \left| {cx} \right|{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}x\cos \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = y\cos \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right) + x{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{y\cos \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right) + x}}{{x\cos \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right)}}{/tex} ...(i)

let y = vx 

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = v.1 + x.\frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex}...(ii)

Put {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} in eq ...(i)

{tex}v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{{vx\cos v + x}}{{x\cos v}}{/tex}

{tex}v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{{v\cos v + 1}}{{\cos v}}{/tex}

{tex}x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{{v\cos v - 1}}{{\cos v}} - v{/tex}

{tex}x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{{v\cos v + 1 - v\cos v}}{{\cos v}}{/tex}

{tex}x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{\cos v}}{/tex}

{tex}\int {\cos vdv} = \int {\frac{{dx}}{x}} {/tex}

{tex}\sin v = \log x + \log c{/tex}sin v = log x + log c

{tex}\sin v = \log \left| {cx} \right|{/tex} sin v = log|cx|{tex}\left[ {\because y = vx} \right]{/tex}[ y = vx ]

{tex}\sin \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right) = \log \left| {cx} \right|{/tex}

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}+ 2y tan x = sin x, given that y = 0 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} + 2y tan x = sin x, given that y = 0 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} + 2ytanx = sinx Given equation is a linear differential equation in the form {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} + Py = Q Here, P = 2tanx and Q = sinx Now, Integration Factor(IF) = {tex}e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int 2 \tan x d x }{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { 2 \int \tan x d x } \\= e ^ { 2 \log | \sec x | }{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { \log \sec ^ { 2 } x } {/tex} {tex}= \sec ^ { 2 } x{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because e ^ { \log f ( x ) } = f ( x ) \right]{/tex} The solution of differential equation is given by {tex}y \cdot ( I F ) = \int ( \operatorname { IF } ) Q d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \sec ^ { 2 } x \cdot \sin x d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \frac { \sin x } { \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \frac { \sin x } { \cos x } \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos x } d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \tan x \cdot \sec x d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \sec x + C{/tex} Dividing with sec 2 x on both sides, we get {tex}\Rightarrow y = \frac { 1 } { \sec x } + \frac { C } { \sec ^ { 2 } x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = cosx + C.cos 2 x According to the question, y = 0 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} {tex}0 = \cos \frac { \pi } { 3 } + C \cdot \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 0 = \frac { 1 } { 2 } + C \cdot \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { - 1 } { 2 } = \frac { C } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow C = - 2{/tex} Now, y = cosx + C.cos 2 x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = cos x - 2 cos 2 x Therefore, the required particular solution is y = cos x - 2 cos 2 x
solution: According to the question, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} + 2ytanx = sinx Given equation is a linear differential equation in the form {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} + Py = Q Here, P = 2tanx and Q = sinx Now, Integration Factor(IF) = {tex}e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int 2 \tan x d x }{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { 2 \int \tan x d x } \\= e ^ { 2 \log | \sec x | }{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { \log \sec ^ { 2 } x } {/tex} {tex}= \sec ^ { 2 } x{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because e ^ { \log f ( x ) } = f ( x ) \right]{/tex} The solution of differential equation is given by {tex}y \cdot ( I F ) = \int ( \operatorname { IF } ) Q d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \sec ^ { 2 } x \cdot \sin x d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \frac { \sin x } { \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \frac { \sin x } { \cos x } \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos x } d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \tan x \cdot \sec x d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \sec x + C{/tex} Dividing with sec 2 x on both sides, we get {tex}\Rightarrow y = \frac { 1 } { \sec x } + \frac { C } { \sec ^ { 2 } x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = cosx + C.cos 2 x According to the question, y = 0 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} {tex}0 = \cos \frac { \pi } { 3 } + C \cdot \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 0 = \frac { 1 } { 2 } + C \cdot \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { - 1 } { 2 } = \frac { C } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow C = - 2{/tex} Now, y = cosx + C.cos 2 x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = cos x - 2 cos 2 x Therefore, the required particular solution is y = cos x - 2 cos 2 x
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}+ 2ytanx = sinx
Given equation is a linear differential equation in the form {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}+ Py = Q
Here, P = 2tanx and Q = sinx
Now, Integration Factor(IF) = {tex}e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int 2 \tan x d x }{/tex}
{tex}= e ^ { 2 \int \tan x d x } \\= e ^ { 2 \log | \sec x | }{/tex}
{tex}= e ^ { \log \sec ^ { 2 } x } {/tex}
{tex}= \sec ^ { 2 } x{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because e ^ { \log f ( x ) } = f ( x ) \right]{/tex}
The solution of differential equation is given by
{tex}y \cdot ( I F ) = \int ( \operatorname { IF } ) Q d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \sec ^ { 2 } x \cdot \sin x d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \frac { \sin x } { \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \frac { \sin x } { \cos x } \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos x } d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \tan x \cdot \sec x d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \sec x + C{/tex}
 Dividing with sec2x on both sides, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow y = \frac { 1 } { \sec x } + \frac { C } { \sec ^ { 2 } x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}y = cosx + C.cos2x
According to the question, y = 0 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
{tex}0 = \cos \frac { \pi } { 3 } + C \cdot \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 0 = \frac { 1 } { 2 } + C \cdot \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { - 1 } { 2 } = \frac { C } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow C = - 2{/tex}
Now, y = cosx + C.cos2x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = cos x - 2 cos2x
Therefore, the required particular solution is y = cos x - 2 cos2x

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } - 3 y \cot x = \sin 2 x{/tex} , given that y = 2 when {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } - 3 y \cot x = \sin 2 x{/tex} , given that y = 2 when {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} .
explanation: Given, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } - 3 y \cot x = \sin 2 x{/tex} ...(i) This is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} , here P = -3 cot x and Q = sin 2x {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { I } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { P } d x } = e ^ { - 3 \int \cot x d x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { - 3 \log | \sin x | } = e ^ { \log | \sin x | ^ { - 3 } } = | \sin x | ^ { - 3 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The general solution of diﬀerential equation is given by {tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( \mathrm { IF } \times Q ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = \int ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } ( \sin 2 x ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { 2 \sin x \cos x } { \sin ^ { 3 } x } d x + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = \int \frac { 2 \cos x } { \sin ^ { 2 } x } d x + C{/tex} ...(i) Therefore,on putting sin x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x dx = dt in Eq. (i), we get {tex}y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = 2 \int \frac { d t } { t ^ { 2 } } + C = 2 \times \frac { t ^ { - 1 } } { - 1 } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = - \frac { 2 } { t } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = \frac { - 2 } { \sin x } + C{/tex} [put t = sin x] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -2 sin 2 x + C sin 3 x ...(ii) Therefore,on putting {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} and y = 2 in Eq. (ii), we get, {tex}2 = - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { \pi } { 2 } + C \sin ^ { 3 } \frac { \pi } { 2 } \Rightarrow 2 = - 2 \cdot 1 + C \cdot 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} C = 4 {tex}\therefore y = - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } x + 4 \sin ^ { 3 } x{/tex}
solution: Given, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } - 3 y \cot x = \sin 2 x{/tex} ...(i) This is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} , here P = -3 cot x and Q = sin 2x {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { I } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { P } d x } = e ^ { - 3 \int \cot x d x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { - 3 \log | \sin x | } = e ^ { \log | \sin x | ^ { - 3 } } = | \sin x | ^ { - 3 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The general solution of diﬀerential equation is given by {tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( \mathrm { IF } \times Q ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = \int ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } ( \sin 2 x ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { 2 \sin x \cos x } { \sin ^ { 3 } x } d x + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = \int \frac { 2 \cos x } { \sin ^ { 2 } x } d x + C{/tex} ...(i) Therefore,on putting sin x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x dx = dt in Eq. (i), we get {tex}y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = 2 \int \frac { d t } { t ^ { 2 } } + C = 2 \times \frac { t ^ { - 1 } } { - 1 } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = - \frac { 2 } { t } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = \frac { - 2 } { \sin x } + C{/tex} [put t = sin x] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -2 sin 2 x + C sin 3 x ...(ii) Therefore,on putting {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} and y = 2 in Eq. (ii), we get, {tex}2 = - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { \pi } { 2 } + C \sin ^ { 3 } \frac { \pi } { 2 } \Rightarrow 2 = - 2 \cdot 1 + C \cdot 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} C = 4 {tex}\therefore y = - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } x + 4 \sin ^ { 3 } x{/tex}
solution_html:
Given,  {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } - 3 y \cot x = \sin 2 x{/tex} ...(i)
This is a linear differential equation of the form
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex}, here P = -3 cot x and Q = sin 2x
{tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { I } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { P } d x } = e ^ { - 3 \int \cot x d x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { - 3 \log | \sin x | } = e ^ { \log | \sin x | ^ { - 3 } } = | \sin x | ^ { - 3 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The general solution of diﬀerential equation is given by
{tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( \mathrm { IF } \times Q ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = \int ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } ( \sin 2 x ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}= \int \frac { 2 \sin x \cos x } { \sin ^ { 3 } x } d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = \int \frac { 2 \cos x } { \sin ^ { 2 } x } d x + C{/tex} ...(i)
Therefore,on putting sin x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x dx = dt  in  Eq. (i), we get
{tex}y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = 2 \int \frac { d t } { t ^ { 2 } } + C = 2 \times \frac { t ^ { - 1 } } { - 1 } + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = - \frac { 2 } { t } + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = \frac { - 2 } { \sin x } + C{/tex} [put t = sin x]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -2 sin2 x + C sin3 x ...(ii)
Therefore,on putting {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} and y = 2 in Eq. (ii), we get,
{tex}2 = - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { \pi } { 2 } + C \sin ^ { 3 } \frac { \pi } { 2 } \Rightarrow 2 = - 2 \cdot 1 + C \cdot 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} C = 4
{tex}\therefore y = - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } x + 4 \sin ^ { 3 } x{/tex}

question_html: Verify that {tex}y=c e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} is a solution of the differential equation {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+(2 x-1) \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}
question_text: Verify that {tex}y=c e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} is a solution of the differential equation {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+(2 x-1) \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}
explanation: We have, {tex}y=c e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} ...(i) Differentiating both sides of (i) with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=c e^{\tan ^{-1} x} \frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} ...(ii) Differentiating both sides of (ii) with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=c \frac{\left(1+x^{2}\right) e^{\tan ^{-1} x} \frac{1}{1+x^{2}}-e^{\tan ^{-1} x}(2 x)}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=c \frac{e^{\tan ^{-1} x}-2 x e^{\tan ^{-1} x}}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=c \frac{(1-2 x) e^{\tan ^{-1} x}}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=c(1-2 x) \frac{e^{\tan ^{-1} x}}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=(1-2 x) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} ...[Using eqation (ii)] {tex}\Rightarrow\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+(2 x-1) \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex} Hence, the given function is the solution to the given differential equation.
solution: We have, {tex}y=c e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} ...(i) Differentiating both sides of (i) with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=c e^{\tan ^{-1} x} \frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} ...(ii) Differentiating both sides of (ii) with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=c \frac{\left(1+x^{2}\right) e^{\tan ^{-1} x} \frac{1}{1+x^{2}}-e^{\tan ^{-1} x}(2 x)}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=c \frac{e^{\tan ^{-1} x}-2 x e^{\tan ^{-1} x}}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=c \frac{(1-2 x) e^{\tan ^{-1} x}}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=c(1-2 x) \frac{e^{\tan ^{-1} x}}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=(1-2 x) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} ...[Using eqation (ii)] {tex}\Rightarrow\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+(2 x-1) \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex} Hence, the given function is the solution to the given differential equation.
solution_html:
We have, {tex}y=c e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex}...(i)
Differentiating both sides of (i) with respect to x, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=c e^{\tan ^{-1} x} \frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} ...(ii)
Differentiating both sides of (ii) with respect to x, we get
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=c \frac{\left(1+x^{2}\right) e^{\tan ^{-1} x} \frac{1}{1+x^{2}}-e^{\tan ^{-1} x}(2 x)}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=c \frac{e^{\tan ^{-1} x}-2 x e^{\tan ^{-1} x}}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=c \frac{(1-2 x) e^{\tan ^{-1} x}}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=c(1-2 x) \frac{e^{\tan ^{-1} x}}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=(1-2 x) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} ...[Using eqation (ii)]
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+(2 x-1) \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}
Hence, the given function is the solution to the given differential equation.
