question_html: In the differential equation show that it is homogeneous and solve it: (x2 - y2)dx + 2xy dy = 0
question_text: In the differential equation show that it is homogeneous and solve it: (x 2 - y 2 )dx + 2xy dy = 0
explanation: The given differential equation is, x 2 - y 2 )dx + 2xy dy = 0 {tex}\begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}-\mathrm{x}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{xy}}=\frac{\mathrm{y}}{2 \mathrm{x}}-\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)^{-1} \end{aligned}{/tex} {tex}\begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right) \end{aligned}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} the given differential equation is a homogenous equation. The solution of the given differential equation is: Put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}\\{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{vx}}{2 \mathrm{x}}-\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{vx}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)^{-1}\\ {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{2}-(2 \mathrm{v})^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{2}-\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{v}}-\mathrm{v}\\{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\frac{\mathrm{v}}{2}-\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{v}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{v}^{2}+2}{4 \mathrm{v}}\right)\\{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{v}^{2}+1}=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} Integrating both the sides we get: {tex}\int \frac{2 \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{v}^{2}+1} \mathrm{dv}=-\int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{c}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} In |v 2 + 1| = -In |x| + Inc Resubstituting the value of y = vx we get {tex}\Rightarrow \ln \left|\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}+1\right|{/tex} + In|x| = Inc {tex}\Rightarrow\left(\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}+1\right){/tex} (x) = c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 + y 2 = cx, which is the required solution.
solution: The given differential equation is, x 2 - y 2 )dx + 2xy dy = 0 {tex}\begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}-\mathrm{x}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{xy}}=\frac{\mathrm{y}}{2 \mathrm{x}}-\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)^{-1} \end{aligned}{/tex} {tex}\begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right) \end{aligned}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} the given differential equation is a homogenous equation. The solution of the given differential equation is: Put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}\\{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{vx}}{2 \mathrm{x}}-\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{vx}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)^{-1}\\ {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{2}-(2 \mathrm{v})^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{2}-\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{v}}-\mathrm{v}\\{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\frac{\mathrm{v}}{2}-\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{v}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{v}^{2}+2}{4 \mathrm{v}}\right)\\{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{v}^{2}+1}=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} Integrating both the sides we get: {tex}\int \frac{2 \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{v}^{2}+1} \mathrm{dv}=-\int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{c}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} In |v 2 + 1| = -In |x| + Inc Resubstituting the value of y = vx we get {tex}\Rightarrow \ln \left|\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}+1\right|{/tex} + In|x| = Inc {tex}\Rightarrow\left(\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}+1\right){/tex} (x) = c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 + y 2 = cx, which is the required solution.
solution_html:
The given differential equation is,

x2 - y2)dx + 2xy dy = 0
{tex}\begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}-\mathrm{x}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{xy}}=\frac{\mathrm{y}}{2 \mathrm{x}}-\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)^{-1} \end{aligned}{/tex}
{tex}\begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right) \end{aligned}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} the given differential equation is a homogenous equation.
The solution of the given differential equation is:
Put y = vx
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}\\{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{vx}}{2 \mathrm{x}}-\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{vx}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)^{-1}\\ {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{2}-(2 \mathrm{v})^{-1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{2}-\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{v}}-\mathrm{v}\\{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\frac{\mathrm{v}}{2}-\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{v}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{v}^{2}+2}{4 \mathrm{v}}\right)\\{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{v}^{2}+1}=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}}{/tex}
Integrating both the sides we get:
{tex}\int \frac{2 \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{v}^{2}+1} \mathrm{dv}=-\int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{c}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} In |v2 + 1| = -In |x| + Inc
Resubstituting the value of y = vx we get
{tex}\Rightarrow \ln \left|\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}+1\right|{/tex} + In|x| = Inc
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}+1\right){/tex}(x) = c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 = cx, which is the required solution.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the differential equation cos {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} = a, (a {tex}\in \mathbb{R}{/tex}).
question_text: Solve the differential equation cos {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} = a, (a {tex}\in \mathbb{R}{/tex} ).
explanation: Here we have, cos {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} = a {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\cos ^{-1} a{/tex} {tex}\int d y=\cos ^{-1} a \cdot \int d x{/tex} y = x cos -1 a + c
solution: Here we have, cos {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} = a {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\cos ^{-1} a{/tex} {tex}\int d y=\cos ^{-1} a \cdot \int d x{/tex} y = x cos -1 a + c
solution_html:
Here we have,  cos {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} = a
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\cos ^{-1} a{/tex}
{tex}\int d y=\cos ^{-1} a \cdot \int d x{/tex}
y = x cos-1 a + c

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}( x - y ) \frac { d y } { d x } {/tex} = x + 2y given that when x = 1, y = 0.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}( x - y ) \frac { d y } { d x } {/tex} = x + 2y given that when x = 1, y = 0.
explanation: (x - y) {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = x + 2y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x+2 y}{x-y}{/tex} This is a homogenous differential equation. Put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = v + x {tex} \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} v + x {tex} \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1+2 v}{1-v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex} \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1+2 v}{1-v}{/tex} - v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex} \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1+2 v-v+v^{2}}{1-v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1-v}{v^{2}+v+1} d v{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} Integrating on both the sides, we get {tex}\int \frac{1-v}{v^{2}+v+1} d v=\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex} ....(1) Now, {tex}\int \frac{1-v}{v^{2}+v+1} d v{/tex} = {tex}-\int \frac{v-1}{v^{2}+v+1}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 v-2}{v^{2}+v+1}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 v+1}{v^{2}+v+1} d v-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{-3 d v}{v^{2}+v+1}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 v+1}{v^{2}+v+1} d v+\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{d v}{v^{2}+v+1}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \log \left|v^{2}+v+1\right|+\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{d v}{v^{2}+v+\frac{1}{4}+1-\frac{1}{4}}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \log \left|v^{2}+v+1\right|{/tex} + {tex}\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{d v}{\left(v+\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \log \left|v^{2}+v+1\right|{/tex} + {tex}\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)} \tan ^{-1} \frac{\left(v+\frac{1}{2}\right)}{\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \log \left|v^{2}+v+1\right|{/tex} + {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 v+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex} From (1), we get {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \log \left|v^{2}+v+1\right|{/tex} + {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 v+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex} = logx + C Putting v = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} , we get {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{2 y}{x}+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}+\frac{y}{x}+1\right){/tex} = logx + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 y+x}{\sqrt{3} x}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{y^{2}+x y+x^{2}}{x^{2}}\right){/tex} = logx + C....(2) Put x = 1 and y = 0 in the above equation, we get {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{2 \times 0+1}{\sqrt{3} \times 1}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{2} \log \left[\frac{0+0+1}{1}\right]{/tex} = log 1 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log 1{/tex} = log 1 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{3} \times \frac{\pi}{6}-0{/tex} = 0 + C .....[as log 1 = 0] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3} \pi}{6}{/tex} Now, putting the value of C in (2) equation, we get {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 y+x}{\sqrt{3} x}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{y^{2}+x y+x^{2}}{x^{2}}\right){/tex} = logx + {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3} \pi}{6}{/tex}
solution: (x - y) {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = x + 2y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x+2 y}{x-y}{/tex} This is a homogenous differential equation. Put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = v + x {tex} \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} v + x {tex} \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1+2 v}{1-v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex} \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1+2 v}{1-v}{/tex} - v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex} \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1+2 v-v+v^{2}}{1-v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1-v}{v^{2}+v+1} d v{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} Integrating on both the sides, we get {tex}\int \frac{1-v}{v^{2}+v+1} d v=\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex} ....(1) Now, {tex}\int \frac{1-v}{v^{2}+v+1} d v{/tex} = {tex}-\int \frac{v-1}{v^{2}+v+1}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 v-2}{v^{2}+v+1}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 v+1}{v^{2}+v+1} d v-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{-3 d v}{v^{2}+v+1}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 v+1}{v^{2}+v+1} d v+\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{d v}{v^{2}+v+1}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \log \left|v^{2}+v+1\right|+\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{d v}{v^{2}+v+\frac{1}{4}+1-\frac{1}{4}}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \log \left|v^{2}+v+1\right|{/tex} + {tex}\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{d v}{\left(v+\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \log \left|v^{2}+v+1\right|{/tex} + {tex}\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)} \tan ^{-1} \frac{\left(v+\frac{1}{2}\right)}{\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \log \left|v^{2}+v+1\right|{/tex} + {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 v+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex} From (1), we get {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \log \left|v^{2}+v+1\right|{/tex} + {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 v+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex} = logx + C Putting v = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} , we get {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{2 y}{x}+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}+\frac{y}{x}+1\right){/tex} = logx + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 y+x}{\sqrt{3} x}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{y^{2}+x y+x^{2}}{x^{2}}\right){/tex} = logx + C....(2) Put x = 1 and y = 0 in the above equation, we get {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{2 \times 0+1}{\sqrt{3} \times 1}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{2} \log \left[\frac{0+0+1}{1}\right]{/tex} = log 1 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log 1{/tex} = log 1 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{3} \times \frac{\pi}{6}-0{/tex} = 0 + C .....[as log 1 = 0] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3} \pi}{6}{/tex} Now, putting the value of C in (2) equation, we get {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 y+x}{\sqrt{3} x}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{y^{2}+x y+x^{2}}{x^{2}}\right){/tex} = logx + {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3} \pi}{6}{/tex}
solution_html:
(x - y){tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = x + 2y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x+2 y}{x-y}{/tex}
This is a homogenous differential equation.
Put y = vx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = v + x{tex} \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} v + x{tex} \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1+2 v}{1-v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x{tex} \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1+2 v}{1-v}{/tex} - v
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x{tex} \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1+2 v-v+v^{2}}{1-v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1-v}{v^{2}+v+1} d v{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
Integrating on both the sides, we get 
{tex}\int \frac{1-v}{v^{2}+v+1} d v=\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex}....(1)
Now,
{tex}\int \frac{1-v}{v^{2}+v+1} d v{/tex} = {tex}-\int \frac{v-1}{v^{2}+v+1}{/tex}
= {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 v-2}{v^{2}+v+1}{/tex}
= {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 v+1}{v^{2}+v+1} d v-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{-3 d v}{v^{2}+v+1}{/tex}
= {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 v+1}{v^{2}+v+1} d v+\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{d v}{v^{2}+v+1}{/tex}
= {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \log \left|v^{2}+v+1\right|+\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{d v}{v^{2}+v+\frac{1}{4}+1-\frac{1}{4}}{/tex}
= {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \log \left|v^{2}+v+1\right|{/tex}+ {tex}\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{d v}{\left(v+\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \log \left|v^{2}+v+1\right|{/tex}+ {tex}\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)} \tan ^{-1} \frac{\left(v+\frac{1}{2}\right)}{\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)}{/tex}
= {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \log \left|v^{2}+v+1\right|{/tex}+ {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 v+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex}
From (1), we get
{tex}-\frac{1}{2} \log \left|v^{2}+v+1\right|{/tex}+ {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 v+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex} = logx + C
Putting v = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex}, we get
{tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{2 y}{x}+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}+\frac{y}{x}+1\right){/tex}= logx + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 y+x}{\sqrt{3} x}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{y^{2}+x y+x^{2}}{x^{2}}\right){/tex} = logx + C....(2)
Put x = 1 and y = 0 in the above equation, we get
{tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{2 \times 0+1}{\sqrt{3} \times 1}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\frac{1}{2} \log \left[\frac{0+0+1}{1}\right]{/tex} = log 1 + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log 1{/tex} = log 1 + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{3} \times \frac{\pi}{6}-0{/tex} = 0 + C .....[as log 1 = 0]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3} \pi}{6}{/tex}
Now, putting the value of C in (2) equation, we get
 {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 y+x}{\sqrt{3} x}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{y^{2}+x y+x^{2}}{x^{2}}\right){/tex} = logx + {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3} \pi}{6}{/tex}

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation x cos{tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = y cos{tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} + x, given that when x = 1, y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation x cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = y cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} + x, given that when x = 1, y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
explanation: The given differential equation is, xcos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = y cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} + x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+x}{x \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{/tex} This is a homogeneous differential equation Put y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} , we get {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v x \cos v+x}{x \cos v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v \cos v+1}{\cos v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v \cos v+1-v \cos v}{\cos v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{\cos v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos v dv = {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} dx Integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int{/tex} cos v dv = {tex}\int \frac{1}{x}{/tex} dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin v = log |x| + C Put v = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} , we get sin {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = log |x| + C ...(i) At x = 1, y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} ...(given) Putting C = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} in (i), we get sin {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = log |x| + {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} Hence, sin {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = log x + {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} is the required solution.
solution: The given differential equation is, xcos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = y cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} + x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+x}{x \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{/tex} This is a homogeneous differential equation Put y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} , we get {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v x \cos v+x}{x \cos v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v \cos v+1}{\cos v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v \cos v+1-v \cos v}{\cos v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{\cos v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos v dv = {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} dx Integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int{/tex} cos v dv = {tex}\int \frac{1}{x}{/tex} dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin v = log |x| + C Put v = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} , we get sin {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = log |x| + C ...(i) At x = 1, y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} ...(given) Putting C = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} in (i), we get sin {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = log |x| + {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} Hence, sin {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = log x + {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} is the required solution.
solution_html:
The given differential equation is,

xcos{tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = y cos{tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} + x
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+x}{x \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{/tex}
This is a homogeneous differential equation
Put y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}, we get
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v x \cos v+x}{x \cos v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v \cos v+1}{\cos v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v \cos v+1-v \cos v}{\cos v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{\cos v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos v dv = {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} dx
Integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int{/tex} cos v dv = {tex}\int \frac{1}{x}{/tex} dx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin v = log |x| + C
Put v = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex}, we get
sin{tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = log |x| + C ...(i)
At x = 1, y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} ...(given)
Putting C = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} in (i), we get
sin{tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = log |x| + {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
Hence, sin{tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = log x + {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} is the required solution.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex}x \frac { d y } { d x } + y = x \cdot \cos x + \sin x{/tex}, given that y = 1 when {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}.
question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex}x \frac { d y } { d x } + y = x \cdot \cos x + \sin x{/tex} , given that y = 1 when {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question, Given differential equation is {tex}x \frac { d y } { d x } + y = x \cos x + \sin x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } + \frac { y } { x } = \cos x + \frac { \sin x } { x }{/tex} [dividing both sides by x] which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} , here {tex}P = \frac { 1 } { x }{/tex} and {tex}Q = \cos x + \frac { \sin x } { x }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x } = e ^ { \log | x | } = x{/tex} The general solution is given by {tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y x = \int \left( \cos x + \frac { \sin x } { x } \right) x d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y x = \int ( x \cos x + \sin x ) d x + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad xy = \int {\mathop x\limits_I } \mathop {\cos }\limits_{II} xdx + \int {\sin } xdx + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x y = x \int \cos x d x - \int \left[ \frac { d } { d x } ( x ) \int \cos x d x \right] d x{/tex} {tex}+ \int \sin x d x + C{/tex} [using integration by parts] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x y = x \sin x - \int 1 \cdot \sin x d x - \cos x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x y = x \sin x + \cos x - \cos x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x y = x \sin x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin x + C \cdot \frac { 1 } { x }{/tex} ...(i) Also, we have, {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } ; y = 1{/tex} Therefore,on putting {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} and y = 1 in Eq. (i), we get, {tex}1 = 1 + C \cdot \frac { 2 } { \pi } \Rightarrow C = 0{/tex} Therefore,on putting the value of C in Eq. (i), we get y = sin x, as the solution of given differential equation.
solution: According to the question, Given differential equation is {tex}x \frac { d y } { d x } + y = x \cos x + \sin x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } + \frac { y } { x } = \cos x + \frac { \sin x } { x }{/tex} [dividing both sides by x] which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} , here {tex}P = \frac { 1 } { x }{/tex} and {tex}Q = \cos x + \frac { \sin x } { x }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x } = e ^ { \log | x | } = x{/tex} The general solution is given by {tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y x = \int \left( \cos x + \frac { \sin x } { x } \right) x d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y x = \int ( x \cos x + \sin x ) d x + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad xy = \int {\mathop x\limits_I } \mathop {\cos }\limits_{II} xdx + \int {\sin } xdx + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x y = x \int \cos x d x - \int \left[ \frac { d } { d x } ( x ) \int \cos x d x \right] d x{/tex} {tex}+ \int \sin x d x + C{/tex} [using integration by parts] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x y = x \sin x - \int 1 \cdot \sin x d x - \cos x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x y = x \sin x + \cos x - \cos x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x y = x \sin x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin x + C \cdot \frac { 1 } { x }{/tex} ...(i) Also, we have, {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } ; y = 1{/tex} Therefore,on putting {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} and y = 1 in Eq. (i), we get, {tex}1 = 1 + C \cdot \frac { 2 } { \pi } \Rightarrow C = 0{/tex} Therefore,on putting the value of C in Eq. (i), we get y = sin x, as the solution of given differential equation.
solution_html:
According to the question, Given differential equation is
{tex}x \frac { d y } { d x } + y = x \cos x + \sin x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } + \frac { y } { x } = \cos x + \frac { \sin x } { x }{/tex} [dividing both sides by x]
which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} , here {tex}P = \frac { 1 } { x }{/tex} and {tex}Q = \cos x + \frac { \sin x } { x }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x } = e ^ { \log | x | } = x{/tex}
The general solution is given by
{tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y x = \int \left( \cos x + \frac { \sin x } { x } \right) x d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y x = \int ( x \cos x + \sin x ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad xy = \int {\mathop x\limits_I } \mathop {\cos }\limits_{II} xdx + \int {\sin } xdx + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x y = x \int \cos x d x - \int \left[ \frac { d } { d x } ( x ) \int \cos x d x \right] d x{/tex}{tex}+ \int \sin x d x + C{/tex} [using integration by parts]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x y = x \sin x - \int 1 \cdot \sin x d x - \cos x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x y = x \sin x + \cos x - \cos x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x y = x \sin x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin x + C \cdot \frac { 1 } { x }{/tex} ...(i)
Also, we have, {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } ; y = 1{/tex}
Therefore,on putting {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} and y = 1 in Eq. (i), we get,
{tex}1 = 1 + C \cdot \frac { 2 } { \pi } \Rightarrow C = 0{/tex}
Therefore,on putting the value of C in Eq. (i), we get
y = sin x, as the solution of given differential equation.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Show that the family of curves for which the slope of the tangent at any point (x, y) on it is {tex} \frac { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } { 2 x y }{/tex}, is given by x2 - y2 = cx.
question_text: Show that the family of curves for which the slope of the tangent at any point (x, y) on it is {tex} \frac { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } { 2 x y }{/tex} , is given by x 2 - y 2 = cx.
explanation: We have, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } { 2 x y }{/tex} Clearly, each of the function x 2 + y 2 and 2xy is a homogeneous function of degree 2, so the given equation is homogeneous. Put y = vx and {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} The given equation becomes {tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { x ^ { 2 } + v ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } } { 2 v x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v ^ { 2 } + 1 } { 2 v }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \left( \frac { v ^ { 2 } + 1 } { 2 v } - v \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v ^ { 2 } + 1 - 2 v ^ { 2 } } { 2 v } = \frac { 1 - v ^ { 2 } } { 2 v }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { - \left( v ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) } { 2 v }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { 2 v } { v ^ { 2 } - 1 } d v = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} [using variable separable form] On integrating both sides, we get -log|v 2 - 1| = log x - log C 1 {tex}\Rightarrow - \log \left| v ^ { 2 } - 1 \right| - \log x = - \log C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| x \left( v ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \right| = \log C _ { 1 } \Rightarrow x \left( v ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) = C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \left( \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } - 1 \right) = C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \left( \frac { y ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } \right) = C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { y ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } { x } = C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } = - C _ { 1 } x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } = C x{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because C = - C _ { 1 } \right]{/tex}
solution: We have, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } { 2 x y }{/tex} Clearly, each of the function x 2 + y 2 and 2xy is a homogeneous function of degree 2, so the given equation is homogeneous. Put y = vx and {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} The given equation becomes {tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { x ^ { 2 } + v ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } } { 2 v x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v ^ { 2 } + 1 } { 2 v }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \left( \frac { v ^ { 2 } + 1 } { 2 v } - v \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v ^ { 2 } + 1 - 2 v ^ { 2 } } { 2 v } = \frac { 1 - v ^ { 2 } } { 2 v }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { - \left( v ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) } { 2 v }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { 2 v } { v ^ { 2 } - 1 } d v = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} [using variable separable form] On integrating both sides, we get -log|v 2 - 1| = log x - log C 1 {tex}\Rightarrow - \log \left| v ^ { 2 } - 1 \right| - \log x = - \log C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| x \left( v ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \right| = \log C _ { 1 } \Rightarrow x \left( v ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) = C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \left( \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } - 1 \right) = C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \left( \frac { y ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } \right) = C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { y ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } { x } = C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } = - C _ { 1 } x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } = C x{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because C = - C _ { 1 } \right]{/tex}

We have, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } { 2 x y }{/tex}
Clearly, each of the function x2 + y2 and 2xy is a homogeneous function of degree 2, so the given equation is homogeneous.
Put y = vx and {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex}
The given equation becomes
{tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { x ^ { 2 } + v ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } } { 2 v x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v ^ { 2 } + 1 } { 2 v }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \left( \frac { v ^ { 2 } + 1 } { 2 v } - v \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v ^ { 2 } + 1 - 2 v ^ { 2 } } { 2 v } = \frac { 1 - v ^ { 2 } } { 2 v }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { - \left( v ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) } { 2 v }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { 2 v } { v ^ { 2 } - 1 } d v = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} [using variable separable form]
On integrating both sides, we get
-log|v2 - 1| = log x - log C1
{tex}\Rightarrow - \log \left| v ^ { 2 } - 1 \right| - \log x = - \log C _ { 1 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| x \left( v ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \right| = \log C _ { 1 } \Rightarrow x \left( v ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) = C _ { 1 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \left( \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } - 1 \right) = C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \left( \frac { y ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } \right) = C _ { 1 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { y ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } { x } = C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } = - C _ { 1 } x{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } = C x{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because C = - C _ { 1 } \right]{/tex}

explanation: Given differential equation can be rewritten as {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x ^ { 3 } - 3 x y ^ { 2 } } { y ^ { 3 } - 3 x ^ { 2 }y }{/tex} ...(i) This is a homogeneous differential equation, so, put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} Then, Eq. (i) becomes {tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { x ^ { 3 } - 3 x ( v x ) ^ { 2 } } { ( v x ) ^ { 3 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } ( v x ) }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 - 3 v ^ { 2 } } { v ^ { 3 } - 3 v }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 - 3 v ^ { 2 } } { v ^ { 3 } - 3 v } - v{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 - 3 v ^ { 2 } - v ^ { 4 } + 3 v ^ { 2 } } { v ^ { 3 } - 3 v }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 - v ^ { 4 } } { v ^ { 3 } - 3 v }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left( \frac { v ^ { 3 } - 3 v } { 1 - v ^ { 4 } } \right) d v = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex} \int \left( \frac { v ^ { 3 } - 3 v } { 1 - v ^ { 4 } } \right) d v = \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \int \frac { v ^ { 3 } } { 1 - v ^ { 4 } } d v - 3 \int \frac { v } { 1 - v ^ { 4 } } d v = \log x + \log C{/tex} ...(ii) {tex} \Rightarrow - \frac{1}{4}\log \left( {1 - {v^4}} \right) - \frac{3}{4}\log \left| {\frac{{1 + {v^2}}}{{1 - {v^2}}}} \right| = \log x + \log C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log \left[ \left( 1 - v ^ { 4 } \right) \left( \frac { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } { 1 - v ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 3 } \right] = \log ( C x ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log \left[ \left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) \left( 1 + v ^ { 2 } \right) \times \frac { \left( 1 + v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 } } { \left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 } } \right] = \log ( C x ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left[ \frac { \left( 1 + v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } } { \left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } \right] ^ { - 1 / 4 } = \log Cx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \left( 1 + v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } } { \left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } = ( C x ) ^ { - 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } / x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } } { \left( 1 - y ^ { 2 } / x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { C ^ { 4 } x ^ { 4 } }{/tex} {tex}[ \because y = v x ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } } { x ^ { 4 } \left( x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { C ^ { 4 } x ^ { 4 } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left( x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } \right) = C ^ { 2 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex} [taking square root] {tex} \Rightarrow \left( x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } \right) = C _ { 1 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex} (where C 1 = C {tex}^2{/tex} ) .

v ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 3 } \right] = \log ( C x ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log \left[ \left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) \left( 1 + v ^ { 2 } \right) \times \frac { \left( 1 + v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 } } { \left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 } } \right] = \log ( C x ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left[ \frac { \left( 1 + v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } } { \left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } \right] ^ { - 1 / 4 } = \log Cx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \left( 1 + v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } } { \left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } = ( C x ) ^ { - 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } / x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } } { \left( 1 - y ^ { 2 } / x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { C ^ { 4 } x ^ { 4 } }{/tex} {tex}[ \because y = v x ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } } { x ^ { 4 } \left( x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } \right) = C _ { 1 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex} (where C 1 = C {tex}^2{/tex} ) .
solution_html:
Given  differential equation can be rewritten as
{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x ^ { 3 } - 3 x y ^ { 2 } } { y ^ { 3 } - 3 x ^ { 2 }y }{/tex} ...(i)
This is a homogeneous differential equation, so, put y = vx
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex}
Then, Eq. (i) becomes
{tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { x ^ { 3 } - 3 x ( v x ) ^ { 2 } } { ( v x ) ^ { 3 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } ( v x ) }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 - 3 v ^ { 2 } } { v ^ { 3 } - 3 v }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 - 3 v ^ { 2 } } { v ^ { 3 } - 3 v } - v{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 - 3 v ^ { 2 } - v ^ { 4 } + 3 v ^ { 2 } } { v ^ { 3 } - 3 v }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 - v ^ { 4 } } { v ^ { 3 } - 3 v }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \left( \frac { v ^ { 3 } - 3 v } { 1 - v ^ { 4 } } \right) d v = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex} \int \left( \frac { v ^ { 3 } - 3 v } { 1 - v ^ { 4 } } \right) d v = \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \int \frac { v ^ { 3 } } { 1 - v ^ { 4 } } d v - 3 \int \frac { v } { 1 - v ^ { 4 } } d v = \log x + \log C{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex} \Rightarrow - \frac{1}{4}\log \left( {1 - {v^4}} \right) - \frac{3}{4}\log \left| {\frac{{1 + {v^2}}}{{1 - {v^2}}}} \right| = \log x + \log C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log \left[ \left( 1 - v ^ { 4 } \right) \left( \frac { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } { 1 - v ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 3 } \right] = \log ( C x ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log \left[ \left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) \left( 1 + v ^ { 2 } \right) \times \frac { \left( 1 + v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 } } { \left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 } } \right] = \log ( C x ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left[ \frac { \left( 1 + v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } } { \left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } \right] ^ { - 1 / 4 } = \log Cx{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \left( 1 + v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } } { \left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } = ( C x ) ^ { - 4 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } / x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } } { \left( 1 - y ^ { 2 } / x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { C ^ { 4 } x ^ { 4 } }{/tex} {tex}[ \because y = v x ]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } } { x ^ { 4 } \left( x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { C ^ { 4 } x ^ { 4 } }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \left( x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } \right) = C ^ { 2 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex} [taking square root]
{tex} \Rightarrow \left( x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } \right) = C _ { 1 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex}(where C1 = C{tex}^2{/tex}).

question_html: Solve the differential equation (tan-1x - y)dx = (1 + x2)dy.
question_text: Solve the differential equation (tan -1 x - y)dx = (1 + x 2 )dy.
explanation: Given differential equation, (tan -1 x - y)dx = (1 + x 2 ) dy The above differential equation can be rewritten as, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \tan ^ { - 1 } x - y } { \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } + \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } y = \frac { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...(i) which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} , here {tex}P = \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} and {tex}Q = \frac { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Now, {tex}\mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }dx } = e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The general solution is given by {tex}y \cdot \mathrm { IF } = \int Q \cdot \mathrm { IF } d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \cdot e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } = \int \frac { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \cdot e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } d x + C{/tex} Put {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } x = t \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x = d t{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } = \int t \cdot e ^ { t } d t + C = t \cdot e ^ { t } - \int 1 \cdot e ^ { t } d t + C{/tex} [using integration by parts] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } = t \cdot e ^ { t } - e ^ { t } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } } x = \tan ^ { - 1 } x \cdot e ^ { \operatorname { tan } ^ { - 1 } x } - e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because t = e ^ { { tan } ^ { - 1 } x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } = \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x - 1 \right) e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } + C{/tex}
solution: Given differential equation, (tan -1 x - y)dx = (1 + x 2 ) dy The above differential equation can be rewritten as, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \tan ^ { - 1 } x - y } { \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } + \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } y = \frac { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...(i) which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} , here {tex}P = \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} and {tex}Q = \frac { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Now, {tex}\mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }dx } = e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The general solution is given by {tex}y \cdot \mathrm { IF } = \int Q \cdot \mathrm { IF } d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \cdot e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } = \int \frac { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \cdot e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } d x + C{/tex} Put {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } x = t \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x = d t{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } = \int t \cdot e ^ { t } d t + C = t \cdot e ^ { t } - \int 1 \cdot e ^ { t } d t + C{/tex} [using integration by parts] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } = t \cdot e ^ { t } - e ^ { t } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } } x = \tan ^ { - 1 } x \cdot e ^ { \operatorname { tan } ^ { - 1 } x } - e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because t = e ^ { { tan } ^ { - 1 } x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } = \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x - 1 \right) e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } + C{/tex}
solution_html:
Given differential equation, (tan-1 x - y)dx = (1 + x2) dy

The above differential equation can be rewritten as,
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \tan ^ { - 1 } x - y } { \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } + \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } y = \frac { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...(i)
which is a linear differential equation of the form
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex}, here {tex}P = \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} and {tex}Q = \frac { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
Now, {tex}\mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }dx } = e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The general solution is given by
{tex}y \cdot \mathrm { IF } = \int Q \cdot \mathrm { IF } d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \cdot e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } = \int \frac { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \cdot e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } d x + C{/tex}
Put {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } x = t \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x = d t{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad y e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } = \int t \cdot e ^ { t } d t + C = t \cdot e ^ { t } - \int 1 \cdot e ^ { t } d t + C{/tex} [using integration by parts]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } = t \cdot e ^ { t } - e ^ { t } + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } } x = \tan ^ { - 1 } x \cdot e ^ { \operatorname { tan } ^ { - 1 } x } - e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because t = e ^ { { tan } ^ { - 1 } x } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } = \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x - 1 \right) e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } + C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation: ydx - (x + 2y2)dy = 0.
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation: ydx - (x + 2y 2 )dy = 0.
explanation: According to the question , {tex}ydx - (x + 2y^2)dy = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y \frac { d x } { d y } = x + 2 y ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d x } { d y } - \frac { 1 } { y } x = 2 y{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d x } { d y } + P x = Q{/tex} here {tex} P = \frac { - 1 } { y }{/tex} and {tex}Q = 2y.{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { P } \mathrm { d } y } = e ^ { \int - \frac { 1 } { y } d y } = e ^ { - \mathrm { log } y } = \frac { 1 } { y }{/tex} Hence, required general solution of the differential equation is {tex} x \cdot \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \cdot \mathrm { IF } ) d y + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x \times \frac { 1 } { y } = \int 2 y \times \frac { 1 } { y } d y + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { x } { y } = 2 y + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x = 2 y ^ { 2 } + C y{/tex}
solution: According to the question , {tex}ydx - (x + 2y^2)dy = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y \frac { d x } { d y } = x + 2 y ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d x } { d y } - \frac { 1 } { y } x = 2 y{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d x } { d y } + P x = Q{/tex} here {tex} P = \frac { - 1 } { y }{/tex} and {tex}Q = 2y.{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { P } \mathrm { d } y } = e ^ { \int - \frac { 1 } { y } d y } = e ^ { - \mathrm { log } y } = \frac { 1 } { y }{/tex} Hence, required general solution of the differential equation is {tex} x \cdot \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \cdot \mathrm { IF } ) d y + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x \times \frac { 1 } { y } = \int 2 y \times \frac { 1 } { y } d y + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { x } { y } = 2 y + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x = 2 y ^ { 2 } + C y{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question , 

{tex}ydx - (x + 2y^2)dy = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow y \frac { d x } { d y } = x + 2 y ^ { 2 }{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d x } { d y } - \frac { 1 } { y } x = 2 y{/tex}
which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d x } { d y } + P x = Q{/tex}
here {tex} P = \frac { - 1 } { y }{/tex} and {tex}Q = 2y.{/tex}
 {tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { P } \mathrm { d } y } = e ^ { \int - \frac { 1 } { y } d y } = e ^ { - \mathrm { log } y } = \frac { 1 } { y }{/tex}
Hence, required general solution of the differential equation is
{tex} x \cdot \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \cdot \mathrm { IF } ) d y + C{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow x \times \frac { 1 } { y } = \int 2 y \times \frac { 1 } { y } d y + C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { x } { y } = 2 y + C{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow x = 2 y ^ { 2 } + C y{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } - y = \sin x{/tex} .
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } - y = \sin x{/tex} .
explanation: Given differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } - y = \sin x{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} , here p = -1 and Q = sinx {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int ( - 1 ) d x } = e ^ { - x }{/tex} Now, the general solution of given differential equation is given by {tex}y \cdot ( \mathrm { IF } ) = \int ( \mathrm { IF } ) \cdot Q d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \cdot e ^ { - x } = \int e ^ { - x } \sin x d x + C{/tex} ...(i) {tex}I = \int {\mathop {{e^{ - x}}}\limits_{II} } \mathop {\sin }\limits_I xdx{/tex} ...(ii) By using the method of integration by parts, we get, {tex}Let\ I = \sin x \frac { e ^ { - x } } { ( - 1 ) } - \int \cos x \frac { e ^ { -x } } { ( - 1 ) } d x{/tex} {tex} = - \sin x{e^{ - x}} + \int {\mathop {{e^{ - x}}}\limits_{II} } \mathop {\cos }\limits_I xdx{/tex} Again, by using integration by parts, we get , from Eq. (i), {tex}I = - \sin x e ^ { - x } + \cos x \frac { e ^ { - x } } { ( - 1 ) } - \int ( - \sin x ) \frac { e ^ { - x } } { ( - 1 ) } d x{/tex} {tex}= - \sin x e ^ { - x } - \cos x e ^ { - x } - \int e ^ { - x } \sin x d x{/tex} {tex}= - \sin x e ^ { - x } - \cos x e ^ { - x } - I{/tex} [from Eq. (ii)] {tex}\Rightarrow 2 I = - e ^ { - x } ( \sin x + \cos x ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = - \frac { e ^ { - x } } { 2 } ( \sin x + \cos x ){/tex} Therefore, from Eq. (i), we get, {tex}y \cdot e ^ { - x } = - \frac { e ^ { - x } } { 2 } ( \sin x + \cos x ) + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( \sin x + \cos x ) + C e ^ { x }{/tex}
solution: Given differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } - y = \sin x{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} , here p = -1 and Q = sinx {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int ( - 1 ) d x } = e ^ { - x }{/tex} Now, the general solution of given differential equation is given by {tex}y \cdot ( \mathrm { IF } ) = \int ( \mathrm { IF } ) \cdot Q d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \cdot e ^ { - x } = \int e ^ { - x } \sin x d x + C{/tex} ...(i) {tex}I = \int {\mathop {{e^{ - x}}}\limits_{II} } \mathop {\sin }\limits_I xdx{/tex} ...(ii) By using the method of integration by parts, we get, {tex}Let\ I = \sin x \frac { e ^ { - x } } { ( - 1 ) } - \int \cos x \frac { e ^ { -x } } { ( - 1 ) } d x{/tex} {tex} = - \sin x{e^{ - x}} + \int {\mathop {{e^{ - x}}}\limits_{II} } \mathop {\cos }\limits_I xdx{/tex} Again, by using integration by parts, we get , from Eq. (i), {tex}I = - \sin x e ^ { - x } + \cos x \frac { e ^ { - x } } { ( - 1 ) } - \int ( - \sin x ) \frac { e ^ { - x } } { ( - 1 ) } d x{/tex} {tex}= - \sin x e ^ { - x } - \cos x e ^ { - x } - \int e ^ { - x } \sin x d x{/tex} {tex}= - \sin x e ^ { - x } - \cos x e ^ { - x } - I{/tex} [from Eq. (ii)] {tex}\Rightarrow 2 I = - e ^ { - x } ( \sin x + \cos x ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = - \frac { e ^ { - x } } { 2 } ( \sin x + \cos x ){/tex} Therefore, from Eq. (i), we get, {tex}y \cdot e ^ { - x } = - \frac { e ^ { - x } } { 2 } ( \sin x + \cos x ) + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( \sin x + \cos x ) + C e ^ { x }{/tex}
solution_html:
Given differential equation  {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } - y = \sin x{/tex}  which is a linear differential equation of the form
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} , here p = -1 and Q = sinx
{tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int ( - 1 ) d x } = e ^ { - x }{/tex}
Now, the general solution of given differential equation is given by
{tex}y \cdot ( \mathrm { IF } ) = \int ( \mathrm { IF } ) \cdot Q d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \cdot e ^ { - x } = \int e ^ { - x } \sin x d x + C{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}I = \int {\mathop {{e^{ - x}}}\limits_{II} } \mathop {\sin }\limits_I xdx{/tex} ...(ii)
By using the method of integration by parts, we get,
{tex}Let\ I = \sin x \frac { e ^ { - x } } { ( - 1 ) } - \int \cos x \frac { e ^ { -x } } { ( - 1 ) } d x{/tex}
{tex} = - \sin x{e^{ - x}} + \int {\mathop {{e^{ - x}}}\limits_{II} } \mathop {\cos }\limits_I xdx{/tex}
Again, by using integration by parts, we get , from Eq. (i), 
{tex}I = - \sin x e ^ { - x } + \cos x \frac { e ^ { - x } } { ( - 1 ) } - \int ( - \sin x ) \frac { e ^ { - x } } { ( - 1 ) } d x{/tex}
{tex}= - \sin x e ^ { - x } - \cos x e ^ { - x } - \int e ^ { - x } \sin x d x{/tex}
{tex}= - \sin x e ^ { - x } - \cos x e ^ { - x } - I{/tex}     [from Eq. (ii)]
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 I = - e ^ { - x } ( \sin x + \cos x ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I = - \frac { e ^ { - x } } { 2 } ( \sin x + \cos x ){/tex}
Therefore, from Eq. (i), we get,
{tex}y \cdot e ^ { - x } = - \frac { e ^ { - x } } { 2 } ( \sin x + \cos x ) + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( \sin x + \cos x ) + C e ^ { x }{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Show that the differential equation representing one parameter family of curves is:
(x2 - y2) = c(x2 + y2)2 is (x3 - 3xy2) dx = (y3 - 3x2y)dy
question_text: Show that the differential equation representing one parameter family of curves is: (x 2 - y 2 ) = c(x 2 + y 2 ) 2 is (x 3 - 3xy 2 ) dx = (y 3 - 3x 2 y)dy
explanation: The given equation of one parameter family of curves is (x 2 - y 2 ) = c(x 2 + y 2 ) 2 ...(i) Differentiating (i) with respect to x, have, {tex}2 x-2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=2 c\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)\left(2 x+2 y \frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(x-y \frac{d y}{d x}\right)=2 c\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)\left(x+y \frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} ...(ii) On using the value of c obtained from (i) in (ii), we get {tex}\left(x-y \frac{d y}{d x}\right)=\frac{2\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right)\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)}{\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)^{2}}\left(x+y \frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)\left(x-y \frac{d y}{d x}\right)=2\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right)\left(x+y \frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left\{x\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)-2 x\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right)\right\}=\frac{d y}{d x}\left\{2 y\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right)+y\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)\right\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(3 x y^{2}-x^{3}\right)=\frac{d y}{d x}\left(3 x^{2} y-y^{3}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x 3 - 3xy 2 )dx = (y 3 - 3x 2 y )dy, which is the given differential equation.
solution: The given equation of one parameter family of curves is (x 2 - y 2 ) = c(x 2 + y 2 ) 2 ...(i) Differentiating (i) with respect to x, have, {tex}2 x-2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=2 c\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)\left(2 x+2 y \frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(x-y \frac{d y}{d x}\right)=2 c\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)\left(x+y \frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} ...(ii) On using the value of c obtained from (i) in (ii), we get {tex}\left(x-y \frac{d y}{d x}\right)=\frac{2\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right)\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)}{\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)^{2}}\left(x+y \frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)\left(x-y \frac{d y}{d x}\right)=2\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right)\left(x+y \frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left\{x\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)-2 x\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right)\right\}=\frac{d y}{d x}\left\{2 y\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right)+y\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)\right\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(3 x y^{2}-x^{3}\right)=\frac{d y}{d x}\left(3 x^{2} y-y^{3}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x 3 - 3xy 2 )dx = (y 3 - 3x 2 y )dy, which is the given differential equation.
solution_html:
The given equation of one parameter family of curves is
(x2 - y2) = c(x2 + y2)2 ...(i)
Differentiating (i) with respect to x, have,
{tex}2 x-2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=2 c\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)\left(2 x+2 y \frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(x-y \frac{d y}{d x}\right)=2 c\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)\left(x+y \frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} ...(ii)
On using the value of c obtained from (i) in (ii), we get
{tex}\left(x-y \frac{d y}{d x}\right)=\frac{2\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right)\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)}{\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)^{2}}\left(x+y \frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)\left(x-y \frac{d y}{d x}\right)=2\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right)\left(x+y \frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left\{x\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)-2 x\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right)\right\}=\frac{d y}{d x}\left\{2 y\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right)+y\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)\right\}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(3 x y^{2}-x^{3}\right)=\frac{d y}{d x}\left(3 x^{2} y-y^{3}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x3 - 3xy2)dx = (y3 - 3x2y )dy, which is the given differential equation.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the differential equation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 2y = e3x
question_text: Solve the differential equation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 2y = e 3x
explanation: The given differential equation is, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 2y = e 3x This is a linear differential equation,comparing it with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q P = 2, Q = e 3x I.F. = {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex} {tex}=e^{\int 2 d x}{/tex} = e 2x Multiplying both the sides by I.F. e 2x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + e 2x 2y = e 2x xe 3x e 2x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + e 2x 2y = e 5x Integrating it with respect to x, ye 2x = {tex}\int{/tex} e 5x dx + c ye 2x = {tex}\frac{e^{5 x}}{5}{/tex} + c y = {tex}\frac{e^{3 x}}{5}{/tex} + ce -2x
solution: The given differential equation is, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 2y = e 3x This is a linear differential equation,comparing it with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q P = 2, Q = e 3x I.F. = {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex} {tex}=e^{\int 2 d x}{/tex} = e 2x Multiplying both the sides by I.F. e 2x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + e 2x 2y = e 2x xe 3x e 2x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + e 2x 2y = e 5x Integrating it with respect to x, ye 2x = {tex}\int{/tex} e 5x dx + c ye 2x = {tex}\frac{e^{5 x}}{5}{/tex} + c y = {tex}\frac{e^{3 x}}{5}{/tex} + ce -2x
solution_html:
The given differential equation is,
 {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 2y = e3x
This is a linear differential equation,comparing it with
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q
P = 2, Q = e3x
I.F. = {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex}
{tex}=e^{\int 2 d x}{/tex}
= e2x
Multiplying both the sides by I.F.
e2x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + e2x 2y = e2x xe3x
e2x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + e2x 2y = e5x
Integrating it with respect to x,
ye2x = {tex}\int{/tex} e5x dx + c
ye2x = {tex}\frac{e^{5 x}}{5}{/tex} + c
y = {tex}\frac{e^{3 x}}{5}{/tex} + ce-2x

question_html: Solve the initial value problem: {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y = x cos x + sin x, y{tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} = 1
question_text: Solve the initial value problem: {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y = x cos x + sin x, y {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} = 1
explanation: The given differential equation is, {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y = x cos x + sin x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = cos x + {tex}\frac{\sin x}{x}{/tex} It is a linear differential equation. Comparing it with, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q P = {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} , Q = cos x + {tex}\frac{\sin x}{x}{/tex} I.F. {tex}=e^{\int p d x}{/tex} {tex}=e^{\int \frac{1}{x} d x}{/tex} = e log |x| , x > 0 = x, x > 0 Solution of the equation is given by, y {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) = {tex}\int{/tex} Q {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) dx + c y(x) = {tex}\int\left(\cos x+\frac{\sin x}{x}\right){/tex} x dx + c = {tex}\int{/tex} (x cos x + sin x) dx + c xy = {tex}\int{/tex} x cos x dx + {tex}\int{/tex} sin x dx + c = x {tex}\int{/tex} cos x dx - {tex}\int{/tex} (1 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} cos x dx) dx - cos x + c = x sin x - {tex}\int{/tex} sin x dx - cos x + c = x sin x + cos x - cos x + c xy = x sin x + c Put x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} , y = 1 {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} + c c = 0 Put c = 0 in equation (i), xy = x sin x y = sin x
solution: The given differential equation is, {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y = x cos x + sin x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = cos x + {tex}\frac{\sin x}{x}{/tex} It is a linear differential equation. Comparing it with, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q P = {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} , Q = cos x + {tex}\frac{\sin x}{x}{/tex} I.F. {tex}=e^{\int p d x}{/tex} {tex}=e^{\int \frac{1}{x} d x}{/tex} = e log |x| , x > 0 = x, x > 0 Solution of the equation is given by, y {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) = {tex}\int{/tex} Q {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) dx + c y(x) = {tex}\int\left(\cos x+\frac{\sin x}{x}\right){/tex} x dx + c = {tex}\int{/tex} (x cos x + sin x) dx + c xy = {tex}\int{/tex} x cos x dx + {tex}\int{/tex} sin x dx + c = x {tex}\int{/tex} cos x dx - {tex}\int{/tex} (1 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} cos x dx) dx - cos x + c = x sin x - {tex}\int{/tex} sin x dx - cos x + c = x sin x + cos x - cos x + c xy = x sin x + c Put x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} , y = 1 {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} + c c = 0 Put c = 0 in equation (i), xy = x sin x y = sin x
solution_html:
The given differential equation is,

  {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y = x cos x + sin x
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = cos x + {tex}\frac{\sin x}{x}{/tex}
It is a linear differential equation. Comparing it with,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q
P = {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex}, Q = cos x + {tex}\frac{\sin x}{x}{/tex}
I.F. {tex}=e^{\int p d x}{/tex}
{tex}=e^{\int \frac{1}{x} d x}{/tex}
= elog |x|, x > 0
= x, x > 0
Solution of the equation is given by,
y {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) = {tex}\int{/tex} Q {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) dx + c
y(x) = {tex}\int\left(\cos x+\frac{\sin x}{x}\right){/tex} x dx + c
= {tex}\int{/tex} (x cos x + sin x) dx + c
xy = {tex}\int{/tex} x cos x dx + {tex}\int{/tex} sin x dx + c 
= x{tex}\int{/tex} cos x dx - {tex}\int{/tex} (1 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} cos x dx) dx - cos x + c
= x sin x - {tex}\int{/tex} sin x dx - cos x + c
= x sin x + cos x - cos x + c
xy = x sin x + c
Put x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}, y = 1
{tex}\frac{\pi}{2}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} + c
c = 0
Put c = 0 in equation (i),
xy = x sin x
y = sin x

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the initial value problem: tan {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 2x tan x + x2 - y; tan x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 given that y = 0 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
question_text: Solve the initial value problem: tan {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 2x tan x + x 2 - y; tan x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 given that y = 0 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
explanation: The given differential equation is, {tex}tan\ x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 2x tan x + x 2 - y {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{\tan x} y=\frac{2 x \tan x+x^{2}}{\tan x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + (cot x) y = 2x + x 2 cot x This is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q {tex}\text { I.F. }=e^{\int P d x}=e^{\int \cot x d x}=e^{\log \sin x}=\sin x{/tex} The solution of the given differential equation is given by y {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) = {tex}\int{/tex} Q {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) dx + C y {tex}\times{/tex} sin x = {tex}\int{/tex} (2x + x 2 + cot x) sin x dx + C y sin x = {tex}\int{/tex} 2x sin x dx + {tex}\int{/tex} x 2 cos x dx + C y sin x = {tex}\int{/tex} 2x sin x dx + [x 2 {tex}\int{/tex} cos x dx - {tex}\int{/tex} ( {tex}\frac{d}{d x}{/tex} x 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} cos x dx] + C y sin x = {tex}\int{/tex} 2x sin x dx + x 2 sin x - {tex}\int{/tex} 2x sin x dx + C y sin x = x 2 sin x + C y = x 2 + cosec x {tex}\times{/tex} C...(i) It is given that, y = 0 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} 0 = {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)^{2}{/tex} + cosec {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} C C = {tex}-\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}{/tex} Putting C = {tex}-\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}{/tex} in (i), we get y = x 2 {tex}-\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}{/tex} cosec x Hence, y = x 2 {tex}-\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}{/tex} cosec x is the required solution.
solution: The given differential equation is, {tex}tan\ x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 2x tan x + x 2 - y {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{\tan x} y=\frac{2 x \tan x+x^{2}}{\tan x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + (cot x) y = 2x + x 2 cot x This is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q {tex}\text { I.F. }=e^{\int P d x}=e^{\int \cot x d x}=e^{\log \sin x}=\sin x{/tex} The solution of the given differential equation is given by y {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) = {tex}\int{/tex} Q {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) dx + C y {tex}\times{/tex} sin x = {tex}\int{/tex} (2x + x 2 + cot x) sin x dx + C y sin x = {tex}\int{/tex} 2x sin x dx + {tex}\int{/tex} x 2 cos x dx + C y sin x = {tex}\int{/tex} 2x sin x dx + [x 2 {tex}\int{/tex} cos x dx - {tex}\int{/tex} ( {tex}\frac{d}{d x}{/tex} x 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} cos x dx] + C y sin x = {tex}\int{/tex} 2x sin x dx + x 2 sin x - {tex}\int{/tex} 2x sin x dx + C y sin x = x 2 sin x + C y = x 2 + cosec x {tex}\times{/tex} C...(i) It is given that, y = 0 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} 0 = {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)^{2}{/tex} + cosec {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} C C = {tex}-\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}{/tex} Putting C = {tex}-\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}{/tex} in (i), we get y = x 2 {tex}-\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}{/tex} cosec x Hence, y = x 2 {tex}-\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}{/tex} cosec x is the required solution.
solution_html:
The given differential equation is,

{tex}tan\ x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 2x tan x + x2 - y
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{\tan x} y=\frac{2 x \tan x+x^{2}}{\tan x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + (cot x) y = 2x + x2 cot x
This is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q
{tex}\text { I.F. }=e^{\int P d x}=e^{\int \cot x d x}=e^{\log \sin x}=\sin x{/tex}
The solution of the given differential equation is given by
y {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) = {tex}\int{/tex}  Q {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) dx + C
y {tex}\times{/tex} sin x = {tex}\int{/tex} (2x + x2 + cot x) sin x dx + C
y sin x = {tex}\int{/tex} 2x sin x dx + {tex}\int{/tex} x2 cos x dx + C
y sin x = {tex}\int{/tex} 2x sin x dx + [x2 {tex}\int{/tex} cos x dx - {tex}\int{/tex} ({tex}\frac{d}{d x}{/tex}x2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\int{/tex} cos x dx] + C
y sin x = {tex}\int{/tex} 2x sin x dx + x2 sin x - {tex}\int{/tex} 2x sin x dx + C
y sin x = x2 sin x + C
y = x2 + cosec x {tex}\times{/tex} C...(i)
It is given that, y = 0 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0 = {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)^{2}{/tex} + cosec  {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} C
C = {tex}-\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}{/tex}
Putting C = {tex}-\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}{/tex} in (i), we get
y = x2 {tex}-\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}{/tex} cosec x
Hence, y = x2 {tex}-\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}{/tex} cosec x is the required solution.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the differential equation: {tex}\left( {1 + {e^{\frac{x}{y}}}} \right)dx + {e^{\frac{x}{y}}}\left( {1 - \frac{x}{y}} \right)dy = 0{/tex}
question_text: Solve the differential equation: {tex}\left( {1 + {e^{\frac{x}{y}}}} \right)dx + {e^{\frac{x}{y}}}\left( {1 - \frac{x}{y}} \right)dy = 0{/tex}
explanation: {tex}\left( {1 + {e^{\frac{x}{y}}}} \right)dx + {e^{\frac{x}{y}}}\left( {1 - \frac{x}{y}} \right)dy = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = - \frac{{{e^{x/y}}\left( {1 - \frac{x}{y}} \right)}}{{1 + {e^{x/y}}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{{e^{x/y}}\left( {\frac{x}{y} - 1} \right)}}{{1 + {e^{x/y}}}}{/tex} ........(1) Let x = vy, then, {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = v + y\frac{{dv}}{{dy}}{/tex} Put {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}}{/tex} in eq (1),we get, {tex}v + y \frac{{dv}}{{dy}} = \frac{{{e^v}(v - 1)}}{{{e^v} + 1}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y\frac{{dv}}{{dy}} = \frac{{v{e^v} - {e^v}}}{{{e^v} + 1}} - v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y\frac{{dv}}{{dy}} = \frac{{v{e^v} - {e^v} - v{e^v} - v}}{{{e^v} + 1}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow- \int {\frac{{dy}}{y}} = \int {\frac{{{e^v} + 1}}{{v + {e^v}}}dv} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\log ({e^v} + v) = - \log (y) + c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\log (({e^v} + v).y) = c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow({e^v} + v)y = {e^c}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow({e^v} + v)y = A{/tex} [ Putting e c = A ] {tex}\Rightarrow\left( {{e^{x/y}} + \frac{x}{y}} \right)y = A{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y{e^{x/y}} + x = A{/tex}
solution: {tex}\left( {1 + {e^{\frac{x}{y}}}} \right)dx + {e^{\frac{x}{y}}}\left( {1 - \frac{x}{y}} \right)dy = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = - \frac{{{e^{x/y}}\left( {1 - \frac{x}{y}} \right)}}{{1 + {e^{x/y}}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{{e^{x/y}}\left( {\frac{x}{y} - 1} \right)}}{{1 + {e^{x/y}}}}{/tex} ........(1) Let x = vy, then, {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = v + y\frac{{dv}}{{dy}}{/tex} Put {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}}{/tex} in eq (1),we get, {tex}v + y \frac{{dv}}{{dy}} = \frac{{{e^v}(v - 1)}}{{{e^v} + 1}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y\frac{{dv}}{{dy}} = \frac{{v{e^v} - {e^v}}}{{{e^v} + 1}} - v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y\frac{{dv}}{{dy}} = \frac{{v{e^v} - {e^v} - v{e^v} - v}}{{{e^v} + 1}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow- \int {\frac{{dy}}{y}} = \int {\frac{{{e^v} + 1}}{{v + {e^v}}}dv} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\log ({e^v} + v) = - \log (y) + c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\log (({e^v} + v).y) = c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow({e^v} + v)y = {e^c}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow({e^v} + v)y = A{/tex} [ Putting e c = A ] {tex}\Rightarrow\left( {{e^{x/y}} + \frac{x}{y}} \right)y = A{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y{e^{x/y}} + x = A{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\left( {1 + {e^{\frac{x}{y}}}} \right)dx + {e^{\frac{x}{y}}}\left( {1 - \frac{x}{y}} \right)dy = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = - \frac{{{e^{x/y}}\left( {1 - \frac{x}{y}} \right)}}{{1 + {e^{x/y}}}}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{{e^{x/y}}\left( {\frac{x}{y} - 1} \right)}}{{1 + {e^{x/y}}}}{/tex}........(1)

Let x = vy, then,

{tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = v + y\frac{{dv}}{{dy}}{/tex}

Put {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}}{/tex} in eq (1),we get,

{tex}v + y \frac{{dv}}{{dy}} = \frac{{{e^v}(v - 1)}}{{{e^v} + 1}}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow y\frac{{dv}}{{dy}} = \frac{{v{e^v} - {e^v}}}{{{e^v} + 1}} - v{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow y\frac{{dv}}{{dy}} = \frac{{v{e^v} - {e^v} - v{e^v} - v}}{{{e^v} + 1}}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow- \int {\frac{{dy}}{y}} = \int {\frac{{{e^v} + 1}}{{v + {e^v}}}dv} {/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow\log ({e^v} + v) = - \log (y) + c{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow\log (({e^v} + v).y) = c{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow({e^v} + v)y = {e^c}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow({e^v} + v)y = A{/tex} [ Putting ec = A ]

{tex}\Rightarrow\left( {{e^{x/y}} + \frac{x}{y}} \right)y = A{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow y{e^{x/y}} + x = A{/tex}

question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation log {tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} x}\right)={/tex} 3x + 4y.
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation log {tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} x}\right)={/tex} 3x + 4y.
explanation: We have, {tex}\log \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} = 3x + 4y {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{3 x} \cdot e^{4 y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int e^{-4 y} d y{/tex} = {tex}\int e^{3 x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{4} e^{-4 y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3} e^{3 x}{/tex}
solution: We have, {tex}\log \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} = 3x + 4y {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{3 x} \cdot e^{4 y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int e^{-4 y} d y{/tex} = {tex}\int e^{3 x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{4} e^{-4 y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3} e^{3 x}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have,

{tex}\log \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex}= 3x + 4y
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}={tex}e^{3 x} \cdot e^{4 y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int e^{-4 y} d y{/tex} = {tex}\int e^{3 x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-\frac{1}{4} e^{-4 y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3} e^{3 x}{/tex}

question_html: Find the integrating factor of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+y=\frac{1+y}{x}{/tex}
question_text: Find the integrating factor of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+y=\frac{1+y}{x}{/tex}
explanation: Given differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\left(1-\frac{1}{x}\right) y=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} Therefore integrating factor = {tex}e^{x-\log x}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{e^{x}}{x}{/tex}
solution: Given differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\left(1-\frac{1}{x}\right) y=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} Therefore integrating factor = {tex}e^{x-\log x}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{e^{x}}{x}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given differential equation can be written as
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\left(1-\frac{1}{x}\right) y=\frac{1}{x}{/tex}
Therefore integrating factor = {tex}e^{x-\log x}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{e^{x}}{x}{/tex}
