question_html: Find the general solution {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } + y - x + x y \cot x = 0 \ (x \neq 0){/tex}.
question_text: Find the general solution {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } + y - x + x y \cot x = 0 \ (x \neq 0){/tex} .
explanation: Given differential equation is, {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } + y - x + x y \cot x = 0{/tex} Above equation can be written as {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } + y (1 + x \cot x ) = x{/tex} On dividing both sides with x, we get {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \left( \frac { 1 + x \cot x } { x } \right) = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } + y \left( \frac { 1 } { x } + \cot x \right) = 1{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} , where {tex} P = \frac { 1 } { x } + \cot x{/tex} and {tex} Q = 1.{/tex} we know that , {tex}\mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int { Pdx } } = e ^ { \int \left( \frac { 1 } { x } + \cot x \right) d x } = e ^ { \log | x | + \log \sin x }{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { \log | x \sin x | } [ \because \log m + \log n = \log m n ]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} IF = x sin x {tex}y \times { IF } = \int ( Q \times {I F } ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y \times x \sin x = \int 1 \times x \sin x d x + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow yx\sin x = \int {\mathop x\limits_I } \mathop {\sin }\limits_{II} xdx + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot x \sin x = x \int \sin x d x{/tex} {tex}- \int \left( \frac { d } { d x } ( x ) \int \sin x d x \right) d x + C{/tex} [using integration by parts] {tex}\Rightarrow y x \sin x = - x \cos x - \int 1 ( - \cos x ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y x \sin x = - x \cos x + \int \cos x d x + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y x \sin x = - x \cos x + \sin x + C{/tex} On dividing both sides by x sin x, we get {tex}y = \frac { - x \cos x + \sin x + C } { x \sin x }{/tex} {tex} \therefore y = - \cot x + \frac { 1 } { x } + \frac { C } { x \sin x }{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution: Given differential equation is, {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } + y - x + x y \cot x = 0{/tex} Above equation can be written as {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } + y (1 + x \cot x ) = x{/tex} On dividing both sides with x, we get {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \left( \frac { 1 + x \cot x } { x } \right) = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } + y \left( \frac { 1 } { x } + \cot x \right) = 1{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} , where {tex} P = \frac { 1 } { x } + \cot x{/tex} and {tex} Q = 1.{/tex} we know that , {tex}\mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int { Pdx } } = e ^ { \int \left( \frac { 1 } { x } + \cot x \right) d x } = e ^ { \log | x | + \log \sin x }{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { \log | x \sin x | } [ \because \log m + \log n = \log m n ]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} IF = x sin x {tex}y \times { IF } = \int ( Q \times {I F } ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y \times x \sin x = \int 1 \times x \sin x d x + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow yx\sin x = \int {\mathop x\limits_I } \mathop {\sin }\limits_{II} xdx + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot x \sin x = x \int \sin x d x{/tex} {tex}- \int \left( \frac { d } { d x } ( x ) \int \sin x d x \right) d x + C{/tex} [using integration by parts] {tex}\Rightarrow y x \sin x = - x \cos x - \int 1 ( - \cos x ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y x \sin x = - x \cos x + \int \cos x d x + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y x \sin x = - x \cos x + \sin x + C{/tex} On dividing both sides by x sin x, we get {tex}y = \frac { - x \cos x + \sin x + C } { x \sin x }{/tex} {tex} \therefore y = - \cot x + \frac { 1 } { x } + \frac { C } { x \sin x }{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution_html:
Given differential equation is,
{tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } + y - x + x y \cot x = 0{/tex}
Above equation can be written as
{tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } + y (1 + x \cot x ) = x{/tex}
On dividing both sides with x, we get
{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \left( \frac { 1 + x \cot x } { x } \right) = 1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } + y \left( \frac { 1 } { x } + \cot x \right) = 1{/tex}
which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex},
where {tex} P = \frac { 1 } { x } + \cot x{/tex} and {tex} Q = 1.{/tex}
we know that , 
{tex}\mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int { Pdx } } = e ^ { \int \left( \frac { 1 } { x } + \cot x \right) d x } = e ^ { \log | x | + \log \sin x }{/tex}
{tex}= e ^ { \log | x \sin x | } [ \because \log m + \log n = \log m n ]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} IF = x sin x
{tex}y \times { IF } = \int ( Q \times {I F } ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad y \times x \sin x = \int 1 \times x \sin x d x + C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow yx\sin x = \int {\mathop x\limits_I } \mathop {\sin }\limits_{II} xdx + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot x \sin x = x \int \sin x d x{/tex} {tex}- \int \left( \frac { d } { d x } ( x ) \int \sin x d x \right) d x + C{/tex} [using integration by parts]
{tex}\Rightarrow y x \sin x = - x \cos x - \int 1 ( - \cos x ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y x \sin x = - x \cos x + \int \cos x d x + C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow y x \sin x = - x \cos x + \sin x + C{/tex}
On dividing both sides by x sin x, we get
{tex}y = \frac { - x \cos x + \sin x + C } { x \sin x }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore y = - \cot x + \frac { 1 } { x } + \frac { C } { x \sin x }{/tex}
which is the required solution.

question_html: Show that the differential equation {tex}\left[ x \sin ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - y \right] d x + x d y = 0{/tex} is homogenous and  find the particular solution, given that {tex}y = \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex}, when x = 1.
question_text: Show that the differential equation {tex}\left[ x \sin ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - y \right] d x + x d y = 0{/tex} is homogenous and find the particular solution, given that {tex}y = \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} , when x = 1.
explanation: [x sin 2 ( {tex}\frac yx{/tex} ) − y]dx + xdy = 0 xdy = -[x sin 2 ( {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} ) − y]dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = - {tex}\frac{[x \sin ^2(\frac{y}{x})-y]}{x}{/tex} ....(1) F(x, y) = - {tex}\frac{[x \sin ^2(\frac{y}{x})-y]}{x}{/tex} F(tx, ty) = {tex}\frac{-\left(t x \sin ^{2}(\frac{t y} {t x})-t y\right)}{t x}{/tex} = t 0 F(x, y) Hence F(x, y) is a homogenous function of zero degree. Now put y = vx in (1), v + x {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-(x \sin ^2v-vx)}{x}{/tex} Therefore v + x {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = -sin 2 v + v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = -sin 2 v + v - v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = -sin 2 v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\large\frac{dv}{\sin ^2 v}=-\frac{dx}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosec 2 v dv {tex}=\large\frac{-dx}{x}{/tex} Integrating on both sides, {tex}\int cosec ^2 v\; dv=-\int \large\frac{dx}{x}{/tex} -cot v = -log x - log c Now substituting for v, -cot {tex}\left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right){/tex} = -(log cx) cot {tex}\left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right){/tex} = log cx {tex}e^{\cot (y/x)}=cx{/tex} When y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} and x = 1, cot {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} = 1, so e 1 = c Hence e cot( {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} ) = ex {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}e^{\cot (x / y)-1}{/tex} = x
solution: [x sin 2 ( {tex}\frac yx{/tex} ) − y]dx + xdy = 0 xdy = -[x sin 2 ( {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} ) − y]dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = - {tex}\frac{[x \sin ^2(\frac{y}{x})-y]}{x}{/tex} ....(1) F(x, y) = - {tex}\frac{[x \sin ^2(\frac{y}{x})-y]}{x}{/tex} F(tx, ty) = {tex}\frac{-\left(t x \sin ^{2}(\frac{t y} {t x})-t y\right)}{t x}{/tex} = t 0 F(x, y) Hence F(x, y) is a homogenous function of zero degree. Now put y = vx in (1), v + x {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-(x \sin ^2v-vx)}{x}{/tex} Therefore v + x {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = -sin 2 v + v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = -sin 2 v + v - v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = -sin 2 v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\large\frac{dv}{\sin ^2 v}=-\frac{dx}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosec 2 v dv {tex}=\large\frac{-dx}{x}{/tex} Integrating on both sides, {tex}\int cosec ^2 v\; dv=-\int \large\frac{dx}{x}{/tex} -cot v = -log x - log c Now substituting for v, -cot {tex}\left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right){/tex} = -(log cx) cot {tex}\left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right){/tex} = log cx {tex}e^{\cot (y/x)}=cx{/tex} When y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} and x = 1, cot {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} = 1, so e 1 = c Hence e cot( {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} ) = ex {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}e^{\cot (x / y)-1}{/tex} = x
solution_html:
[x sin2({tex}\frac yx{/tex}) − y]dx + xdy = 0
xdy = -[x sin2({tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex}) − y]dx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = -{tex}\frac{[x \sin ^2(\frac{y}{x})-y]}{x}{/tex} ....(1)
F(x, y) = -{tex}\frac{[x \sin ^2(\frac{y}{x})-y]}{x}{/tex}
F(tx, ty) = {tex}\frac{-\left(t x \sin ^{2}(\frac{t y} {t x})-t y\right)}{t x}{/tex}
= t0 F(x, y)
Hence F(x, y) is a homogenous function of zero degree.
Now put y = vx in (1),
v + x{tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-(x \sin ^2v-vx)}{x}{/tex}
Therefore v + x{tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = -sin2v + v
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x{tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = -sin2v + v - v
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x{tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = -sin2v
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\large\frac{dv}{\sin ^2 v}=-\frac{dx}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosec2v dv {tex}=\large\frac{-dx}{x}{/tex}
Integrating on both sides,
{tex}\int cosec ^2 v\; dv=-\int \large\frac{dx}{x}{/tex}
-cot v = -log x - log c
Now substituting for v,
-cot{tex}\left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right){/tex} = -(log cx)
cot{tex}\left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right){/tex} = log cx
{tex}e^{\cot (y/x)}=cx{/tex}
When y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} and x = 1,
cot{tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} = 1, so e1 = c
Hence ecot({tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex}) = ex
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}e^{\cot (x / y)-1}{/tex} = x

question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex}\left( {\frac{{{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}}}{{\sqrt x }} - \frac{y}{{\sqrt x }}} \right)\frac{{dx}}{{dy}}{/tex} = 1 (x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0)
question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex}\left( {\frac{{{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}}}{{\sqrt x }} - \frac{y}{{\sqrt x }}} \right)\frac{{dx}}{{dy}}{/tex} = 1 (x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0)
explanation: {tex}\left( {\frac{{{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}}}{{\sqrt x }} - \frac{y}{{\sqrt x }}} \right)\frac{{dx}}{{dy}}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}}}{{\sqrt x }} - \frac{y}{{\sqrt x }}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \frac{y}{{\sqrt x }} = \frac{{{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}}}{{\sqrt x }}{/tex} Given differential equation is of the form: {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + Py = Q{/tex} {tex}I.F = {e^{\int {\frac{1}{{\sqrt x }}dx} }} = {e^{2\sqrt x }}{/tex} Solution is, {tex}y \times {e^{2\sqrt x }} = \int {{e^{2\sqrt x }} \times \frac{{{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}}}{{\sqrt x }}dx + c}{/tex} {tex}y{e^{2\sqrt x }} = \int {\frac{1}{{\sqrt x }}dx + C} {/tex} {tex}y{e^{2\sqrt x }} = 2\sqrt x + C{/tex}
solution: {tex}\left( {\frac{{{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}}}{{\sqrt x }} - \frac{y}{{\sqrt x }}} \right)\frac{{dx}}{{dy}}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}}}{{\sqrt x }} - \frac{y}{{\sqrt x }}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \frac{y}{{\sqrt x }} = \frac{{{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}}}{{\sqrt x }}{/tex} Given differential equation is of the form: {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + Py = Q{/tex} {tex}I.F = {e^{\int {\frac{1}{{\sqrt x }}dx} }} = {e^{2\sqrt x }}{/tex} Solution is, {tex}y \times {e^{2\sqrt x }} = \int {{e^{2\sqrt x }} \times \frac{{{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}}}{{\sqrt x }}dx + c}{/tex} {tex}y{e^{2\sqrt x }} = \int {\frac{1}{{\sqrt x }}dx + C} {/tex} {tex}y{e^{2\sqrt x }} = 2\sqrt x + C{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\left( {\frac{{{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}}}{{\sqrt x }} - \frac{y}{{\sqrt x }}} \right)\frac{{dx}}{{dy}}{/tex} = 1
{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}}}{{\sqrt x }} - \frac{y}{{\sqrt x }}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \frac{y}{{\sqrt x }} = \frac{{{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}}}{{\sqrt x }}{/tex}
Given differential equation is of the form:
{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + Py = Q{/tex}
{tex}I.F = {e^{\int {\frac{1}{{\sqrt x }}dx} }} = {e^{2\sqrt x }}{/tex}
Solution is,
{tex}y \times {e^{2\sqrt x }} = \int {{e^{2\sqrt x }} \times \frac{{{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}}}{{\sqrt x }}dx + c}{/tex}
{tex}y{e^{2\sqrt x }} = \int {\frac{1}{{\sqrt x }}dx + C} {/tex}
{tex}y{e^{2\sqrt x }} = 2\sqrt x + C{/tex}

question_html: Write the sum of the order and degree of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d } { d x } \left\{ \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 3 } \right\} = 0{/tex}.
question_text: Write the sum of the order and degree of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d } { d x } \left\{ \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 3 } \right\} = 0{/tex} .
explanation: Given differential equation is {tex}\frac { d } { d x } \left\{ \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 3 } \right\} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 3 - 1 } \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = 0{/tex} Here, order = 2 and degree = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Sum of the order and degree = 2 + 1 = 3
solution: Given differential equation is {tex}\frac { d } { d x } \left\{ \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 3 } \right\} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 3 - 1 } \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = 0{/tex} Here, order = 2 and degree = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Sum of the order and degree = 2 + 1 = 3
solution_html:
Given differential equation is {tex}\frac { d } { d x } \left\{ \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 3 } \right\} = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 3 - 1 } \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = 0{/tex}
Here, order = 2 and degree = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Sum of the order and degree = 2 + 1 = 3

question_html: Write the sum of the order and degree of the differential equation {tex}\left( \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 4 } = 0{/tex}.
question_text: Write the sum of the order and degree of the differential equation {tex}\left( \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 4 } = 0{/tex} .
explanation: Given differential equation is {tex} \left( \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 4 } = 0{/tex} . Here, we see that highest order derivative is {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^{2} }{/tex} , whose degree is 2. Here, order = 2 and degree = 2 {tex} \therefore{/tex} Sum of the order and degree = 2 + 2 = 4.
solution: Given differential equation is {tex} \left( \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 4 } = 0{/tex} . Here, we see that highest order derivative is {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^{2} }{/tex} , whose degree is 2. Here, order = 2 and degree = 2 {tex} \therefore{/tex} Sum of the order and degree = 2 + 2 = 4.
solution_html:
Given differential equation is
{tex} \left( \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 4 } = 0{/tex}.
Here, we see that highest order derivative is {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^{2} }{/tex}, whose degree is 2.
Here, order = 2 and degree = 2
{tex} \therefore{/tex} Sum of the order and degree = 2 + 2 = 4.

question_html:
Write the integrating factor of the following differential equation.
{tex}\left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right) + ( 2 x y - \cot y ) \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex}.
question_text: Write the integrating factor of the following differential equation. {tex}\left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right) + ( 2 x y - \cot y ) \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex} .
explanation: Given differential equation is {tex}\left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right) + ( 2 x y - \cot y ) \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex} . The above equation can be rewritten as {tex}( \cot y - 2 x y ) \frac { d y } { d x } = 1 + y ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { \cot y - 2 x y } { \left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right) } = \frac { d x } { d y }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d x } { d y } = \frac { \cot y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 2 x y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d x } { d y } + \frac { 2 y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } \cdot x = \frac { \cot y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac { d x } { d y } + P x = Q{/tex} , here {tex}P = \frac { 2 y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex} and {tex}Q = \frac { \cot y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex} . Now, integrating factor {tex}= e ^ { \int p d y } = e ^ { \int \frac { 2 y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } d y }{/tex} Put 1 + y 2 = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2ydy = dt {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \frac { dt } { t } } = e ^ { \mathrm { log } | t | } = t = 1 + y ^ { 2 }{/tex}
solution: Given differential equation is {tex}\left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right) + ( 2 x y - \cot y ) \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex} . The above equation can be rewritten as {tex}( \cot y - 2 x y ) \frac { d y } { d x } = 1 + y ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { \cot y - 2 x y } { \left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right) } = \frac { d x } { d y }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d x } { d y } = \frac { \cot y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 2 x y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d x } { d y } + \frac { 2 y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } \cdot x = \frac { \cot y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac { d x } { d y } + P x = Q{/tex} , here {tex}P = \frac { 2 y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex} and {tex}Q = \frac { \cot y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex} . Now, integrating factor {tex}= e ^ { \int p d y } = e ^ { \int \frac { 2 y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } d y }{/tex} Put 1 + y 2 = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2ydy = dt {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \frac { dt } { t } } = e ^ { \mathrm { log } | t | } = t = 1 + y ^ { 2 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Given differential equation is
{tex}\left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right) + ( 2 x y - \cot y ) \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex}.
The above equation can be rewritten as
{tex}( \cot y - 2 x y ) \frac { d y } { d x } = 1 + y ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { \cot y - 2 x y } { \left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right) } = \frac { d x } { d y }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d x } { d y } = \frac { \cot y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 2 x y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d x } { d y } + \frac { 2 y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } \cdot x = \frac { \cot y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
which is a linear differential equation of the form
{tex}\frac { d x } { d y } + P x = Q{/tex}, here {tex}P = \frac { 2 y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex} and {tex}Q = \frac { \cot y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex} .  
Now, integrating factor {tex}= e ^ { \int p d y } = e ^ { \int \frac { 2 y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } d y }{/tex}
Put 1 + y2 = t
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2ydy = dt
{tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \frac { dt } { t } } = e ^ { \mathrm { log } | t | } = t = 1 + y ^ { 2 }{/tex}

question_html: Write the solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = 2 ^ { - y }{/tex}.
question_text: Write the solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = 2 ^ { - y }{/tex} .
explanation: Given differential equation is {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = 2 ^ { -y }{/tex} on separating the variables, we get 2 y dy = dx On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int 2 ^ { y } d y = \int d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 2 ^ { y } } { \log 2 } = x + C_1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 y = x log 2 + C 1 log 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} 2 y = x log 2 + C, where C = C 1 log 2
solution: Given differential equation is {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = 2 ^ { -y }{/tex} on separating the variables, we get 2 y dy = dx On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int 2 ^ { y } d y = \int d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 2 ^ { y } } { \log 2 } = x + C_1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 y = x log 2 + C 1 log 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} 2 y = x log 2 + C, where C = C 1 log 2
solution_html:
Given differential equation is
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = 2 ^ { -y }{/tex}
on separating the variables, we get
2ydy = dx
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int 2 ^ { y } d y = \int d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 2 ^ { y } } { \log 2 } = x + C_1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y = x log 2 + C1 log 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2y = x log 2 + C, where C = C1 log 2

question_html: Find the solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = x ^ { 3 } e ^ { - 2 y }{/tex}.
question_text: Find the solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = x ^ { 3 } e ^ { - 2 y }{/tex} .
explanation: Given differential equation is {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = x ^ { 3 } e ^ { - 2 y }{/tex} On separating the variables, we get e 2y dy = x 3 dx On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int e ^ { 2 y } d y = \int x ^ { 3 } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { e ^ { 2 y } } { 2 } = \frac { x ^ { 4 } } { 4 } + C _ { 1 } \\ \Rightarrow 2 e ^ { 2 y } = x ^ { 4 } + 4 C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad 2 e ^ { 2 y } = x ^ { 4 } + C,{/tex} where C = 4 C 1
solution: Given differential equation is {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = x ^ { 3 } e ^ { - 2 y }{/tex} On separating the variables, we get e 2y dy = x 3 dx On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int e ^ { 2 y } d y = \int x ^ { 3 } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { e ^ { 2 y } } { 2 } = \frac { x ^ { 4 } } { 4 } + C _ { 1 } \\ \Rightarrow 2 e ^ { 2 y } = x ^ { 4 } + 4 C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad 2 e ^ { 2 y } = x ^ { 4 } + C,{/tex} where C = 4 C 1
solution_html:
Given differential equation is
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = x ^ { 3 } e ^ { - 2 y }{/tex}
On separating the variables, we get
e2ydy = x3dx
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int e ^ { 2 y } d y = \int x ^ { 3 } d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { e ^ { 2 y } } { 2 } = \frac { x ^ { 4 } } { 4 } + C _ { 1 } \\ \Rightarrow 2 e ^ { 2 y } = x ^ { 4 } + 4 C _ { 1 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad 2 e ^ { 2 y } = x ^ { 4 } + C,{/tex}where C = 4 C1

question_html: Show that the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x y - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} is homogeneous and also solve it.
question_text: Show that the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x y - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} is homogeneous and also solve it.
explanation: According to the question , Given differential equation is {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x y - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...(i) Let {tex}F ( x , y ) = \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x y - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Now, on replacing x by {tex}\lambda x{/tex} and y by {tex}\lambda y{/tex} , we get {tex}F ( \lambda x , \lambda y ) = \frac { \lambda ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 } } { \lambda ^ { 2 } \left( x y - x ^ { 2 } \right) } = \lambda ^ { 0 } \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x y - x ^ { 2 } } = \lambda ^ { 0 } F ( x , y ){/tex} Thus, the given diﬀerential equation is a homogeneous diﬀerential equation. Now, to solve it, put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} From Eq. (i), we get {tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } } { v x ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { v ^ { 2 } } { v - 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v ^ { 2 } } { v - 1 } - v = \frac { v ^ { 2 } - v ^ { 2 } + v } { v - 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v } { v - 1 } \Rightarrow \frac { v - 1 } { v } d v = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \left( 1 - \frac { 1 } { v } \right) d v = \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad v - \log | v | = \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { y } { x } - \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| = \log | x | + C \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { y } { x } - \log | y | + \log | x | = \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log \left( \frac { m } { n } \right) = \log m - \log n \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { y } { x } - \log | y | = C{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution: According to the question , Given differential equation is {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x y - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...(i) Let {tex}F ( x , y ) = \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x y - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Now, on replacing x by {tex}\lambda x{/tex} and y by {tex}\lambda y{/tex} , we get {tex}F ( \lambda x , \lambda y ) = \frac { \lambda ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 } } { \lambda ^ { 2 } \left( x y - x ^ { 2 } \right) } = \lambda ^ { 0 } \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x y - x ^ { 2 } } = \lambda ^ { 0 } F ( x , y ){/tex} Thus, the given diﬀerential equation is a homogeneous diﬀerential equation. Now, to solve it, put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} From Eq. (i), we get {tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } } { v x ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { v ^ { 2 } } { v - 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v ^ { 2 } } { v - 1 } - v = \frac { v ^ { 2 } - v ^ { 2 } + v } { v - 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v } { v - 1 } \Rightarrow \frac { v - 1 } { v } d v = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \left( 1 - \frac { 1 } { v } \right) d v = \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad v - \log | v | = \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { y } { x } - \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| = \log | x | + C \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { y } { x } - \log | y | + \log | x | = \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log \left( \frac { m } { n } \right) = \log m - \log n \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { y } { x } - \log | y | = C{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution_html:
According to the question ,

Given differential equation is
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x y - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...(i)
Let {tex}F ( x , y ) = \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x y - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
Now, on replacing x by {tex}\lambda x{/tex} and y by {tex}\lambda y{/tex} , we get
{tex}F ( \lambda x , \lambda y ) = \frac { \lambda ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 } } { \lambda ^ { 2 } \left( x y - x ^ { 2 } \right) } = \lambda ^ { 0 } \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x y - x ^ { 2 } } = \lambda ^ { 0 } F ( x , y ){/tex}
Thus, the given diﬀerential equation is a homogeneous diﬀerential equation.
Now, to solve it, put y = vx 

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex}
From Eq. (i), we get
{tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } } { v x ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { v ^ { 2 } } { v - 1 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v ^ { 2 } } { v - 1 } - v = \frac { v ^ { 2 } - v ^ { 2 } + v } { v - 1 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v } { v - 1 } \Rightarrow \frac { v - 1 } { v } d v = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int \left( 1 - \frac { 1 } { v } \right) d v = \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad v - \log | v | = \log | x | + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { y } { x } - \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| = \log | x | + C \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { y } { x } - \log | y | + \log | x | = \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log \left( \frac { m } { n } \right) = \log m - \log n \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac { y } { x } - \log | y | = C{/tex}
which is the required solution.

question_html:
Solve the following differential equation.
{tex}\left[ {y - x\cos \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right)} \right]dy + \left[ {y\cos \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right) - 2x\sin \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right)} \right]dx = 0{/tex}
question_text: Solve the following differential equation. {tex}\left[ {y - x\cos \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right)} \right]dy + \left[ {y\cos \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right) - 2x\sin \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right)} \right]dx = 0{/tex}
explanation: According to the question , Given differential equation is {tex}\left[ y - x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \right] d y + \left[ y \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - 2 x \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \right] d x = 0{/tex} {tex}\left[ y - x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \right] d y =- \left[ y \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - 2 x \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \right] d x {/tex} {tex}\left[ y - x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \right] d y = \left[ 2 x \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) -y \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right)\right] d x {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 2 x \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - y \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) } { y - x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) }{/tex} By dividing numerator and denominator by x on R.H.S , we get {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 2 \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - \frac { y } { x } \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) } { \frac { y } { x } - \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) }{/tex} ...(i) Eq. (i) represents a homogeneous differential equation as {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} . put , {tex}y = vx {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} {tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 2 \sin v - v \cos v } { v - \cos v }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 2 \sin v - v \cos v } { v - \cos v } - v{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 2 \sin v - v \cos v - v ^ { 2 } + v \cos v } { v - \cos v }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 2 \sin v - v ^ { 2 } } { v - \cos v } {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( \frac { v - \cos v } { v ^ { 2 } - 2 \sin v } \right) d v = - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \left( \frac { v - \cos v } { v ^ { 2 } - 2 \sin v } \right) d v = - \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \left| v ^ { 2 } - 2 \sin v \right| = - \log | x | + \log C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \frac { d } { d v } \left( v ^ { 2 } - 2 \sin v \right) = 2 v - 2 \cos v = 2 ( v - \cos v ) \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \sqrt { v ^ { 2 } - 2 \sin v } = \log \left| \frac { C _ { 1 } } { x } \right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sqrt { v ^ { 2 } - 2 \sin v } = \frac { C _ { 1 } } { x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sqrt { \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } - 2 \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) } = \frac { C _ { 1 } } { x } \quad \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { y ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) } = C _ { 1 }{/tex} By squaring on both sides , we get {tex} \Rightarrow y ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = C _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = C , \text { where } C = C _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }{/tex}
solution: According to the question , Given differential equation is {tex}\left[ y - x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \right] d y + \left[ y \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - 2 x \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \right] d x = 0{/tex} {tex}\left[ y - x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \right] d y =- \left[ y \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - 2 x \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \right] d x {/tex} {tex}\left[ y - x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \right] d y = \left[ 2 x \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) -y \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right)\right] d x {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 2 x \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - y \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) } { y - x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) }{/tex} By dividing numerator and denominator by x on R.H.S , we get {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 2 \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - \frac { y } { x } \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) } { \frac { y } { x } - \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) }{/tex} ...(i) Eq. (i) represents a homogeneous differential equation as {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} . put , {tex}y = vx {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} {tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 2 \sin v - v \cos v } { v - \cos v }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 2 \sin v - v \cos v } { v - \cos v } - v{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 2 \sin v - v \cos v - v ^ { 2 } + v \cos v } { v - \cos v }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 2 \sin v - v ^ { 2 } } { v - \cos v } {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( \frac { v - \cos v } { v ^ { 2 } - 2 \sin v } \right) d v = - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \left( \frac { v - \cos v } { v ^ { 2 } - 2 \sin v } \right) d v = - \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \left| v ^ { 2 } - 2 \sin v \right| = - \log | x | + \log C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \frac { d } { d v } \left( v ^ { 2 } - 2 \sin v \right) = 2 v - 2 \cos v = 2 ( v - \cos v ) \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \sqrt { v ^ { 2 } - 2 \sin v } = \log \left| \frac { C _ { 1 } } { x } \right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sqrt { v ^ { 2 } - 2 \sin v } = \frac { C _ { 1 } } { x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sqrt { \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } - 2 \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) } = \frac { C _ { 1 } } { x } \quad \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { y ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) } = C _ { 1 }{/tex} By squaring on both sides , we get {tex} \Rightarrow y ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = C _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = C , \text { where } C = C _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question , Given differential equation is
{tex}\left[ y - x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \right] d y + \left[ y \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - 2 x \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \right] d x = 0{/tex}

{tex}\left[ y - x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \right] d y =- \left[ y \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - 2 x \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \right] d x {/tex}

{tex}\left[ y - x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \right] d y = \left[ 2 x \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) -y \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right)\right] d x {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 2 x \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - y \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) } { y - x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) }{/tex}

By dividing numerator and denominator by x on R.H.S , we get 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 2 \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - \frac { y } { x } \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) } { \frac { y } { x } - \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) }{/tex} ...(i)
Eq. (i) represents a homogeneous differential equation as {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex}.
put , {tex}y = vx {/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex}
{tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 2 \sin v - v \cos v } { v - \cos v }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 2 \sin v - v \cos v } { v - \cos v } - v{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 2 \sin v - v \cos v - v ^ { 2 } + v \cos v } { v - \cos v }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 2 \sin v - v ^ { 2 } } { v - \cos v } {/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left( \frac { v - \cos v } { v ^ { 2 } - 2 \sin v } \right) d v = - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int \left( \frac { v - \cos v } { v ^ { 2 } - 2 \sin v } \right) d v = - \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \left| v ^ { 2 } - 2 \sin v \right| = - \log | x | + \log C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \frac { d } { d v } \left( v ^ { 2 } - 2 \sin v \right) = 2 v - 2 \cos v = 2 ( v - \cos v ) \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \sqrt { v ^ { 2 } - 2 \sin v } = \log \left| \frac { C _ { 1 } } { x } \right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sqrt { v ^ { 2 } - 2 \sin v } = \frac { C _ { 1 } } { x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sqrt { \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } - 2 \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) } = \frac { C _ { 1 } } { x } \quad \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { y ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) } = C _ { 1 }{/tex}

By squaring on both sides , we get
{tex} \Rightarrow y ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = C _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore \quad y ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = C , \text { where } C = C _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }{/tex}

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation (tan-1 y - x)dy = (1 + y2)dx, given that x = 1 when y = 0.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation (tan -1 y - x)dy = (1 + y 2 )dx, given that x = 1 when y = 0.
explanation: Given (tan -1 y - x)dy = (1 + y 2 )dx or {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\tan ^{-1} y-x}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{x}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} ..(i) This is a linear differential equation with : P = {tex}\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} and Q = {tex}\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} So I.F. = {tex}e^{\int \frac{1}{1+y^{2}}d y} {/tex} = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} Multiplying both sides of eqn. (i) by I.F. = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} , we get x . IF = {tex}\int Q . I F d y{/tex} x {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}} e^{\tan ^{-1} y} d y{/tex} + C Let t = tan -1 y, dt = {tex}\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} dy Thus, x {tex}e^{t}{/tex} = {tex}\int t e^{t} d t+C{/tex} x {tex}e^{t}{/tex} = {tex}e^{t}{/tex} (t - 1) + C x {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} (tan -1 y - 1) + C .....(ii) It is given that y(0) = 0 i.e., y = 0 when x = 0 Putting x = 0, y = 0 in eqn. (ii), we get 0 = e 0 (0 - 1) + C or C = 1 Putting C = 1 in eq (ii), we get {tex}x e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} (tan -1 y - 1) + 1 or (x - tan -1 y + 1) {tex} e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} = 1
solution: Given (tan -1 y - x)dy = (1 + y 2 )dx or {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\tan ^{-1} y-x}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{x}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} ..(i) This is a linear differential equation with : P = {tex}\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} and Q = {tex}\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} So I.F. = {tex}e^{\int \frac{1}{1+y^{2}}d y} {/tex} = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} Multiplying both sides of eqn. (i) by I.F. = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} , we get x . IF = {tex}\int Q . I F d y{/tex} x {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}} e^{\tan ^{-1} y} d y{/tex} + C Let t = tan -1 y, dt = {tex}\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} dy Thus, x {tex}e^{t}{/tex} = {tex}\int t e^{t} d t+C{/tex} x {tex}e^{t}{/tex} = {tex}e^{t}{/tex} (t - 1) + C x {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} (tan -1 y - 1) + C .....(ii) It is given that y(0) = 0 i.e., y = 0 when x = 0 Putting x = 0, y = 0 in eqn. (ii), we get 0 = e 0 (0 - 1) + C or C = 1 Putting C = 1 in eq (ii), we get {tex}x e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} (tan -1 y - 1) + 1 or (x - tan -1 y + 1) {tex} e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} = 1
solution_html:
Given (tan-1y - x)dy = (1 + y2)dx
or {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\tan ^{-1} y-x}{1+y^{2}}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{x}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} ..(i)
This is a linear differential equation with :
P = {tex}\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} and Q = {tex}\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex}
So I.F. = {tex}e^{\int \frac{1}{1+y^{2}}d y} {/tex} = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex}
Multiplying both sides of eqn. (i) by I.F. = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex}, we get 
x . IF = {tex}\int Q . I F d y{/tex}
x{tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}} e^{\tan ^{-1} y} d y{/tex} + C
Let t = tan-1y, dt = {tex}\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}{/tex}dy
Thus,
x{tex}e^{t}{/tex}  = {tex}\int t e^{t} d t+C{/tex}
x{tex}e^{t}{/tex}  = {tex}e^{t}{/tex}(t - 1) + C
x{tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex}  = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex}(tan-1y - 1) + C .....(ii)
It is given that y(0) = 0 i.e., y = 0 when x = 0
Putting x = 0, y = 0 in eqn. (ii), we get
0 = e0 (0 - 1) + C or C = 1
Putting C = 1 in eq (ii), we get
{tex}x e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} (tan-1y - 1) + 1
or (x - tan-1y + 1){tex} e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} = 1

question_html: Find the sum of the order and degree of the differential equation y {tex}=x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}+\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the sum of the order and degree of the differential equation y {tex}=x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}+\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} .
explanation: The order of D.E = 2 The degree of highest derivative is 1.. Therefore degree = 1 Hence sum of degree and order = 2 + 1 =3
solution: The order of D.E = 2 The degree of highest derivative is 1.. Therefore degree = 1 Hence sum of degree and order = 2 + 1 =3
solution_html:
The order of D.E = 2
The degree of highest derivative is  1..
Therefore degree = 1
Hence sum of degree and order = 2 + 1 =3

question_html: Find the general solution for differential equation: {tex}x \sqrt{1+y^{2}} d x+y \sqrt{1+x^{2}} d y=0{/tex}
question_text: Find the general solution for differential equation: {tex}x \sqrt{1+y^{2}} d x+y \sqrt{1+x^{2}} d y=0{/tex}
explanation: The given differential equation can be rewritten as, {tex}\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}} d x+\frac{y}{\sqrt{1+y^{2}}} d y=0{/tex} Integrating, {tex}\int \frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}} d x+\int \frac{y}{\sqrt{1+y^{2}}} d y{/tex} =c {tex}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\sqrt{1+x^{2}}+\sqrt{1+y^{2}}=c{/tex} {tex}\left\{\frac{d}{d x}(\sqrt{1+x^{2}})=\frac{2 x}{2 \cdot \sqrt{1+x^{2}}}=\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}\right\}{/tex}
solution: The given differential equation can be rewritten as, {tex}\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}} d x+\frac{y}{\sqrt{1+y^{2}}} d y=0{/tex} Integrating, {tex}\int \frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}} d x+\int \frac{y}{\sqrt{1+y^{2}}} d y{/tex} =c {tex}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\sqrt{1+x^{2}}+\sqrt{1+y^{2}}=c{/tex} {tex}\left\{\frac{d}{d x}(\sqrt{1+x^{2}})=\frac{2 x}{2 \cdot \sqrt{1+x^{2}}}=\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}\right\}{/tex}
solution_html:
The given differential equation can be rewritten as,

{tex}\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}} d x+\frac{y}{\sqrt{1+y^{2}}} d y=0{/tex}
Integrating,
{tex}\int \frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}} d x+\int \frac{y}{\sqrt{1+y^{2}}} d y{/tex} =c  {tex}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\sqrt{1+x^{2}}+\sqrt{1+y^{2}}=c{/tex} {tex}\left\{\frac{d}{d x}(\sqrt{1+x^{2}})=\frac{2 x}{2 \cdot \sqrt{1+x^{2}}}=\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}\right\}{/tex}

question_html: Write the integrating factor of the differential equation {tex}\sqrt{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x}+y=\mathrm{e}^{-2 \sqrt{x}}{/tex}.
question_text: Write the integrating factor of the differential equation {tex}\sqrt{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x}+y=\mathrm{e}^{-2 \sqrt{x}}{/tex} .
explanation: The given differential equation may be rewritten as, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}} y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{e^{-2 \sqrt{x}}}{\sqrt{x}}{/tex} Here, P = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I.F. = {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex} = {tex}{e^{\int {\frac{1}{{\sqrt x }}} dx}}{/tex} I.F. = {tex}e^{2 \sqrt{x}}{/tex}
solution: The given differential equation may be rewritten as, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}} y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{e^{-2 \sqrt{x}}}{\sqrt{x}}{/tex} Here, P = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I.F. = {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex} = {tex}{e^{\int {\frac{1}{{\sqrt x }}} dx}}{/tex} I.F. = {tex}e^{2 \sqrt{x}}{/tex}
solution_html:
The given differential equation may be rewritten as,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}} y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{e^{-2 \sqrt{x}}}{\sqrt{x}}{/tex} 
Here, P = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} I.F. = {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex} 
= {tex}{e^{\int {\frac{1}{{\sqrt x }}} dx}}{/tex} 
I.F. = {tex}e^{2 \sqrt{x}}{/tex}

question_html: If y = x3 log {tex}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right){/tex}, then prove that {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}^{2}}-2 \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{/tex} + 3x2 = 0
question_text: If y = x 3 log {tex}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right){/tex} , then prove that {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}^{2}}-2 \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{/tex} + 3x 2 = 0
explanation: Given that: y = x 3 log {tex}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right){/tex} To prove: {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}-2 \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+3 \mathrm{x}^{2}{/tex} = 0 Proof: We have, y = x 3 log {tex}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right){/tex} , then {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = x 3 {tex}\left(\frac{1}{\frac{1}{x}}\right)\left(\frac{(-1)}{x^{2}}\right){/tex} + log {tex}(\frac{1}{x}){/tex} (3x 2 ) = -x 2 + 3x 2 [log {tex}(\frac{1}{x}){/tex} ] {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -2x + 6x [log {tex}(\frac{1}{x}){/tex} ] + {tex}\left\{3 \mathrm{x}^{2} \times(\mathrm{x}) \times\left(\frac{-1}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}\right)\right\}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -5x + 6x [log {tex}(\frac{1}{x}){/tex} ] Now put the values in {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}-2 \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+3 \mathrm{x}^{2}{/tex} , we get {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}-2 \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+3 \mathrm{x}^{2}{/tex} = x {tex}\left\{-5 x+6 x\left[\log \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]\right\}{/tex} - 2{-x 2 + 3x 2 {tex}\left[\log \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex} } + 3x 2 = -5x 2 + 6x 2 {tex}\left[\log \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex} + 2x 2 - 6x 2 {tex}\left[\log \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex} + 3x 2 = -5x 2 + 2x 2 + 3x 2 = 0 Hence Proved
solution: Given that: y = x 3 log {tex}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right){/tex} To prove: {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}-2 \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+3 \mathrm{x}^{2}{/tex} = 0 Proof: We have, y = x 3 log {tex}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right){/tex} , then {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = x 3 {tex}\left(\frac{1}{\frac{1}{x}}\right)\left(\frac{(-1)}{x^{2}}\right){/tex} + log {tex}(\frac{1}{x}){/tex} (3x 2 ) = -x 2 + 3x 2 [log {tex}(\frac{1}{x}){/tex} ] {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -2x + 6x [log {tex}(\frac{1}{x}){/tex} ] + {tex}\left\{3 \mathrm{x}^{2} \times(\mathrm{x}) \times\left(\frac{-1}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}\right)\right\}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -5x + 6x [log {tex}(\frac{1}{x}){/tex} ] Now put the values in {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}-2 \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+3 \mathrm{x}^{2}{/tex} , we get {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}-2 \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+3 \mathrm{x}^{2}{/tex} = x {tex}\left\{-5 x+6 x\left[\log \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]\right\}{/tex} - 2{-x 2 + 3x 2 {tex}\left[\log \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex} } + 3x 2 = -5x 2 + 6x 2 {tex}\left[\log \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex} + 2x 2 - 6x 2 {tex}\left[\log \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex} + 3x 2 = -5x 2 + 2x 2 + 3x 2 = 0 Hence Proved
solution_html:
Given that: y = x3 log {tex}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right){/tex} 
To prove:  {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}-2 \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+3 \mathrm{x}^{2}{/tex} = 0
Proof: We have, y = x3 log {tex}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right){/tex}, then
{tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = x3 {tex}\left(\frac{1}{\frac{1}{x}}\right)\left(\frac{(-1)}{x^{2}}\right){/tex} + log {tex}(\frac{1}{x}){/tex} (3x2) = -x2 + 3x2 [log {tex}(\frac{1}{x}){/tex}]
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -2x + 6x [log {tex}(\frac{1}{x}){/tex} ] + {tex}\left\{3 \mathrm{x}^{2} \times(\mathrm{x}) \times\left(\frac{-1}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}\right)\right\}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -5x + 6x [log {tex}(\frac{1}{x}){/tex}]
Now put the values in {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}-2 \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+3 \mathrm{x}^{2}{/tex}, we get
 {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}-2 \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+3 \mathrm{x}^{2}{/tex} 
=  x{tex}\left\{-5 x+6 x\left[\log \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]\right\}{/tex} - 2{-x2 + 3x2 {tex}\left[\log \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex}} + 3x2 
=  -5x2 + 6x2 {tex}\left[\log \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex} + 2x2 - 6x2 {tex}\left[\log \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex} + 3x2 
=  -5x2 + 2x2 + 3x2 
=  0
Hence Proved

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation (y - sin x) dx + (tan x) dy = 0 satisfying the condition that y = 0 when x = 0.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation (y - sin x) dx + (tan x) dy = 0 satisfying the condition that y = 0 when x = 0.
explanation: The given differential equation is, (y - sin x)dx + tan x.dy = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} tan x + (y - sin x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} tan x + y = sin x Dividing each term by tan (x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{\tan x}=\frac{\sin x}{\tan x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y cot x = cos x This is first order linear differential equation of type: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P(x) y=Q(x){/tex} Integrating factor = {tex}e^{\int P(x) \cdot d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\int \cot x \cdot d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\ln (\sin x)}{/tex} = sin x [As e In(A) = A] Therefore,the solution is, y×(I.F)= {tex}\int {(I.F)×Q}\ dx{/tex} {tex}(y \times \sin x){/tex} = {tex}\int \sin x \cdot \cos x \cdot d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y sin x = {tex}\frac{(\sin x)^{2}}{2}{/tex} + C [On R.H.S. take sin x = t, hence cosx. dx = dt] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y sin x = {tex}\frac{\sin ^{2} x}{2}{/tex} + C When x = 0, y= 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0 = 0 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y sin x = {tex}\frac{\sin ^{2} x}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{sin \ x}{2} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y - sin x = 0 This is the required solution.
solution: The given differential equation is, (y - sin x)dx + tan x.dy = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} tan x + (y - sin x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} tan x + y = sin x Dividing each term by tan (x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{\tan x}=\frac{\sin x}{\tan x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y cot x = cos x This is first order linear differential equation of type: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P(x) y=Q(x){/tex} Integrating factor = {tex}e^{\int P(x) \cdot d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\int \cot x \cdot d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\ln (\sin x)}{/tex} = sin x [As e In(A) = A] Therefore,the solution is, y×(I.F)= {tex}\int {(I.F)×Q}\ dx{/tex} {tex}(y \times \sin x){/tex} = {tex}\int \sin x \cdot \cos x \cdot d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y sin x = {tex}\frac{(\sin x)^{2}}{2}{/tex} + C [On R.H.S. take sin x = t, hence cosx. dx = dt] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y sin x = {tex}\frac{\sin ^{2} x}{2}{/tex} + C When x = 0, y= 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0 = 0 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y sin x = {tex}\frac{\sin ^{2} x}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{sin \ x}{2} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y - sin x = 0 This is the required solution.
solution_html:
The given differential equation is,
(y - sin x)dx + tan x.dy = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} tan x + (y - sin x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} tan x + y = sin x
Dividing each term by tan (x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{\tan x}=\frac{\sin x}{\tan x}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y cot x = cos x
This is first order linear differential equation of type: 
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P(x) y=Q(x){/tex} 
Integrating factor = {tex}e^{\int P(x) \cdot d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\int \cot x \cdot d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\ln (\sin x)}{/tex} = sin x [As eIn(A) = A]

Therefore,the solution is,

y×(I.F)={tex}\int {(I.F)×Q}\ dx{/tex}
{tex}(y \times \sin x){/tex} = {tex}\int \sin x \cdot \cos x \cdot d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y sin x = {tex}\frac{(\sin x)^{2}}{2}{/tex} + C [On R.H.S. take sin x = t, hence cosx. dx = dt]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y sin x = {tex}\frac{\sin ^{2} x}{2}{/tex} + C
When x = 0, y= 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  0 = 0 + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y sin x = {tex}\frac{\sin ^{2} x}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{sin \ x}{2} {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y - sin x = 0

This is the required solution.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the point on the curve {tex}y=\frac{x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}, where the tangent to the curve has the greatest slope.
question_text: Find the point on the curve {tex}y=\frac{x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} , where the tangent to the curve has the greatest slope.
explanation: Slope = {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{x}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left(1+ x^{2}\right) \times 1-x(2 x)}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1-x^{2}}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=1-\left(\frac{x}{1+x^{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} Now, {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}\right)^2\ge0{/tex} {tex}1-\left(\frac{x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}\right)^2 \le1{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}\le1{/tex} Therefore, maximum value of slope is 1. Now, {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}1-\left(\frac{x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}\right)^2=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}\right)^2=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0 Now, {tex}y=\frac{x}{1+x^2}{/tex} x=0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y=0 Thus, slope is maximum at (0,0)
solution: Slope = {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{x}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left(1+ x^{2}\right) \times 1-x(2 x)}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1-x^{2}}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=1-\left(\frac{x}{1+x^{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} Now, {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}\right)^2\ge0{/tex} {tex}1-\left(\frac{x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}\right)^2 \le1{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}\le1{/tex} Therefore, maximum value of slope is 1. Now, {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}1-\left(\frac{x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}\right)^2=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}\right)^2=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0 Now, {tex}y=\frac{x}{1+x^2}{/tex} x=0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y=0 Thus, slope is maximum at (0,0)
solution_html:
Slope = {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{x}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\left(1+ x^{2}\right) \times 1-x(2 x)}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1-x^{2}}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=1-\left(\frac{x}{1+x^{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}{/tex} {tex}{/tex}Now, {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}\right)^2\ge0{/tex}
{tex}1-\left(\frac{x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}\right)^2 \le1{/tex}
{tex}\frac{dy}{dx}\le1{/tex}
Therefore, maximum value of slope is 1.
Now, {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}1-\left(\frac{x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}\right)^2=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}\right)^2=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0 
Now, {tex}y=\frac{x}{1+x^2}{/tex}
x=0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y=0 
Thus, slope is maximum at (0,0)

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation x2 dy = (2xy + y2) dx, given that y = 1 when x = 1.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation x 2 dy = (2xy + y 2 ) dx, given that y = 1 when x = 1.
explanation: We have x 2 dy = (2xy + y 2 ) dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 x y+y^{2}}{x^{2}}{/tex} ... (i) Put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = v + x {tex}\frac{dv}{d x}{/tex} By (i), v +x {tex}\frac{dv}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 x^{2} v+v^{2} x^{2}}{x^{2}}{/tex} = 2v + v 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\frac{dv}{d x}{/tex} = v + v 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dv}{v(1+v)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{dx}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{dv}{v(1+v)}{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{dx}{x}{/tex} [integrating both the sides] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{(v+1)-(v)dv}{v(1+v)}{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{dx}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int\left(\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{v+1}\right) d v{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\log |v|-\log |v+1|{/tex} = {tex}\log |x|+\log |C|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\log \left|\frac{v}{v+1}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\log |x C|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{y+x}{/tex} = Cx It is given that y = 1 when x = 1 , so {tex}\frac{1}{1+1}{/tex} = C {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = C = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} So the required solution is {tex}\frac{y}{y+x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 + xy - 2y = 0
solution: We have x 2 dy = (2xy + y 2 ) dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 x y+y^{2}}{x^{2}}{/tex} ... (i) Put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = v + x {tex}\frac{dv}{d x}{/tex} By (i), v +x {tex}\frac{dv}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 x^{2} v+v^{2} x^{2}}{x^{2}}{/tex} = 2v + v 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\frac{dv}{d x}{/tex} = v + v 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dv}{v(1+v)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{dx}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{dv}{v(1+v)}{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{dx}{x}{/tex} [integrating both the sides] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{(v+1)-(v)dv}{v(1+v)}{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{dx}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int\left(\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{v+1}\right) d v{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\log |v|-\log |v+1|{/tex} = {tex}\log |x|+\log |C|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\log \left|\frac{v}{v+1}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\log |x C|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{y+x}{/tex} = Cx It is given that y = 1 when x = 1 , so {tex}\frac{1}{1+1}{/tex} = C {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = C = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} So the required solution is {tex}\frac{y}{y+x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 + xy - 2y = 0
solution_html:
We have x2 dy = (2xy + y2) dx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 x y+y^{2}}{x^{2}}{/tex} ... (i)
Put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = v + x{tex}\frac{dv}{d x}{/tex}
By (i), v +x {tex}\frac{dv}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 x^{2} v+v^{2} x^{2}}{x^{2}}{/tex} = 2v + v2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x{tex}\frac{dv}{d x}{/tex} = v + v2  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dv}{v(1+v)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{dx}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{dv}{v(1+v)}{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{dx}{x}{/tex}  [integrating both the sides]

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{(v+1)-(v)dv}{v(1+v)}{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{dx}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int\left(\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{v+1}\right) d v{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\log |v|-\log |v+1|{/tex} = {tex}\log |x|+\log |C|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\log \left|\frac{v}{v+1}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\log |x C|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{y+x}{/tex} = Cx
It is given that y = 1 when x = 1 , so {tex}\frac{1}{1+1}{/tex} = C {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = C = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
So the required solution is{tex}\frac{y}{y+x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  x2 + xy - 2y = 0

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation (1 + x2){tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\left(e^{m \tan ^{-1} x}-y\right){/tex}, given that y = 1 when x = 0.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation (1 + x 2 ) {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\left(e^{m \tan ^{-1} x}-y\right){/tex} , given that y = 1 when x = 0.
explanation: We have (1 + x 2 ) {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\left(e^{m \tan ^{-1} x}-y\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} e^{m \tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} It is linear difference equation of the form {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} + P(x)y = Q(x) where P(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} and Q(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} e^{m \tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} } Now I.F. = {tex}\int \limits_{e}^{} \frac{1}{1+x^{2}} d x{/tex} = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} Required solution is given by ==> y(I.F.) = {tex}\int{/tex} {Q(x) (I.F.)} dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} = {tex}\int e^{\tan ^{-1} x} \frac{1}{1+x^{2}} e^{m \tan ^{-1} x} d x{/tex} + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{1}{1+x^{2}} e^{(m+1) \tan ^{-1} x} d x{/tex} + C Put tan -1 x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} = dt, we get {tex}y e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} = {tex}\int e^{(m+1) t} d t{/tex} + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{(m+1)} e^{(m+1) t}{/tex} + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{(m+1)} e^{(m+1) \tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} + C Since y = 1 when x = 0 so, {tex}1 . \mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} 0}=\frac{1}{(\mathrm{m}+1)} \mathrm{e}^{(\mathrm{m}+1) \log ^{-1} 0}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{m}{m+1}{/tex} Hence the solution is {tex}y e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{(m+1)} e^{(m+1) \tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{m}{m+1}{/tex}
solution: We have (1 + x 2 ) {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\left(e^{m \tan ^{-1} x}-y\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} e^{m \tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} It is linear difference equation of the form {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} + P(x)y = Q(x) where P(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} and Q(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} e^{m \tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} } Now I.F. = {tex}\int \limits_{e}^{} \frac{1}{1+x^{2}} d x{/tex} = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} Required solution is given by ==> y(I.F.) = {tex}\int{/tex} {Q(x) (I.F.)} dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} = {tex}\int e^{\tan ^{-1} x} \frac{1}{1+x^{2}} e^{m \tan ^{-1} x} d x{/tex} + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{1}{1+x^{2}} e^{(m+1) \tan ^{-1} x} d x{/tex} + C Put tan -1 x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} = dt, we get {tex}y e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} = {tex}\int e^{(m+1) t} d t{/tex} + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{(m+1)} e^{(m+1) t}{/tex} + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{(m+1)} e^{(m+1) \tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} + C Since y = 1 when x = 0 so, {tex}1 . \mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} 0}=\frac{1}{(\mathrm{m}+1)} \mathrm{e}^{(\mathrm{m}+1) \log ^{-1} 0}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{m}{m+1}{/tex} Hence the solution is {tex}y e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{(m+1)} e^{(m+1) \tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{m}{m+1}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have (1 + x2){tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\left(e^{m \tan ^{-1} x}-y\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} e^{m \tan ^{-1} x}{/tex}
It is linear difference equation of the form {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} + P(x)y = Q(x) where P(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} and Q(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} e^{m \tan ^{-1} x}{/tex}}
Now I.F. = {tex}\int \limits_{e}^{} \frac{1}{1+x^{2}} d x{/tex} = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex}
Required solution is given by ==> y(I.F.) = {tex}\int{/tex}{Q(x) (I.F.)} dx

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  {tex}y e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} = {tex}\int e^{\tan ^{-1} x} \frac{1}{1+x^{2}} e^{m \tan ^{-1} x} d x{/tex} + C  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{1}{1+x^{2}} e^{(m+1) \tan ^{-1} x} d x{/tex} + C
Put tan-1 x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} = dt, we get {tex}y e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} = {tex}\int e^{(m+1) t} d t{/tex} + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{(m+1)} e^{(m+1) t}{/tex} + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{(m+1)} e^{(m+1) \tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} + C
Since y = 1 when x = 0 so, {tex}1 . \mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} 0}=\frac{1}{(\mathrm{m}+1)} \mathrm{e}^{(\mathrm{m}+1) \log ^{-1} 0}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{m}{m+1}{/tex}
Hence the solution is {tex}y e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{(m+1)} e^{(m+1) \tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{m}{m+1}{/tex}

question_html: Write the sum of the order and degree of the differential equation {tex}1+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{4}=7\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)^{3}{/tex}
question_text: Write the sum of the order and degree of the differential equation {tex}1+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{4}=7\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)^{3}{/tex}
explanation: {tex}1+\left(\frac{d y}{d x^{\prime}}\right)^{4}=7\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)^{3}{/tex} order = 2 Degree = 3 Therefore, sum of order and degree = 2 + 3 = 5
solution: {tex}1+\left(\frac{d y}{d x^{\prime}}\right)^{4}=7\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)^{3}{/tex} order = 2 Degree = 3 Therefore, sum of order and degree = 2 + 3 = 5
solution_html:
{tex}1+\left(\frac{d y}{d x^{\prime}}\right)^{4}=7\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)^{3}{/tex}
order = 2
Degree = 3
Therefore, sum of order and degree = 2 + 3 = 5

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the sum of the degree and the order for the following differential equation: {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left[\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)^{4}\right]=0{/tex}
question_text: Find the sum of the degree and the order for the following differential equation: {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left[\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)^{4}\right]=0{/tex}
explanation: By using chan rule, we get: {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left[\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)^{4}\right]=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 4 \left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)^{3} \cdot \left(\frac{d^{3} y}{d x^{3}}\right) =0{/tex} The order of this differential equation is 3 because the highest order derivative appearing in the equation is third order. The degree is the power of this highest order derivative. In this case, degree is 1. So, the sum of order and degree is 3 + 1 = 4.
solution: By using chan rule, we get: {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left[\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)^{4}\right]=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 4 \left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)^{3} \cdot \left(\frac{d^{3} y}{d x^{3}}\right) =0{/tex} The order of this differential equation is 3 because the highest order derivative appearing in the equation is third order. The degree is the power of this highest order derivative. In this case, degree is 1. So, the sum of order and degree is 3 + 1 = 4.
solution_html:
By using chan rule, we get:
{tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left[\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)^{4}\right]=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 4 \left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)^{3} \cdot \left(\frac{d^{3} y}{d x^{3}}\right) =0{/tex}
The order of this differential equation is 3 because the highest order derivative appearing in the equation is third order.
The degree is the power of this highest order derivative. In this case, degree is 1.
So, the sum of order and degree is 3 + 1 = 4.

question_html: If {tex}(a x+b) e^{y / x}=x{/tex}, then show that {tex}\mathrm{x}^{3}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}\right)=\left(\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}-\mathrm{y}\right)^{2}{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}(a x+b) e^{y / x}=x{/tex} , then show that {tex}\mathrm{x}^{3}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}\right)=\left(\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}-\mathrm{y}\right)^{2}{/tex}
explanation: Given : {tex}(a x+b) e^{y / x}=x{/tex} ...(i) On differentiating both sides with respect to x, we get {tex}(a+b x) \cdot e^{y / x} \cdot\left\{\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}\right\}+b e^{y / x}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{\left\{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y\right\}}{x^{2}}+b e^{y / x}=1{/tex} ...[From Eq. (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}+b e^{y / x}=1{/tex} .... (ii) Again, differentiating both sides with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}+b e^{y / x}\left\{\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}\right\}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}{/tex} {1 - be y/x } = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}\right)=0{/tex} {From Eq, (ii)} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}\left(\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x}\right)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^{3} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\left(x \frac{d y}{d x}-y\right)^{2}{/tex}
solution: Given : {tex}(a x+b) e^{y / x}=x{/tex} ...(i) On differentiating both sides with respect to x, we get {tex}(a+b x) \cdot e^{y / x} \cdot\left\{\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}\right\}+b e^{y / x}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{\left\{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y\right\}}{x^{2}}+b e^{y / x}=1{/tex} ...[From Eq. (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}+b e^{y / x}=1{/tex} .... (ii) Again, differentiating both sides with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}+b e^{y / x}\left\{\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}\right\}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}{/tex} {1 - be y/x } = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}\right)=0{/tex} {From Eq, (ii)} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}\left(\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x}\right)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^{3} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\left(x \frac{d y}{d x}-y\right)^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given : {tex}(a x+b) e^{y / x}=x{/tex} ...(i)
On differentiating both sides with respect to x, we get
{tex}(a+b x) \cdot e^{y / x} \cdot\left\{\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}\right\}+b e^{y / x}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{\left\{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y\right\}}{x^{2}}+b e^{y / x}=1{/tex}...[From Eq. (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}+b e^{y / x}=1{/tex}  .... (ii)
Again, differentiating both sides with respect to x, we get
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}+b e^{y / x}\left\{\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}\right\}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}{/tex} {1 - bey/x} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}\right)=0{/tex} {From Eq, (ii)}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}\left(\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x}\right)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{3} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\left(x \frac{d y}{d x}-y\right)^{2}{/tex}

question_html: If y = log {tex}\left(\frac{x}{a+b x}\right)^{x}{/tex}, prove that {tex}\mathrm{x}^{3} \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\left(\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}}-\mathrm{y}\right)^{2}{/tex}
question_text: If y = log {tex}\left(\frac{x}{a+b x}\right)^{x}{/tex} , prove that {tex}\mathrm{x}^{3} \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\left(\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}}-\mathrm{y}\right)^{2}{/tex}
explanation: Here we have y = log {tex}\left(\frac{x}{a+b x}\right)^{x}{/tex} y = x log {tex}\left(\frac{x}{a+b x}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = log x - log (a + bx) Diff. w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}=\frac{1}{x}-\frac{b}{a+b x} {/tex} or {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=x-\frac{b x^{2}}{a+b x}{/tex} Diff. again w.r.t. x {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{d y}{d x}=1-\frac{\left[(a+b x) 2 b x-b x^{2}(b)\right]}{(a+b x)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{(a+b x)^{2}-2 b x(a+b x)+b^{2} x^{2}}{(a+b x)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{(a+b x)[a+b x-2 b x]+b^{2} x^{2}}{(a+b x)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{(a+b x)(a-b x)+b^{2} x^{2}}{(a+b x)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{a^{2}}{(a+b x)^{2}}=\left(\frac{a}{a+b x}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\left(\frac{a}{a+b x}\right)^{2}{/tex} ....(i) and y = {tex}x \log \left(\frac{x}{a+b x}\right){/tex} Diff. w.r.t. x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=x \frac{(a+b x-b x)}{(a+b x)^{2}}\left(\frac{a+b x}{x}\right)+\log \left(\frac{x}{a+b x}\right){/tex} or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{a}{a+b x}+\frac{y}{x}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}=\frac{a}{a+b x}{/tex} ...(ii) From (i), {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\left(\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}{/tex} or {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{1}{x^{2}}\left(x \frac{d y}{d x}-y\right)^{2}{/tex} or {tex}x^{3} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\left(x \frac{d y}{d x}-y\right)^{2}{/tex}
solution: Here we have y = log {tex}\left(\frac{x}{a+b x}\right)^{x}{/tex} y = x log {tex}\left(\frac{x}{a+b x}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = log x - log (a + bx) Diff. w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}=\frac{1}{x}-\frac{b}{a+b x} {/tex} or {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=x-\frac{b x^{2}}{a+b x}{/tex} Diff. again w.r.t. x {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{d y}{d x}=1-\frac{\left[(a+b x) 2 b x-b x^{2}(b)\right]}{(a+b x)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{(a+b x)^{2}-2 b x(a+b x)+b^{2} x^{2}}{(a+b x)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{(a+b x)[a+b x-2 b x]+b^{2} x^{2}}{(a+b x)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{(a+b x)(a-b x)+b^{2} x^{2}}{(a+b x)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{a^{2}}{(a+b x)^{2}}=\left(\frac{a}{a+b x}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\left(\frac{a}{a+b x}\right)^{2}{/tex} ....(i) and y = {tex}x \log \left(\frac{x}{a+b x}\right){/tex} Diff. w.r.t. x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=x \frac{(a+b x-b x)}{(a+b x)^{2}}\left(\frac{a+b x}{x}\right)+\log \left(\frac{x}{a+b x}\right){/tex} or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{a}{a+b x}+\frac{y}{x}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}=\frac{a}{a+b x}{/tex} ...(ii) From (i), {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\left(\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}{/tex} or {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{1}{x^{2}}\left(x \frac{d y}{d x}-y\right)^{2}{/tex} or {tex}x^{3} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\left(x \frac{d y}{d x}-y\right)^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here we have
y = log {tex}\left(\frac{x}{a+b x}\right)^{x}{/tex}
y = x log {tex}\left(\frac{x}{a+b x}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = log x - log (a + bx)
Diff. w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}=\frac{1}{x}-\frac{b}{a+b x} {/tex} or 
{tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=x-\frac{b x^{2}}{a+b x}{/tex}
Diff. again w.r.t. x
{tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{d y}{d x}=1-\frac{\left[(a+b x) 2 b x-b x^{2}(b)\right]}{(a+b x)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{(a+b x)^{2}-2 b x(a+b x)+b^{2} x^{2}}{(a+b x)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{(a+b x)[a+b x-2 b x]+b^{2} x^{2}}{(a+b x)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{(a+b x)(a-b x)+b^{2} x^{2}}{(a+b x)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{a^{2}}{(a+b x)^{2}}=\left(\frac{a}{a+b x}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\left(\frac{a}{a+b x}\right)^{2}{/tex}....(i)
and y ={tex}x \log \left(\frac{x}{a+b x}\right){/tex}
Diff. w.r.t. x
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=x \frac{(a+b x-b x)}{(a+b x)^{2}}\left(\frac{a+b x}{x}\right)+\log \left(\frac{x}{a+b x}\right){/tex}
or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{a}{a+b x}+\frac{y}{x}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}=\frac{a}{a+b x}{/tex}...(ii)
From (i), {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\left(\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}{/tex}
or {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{1}{x^{2}}\left(x \frac{d y}{d x}-y\right)^{2}{/tex}
or {tex}x^{3} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\left(x \frac{d y}{d x}-y\right)^{2}{/tex}

question_html: Write the sum of the order and the degree of the differential equation: {tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)^{3}{/tex} = y3
question_text: Write the sum of the order and the degree of the differential equation: {tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)^{3}{/tex} = y 3
explanation: The order of this differential equation is 2, because the highest order is second order. The degree is the power of this highest order derivative. In this case degree is 2. So, the answer is 2 + 2 = 4.
solution: The order of this differential equation is 2, because the highest order is second order. The degree is the power of this highest order derivative. In this case degree is 2. So, the answer is 2 + 2 = 4.

explanation: The order of this differential equation is 2, because the highest order derivative in is second order. The degree is the power of this highest order derivative. In this case degree is 2. So, the answer is 2 × 2 = 4.
