subject: Mathematics
question_html: (x2 + y2) dy = xydx. If y(1) = 1 and y (x0) = e, then find the value of x0.
question_text: (x 2 + y 2 ) dy = xydx. If y(1) = 1 and y (x 0 ) = e, then find the value of x 0 .
explanation: {tex}\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right) d y=x y d x\\ \Rightarrow \int \frac{x}{y} d y+\int \frac{y}{x} d y=\int d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \log y+\frac{y^{2}}{2 x}=x+c{/tex} Now, at x = 1; y = e {tex}x \log y+\frac{y^{2}}{2 x}=x+c \Rightarrow x+\frac{e^{2}}{2}=x+c \Rightarrow c=\frac{e^{2}}{2}{/tex} Now at x = x 0 ; y = e {tex}x_{0} \log e+\frac{e^{2}}{2 x_{0}}=x_{0}+\frac{e^{2}}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{e^{2}}{2 x_{0}}=\frac{e^{2}}{2} \Rightarrow x_{0}=1{/tex}
solution: {tex}\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right) d y=x y d x\\ \Rightarrow \int \frac{x}{y} d y+\int \frac{y}{x} d y=\int d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \log y+\frac{y^{2}}{2 x}=x+c{/tex} Now, at x = 1; y = e {tex}x \log y+\frac{y^{2}}{2 x}=x+c \Rightarrow x+\frac{e^{2}}{2}=x+c \Rightarrow c=\frac{e^{2}}{2}{/tex} Now at x = x 0 ; y = e {tex}x_{0} \log e+\frac{e^{2}}{2 x_{0}}=x_{0}+\frac{e^{2}}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{e^{2}}{2 x_{0}}=\frac{e^{2}}{2} \Rightarrow x_{0}=1{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right) d y=x y d x\\ \Rightarrow \int \frac{x}{y} d y+\int \frac{y}{x} d y=\int d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow x \log y+\frac{y^{2}}{2 x}=x+c{/tex}
Now, at x = 1; y = e 
{tex}x \log y+\frac{y^{2}}{2 x}=x+c \Rightarrow x+\frac{e^{2}}{2}=x+c \Rightarrow c=\frac{e^{2}}{2}{/tex}
Now at x = x0; y = e
{tex}x_{0} \log e+\frac{e^{2}}{2 x_{0}}=x_{0}+\frac{e^{2}}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{e^{2}}{2 x_{0}}=\frac{e^{2}}{2} \Rightarrow x_{0}=1{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y tan x = 3x2 + x3 tan x, x {tex}\neq \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}, given that y = 0 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y tan x = 3x 2 + x 3 tan x, x {tex}\neq \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} , given that y = 0 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
explanation: Given differental equation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+y \tan x=3 x^{2}+x^{3} \tan x{/tex} , y = 0 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} The above equation is of the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} , which is a first order linear differential equation. {tex}\therefore{/tex} P = tanx and Q = 3x 2 + x 3 tanx Now, Integrating Factor (IF) = {tex}e^{\int P \, d x}=e^{\int \tan x \, d x}=e^{-\log |\cos x|}=e^{\log |\sec x|}=\sec x{/tex} {tex}\therefore y(I F)=\int(Q \times I F) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \sec x=\int\left(3 x^{2} \sec x+x^{3} \tan x \sec x\right) d x{/tex} ....(i) Let I = {tex}\int\left(3 x^{2} \sec x+x^{3} \tan x \sec x\right) d x{/tex} ....(ii) Let x 3 secx = t {tex}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(3 x^{2} \sec x+x^{3} \sec x \tan x\right) d x=d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int\left(3 x^{2} \sec x+x^{3} \tan x \sec x\right) d x=\int d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = t + C [using (ii)] {tex}\Rightarrow I=\int\left(3 x^{2} \sec x+x^{3} \tan x \sec x\right) d x=x^{3} \sec x+C{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}\therefore y \sec x=x^{3} \sec x+C{/tex} [From (i), (ii) and (iii)] Putting {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} and y = 0 in the above equation {tex}0=\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)^{3} \sec \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow C=-\frac{2 \pi^{3}}{27}{/tex} Thus, required differential equation: {tex}\text { ysecx }=x^{3} \sec x-\frac{2 \pi^{3}}{27}{/tex}
solution: Given differental equation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+y \tan x=3 x^{2}+x^{3} \tan x{/tex} , y = 0 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} The above equation is of the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} , which is a first order linear differential equation. {tex}\therefore{/tex} P = tanx and Q = 3x 2 + x 3 tanx Now, Integrating Factor (IF) = {tex}e^{\int P \, d x}=e^{\int \tan x \, d x}=e^{-\log |\cos x|}=e^{\log |\sec x|}=\sec x{/tex} {tex}\therefore y(I F)=\int(Q \times I F) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \sec x=\int\left(3 x^{2} \sec x+x^{3} \tan x \sec x\right) d x{/tex} ....(i) Let I = {tex}\int\left(3 x^{2} \sec x+x^{3} \tan x \sec x\right) d x{/tex} ....(ii) Let x 3 secx = t {tex}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(3 x^{2} \sec x+x^{3} \sec x \tan x\right) d x=d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int\left(3 x^{2} \sec x+x^{3} \tan x \sec x\right) d x=\int d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = t + C [using (ii)] {tex}\Rightarrow I=\int\left(3 x^{2} \sec x+x^{3} \tan x \sec x\right) d x=x^{3} \sec x+C{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}\therefore y \sec x=x^{3} \sec x+C{/tex} [From (i), (ii) and (iii)] Putting {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} and y = 0 in the above equation {tex}0=\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)^{3} \sec \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow C=-\frac{2 \pi^{3}}{27}{/tex} Thus, required differential equation: {tex}\text { ysecx }=x^{3} \sec x-\frac{2 \pi^{3}}{27}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given differental equation: 
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+y \tan x=3 x^{2}+x^{3} \tan x{/tex}, y = 0 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
The above equation is of the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex}, which is a first order linear differential equation.
{tex}\therefore{/tex}P = tanx and Q = 3x2 + x3tanx
Now, Integrating Factor (IF) = {tex}e^{\int P \, d x}=e^{\int \tan x \, d x}=e^{-\log |\cos x|}=e^{\log |\sec x|}=\sec x{/tex}
{tex}\therefore y(I F)=\int(Q \times I F) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \sec x=\int\left(3 x^{2} \sec x+x^{3} \tan x \sec x\right) d x{/tex} ....(i)
Let I = {tex}\int\left(3 x^{2} \sec x+x^{3} \tan x \sec x\right) d x{/tex} ....(ii)
Let x3 secx = t
{tex}{/tex}{tex}\Rightarrow\left(3 x^{2} \sec x+x^{3} \sec x \tan x\right) d x=d t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int\left(3 x^{2} \sec x+x^{3} \tan x \sec x\right) d x=\int d t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = t + C [using (ii)]
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\int\left(3 x^{2} \sec x+x^{3} \tan x \sec x\right) d x=x^{3} \sec x+C{/tex}...(iii)
{tex}\therefore y \sec x=x^{3} \sec x+C{/tex} [From (i), (ii) and (iii)]
Putting {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} and y = 0 in the above equation 
{tex}0=\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)^{3} \sec \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow C=-\frac{2 \pi^{3}}{27}{/tex}
Thus, required differential equation: {tex}\text { ysecx }=x^{3} \sec x-\frac{2 \pi^{3}}{27}{/tex}

question_html: If log{tex}\sqrt{\mathbf{x}^{2}+\mathbf{y}^{2}}{/tex} = tan-1{tex}\left(\frac{x}{y}\right){/tex}, then show that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y-x}{y+x}{/tex}
question_text: If log {tex}\sqrt{\mathbf{x}^{2}+\mathbf{y}^{2}}{/tex} = tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{x}{y}\right){/tex} , then show that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y-x}{y+x}{/tex}
explanation: {tex}\log \sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{y}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \log \left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{X}{y}\right){/tex} {tex}\log \left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)=2 \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{y}\right){/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. to x, we get, {tex}\frac{1}{x^{2}+y^{2}}\left[2 x+2 y \frac{d y}{d x}\right]=\frac{2}{1+({1 \over y})^{2}}\left[\frac{y-x \frac{d y}{d x}}{y^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}2\left[\frac{1}{x^{2}+y^{2}}\left[x+y \frac{d y}{d x}\right]=2\left[\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}+y^{2}}\left[\frac{y-x \frac{d y}{d x}}{y^{2}}\right]\right]\right.{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{1}{x^{2}+y^{2}}\left[x+y \frac{d y}{d x}\right]=\frac{1}{x^{2}+y^{2}}\left[y-x \frac{d y}{d x}\right]\right.{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}(y+x)=y-x{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y-x}{y+x}{/tex} Hence proved
solution: {tex}\log \sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{y}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \log \left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{X}{y}\right){/tex} {tex}\log \left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)=2 \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{y}\right){/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. to x, we get, {tex}\frac{1}{x^{2}+y^{2}}\left[2 x+2 y \frac{d y}{d x}\right]=\frac{2}{1+({1 \over y})^{2}}\left[\frac{y-x \frac{d y}{d x}}{y^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}2\left[\frac{1}{x^{2}+y^{2}}\left[x+y \frac{d y}{d x}\right]=2\left[\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}+y^{2}}\left[\frac{y-x \frac{d y}{d x}}{y^{2}}\right]\right]\right.{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{1}{x^{2}+y^{2}}\left[x+y \frac{d y}{d x}\right]=\frac{1}{x^{2}+y^{2}}\left[y-x \frac{d y}{d x}\right]\right.{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}(y+x)=y-x{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y-x}{y+x}{/tex} Hence proved
solution_html:
{tex}\log \sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{y}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \log \left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{X}{y}\right){/tex}
{tex}\log \left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)=2 \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{y}\right){/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. to x, we get,
{tex}\frac{1}{x^{2}+y^{2}}\left[2 x+2 y \frac{d y}{d x}\right]=\frac{2}{1+({1 \over y})^{2}}\left[\frac{y-x \frac{d y}{d x}}{y^{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}2\left[\frac{1}{x^{2}+y^{2}}\left[x+y \frac{d y}{d x}\right]=2\left[\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}+y^{2}}\left[\frac{y-x \frac{d y}{d x}}{y^{2}}\right]\right]\right.{/tex}
{tex}\left[\frac{1}{x^{2}+y^{2}}\left[x+y \frac{d y}{d x}\right]=\frac{1}{x^{2}+y^{2}}\left[y-x \frac{d y}{d x}\right]\right.{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}(y+x)=y-x{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y-x}{y+x}{/tex}
Hence proved

question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y^{2}}{x y-x^{2}}{/tex}
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y^{2}}{x y-x^{2}}{/tex}
explanation: The given differential equation is, {tex}\frac{d y}{a x}=\frac{x y}{x^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} Taking reciprocal,we have, {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{x y}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{x}{y}+\frac{1}{\frac{x}{y}}=f(\frac{x}{y}){/tex} {tex}{/tex} ........(1) Thus it is a homogeneous differential equation {tex}\frac{x}{y}=v \Rightarrow{/tex} x = vy {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=v+y\frac{dv}{dy}{/tex} Therefore,from (1),we have, {tex}v+y \frac{d v}{d y}=v+\frac{1}{v}{/tex} {tex}\int v \, d v=\int \frac{d y}{y}{/tex} {tex}[\therefore \text { using } \int \frac{d x}{x} = logx+c]{/tex} {tex}\frac{v^{2}}{2}=\log |y|+c{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2 y^{2}}=\log |y|+c{/tex} {tex}x^{2}=2 y^{2}|\log y|+ y^{2}C{/tex}
solution: The given differential equation is, {tex}\frac{d y}{a x}=\frac{x y}{x^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} Taking reciprocal,we have, {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{x y}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{x}{y}+\frac{1}{\frac{x}{y}}=f(\frac{x}{y}){/tex} {tex}{/tex} ........(1) Thus it is a homogeneous differential equation {tex}\frac{x}{y}=v \Rightarrow{/tex} x = vy {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=v+y\frac{dv}{dy}{/tex} Therefore,from (1),we have, {tex}v+y \frac{d v}{d y}=v+\frac{1}{v}{/tex} {tex}\int v \, d v=\int \frac{d y}{y}{/tex} {tex}[\therefore \text { using } \int \frac{d x}{x} = logx+c]{/tex} {tex}\frac{v^{2}}{2}=\log |y|+c{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2 y^{2}}=\log |y|+c{/tex} {tex}x^{2}=2 y^{2}|\log y|+ y^{2}C{/tex}
solution_html:
The given differential equation is,

{tex}\frac{d y}{a x}=\frac{x y}{x^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex}
Taking reciprocal,we have,
{tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{x y}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{x}{y}+\frac{1}{\frac{x}{y}}=f(\frac{x}{y}){/tex} {tex}{/tex}........(1)
Thus it is a homogeneous differential equation
{tex}\frac{x}{y}=v \Rightarrow{/tex} x = vy
{tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=v+y\frac{dv}{dy}{/tex}

Therefore,from (1),we have,
{tex}v+y \frac{d v}{d y}=v+\frac{1}{v}{/tex}
{tex}\int v \, d v=\int \frac{d y}{y}{/tex} {tex}[\therefore \text { using } \int \frac{d x}{x} = logx+c]{/tex}
{tex}\frac{v^{2}}{2}=\log |y|+c{/tex} {tex}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2 y^{2}}=\log |y|+c{/tex}
{tex}x^{2}=2 y^{2}|\log y|+ y^{2}C{/tex}

question_html: Solve the following differential equation, given that y = 0, when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}: sin 2x{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - y = tan x
question_text: Solve the following differential equation, given that y = 0, when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} : sin 2x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - y = tan x
explanation: Given that, {tex}\sin 2 x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=\tan x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-(cosec2 x) y=\frac{\sec^2 x}{2 }{/tex} Which is a Linear differential equation of the form, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} where P = - cosec 2x and Q = {tex}\frac{\sec^2 x}{2 }{/tex} I.F {tex}=e^{\int p \, d x}=e^{\int-\operatorname{cosec} 2 x \, d x}=e^{-\frac{1}{2} \ln \tan x} =\frac{1}{\sqrt{\tan x}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore y \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\tan x}}=\int \frac{\sec ^{2} x}{2 \sqrt{\tan x}} d x+c{/tex} Put tan x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sec 2 x dx = ft {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{\sqrt{\tan x}}=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d t}{\sqrt{t}}+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{\sqrt{\tan x}}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{t^{\frac{1}{2}}}{\frac{1}{2}} + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{\sqrt{\tan x}}=\sqrt{\tan x}+c{/tex} ....(i) Using initial conditions, {tex}x=\frac {\pi}{4}, \ y=0{/tex} {tex}\frac{0}{\sqrt{\tan \pi}/4}=\sqrt{\tan \pi / 4}+C{/tex} 0 = 1 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = -1 Using value of C in equation (i), we have, {tex} \frac{y}{\sqrt{\tan x}}=\sqrt{\tan x}-1{/tex} {tex}\therefore y=\tan x-\sqrt{\tan x}{/tex}
solution: Given that, {tex}\sin 2 x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=\tan x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-(cosec2 x) y=\frac{\sec^2 x}{2 }{/tex} Which is a Linear differential equation of the form, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} where P = - cosec 2x and Q = {tex}\frac{\sec^2 x}{2 }{/tex} I.F {tex}=e^{\int p \, d x}=e^{\int-\operatorname{cosec} 2 x \, d x}=e^{-\frac{1}{2} \ln \tan x} =\frac{1}{\sqrt{\tan x}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore y \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\tan x}}=\int \frac{\sec ^{2} x}{2 \sqrt{\tan x}} d x+c{/tex} Put tan x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sec 2 x dx = ft {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{\sqrt{\tan x}}=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d t}{\sqrt{t}}+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{\sqrt{\tan x}}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{t^{\frac{1}{2}}}{\frac{1}{2}} + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{\sqrt{\tan x}}=\sqrt{\tan x}+c{/tex} ....(i) Using initial conditions, {tex}x=\frac {\pi}{4}, \ y=0{/tex} {tex}\frac{0}{\sqrt{\tan \pi}/4}=\sqrt{\tan \pi / 4}+C{/tex} 0 = 1 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = -1 Using value of C in equation (i), we have, {tex} \frac{y}{\sqrt{\tan x}}=\sqrt{\tan x}-1{/tex} {tex}\therefore y=\tan x-\sqrt{\tan x}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that, {tex}\sin 2 x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=\tan x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-(cosec2 x) y=\frac{\sec^2 x}{2 }{/tex}
Which is a Linear differential equation of the form,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex}
where P = - cosec 2x and Q = {tex}\frac{\sec^2 x}{2 }{/tex}
I.F  {tex}=e^{\int p \, d x}=e^{\int-\operatorname{cosec} 2 x \, d x}=e^{-\frac{1}{2} \ln \tan x} =\frac{1}{\sqrt{\tan x}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore y \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\tan x}}=\int \frac{\sec ^{2} x}{2 \sqrt{\tan x}} d x+c{/tex}
Put tan x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sec2x dx = ft
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{\sqrt{\tan x}}=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d t}{\sqrt{t}}+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{\sqrt{\tan x}}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{t^{\frac{1}{2}}}{\frac{1}{2}} + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{\sqrt{\tan x}}=\sqrt{\tan x}+c{/tex} ....(i)
Using initial conditions, {tex}x=\frac {\pi}{4}, \ y=0{/tex}
{tex}\frac{0}{\sqrt{\tan \pi}/4}=\sqrt{\tan \pi / 4}+C{/tex}
0 = 1 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = -1
Using value of C in equation (i), we have,
{tex} \frac{y}{\sqrt{\tan x}}=\sqrt{\tan x}-1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore y=\tan x-\sqrt{\tan x}{/tex}

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\left[x \sin ^{2}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-y\right]{/tex}dx + x dy = 0, given that y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}when x = 1
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\left[x \sin ^{2}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-y\right]{/tex} dx + x dy = 0, given that y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} when x = 1
explanation: We can rewrite the given differential equation as, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\sin ^{2}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} This is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} So, it is homogeneous. Putting y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} in (i), we get {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = v -sin 2 v {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = -sin 2 v {tex}\Rightarrow-cosec ^{2} v d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int\left(-cosec ^{2} v\right) d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} [on integrating both sides] {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} cot v = log |x| + C, where C is an arbitrary constant {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} cot {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = log |x| + C ...(ii) {tex}\left[\because v=\frac{y}{x}\right]{/tex} Putting x = 1 and y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} in (ii), we get C = 1. {tex}\therefore{/tex} cot {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = log |x| + 1 is the desired solution.
solution: We can rewrite the given differential equation as, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\sin ^{2}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} This is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} So, it is homogeneous. Putting y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} in (i), we get {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = v -sin 2 v {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = -sin 2 v {tex}\Rightarrow-cosec ^{2} v d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int\left(-cosec ^{2} v\right) d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} [on integrating both sides] {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} cot v = log |x| + C, where C is an arbitrary constant {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} cot {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = log |x| + C ...(ii) {tex}\left[\because v=\frac{y}{x}\right]{/tex} Putting x = 1 and y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} in (ii), we get C = 1. {tex}\therefore{/tex} cot {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = log |x| + 1 is the desired solution.
solution_html:
We can rewrite the given differential equation as,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\sin ^{2}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex}
This is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} So, it is homogeneous.
Putting y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} in (i), we get
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = v -sin2 v
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = -sin2 v
{tex}\Rightarrow-cosec ^{2} v d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int\left(-cosec ^{2} v\right) d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} [on integrating both sides]
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} cot v = log |x| + C, where C is an arbitrary constant
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} cot {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = log |x| + C ...(ii) {tex}\left[\because v=\frac{y}{x}\right]{/tex}
Putting x = 1 and y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} in (ii), we get C = 1.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} cot {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex}= log |x| + 1 is the desired solution.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex} \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } + y = e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x }{/tex}.
question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex} \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } + y = e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } x }{/tex} .
explanation: Given the differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} \cdot e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} Interesting factor = {tex}e^{\int \frac{1}{1+x^{2}} d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Solution is : {tex}y \cdot e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{1}{1+x^{2}}\left(e^{\tan ^{-1} x}\right)^{2}{/tex} or {tex}y \cdot e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(e^{\tan ^{-1} x}\right)^{2}{/tex} + C or y = {tex}\frac{1}{2} e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} + {tex}c e^{-\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex}
solution: Given the differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} \cdot e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} Interesting factor = {tex}e^{\int \frac{1}{1+x^{2}} d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Solution is : {tex}y \cdot e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{1}{1+x^{2}}\left(e^{\tan ^{-1} x}\right)^{2}{/tex} or {tex}y \cdot e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(e^{\tan ^{-1} x}\right)^{2}{/tex} + C or y = {tex}\frac{1}{2} e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} + {tex}c e^{-\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given the differential equation can be written as 
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} \cdot e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex}
Interesting factor = {tex}e^{\int \frac{1}{1+x^{2}} d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Solution is :
{tex}y \cdot e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{1}{1+x^{2}}\left(e^{\tan ^{-1} x}\right)^{2}{/tex}
or {tex}y \cdot e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(e^{\tan ^{-1} x}\right)^{2}{/tex} + C
or y = {tex}\frac{1}{2} e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} + {tex}c e^{-\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the following differential equation {tex}\left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 x y = \frac { 2 } { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } , x \neq 1{/tex}.
question_text: Solve the following differential equation {tex}\left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 x y = \frac { 2 } { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } , x \neq 1{/tex} .
explanation: According to question, Given differential equation is, {tex}\left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 x y = \frac { 2 } { x ^ { 2 } - 1 }{/tex} Dividing both sides with (x 2 - 1), we get {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + \frac { 2 x } { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } y = \frac { 2 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} It is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} Where {tex}P = \frac { 2 x } { x ^ { 2 } - 1 }{/tex} and {tex}Q = \frac { 2 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} We know that , {tex}{\text{IF}} = {e^{\int {\text{P}} x}} = {e^{\int {\frac{{2{\text{x}}}}{{{x^2} - 1}}} dx}}{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { \log \left| x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right| } = x ^ { 2 } - 1{/tex} {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { \text { put } x ^ { 2 } - 1 = t \Rightarrow 2 x d x = d t , \text { then } } \\ { \int \frac { 2 x } { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } d x = \int \frac { 1 } { t } d t = \log | t | = \log \left| x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right| } \end{array} \right]{/tex} So, the required general solution is {tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) = \int \frac { 2 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) ^ { 2 } } \times \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) = \int \frac { 2 } { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) = \frac { 2 } { 2 \times 1 } \log \left| \frac { x - 1 } { x + 1 } \right| + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { 1 } { 2 a } \log \left| \frac { x - a } { x + a } \right| \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) = \log \left| \frac { x - 1 } { x + 1 } \right| + C{/tex} which is the required differential equation.
solution: According to question, Given differential equation is, {tex}\left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 x y = \frac { 2 } { x ^ { 2 } - 1 }{/tex} Dividing both sides with (x 2 - 1), we get {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + \frac { 2 x } { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } y = \frac { 2 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} It is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} Where {tex}P = \frac { 2 x } { x ^ { 2 } - 1 }{/tex} and {tex}Q = \frac { 2 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} We know that , {tex}{\text{IF}} = {e^{\int {\text{P}} x}} = {e^{\int {\frac{{2{\text{x}}}}{{{x^2} - 1}}} dx}}{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { \log \left| x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right| } = x ^ { 2 } - 1{/tex} {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { \text { put } x ^ { 2 } - 1 = t \Rightarrow 2 x d x = d t , \text { then } } \\ { \int \frac { 2 x } { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } d x = \int \frac { 1 } { t } d t = \log | t | = \log \left| x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right| } \end{array} \right]{/tex} So, the required general solution is {tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) = \int \frac { 2 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) ^ { 2 } } \times \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) = \int \frac { 2 } { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) = \frac { 2 } { 2 \times 1 } \log \left| \frac { x - 1 } { x + 1 } \right| + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { 1 } { 2 a } \log \left| \frac { x - a } { x + a } \right| \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) = \log \left| \frac { x - 1 } { x + 1 } \right| + C{/tex} which is the required differential equation.
solution_html:
According to question, Given differential equation is, 
{tex}\left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 x y = \frac { 2 } { x ^ { 2 } - 1 }{/tex}
Dividing both sides with (x2 - 1), we get
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + \frac { 2 x } { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } y = \frac { 2 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
It is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex}
Where {tex}P = \frac { 2 x } { x ^ { 2 } - 1 }{/tex} and {tex}Q = \frac { 2 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}  
We know that , 

 {tex}{\text{IF}} = {e^{\int {\text{P}} x}} = {e^{\int {\frac{{2{\text{x}}}}{{{x^2} - 1}}} dx}}{/tex}
{tex}= e ^ { \log \left| x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right| } = x ^ { 2 } - 1{/tex} {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { \text { put } x ^ { 2 } - 1 = t \Rightarrow 2 x d x = d t , \text { then } } \\ { \int \frac { 2 x } { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } d x = \int \frac { 1 } { t } d t = \log | t | = \log \left| x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right| } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
So, the required general solution is {tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) = \int \frac { 2 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) ^ { 2 } } \times \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) = \int \frac { 2 } { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) = \frac { 2 } { 2 \times 1 } \log \left| \frac { x - 1 } { x + 1 } \right| + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { 1 } { 2 a } \log \left| \frac { x - a } { x + a } \right| \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad y \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) = \log \left| \frac { x - 1 } { x + 1 } \right| + C{/tex}
which is the required differential equation.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the general solution of {tex}x \log x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=\frac{2}{x} \log x{/tex}
question_text: Find the general solution of {tex}x \log x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=\frac{2}{x} \log x{/tex}
explanation: It is given that {tex}x \log x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=\frac{2}{x} \log x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{x \log x}=\frac{2}{x^{2}}{/tex} , This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} (where, p = {tex}\frac{1}{x \log x}{/tex} and {tex}Q=\frac{2}{x^{2}}{/tex} ) Now, I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{pdx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{1}{\mathrm{xlog} \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\log |\log \mathrm{x}|}=\log \mathrm{x}{/tex} Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation: {tex}\mathrm{y}(\mathrm{I}. \mathrm{F} )=\int(\mathrm{Q} \times \mathrm{I.F}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} . \log \mathrm{x}=\int\left[\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}^{2}} \cdot \log \mathrm{x}\right] \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} ....(i) Now, {tex}\int\left[\frac{2}{x^{2}} \cdot \log x\right] d x=2 \int\left(\log x \cdot \frac{1}{x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex} = {tex}2\left[\log x . \int \frac{1}{x^{2}} d x-\int\left\{\frac{d}{d x}(\log x) \cdot \int \frac{1}{x^{2}} d x\right\} d x\right]{/tex} = {tex}2\left[\log _{\mathrm{X}}\left(-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)-\int\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \cdot\left(-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}\right]{/tex} = {tex}2\left[-\frac{\log x}{x}+\int \frac{1}{x^{2}} d x\right]{/tex} = {tex}2\left[-\frac{\log x}{x}-\frac{1}{x}\right]{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{2}{x}(1+\log x){/tex} Now, substituting the value in (i), we get, {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} \cdot \log \mathrm{x}=-\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}}(1+\log \mathrm{x})+\mathrm{C}{/tex} Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is {tex}\text { y. } \log x=-\frac{2}{x}(1+\log x)+C{/tex}
solution: It is given that {tex}x \log x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=\frac{2}{x} \log x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{x \log x}=\frac{2}{x^{2}}{/tex} , This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} (where, p = {tex}\frac{1}{x \log x}{/tex} and {tex}Q=\frac{2}{x^{2}}{/tex} ) Now, I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{pdx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{1}{\mathrm{xlog} \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\log |\log \mathrm{x}|}=\log \mathrm{x}{/tex} Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation: {tex}\mathrm{y}(\mathrm{I}. \mathrm{F} )=\int(\mathrm{Q} \times \mathrm{I.F}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} . \log \mathrm{x}=\int\left[\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}^{2}} \cdot \log \mathrm{x}\right] \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} ....(i) Now, {tex}\int\left[\frac{2}{x^{2}} \cdot \log x\right] d x=2 \int\left(\log x \cdot \frac{1}{x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex} = {tex}2\left[\log x . \int \frac{1}{x^{2}} d x-\int\left\{\frac{d}{d x}(\log x) \cdot \int \frac{1}{x^{2}} d x\right\} d x\right]{/tex} = {tex}2\left[\log _{\mathrm{X}}\left(-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)-\int\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \cdot\left(-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}\right]{/tex} = {tex}2\left[-\frac{\log x}{x}+\int \frac{1}{x^{2}} d x\right]{/tex} = {tex}2\left[-\frac{\log x}{x}-\frac{1}{x}\right]{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{2}{x}(1+\log x){/tex} Now, substituting the value in (i), we get, {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} \cdot \log \mathrm{x}=-\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}}(1+\log \mathrm{x})+\mathrm{C}{/tex} Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is {tex}\text { y. } \log x=-\frac{2}{x}(1+\log x)+C{/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that {tex}x \log x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=\frac{2}{x} \log x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{x \log x}=\frac{2}{x^{2}}{/tex},
This is equation in the form of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} (where, p = {tex}\frac{1}{x \log x}{/tex} and {tex}Q=\frac{2}{x^{2}}{/tex} )
Now, I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{pdx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{1}{\mathrm{xlog} \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\log |\log \mathrm{x}|}=\log \mathrm{x}{/tex} 
Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by the relation:
{tex}\mathrm{y}(\mathrm{I}. \mathrm{F} )=\int(\mathrm{Q} \times \mathrm{I.F}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} . \log \mathrm{x}=\int\left[\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}^{2}} \cdot \log \mathrm{x}\right] \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} ....(i)
Now, {tex}\int\left[\frac{2}{x^{2}} \cdot \log x\right] d x=2 \int\left(\log x \cdot \frac{1}{x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex} 
= {tex}2\left[\log x . \int \frac{1}{x^{2}} d x-\int\left\{\frac{d}{d x}(\log x) \cdot \int \frac{1}{x^{2}} d x\right\} d x\right]{/tex} 
= {tex}2\left[\log _{\mathrm{X}}\left(-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)-\int\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \cdot\left(-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}\right]{/tex} 
= {tex}2\left[-\frac{\log x}{x}+\int \frac{1}{x^{2}} d x\right]{/tex} 
= {tex}2\left[-\frac{\log x}{x}-\frac{1}{x}\right]{/tex} 
= {tex}-\frac{2}{x}(1+\log x){/tex} 
Now, substituting the value in (i), we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} \cdot \log \mathrm{x}=-\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}}(1+\log \mathrm{x})+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
Therefore, the required general solution of the given differential equation is
{tex}\text { y. } \log x=-\frac{2}{x}(1+\log x)+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the differential equation (x2 - 1) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y=\frac{1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex}
question_text: Solve the differential equation (x 2 - 1) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y=\frac{1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex}
explanation: Given that, {tex}\left(x^{2}-1\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y=\frac{1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{2 x}{x^{2}-1}\right) y=\frac{1}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} which is a linear differential equation. On comparing it with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} , we get P = {tex}\frac{2 x}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} , Q = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} I.F. = {tex}e^{\int \frac{2 x}{x^{2}-1} d x}=e^{\log \left(x^{2}-1\right)}{/tex} = x 2 - 1 Thus, solution is: {tex}y(\mathrm{I}.\mathrm{F})=\int Q(\mathrm{I.F}) d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x 2 - 1) = {tex}\int \frac{1}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}} \cdot\left(x^{2}-1\right) d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(x 2 - 1) = {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x^{2}-1}+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y\left(x^{2}-1\right)=\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right)+C{/tex}
solution: Given that, {tex}\left(x^{2}-1\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y=\frac{1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{2 x}{x^{2}-1}\right) y=\frac{1}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} which is a linear differential equation. On comparing it with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} , we get P = {tex}\frac{2 x}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} , Q = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} I.F. = {tex}e^{\int \frac{2 x}{x^{2}-1} d x}=e^{\log \left(x^{2}-1\right)}{/tex} = x 2 - 1 Thus, solution is: {tex}y(\mathrm{I}.\mathrm{F})=\int Q(\mathrm{I.F}) d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x 2 - 1) = {tex}\int \frac{1}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}} \cdot\left(x^{2}-1\right) d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(x 2 - 1) = {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x^{2}-1}+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y\left(x^{2}-1\right)=\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right)+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that,
{tex}\left(x^{2}-1\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y=\frac{1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{2 x}{x^{2}-1}\right) y=\frac{1}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} 
which is a linear differential equation.
On comparing it with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex}, we get
P = {tex}\frac{2 x}{x^{2}-1}{/tex}, Q = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} 
I.F. = {tex}e^{\int \frac{2 x}{x^{2}-1} d x}=e^{\log \left(x^{2}-1\right)}{/tex} = x2 - 1
Thus, solution is: 
{tex}y(\mathrm{I}.\mathrm{F})=\int Q(\mathrm{I.F}) d x+C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x2 - 1) = {tex}\int \frac{1}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}} \cdot\left(x^{2}-1\right) d x+C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(x2 - 1) = {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x^{2}-1}+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y\left(x^{2}-1\right)=\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right)+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}x \frac { d y } { d x } - y + x \ cosec \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = 0{/tex}.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}x \frac { d y } { d x } - y + x \ cosec \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = 0{/tex} .
explanation: Given differential equation is {tex}x \frac { d y } { d x } - y + x cosec \; \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = 0{/tex} On dividing both sides by x , we get {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } - \frac { y } { x } + cosec \; \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } - cosec \; \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} which is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} . put , {tex}y=vx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} {tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v x } { x } - cosec \; \left( \frac { v x } { x } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = v - cosec \; v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = - cosec \; v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d v } { cosec \; v } = \frac { - d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac { d v } { cosec \; v } = \int - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \sin v d v = \int - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \cos v = - \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \cos \frac { y } { x } = - \log | x | + c \quad \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \frac { y } { x } = ( \log | x | - C ){/tex} ....(i) Also, given that x = 1 and y = 0. On putting above values in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos 0 = \log | 1 | - C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 = 0 - C \Rightarrow C = - 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \cos \frac { y } { x } = \log | x | + 1{/tex} [from Eq. (i)] This is required solution of given differential equation.
solution: Given differential equation is {tex}x \frac { d y } { d x } - y + x cosec \; \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = 0{/tex} On dividing both sides by x , we get {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } - \frac { y } { x } + cosec \; \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } - cosec \; \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} which is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} . put , {tex}y=vx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} {tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v x } { x } - cosec \; \left( \frac { v x } { x } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = v - cosec \; v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = - cosec \; v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d v } { cosec \; v } = \frac { - d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac { d v } { cosec \; v } = \int - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \sin v d v = \int - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \cos v = - \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \cos \frac { y } { x } = - \log | x | + c \quad \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \frac { y } { x } = ( \log | x | - C ){/tex} ....(i) Also, given that x = 1 and y = 0. On putting above values in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos 0 = \log | 1 | - C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 = 0 - C \Rightarrow C = - 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \cos \frac { y } { x } = \log | x | + 1{/tex} [from Eq. (i)] This is required solution of given differential equation.
solution_html:
Given differential equation is
{tex}x \frac { d y } { d x } - y + x cosec \; \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = 0{/tex}

On dividing both sides by x , we get 
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } - \frac { y } { x } + cosec \; \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } - cosec \; \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} 
which is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex}.
put , {tex}y=vx{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} 
{tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v x } { x } - cosec \; \left( \frac { v x } { x } \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = v - cosec \; v{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = - cosec \; v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d v } { cosec \; v } = \frac { - d x } { x }{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int \frac { d v } { cosec \; v } = \int - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \sin v d v = \int - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \cos v = - \log | x | + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \cos \frac { y } { x } = - \log | x | + c \quad \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \frac { y } { x } = ( \log | x | - C ){/tex}....(i)
Also, given that x = 1 and y = 0.
On putting above values in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos 0 = \log | 1 | - C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 = 0 - C \Rightarrow C = - 1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \cos \frac { y } { x } = \log | x | + 1{/tex} [from Eq. (i)]
This is required solution of given differential equation.

question_html: If y(x) is a solution of the differential equation {tex}\left( \frac { 2 + \sin x } { 1 + y } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = - \cos x{/tex} and y(0) = 1, then find the value of {tex}y \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex}.
question_text: If y(x) is a solution of the differential equation {tex}\left( \frac { 2 + \sin x } { 1 + y } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = - \cos x{/tex} and y(0) = 1, then find the value of {tex}y \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} .
explanation: We have, {tex}\left( \frac { 2 + \sin x } { 1 + y } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = - \cos x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 1 + y } d y = - \frac { \cos x } { 2 + \sin x } d x{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get, {tex}\int \frac { 1 } { 1 + y } d y = - \int \frac { \cos x } { 2 + \sin x } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | 1 + y | = - \log | 2 + \sin x | + \log C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c } { \text { put } 2 + \sin x = t \Rightarrow \cos x d x = d t } \\ { \text { then } \int \frac { \cos x } { 2 + \sin x } d x = \int \frac { d t } { t } = \log | t | + C } \\ { = \log | 2 + \sin x | + C } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | 1 + y | + \log | 2 + \sin x | = \log C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log ( | 1 + y | | 2 + \sin x | ) = \log C{/tex} {tex}[ \because \log m + \log n = \log m n ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( 1 + y ) ( 2 + \sin x ) = C{/tex} ...(i) Also, given that at x = 0, y = 1. On putting x = 0 and y = 1 in Eq. (i), we get, ( 1+ 1) (2+ sin 0) = C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 4 On putting C = 4 in Eq. (i), we get, (1 + y ) (2 + sin x) = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 + y = \frac { 4 } { 2 + \sin x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { 4 } { 2 + \sin x } - 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { 4 - 2 - \sin x } { 2 + \sin x } \Rightarrow y = \frac { 2 - \sin x } { 2 + \sin x }{/tex} Now, at {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } , y \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = \frac { 2 - \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } } { 2 + \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \quad \left[ \because \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } = 1 \right]{/tex}
solution: We have, {tex}\left( \frac { 2 + \sin x } { 1 + y } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = - \cos x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 1 + y } d y = - \frac { \cos x } { 2 + \sin x } d x{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get, {tex}\int \frac { 1 } { 1 + y } d y = - \int \frac { \cos x } { 2 + \sin x } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | 1 + y | = - \log | 2 + \sin x | + \log C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c } { \text { put } 2 + \sin x = t \Rightarrow \cos x d x = d t } \\ { \text { then } \int \frac { \cos x } { 2 + \sin x } d x = \int \frac { d t } { t } = \log | t | + C } \\ { = \log | 2 + \sin x | + C } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | 1 + y | + \log | 2 + \sin x | = \log C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log ( | 1 + y | | 2 + \sin x | ) = \log C{/tex} {tex}[ \because \log m + \log n = \log m n ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( 1 + y ) ( 2 + \sin x ) = C{/tex} ...(i) Also, given that at x = 0, y = 1. On putting x = 0 and y = 1 in Eq. (i), we get, ( 1+ 1) (2+ sin 0) = C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 4 On putting C = 4 in Eq. (i), we get, (1 + y ) (2 + sin x) = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 + y = \frac { 4 } { 2 + \sin x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { 4 } { 2 + \sin x } - 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { 4 - 2 - \sin x } { 2 + \sin x } \Rightarrow y = \frac { 2 - \sin x } { 2 + \sin x }{/tex} Now, at {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } , y \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = \frac { 2 - \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } } { 2 + \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \quad \left[ \because \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } = 1 \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
We have,
{tex}\left( \frac { 2 + \sin x } { 1 + y } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = - \cos x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 1 + y } d y = - \frac { \cos x } { 2 + \sin x } d x{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get,
{tex}\int \frac { 1 } { 1 + y } d y = - \int \frac { \cos x } { 2 + \sin x } d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | 1 + y | = - \log | 2 + \sin x | + \log C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c } { \text { put } 2 + \sin x = t \Rightarrow \cos x d x = d t } \\ { \text { then } \int \frac { \cos x } { 2 + \sin x } d x = \int \frac { d t } { t } = \log | t | + C } \\ { = \log | 2 + \sin x | + C } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | 1 + y | + \log | 2 + \sin x | = \log C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log ( | 1 + y | | 2 + \sin x | ) = \log C{/tex} {tex}[ \because \log m + \log n = \log m n ]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( 1 + y ) ( 2 + \sin x ) = C{/tex} ...(i)
Also, given that at x = 0, y = 1.
On putting x = 0 and y = 1 in Eq. (i), we get,
( 1+ 1) (2+ sin 0) = C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 4
On putting C = 4 in Eq. (i), we get,
(1 + y ) (2 + sin x) = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 + y = \frac { 4 } { 2 + \sin x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { 4 } { 2 + \sin x } - 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { 4 - 2 - \sin x } { 2 + \sin x } \Rightarrow y = \frac { 2 - \sin x } { 2 + \sin x }{/tex}
Now, at {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } , y \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = \frac { 2 - \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } } { 2 + \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad y \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \quad \left[ \because \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } = 1 \right]{/tex}

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x ( 2 \log | x | + 1 ) } { \sin y + y \cos y }{/tex}, given that {tex}y = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}, when x = 1.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x ( 2 \log | x | + 1 ) } { \sin y + y \cos y }{/tex} , given that {tex}y = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} , when x = 1.
explanation: We have, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x ( 2 \log | x | + 1 ) } { \sin y + y \cos y }{/tex} Therefore,on separating the variables, we get, (sin y + y cos y) dy = x(2 log IxI + 1)dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin y dy + y cos y dy = 2xlog |x| dx + x dx On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int {\sin } ydy + \int {\mathop y\limits_I } \mathop {\cos }\limits_{II} ydy{/tex} {tex} = 2\int {\mathop x\limits_{II} } \mathop {\log }\limits_I |x|dx + \int x dx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - \cos y + \left[ y \int \cos y d y - \int \left\{ \frac { d } { d y } ( y ) \int \cos y d y \right\} d y \right]{/tex} {tex}= 2 \left[ \log | x | \int x d x - \int \left\{ \frac { d } { d x } ( \log | x | ) \int x d x \right\} d x \right] + \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} [by using integration by parts] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \cos y + y \sin y - \int \sin y d y{/tex} {tex}= 2 \left[ \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \log | x | - \int \left\{ \frac { 1 } { x } \cdot \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right\} d x \right] + \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \cos y + y \sin y + \cos y{/tex} {tex}= x ^ { 2 } \log | x | - \int x d x + \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sin y = x ^ { 2 } \log | x | - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sin y = x ^ { 2 } \log | x | + C{/tex} Also, given that {tex}y = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} when x = 1. On putting {tex}y = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} and x = 1 in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\frac { \pi } { 2 } \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } \log ( 1 ) + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = \frac { \pi } { 2 } \quad \left[ \because \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } = 1 , \log 1 = 0 \right]{/tex} On substituting the value of C in Eq. (i), we get {tex}y \sin y = x ^ { 2 } \log | x | + \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} which is the required particular solution.
solution: We have, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x ( 2 \log | x | + 1 ) } { \sin y + y \cos y }{/tex} Therefore,on separating the variables, we get, (sin y + y cos y) dy = x(2 log IxI + 1)dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin y dy + y cos y dy = 2xlog |x| dx + x dx On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int {\sin } ydy + \int {\mathop y\limits_I } \mathop {\cos }\limits_{II} ydy{/tex} {tex} = 2\int {\mathop x\limits_{II} } \mathop {\log }\limits_I |x|dx + \int x dx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - \cos y + \left[ y \int \cos y d y - \int \left\{ \frac { d } { d y } ( y ) \int \cos y d y \right\} d y \right]{/tex} {tex}= 2 \left[ \log | x | \int x d x - \int \left\{ \frac { d } { d x } ( \log | x | ) \int x d x \right\} d x \right] + \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} [by using integration by parts] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \cos y + y \sin y - \int \sin y d y{/tex} {tex}= 2 \left[ \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \log | x | - \int \left\{ \frac { 1 } { x } \cdot \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right\} d x \right] + \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \cos y + y \sin y + \cos y{/tex} {tex}= x ^ { 2 } \log | x | - \int x d x + \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sin y = x ^ { 2 } \log | x | - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sin y = x ^ { 2 } \log | x | + C{/tex} Also, given that {tex}y = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} when x = 1. On putting {tex}y = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} and x = 1 in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\frac { \pi } { 2 } \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } \log ( 1 ) + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = \frac { \pi } { 2 } \quad \left[ \because \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } = 1 , \log 1 = 0 \right]{/tex} On substituting the value of C in Eq. (i), we get {tex}y \sin y = x ^ { 2 } \log | x | + \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} which is the required particular solution.
solution_html:
We have,
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x ( 2 \log | x | + 1 ) } { \sin y + y \cos y }{/tex}
Therefore,on separating the variables, we get,
(sin y + y cos y) dy = x(2 log IxI + 1)dx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin y dy + y cos y dy = 2xlog |x| dx + x dx
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int {\sin } ydy + \int {\mathop y\limits_I } \mathop {\cos }\limits_{II} ydy{/tex}
{tex} = 2\int {\mathop x\limits_{II} } \mathop {\log }\limits_I |x|dx + \int x dx{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow - \cos y + \left[ y \int \cos y d y - \int \left\{ \frac { d } { d y } ( y ) \int \cos y d y \right\} d y \right]{/tex}
{tex}= 2 \left[ \log | x | \int x d x - \int \left\{ \frac { d } { d x } ( \log | x | ) \int x d x \right\} d x \right] + \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} [by using integration by parts]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \cos y + y \sin y - \int \sin y d y{/tex}
{tex}= 2 \left[ \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \log | x | - \int \left\{ \frac { 1 } { x } \cdot \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right\} d x \right] + \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \cos y + y \sin y + \cos y{/tex}
{tex}= x ^ { 2 } \log | x | - \int x d x + \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sin y = x ^ { 2 } \log | x | - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sin y = x ^ { 2 } \log | x | + C{/tex}
Also, given that {tex}y = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} when x = 1.
On putting {tex}y = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} and x = 1 in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}\frac { \pi } { 2 } \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } \log ( 1 ) + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = \frac { \pi } { 2 } \quad \left[ \because \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } = 1 , \log 1 = 0 \right]{/tex}
On substituting the value of C in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}y \sin y = x ^ { 2 } \log | x | + \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}
which is the required particular solution.

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}e ^ { x } \sqrt { 1 - y ^ { 2 } } d x + \frac { y } { x } d y = 0{/tex}, given that y = 1, when x = 0.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}e ^ { x } \sqrt { 1 - y ^ { 2 } } d x + \frac { y } { x } d y = 0{/tex} , given that y = 1, when x = 0.
explanation: We have, {tex}e ^ { x } \sqrt { 1 - y ^ { 2 } } d x + \frac { y } { x } d y = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad e ^ { x } \sqrt { 1 - y ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { - y } { x } d y{/tex} Therefore,on separating the variables, we get, {tex}\frac { - y } { \sqrt { 1 - y ^ { 2 } } } d y = x e ^ { x } d x{/tex} Therefore,on integrating both sides, we get, {tex}\int \frac { - y } { \sqrt { 1 - y ^ { 2 } } } d y = \int x e ^ { x } d x{/tex} On putting {tex}1 - y ^ { 2 } = t \Rightarrow - y d y = \frac { d t } { 2 }{/tex} in LHS, we get {tex}\int {\frac{1}{{2\sqrt t }}} dt = \int {\mathop x\limits_I } \mathop {{e^x}}\limits_{II} dx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } [ 2 \sqrt { t } ] = x \int e ^ { x } d x - \int \left[ \frac { d } { d x } ( x ) \int e ^ { x } d x \right] d x{/tex} [using integration by parts] {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { 1 - y ^ { 2 } } = x e ^ { x } - \int e ^ { x } d x \quad \left[ \text { put } t = 1 - y ^ { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { 1 - y ^ { 2 } } = x e ^ { x } - e ^ { x } + C{/tex} ...(i) Also, given that y = 1, when x = 0 On putting y = 1 and x = 0 in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\sqrt { 1 - 1 } = 0 - e ^ { 0 } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = 1 \quad \left[ \because e ^ { 0 } = 1 \right]{/tex} On substituting the value of C in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\sqrt { 1 - y ^ { 2 } } = x e ^ { x } - e ^ { x } + 1{/tex} which is the required particular solution of given differential equation.
solution: We have, {tex}e ^ { x } \sqrt { 1 - y ^ { 2 } } d x + \frac { y } { x } d y = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad e ^ { x } \sqrt { 1 - y ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { - y } { x } d y{/tex} Therefore,on separating the variables, we get, {tex}\frac { - y } { \sqrt { 1 - y ^ { 2 } } } d y = x e ^ { x } d x{/tex} Therefore,on integrating both sides, we get, {tex}\int \frac { - y } { \sqrt { 1 - y ^ { 2 } } } d y = \int x e ^ { x } d x{/tex} On putting {tex}1 - y ^ { 2 } = t \Rightarrow - y d y = \frac { d t } { 2 }{/tex} in LHS, we get {tex}\int {\frac{1}{{2\sqrt t }}} dt = \int {\mathop x\limits_I } \mathop {{e^x}}\limits_{II} dx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } [ 2 \sqrt { t } ] = x \int e ^ { x } d x - \int \left[ \frac { d } { d x } ( x ) \int e ^ { x } d x \right] d x{/tex} [using integration by parts] {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { 1 - y ^ { 2 } } = x e ^ { x } - \int e ^ { x } d x \quad \left[ \text { put } t = 1 - y ^ { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { 1 - y ^ { 2 } } = x e ^ { x } - e ^ { x } + C{/tex} ...(i) Also, given that y = 1, when x = 0 On putting y = 1 and x = 0 in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\sqrt { 1 - 1 } = 0 - e ^ { 0 } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = 1 \quad \left[ \because e ^ { 0 } = 1 \right]{/tex} On substituting the value of C in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\sqrt { 1 - y ^ { 2 } } = x e ^ { x } - e ^ { x } + 1{/tex} which is the required particular solution of given differential equation.
solution_html:
We have,

{tex}e ^ { x } \sqrt { 1 - y ^ { 2 } } d x + \frac { y } { x } d y = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad e ^ { x } \sqrt { 1 - y ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { - y } { x } d y{/tex}
Therefore,on separating the variables, we get,
{tex}\frac { - y } { \sqrt { 1 - y ^ { 2 } } } d y = x e ^ { x } d x{/tex}
Therefore,on integrating both sides, we get,
{tex}\int \frac { - y } { \sqrt { 1 - y ^ { 2 } } } d y = \int x e ^ { x } d x{/tex}
On putting {tex}1 - y ^ { 2 } = t \Rightarrow - y d y = \frac { d t } { 2 }{/tex} in LHS, we get
{tex}\int {\frac{1}{{2\sqrt t }}} dt = \int {\mathop x\limits_I } \mathop {{e^x}}\limits_{II} dx{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } [ 2 \sqrt { t } ] = x \int e ^ { x } d x - \int \left[ \frac { d } { d x } ( x ) \int e ^ { x } d x \right] d x{/tex} [using integration by parts]
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { 1 - y ^ { 2 } } = x e ^ { x } - \int e ^ { x } d x \quad \left[ \text { put } t = 1 - y ^ { 2 } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { 1 - y ^ { 2 } } = x e ^ { x } - e ^ { x } + C{/tex} ...(i)
Also, given that y = 1, when x = 0
On putting y = 1 and x = 0 in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}\sqrt { 1 - 1 } = 0 - e ^ { 0 } + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = 1 \quad \left[ \because e ^ { 0 } = 1 \right]{/tex}
On substituting the value of C in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}\sqrt { 1 - y ^ { 2 } } = x e ^ { x } - e ^ { x } + 1{/tex}
which is the required particular solution of given differential equation.

question_html: Solve the following differential equation {tex} {cosec} \; x \log | y | \frac { d y } { d x } + x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex}.
question_text: Solve the following differential equation {tex} {cosec} \; x \log | y | \frac { d y } { d x } + x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex} .
explanation: According to given question, Given differential equation is, {tex}cosec \ x \log | y | \frac { d y } { d x } + x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\Rightarrow \ cosec x \log | y | \frac { d y } { d x } = - x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\frac { \log | y | } { y ^ { 2 } } d y = \frac { - x ^ { 2 } } { \ cosec x } d x{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac { \log | y | } { y ^ { 2 } } d y = - \int \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { \ cosec x } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { 1 } = - I _ { 2 }{/tex} ...(ii) where, {tex}I _ { 1 } = \int \frac { \log | y | } { y ^ { 2 } } d y{/tex} and {tex}I _ { 2 } = \int \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { cosec \; x } d x {/tex} {tex}= \int x ^ { 2 } \sin x d x{/tex} Consider {tex}I _ { 1 } = \int \frac { \log | y | } { y ^ { 2 } } d y{/tex} Put {tex}\log y = t \Rightarrow y = e ^ { t }{/tex} , then {tex}\frac { d y } { y } = d t{/tex} By using integration by parts , we get {tex}\therefore {I_1} = \int {\mathop t\limits_I } \mathop {{e^{ - t}}}\limits_{II} dt = t\int {{e^{ - t}}} dt - \int {\left[ {\frac{d}{{dt}}(t)\int {{e^{ - t}}} dt} \right]} dt{/tex} {tex}= - t e ^ { - t } - \int \left( - e ^ { - t } \right) d t{/tex} {tex}= - t e ^ { - t } + \int e ^ { - t } d t = - t e ^ { - t } - e ^ { - t } + C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}= - \frac { \log | y | } { y } - \frac { 1 } { y } + C _ { 1 }{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}\left[ \because t = \log | y | \text { and } e ^ { - t } = \frac { 1 } { y } \right]{/tex} and {tex}{I_2} = \int {\mathop {{x^2}}\limits_I } \mathop {\sin }\limits_{II} xdx{/tex} {tex}= x ^ { 2 } \int \sin x d x - \int \left[ \frac { d } { d x } \left( x ^ { 2 } \right) \int \sin x d x \right] d x{/tex} [using integration by parts] {tex}= x ^ { 2 } ( - \cos x ) - \int [ 2 x ( - \cos x ) ] d x{/tex} {tex} = - {x^2}\cos x + 2\int {\mathop x\limits_I } \mathop {\cos }\limits_{II} xdx{/tex} {tex}= - x ^ { 2 } \cos x + 2 \left[ x \int \cos x d x\right.{/tex} {tex}- \int \left\{ \frac { d } { d x } ( x ) \int \cos x d x \right\} d x +C_2{/tex} {tex}= - x ^ { 2 } \cos x + 2 \left[ x \sin x - \int \sin x d x \right] +C_2{/tex} {tex}= - x ^ { 2 } \cos x + 2 x \sin x + 2 \cos x + C _ { 2 }{/tex} ...(iv) On putting the values of I 1 and I 2 from Eqs. (iii) and (iv) in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}- \frac { \log | y | } { y } - \frac { 1 } { y } + C _ { 1 } = x ^ { 2 } \cos x - 2 x \sin x - 2 \cos x +-C_2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \frac { ( 1 + \log | y | ) } { y } = x ^ { 2 } \cos x - 2 x \sin x - 2 \cos x - C _ { 2 } - C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \frac { ( 1 + \log | y | ) } { y } = x ^ { 2 } \cos x - 2 x \sin x - 2 \cos x + C{/tex} where, C = -C 2 - C 1 which is the required solution of given differential equation.
solution: According to given question, Given differential equation is, {tex}cosec \ x \log | y | \frac { d y } { d x } + x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\Rightarrow \ cosec x \log | y | \frac { d y } { d x } = - x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\frac { \log | y | } { y ^ { 2 } } d y = \frac { - x ^ { 2 } } { \ cosec x } d x{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac { \log | y | } { y ^ { 2 } } d y = - \int \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { \ cosec x } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { 1 } = - I _ { 2 }{/tex} ...(ii) where, {tex}I _ { 1 } = \int \frac { \log | y | } { y ^ { 2 } } d y{/tex} and {tex}I _ { 2 } = \int \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { cosec \; x } d x {/tex} {tex}= \int x ^ { 2 } \sin x d x{/tex} Consider {tex}I _ { 1 } = \int \frac { \log | y | } { y ^ { 2 } } d y{/tex} Put {tex}\log y = t \Rightarrow y = e ^ { t }{/tex} , then {tex}\frac { d y } { y } = d t{/tex} By using integration by parts , we get {tex}\therefore {I_1} = \int {\mathop t\limits_I } \mathop {{e^{ - t}}}\limits_{II} dt = t\int {{e^{ - t}}} dt - \int {\left[ {\frac{d}{{dt}}(t)\int {{e^{ - t}}} dt} \right]} dt{/tex} {tex}= - t e ^ { - t } - \int \left( - e ^ { - t } \right) d t{/tex} {tex}= - t e ^ { - t } + \int e ^ { - t } d t = - t e ^ { - t } - e ^ { - t } + C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}= - \frac { \log | y | } { y } - \frac { 1 } { y } + C _ { 1 }{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}\left[ \because t = \log | y | \text { and } e ^ { - t } = \frac { 1 } { y } \right]{/tex} and {tex}{I_2} = \int {\mathop {{x^2}}\limits_I } \mathop {\sin }\limits_{II} xdx{/tex} {tex}= x ^ { 2 } \int \sin x d x - \int \left[ \frac { d } { d x } \left( x ^ { 2 } \right) \int \sin x d x \right] d x{/tex} [using integration by parts] {tex}= x ^ { 2 } ( - \cos x ) - \int [ 2 x ( - \cos x ) ] d x{/tex} {tex} = - {x^2}\cos x + 2\int {\mathop x\limits_I } \mathop {\cos }\limits_{II} xdx{/tex} {tex}= - x ^ { 2 } \cos x + 2 \left[ x \int \cos x d x\right.{/tex} {tex}- \int \left\{ \frac { d } { d x } ( x ) \int \cos x d x \right\} d x +C_2{/tex} {tex}= - x ^ { 2 } \cos x + 2 \left[ x \sin x - \int \sin x d x \right] +C_2{/tex} {tex}= - x ^ { 2 } \cos x + 2 x \sin x + 2 \cos x + C _ { 2 }{/tex} ...(iv) On putting the values of I 1 and I 2 from Eqs. (iii) and (iv) in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}- \frac { \log | y | } { y } - \frac { 1 } { y } + C _ { 1 } = x ^ { 2 } \cos x - 2 x \sin x - 2 \cos x +-C_2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \frac { ( 1 + \log | y | ) } { y } = x ^ { 2 } \cos x - 2 x \sin x - 2 \cos x - C _ { 2 } - C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \frac { ( 1 + \log | y | ) } { y } = x ^ { 2 } \cos x - 2 x \sin x - 2 \cos x + C{/tex} where, C = -C 2 - C 1 which is the required solution of given differential equation.
solution_html:
According to given question,

Given differential equation is,
{tex}cosec \ x \log | y | \frac { d y } { d x } + x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow \ cosec x \log | y | \frac { d y } { d x } = - x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\frac { \log | y | } { y ^ { 2 } } d y = \frac { - x ^ { 2 } } { \ cosec x } d x{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int \frac { \log | y | } { y ^ { 2 } } d y = - \int \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { \ cosec x } d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { 1 } = - I _ { 2 }{/tex} ...(ii)
where, {tex}I _ { 1 } = \int \frac { \log | y | } { y ^ { 2 } } d y{/tex}
and {tex}I _ { 2 } = \int \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { cosec \; x } d x {/tex}

{tex}= \int x ^ { 2 } \sin x d x{/tex}
Consider {tex}I _ { 1 } = \int \frac { \log | y | } { y ^ { 2 } } d y{/tex}
Put {tex}\log y = t \Rightarrow y = e ^ { t }{/tex} , then {tex}\frac { d y } { y } = d t{/tex}

By using integration by parts , we get
{tex}\therefore {I_1} = \int {\mathop t\limits_I } \mathop {{e^{ - t}}}\limits_{II} dt = t\int {{e^{ - t}}} dt - \int {\left[ {\frac{d}{{dt}}(t)\int {{e^{ - t}}} dt} \right]} dt{/tex}
{tex}= - t e ^ { - t } - \int \left( - e ^ { - t } \right) d t{/tex}
{tex}= - t e ^ { - t } + \int e ^ { - t } d t = - t e ^ { - t } - e ^ { - t } + C _ { 1 }{/tex}
{tex}= - \frac { \log | y | } { y } - \frac { 1 } { y } + C _ { 1 }{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}\left[ \because t = \log | y | \text { and } e ^ { - t } = \frac { 1 } { y } \right]{/tex}
and {tex}{I_2} = \int {\mathop {{x^2}}\limits_I } \mathop {\sin }\limits_{II} xdx{/tex}
{tex}= x ^ { 2 } \int \sin x d x - \int \left[ \frac { d } { d x } \left( x ^ { 2 } \right) \int \sin x d x \right] d x{/tex}[using integration by parts]
{tex}= x ^ { 2 } ( - \cos x ) - \int [ 2 x ( - \cos x ) ] d x{/tex}
{tex} = - {x^2}\cos x + 2\int {\mathop x\limits_I } \mathop {\cos }\limits_{II} xdx{/tex}
{tex}= - x ^ { 2 } \cos x + 2 \left[ x \int \cos x d x\right.{/tex} {tex}- \int \left\{ \frac { d } { d x } ( x ) \int \cos x d x \right\} d x +C_2{/tex}
{tex}= - x ^ { 2 } \cos x + 2 \left[ x \sin x - \int \sin x d x \right] +C_2{/tex}
{tex}= - x ^ { 2 } \cos x + 2 x \sin x + 2 \cos x + C _ { 2 }{/tex} ...(iv)
On putting the values of I1 and I2 from Eqs. (iii) and (iv) in Eq. (ii), we get
{tex}- \frac { \log | y | } { y } - \frac { 1 } { y } + C _ { 1 } = x ^ { 2 } \cos x - 2 x \sin x - 2 \cos x +-C_2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \frac { ( 1 + \log | y | ) } { y } = x ^ { 2 } \cos x - 2 x \sin x - 2 \cos x - C _ { 2 } - C _ { 1 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \frac { ( 1 + \log | y | ) } { y } = x ^ { 2 } \cos x - 2 x \sin x - 2 \cos x + C{/tex}
where, C = -C2 - C1
which is the required solution of given differential equation.

question_html: Show that the differential equation {tex}x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = y \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) + x{/tex} is homogeneous and solve it.
question_text: Show that the differential equation {tex}x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = y \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) + x{/tex} is homogeneous and solve it.
explanation: Given differential equation is {tex}x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = y \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) + x{/tex} which is a homogeneous differential equation because {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} . put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \cos v \left[ v + x \frac { d v } { d x } \right] = v x \cos v + x{/tex} {tex}v x \cos v + x ^ { 2 } \cos v \frac { d v } { d x } = v x \cos v + x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x ^ { 2 } \cos v \frac { d v } { d x } = x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos v d v = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \cos v d v = \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin v = \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = \log | x | + c \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} This is the required solution of given differential equation.
solution: Given differential equation is {tex}x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = y \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) + x{/tex} which is a homogeneous differential equation because {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} . put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \cos v \left[ v + x \frac { d v } { d x } \right] = v x \cos v + x{/tex} {tex}v x \cos v + x ^ { 2 } \cos v \frac { d v } { d x } = v x \cos v + x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x ^ { 2 } \cos v \frac { d v } { d x } = x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos v d v = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \cos v d v = \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin v = \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = \log | x | + c \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} This is the required solution of given differential equation.
solution_html:
Given differential equation is
{tex}x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = y \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) + x{/tex}
which is a homogeneous differential equation because {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex}.
put y = vx
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \cos v \left[ v + x \frac { d v } { d x } \right] = v x \cos v + x{/tex}
{tex}v x \cos v + x ^ { 2 } \cos v \frac { d v } { d x } = v x \cos v + x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x ^ { 2 } \cos v \frac { d v } { d x } = x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos v d v = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int \cos v d v = \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin v = \log | x | + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = \log | x | + c \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex}
This is the required solution of given differential equation.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = 1 + x + y + x y{/tex}, given that y = 0 when x = 1.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = 1 + x + y + x y{/tex} , given that y = 0 when x = 1.
explanation: According to the question, Given differential equation is {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = 1 + x + y + x y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = 1 ( 1 + x ) + y ( 1 + x ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = ( 1 + x ) ( 1 + y ){/tex} {tex}\frac { 1 } { ( 1 + y ) } d y = ( 1 + x ) d x{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac { 1 } { 1 + y } d y = \int ( 1 + x ) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log | 1 + y | = x + \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + C{/tex} ...(i) Also, given that y = 0, when x = 1. On substituting x = 1, y = 0 in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\log | 1 + 0 | = 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } + C \Rightarrow C = - \frac { 3 } { 2 } [ \because \log 1 = 0 ]{/tex} Now, on substituting the value of C in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\log | 1 + y | = x + \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex} which is the required particular solution of given differential equation.
solution: According to the question, Given differential equation is {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = 1 + x + y + x y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = 1 ( 1 + x ) + y ( 1 + x ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = ( 1 + x ) ( 1 + y ){/tex} {tex}\frac { 1 } { ( 1 + y ) } d y = ( 1 + x ) d x{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac { 1 } { 1 + y } d y = \int ( 1 + x ) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log | 1 + y | = x + \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + C{/tex} ...(i) Also, given that y = 0, when x = 1. On substituting x = 1, y = 0 in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\log | 1 + 0 | = 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } + C \Rightarrow C = - \frac { 3 } { 2 } [ \because \log 1 = 0 ]{/tex} Now, on substituting the value of C in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\log | 1 + y | = x + \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex} which is the required particular solution of given differential equation.
solution_html:
According to the question,

Given differential equation is
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = 1 + x + y + x y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = 1 ( 1 + x ) + y ( 1 + x ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = ( 1 + x ) ( 1 + y ){/tex}
{tex}\frac { 1 } { ( 1 + y ) } d y = ( 1 + x ) d x{/tex} 
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int \frac { 1 } { 1 + y } d y = \int ( 1 + x ) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log | 1 + y | = x + \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + C{/tex} ...(i)
Also, given that y = 0, when x = 1.
On substituting x = 1, y = 0 in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}\log | 1 + 0 | = 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } + C \Rightarrow C = - \frac { 3 } { 2 } [ \because \log 1 = 0 ]{/tex}
Now, on substituting the value of C in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}\log | 1 + y | = x + \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex}
which is the required particular solution of given differential equation.

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation x(1 + y2)dx - y(1 + x2)dy = 0, given that y = 1, when x = 0.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation x(1 + y 2 )dx - y(1 + x 2 )dy = 0, given that y = 1, when x = 0.
explanation: Given differential equation - x(1 + y 2 ) dx - y(1 + x 2 ) dy = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} x(1 + y 2 ) dx = y(1 + x 2 )dy Therefore,on separating the variables, we get, {tex} \frac { y } { \left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right) } d y = \frac { x } { \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } d x{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex} \int \frac { y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } d y = \int \frac { x } { \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } d x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \left| 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right| = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \left| 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right| + C{/tex} ...(i) {tex} \left[ \begin{array} { l } { \text { put } 1 + y ^ { 2 } = u \Rightarrow 2 y d y = d u } \\ { \text { then } \int \frac { y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } d y = \int \frac { 1 } { 2 u } d u = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | u | } \\ { \text { and put } 1 + x ^ { 2 } = v \Rightarrow 2 x d x = d v } \\ { \text { then } \int \frac { x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { 1 } { v } d v = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | v | } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Also, given that y = 1, when x = 0. On substituting the values of x and y in Eq. (i), we get, {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | 1 + ( 1) ^ { 2 } \left| = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \right| 1 + ( 0 ) ^ { 2 } | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 2 = C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \therefore \log 1 = 0 \right]{/tex} On putting {tex}C = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 2{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \left| 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right| = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \left| 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right| + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right| = \log \left| 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right| + \log 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right| - \log \left| 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right| = \log 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| \frac { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \right| = \log 2{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log m - \log n = \log \frac { m } { n } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } = 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 + y ^ { 2 } = 2 + 2 x ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } - 1 = 0{/tex} which is the required particular solution of given differential equation.
solution: Given differential equation - x(1 + y 2 ) dx - y(1 + x 2 ) dy = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} x(1 + y 2 ) dx = y(1 + x 2 )dy Therefore,on separating the variables, we get, {tex} \frac { y } { \left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right) } d y = \frac { x } { \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } d x{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex} \int \frac { y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } d y = \int \frac { x } { \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } d x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \left| 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right| = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \left| 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right| + C{/tex} ...(i) {tex} \left[ \begin{array} { l } { \text { put } 1 + y ^ { 2 } = u \Rightarrow 2 y d y = d u } \\ { \text { then } \int \frac { y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } d y = \int \frac { 1 } { 2 u } d u = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | u | } \\ { \text { and put } 1 + x ^ { 2 } = v \Rightarrow 2 x d x = d v } \\ { \text { then } \int \frac { x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { 1 } { v } d v = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | v | } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Also, given that y = 1, when x = 0. On substituting the values of x and y in Eq. (i), we get, {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | 1 + ( 1) ^ { 2 } \left| = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \right| 1 + ( 0 ) ^ { 2 } | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 2 = C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \therefore \log 1 = 0 \right]{/tex} On putting {tex}C = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 2{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \left| 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right| = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \left| 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right| + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right| = \log \left| 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right| + \log 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right| - \log \left| 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right| = \log 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| \frac { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \right| = \log 2{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log m - \log n = \log \frac { m } { n } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } = 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 + y ^ { 2 } = 2 + 2 x ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } - 1 = 0{/tex} which is the required particular solution of given differential equation.
solution_html:
Given differential equation -

x(1 + y2) dx - y(1 + x2) dy = 0
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} x(1 + y2) dx = y(1 + x2)dy
Therefore,on separating the variables, we get,
{tex} \frac { y } { \left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right) } d y = \frac { x } { \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } d x{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex} \int \frac { y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } d y = \int \frac { x } { \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } d x{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \left| 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right| = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \left| 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right| + C{/tex} ...(i)
{tex} \left[ \begin{array} { l } { \text { put } 1 + y ^ { 2 } = u \Rightarrow 2 y d y = d u } \\ { \text { then } \int \frac { y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } d y = \int \frac { 1 } { 2 u } d u = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | u | } \\ { \text { and put } 1 + x ^ { 2 } = v \Rightarrow 2 x d x = d v } \\ { \text { then } \int \frac { x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { 1 } { v } d v = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | v | } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
Also, given that y = 1, when x = 0.
On substituting the values of x and y in Eq. (i),
we get,
{tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | 1 + ( 1) ^ { 2 } \left| = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \right| 1 + ( 0 ) ^ { 2 } | + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 2 = C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \therefore \log 1 = 0 \right]{/tex}
On putting {tex}C = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 2{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \left| 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right| = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \left| 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right| + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right| = \log \left| 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right| + \log 2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right| - \log \left| 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right| = \log 2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| \frac { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \right| = \log 2{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log m - \log n = \log \frac { m } { n } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } = 2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 + y ^ { 2 } = 2 + 2 x ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ^ { 2 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } - 1 = 0{/tex}
which is the required particular solution of given differential equation.
