question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex} x \log | x | \frac { d y } { d x } + y = \frac { 2 } { x } \log | x |{/tex}.
question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex} x \log | x | \frac { d y } { d x } + y = \frac { 2 } { x } \log | x |{/tex} .
explanation: We have to solve, {tex} x \log | x | \frac { d y } { d x } + y = \frac { 2 } { x } \log | x |{/tex} On dividing both sides by x log x, we get {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + \frac { y } { x \log | x | } = \frac { 2 } { x ^ { 2 } } \frac { \log | x | } { \log | x | } = \frac { 2 } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of first order, which is of the form of {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} , Here, {tex} P = \frac { 1 } { x \log | x | }{/tex} and {tex} Q = \frac { 2 } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} We know that , {tex} \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int \frac { 1 } { x \log | x | }d x } {/tex} {tex} { \text { put } \log | x | = t \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { x } d x = d t } {/tex} {tex}{ \therefore IF = \int \frac { 1 } { t } d t = \log | t | = \log | \log x | } {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { IF } = \log | x | \quad \left[ \because e ^ { \mathrm { log } x } = x \right]{/tex} Now, solution of above equation is given by {tex} y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex} {tex} y\log |x| = \int {\mathop {\frac{2}{{{x^2}}}}\limits_{II} } \mathop {\log }\limits_I |x|dx+C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \log | x | = 2 \left[ \log | x | \int \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } d x\right.{/tex} {tex} \left. { - \int {\left( {\frac{d}{{dx}}(\log |x|) \cdot \int {\frac{1}{{{x^2}}}} dx} \right)} dx} \right]+C{/tex} [using integration by parts] {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \log | x | = 2 \left[ \log | x | \cdot \left( - \frac { 1 } { x } \right) - \int \frac { 1 } { x } \cdot \left( - \frac { 1 } { x } \right) d x\right.+C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \log | x | = 2 \left[ - \frac { 1 } { x } \log | x | + \int \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } d x \right]+C{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad y \log | x | = - \frac { 2 } { x } \log | x | - \frac { 2 } { x } + C{/tex}
solution: We have to solve, {tex} x \log | x | \frac { d y } { d x } + y = \frac { 2 } { x } \log | x |{/tex} On dividing both sides by x log x, we get {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + \frac { y } { x \log | x | } = \frac { 2 } { x ^ { 2 } } \frac { \log | x | } { \log | x | } = \frac { 2 } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of first order, which is of the form of {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} , Here, {tex} P = \frac { 1 } { x \log | x | }{/tex} and {tex} Q = \frac { 2 } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} We know that , {tex} \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int \frac { 1 } { x \log | x | }d x } {/tex} {tex} { \text { put } \log | x | = t \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { x } d x = d t } {/tex} {tex}{ \therefore IF = \int \frac { 1 } { t } d t = \log | t | = \log | \log x | } {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { IF } = \log | x | \quad \left[ \because e ^ { \mathrm { log } x } = x \right]{/tex} Now, solution of above equation is given by {tex} y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex} {tex} y\log |x| = \int {\mathop {\frac{2}{{{x^2}}}}\limits_{II} } \mathop {\log }\limits_I |x|dx+C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \log | x | = 2 \left[ \log | x | \int \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } d x\right.{/tex} {tex} \left. { - \int {\left( {\frac{d}{{dx}}(\log |x|) \cdot \int {\frac{1}{{{x^2}}}} dx} \right)} dx} \right]+C{/tex} [using integration by parts] {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \log | x | = 2 \left[ \log | x | \cdot \left( - \frac { 1 } { x } \right) - \int \frac { 1 } { x } \cdot \left( - \frac { 1 } { x } \right) d x\right.+C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \log | x | = 2 \left[ - \frac { 1 } { x } \log | x | + \int \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } d x \right]+C{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad y \log | x | = - \frac { 2 } { x } \log | x | - \frac { 2 } { x } + C{/tex}
solution_html:
We have to solve,

{tex} x \log | x | \frac { d y } { d x } + y = \frac { 2 } { x } \log | x |{/tex}
On dividing both sides by x log x, we get
{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + \frac { y } { x \log | x | } = \frac { 2 } { x ^ { 2 } } \frac { \log | x | } { \log | x | } = \frac { 2 } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
which is a linear differential equation of first order, which is of the form of {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex},
Here, {tex} P = \frac { 1 } { x \log | x | }{/tex} and {tex} Q = \frac { 2 } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}

We know that , 
{tex} \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int \frac { 1 } { x \log | x | }d x } {/tex}
{tex} { \text { put } \log | x | = t \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { x } d x = d t } {/tex}

{tex}{ \therefore IF = \int \frac { 1 } { t } d t = \log | t | = \log | \log x | } {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { IF } = \log | x | \quad \left[ \because e ^ { \mathrm { log } x } = x \right]{/tex}
Now, solution of above equation is given by
{tex} y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex}

{tex} y\log |x| = \int {\mathop {\frac{2}{{{x^2}}}}\limits_{II} } \mathop {\log }\limits_I |x|dx+C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \log | x | = 2 \left[ \log | x | \int \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } d x\right.{/tex} {tex} \left. { - \int {\left( {\frac{d}{{dx}}(\log |x|) \cdot \int {\frac{1}{{{x^2}}}} dx} \right)} dx} \right]+C{/tex}   [using integration by parts]
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \log | x | = 2 \left[ \log | x | \cdot \left( - \frac { 1 } { x } \right) - \int \frac { 1 } { x } \cdot \left( - \frac { 1 } { x } \right) d x\right.+C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \log | x | = 2 \left[ - \frac { 1 } { x } \log | x | + \int \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } d x \right]+C{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad y \log | x | = - \frac { 2 } { x } \log | x | - \frac { 2 } { x } + C{/tex}

question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \cot x = 2 \cos x{/tex}, given that y = 0, when {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}.
question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \cot x = 2 \cos x{/tex} , given that y = 0, when {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} .
explanation: Given: {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \cot x = 2 \cos x{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} Here, p = cot x and Q = 2 cos x {tex} \therefore \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int \cot x d x } = e ^ { \log | \sin x | } \Rightarrow \mathrm { IF } = \sin x{/tex} The general solution is given by {tex} y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( \mathrm { IF } \times Q ) d x + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \sin x = \int 2 \sin x \cos x d x + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \sin x = \int \sin 2 x d x + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \sin x = - \frac { \cos 2 x } { 2 } + C{/tex} ...(i) Also, given that y = 0, when {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} . On putting {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get, {tex}0 \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } = - \frac { \cos \left( 2 \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) } { 2 } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow C - \frac { \cos \pi } { 2 } = 0 \Rightarrow C + \frac { 1 } { 2 } = 0 \quad [ \because \cos \pi = - 1 ]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad C = - \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} On putting the value of C in Eq. (i). we get {tex}y \sin x = - \frac { \cos 2 x } { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore 2 y \sin x + \cos 2 x + 1 = 0{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution: Given: {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \cot x = 2 \cos x{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} Here, p = cot x and Q = 2 cos x {tex} \therefore \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int \cot x d x } = e ^ { \log | \sin x | } \Rightarrow \mathrm { IF } = \sin x{/tex} The general solution is given by {tex} y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( \mathrm { IF } \times Q ) d x + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \sin x = \int 2 \sin x \cos x d x + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \sin x = \int \sin 2 x d x + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \sin x = - \frac { \cos 2 x } { 2 } + C{/tex} ...(i) Also, given that y = 0, when {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} . On putting {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get, {tex}0 \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } = - \frac { \cos \left( 2 \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) } { 2 } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow C - \frac { \cos \pi } { 2 } = 0 \Rightarrow C + \frac { 1 } { 2 } = 0 \quad [ \because \cos \pi = - 1 ]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad C = - \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} On putting the value of C in Eq. (i). we get {tex}y \sin x = - \frac { \cos 2 x } { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore 2 y \sin x + \cos 2 x + 1 = 0{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution_html:
Given:

{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \cot x = 2 \cos x{/tex}
which is a linear differential equation of the form
{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex}
Here, p = cot x and Q = 2 cos x
{tex} \therefore \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int \cot x d x } = e ^ { \log | \sin x | } \Rightarrow \mathrm { IF } = \sin x{/tex}
The general solution is given by
{tex} y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( \mathrm { IF } \times Q ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \sin x = \int 2 \sin x \cos x d x + C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \sin x = \int \sin 2 x d x + C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \sin x = - \frac { \cos 2 x } { 2 } + C{/tex} ...(i)
Also, given that y = 0, when {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}.
On putting {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} in  Eq. (i), we get,
{tex}0 \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } = - \frac { \cos \left( 2 \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) } { 2 } + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow C - \frac { \cos \pi } { 2 } = 0 \Rightarrow C + \frac { 1 } { 2 } = 0 \quad [ \because \cos \pi = - 1 ]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad C = - \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
On putting the value of C in Eq. (i). we get
{tex}y \sin x = - \frac { \cos 2 x } { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore 2 y \sin x + \cos 2 x + 1 = 0{/tex}
which is the required solution.

question_html: Solve the differential equation (x2 - yx2)dy + (y2 + x2y2)dx = 0, given that y = 1, when x = 1.
question_text: Solve the differential equation (x 2 - yx 2 )dy + (y 2 + x 2 y 2 )dx = 0, given that y = 1, when x = 1.
explanation: Given differential equation, {tex}\left( x ^ { 2 } - y x ^ { 2 } \right) d y + \left( y ^ { 2 } + x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 } \right) d x = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x ^ { 2 } ( 1 - y ) d y + y ^ { 2 } \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) d x = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - x ^ { 2 } ( 1 - y ) d y = y ^ { 2 } \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x ^ { 2 } ( y - 1 ) d y = y ^ { 2 } \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { y - 1 } { y ^ { 2 } } d y = \frac { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} On integration both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac { y - 1 } { y ^ { 2 } } d y = \int \frac { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac { 1 } { y } d y - \int \frac { 1 } { y ^ { 2 } } d y = \int \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } d x + \int 1 d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | y | + \frac { 1 } { y } = \frac { - 1 } { x } + x + C{/tex} ...(i) Also, given that y = 1, when x = 1 On putting y = 1 and x = 1 in Eq. (i), we get, {tex}\log | 1 | + 1 = - 1 + 1 + C \Rightarrow C = 1{/tex} On putting the value of C in Eq.(i), we get, {tex} \log | y | + \frac { 1 } { y } = \frac { - 1 } { x } + x + 1{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution: Given differential equation, {tex}\left( x ^ { 2 } - y x ^ { 2 } \right) d y + \left( y ^ { 2 } + x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 } \right) d x = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x ^ { 2 } ( 1 - y ) d y + y ^ { 2 } \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) d x = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - x ^ { 2 } ( 1 - y ) d y = y ^ { 2 } \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x ^ { 2 } ( y - 1 ) d y = y ^ { 2 } \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { y - 1 } { y ^ { 2 } } d y = \frac { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} On integration both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac { y - 1 } { y ^ { 2 } } d y = \int \frac { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac { 1 } { y } d y - \int \frac { 1 } { y ^ { 2 } } d y = \int \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } d x + \int 1 d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | y | + \frac { 1 } { y } = \frac { - 1 } { x } + x + C{/tex} ...(i) Also, given that y = 1, when x = 1 On putting y = 1 and x = 1 in Eq. (i), we get, {tex}\log | 1 | + 1 = - 1 + 1 + C \Rightarrow C = 1{/tex} On putting the value of C in Eq.(i), we get, {tex} \log | y | + \frac { 1 } { y } = \frac { - 1 } { x } + x + 1{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution_html:
Given differential equation,
{tex}\left( x ^ { 2 } - y x ^ { 2 } \right) d y + \left( y ^ { 2 } + x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 } \right) d x = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x ^ { 2 } ( 1 - y ) d y + y ^ { 2 } \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) d x = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad - x ^ { 2 } ( 1 - y ) d y = y ^ { 2 } \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x ^ { 2 } ( y - 1 ) d y = y ^ { 2 } \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { y - 1 } { y ^ { 2 } } d y = \frac { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
On integration both sides, we get
{tex}\int \frac { y - 1 } { y ^ { 2 } } d y = \int \frac { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac { 1 } { y } d y - \int \frac { 1 } { y ^ { 2 } } d y = \int \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } d x + \int 1 d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | y | + \frac { 1 } { y } = \frac { - 1 } { x } + x + C{/tex} ...(i)
Also, given that y = 1, when x = 1
On putting y = 1 and x = 1 in Eq. (i), we get,
{tex}\log | 1 | + 1 = - 1 + 1 + C \Rightarrow C = 1{/tex}
On putting the value of C in Eq.(i), we get,
{tex} \log | y | + \frac { 1 } { y } = \frac { - 1 } { x } + x + 1{/tex}
which is the required solution.

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential {tex} \log \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = 3 x + 4 y{/tex} given that y = 0 when x = 0.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential {tex} \log \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = 3 x + 4 y{/tex} given that y = 0 when x = 0.
explanation: According to the question,We have to find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex} \log \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = 3 x + 4 y{/tex} , given that y = 0, when x = 0. Now,we have, {tex}\log \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = 3 x + 4 y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = e ^ { 3 x + 4 y }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log m = n \Rightarrow e ^ { n } = m \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = e ^ { 3 x } e ^ { 4 y }{/tex} On separating the variables, we get {tex}\frac { 1 } { e ^ { 4 y } } d y = e ^ { 3 x } d x{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int e ^ { - 4 y } d y = \int e ^ { 3 x } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { e ^ { - 4y } } { - 4 } = \frac { e ^ { 3 x } } { 3 } + C{/tex} ...(i) Also, given that y = 0, when x = 0. On putting y = 0 and x = 0 in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\frac { e ^ { - 4 ( 0 ) } } { - 4 } = \frac { e ^ { 3 ( 0 ) } } { 3 } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \frac { 1 } { 4 } = \frac { 1 } { 3 } + C \quad \left[ \because e ^ { - 0 } = e ^ { 0 } = 1 \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = - \frac { 1 } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad C = - \frac { 7 } { 12 }{/tex} On substituting the value of C in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\frac { e ^ { - 4 y } } { - 4 } = \frac { e ^ { 3 x } } { 3 } - \frac { 7 } { 12 } \Rightarrow 4 e ^ { 3 x } + 3 e ^ { - 4 y } - 7 = 0{/tex} which is the required particular solution of given differential equation.
solution: According to the question,We have to find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex} \log \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = 3 x + 4 y{/tex} , given that y = 0, when x = 0. Now,we have, {tex}\log \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = 3 x + 4 y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = e ^ { 3 x + 4 y }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log m = n \Rightarrow e ^ { n } = m \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = e ^ { 3 x } e ^ { 4 y }{/tex} On separating the variables, we get {tex}\frac { 1 } { e ^ { 4 y } } d y = e ^ { 3 x } d x{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int e ^ { - 4 y } d y = \int e ^ { 3 x } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { e ^ { - 4y } } { - 4 } = \frac { e ^ { 3 x } } { 3 } + C{/tex} ...(i) Also, given that y = 0, when x = 0. On putting y = 0 and x = 0 in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\frac { e ^ { - 4 ( 0 ) } } { - 4 } = \frac { e ^ { 3 ( 0 ) } } { 3 } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \frac { 1 } { 4 } = \frac { 1 } { 3 } + C \quad \left[ \because e ^ { - 0 } = e ^ { 0 } = 1 \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = - \frac { 1 } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad C = - \frac { 7 } { 12 }{/tex} On substituting the value of C in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\frac { e ^ { - 4 y } } { - 4 } = \frac { e ^ { 3 x } } { 3 } - \frac { 7 } { 12 } \Rightarrow 4 e ^ { 3 x } + 3 e ^ { - 4 y } - 7 = 0{/tex} which is the required particular solution of given differential equation.
solution_html:
According to the question,We have to find the particular solution of the differential equation
 {tex} \log \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = 3 x + 4 y{/tex} , given that y = 0, when x = 0.
Now,we have,
{tex}\log \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = 3 x + 4 y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = e ^ { 3 x + 4 y }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log m = n \Rightarrow e ^ { n } = m \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = e ^ { 3 x } e ^ { 4 y }{/tex}
On separating the variables, we get
{tex}\frac { 1 } { e ^ { 4 y } } d y = e ^ { 3 x } d x{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int e ^ { - 4 y } d y = \int e ^ { 3 x } d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { e ^ { - 4y } } { - 4 } = \frac { e ^ { 3 x } } { 3 } + C{/tex} ...(i)
Also, given that y = 0, when x = 0.
On putting y = 0 and x = 0 in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}\frac { e ^ { - 4 ( 0 ) } } { - 4 } = \frac { e ^ { 3 ( 0 ) } } { 3 } + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \frac { 1 } { 4 } = \frac { 1 } { 3 } + C \quad \left[ \because e ^ { - 0 } = e ^ { 0 } = 1 \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = - \frac { 1 } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad C = - \frac { 7 } { 12 }{/tex}
On substituting the value of C in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}\frac { e ^ { - 4 y } } { - 4 } = \frac { e ^ { 3 x } } { 3 } - \frac { 7 } { 12 } \Rightarrow 4 e ^ { 3 x } + 3 e ^ { - 4 y } - 7 = 0{/tex}
which is the required particular solution of given differential equation.

question_html: If y(x) is a solution of {tex}\left( {\frac{{2 + \sin x}}{{1 + y}}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \cos x{/tex} and y(0) = 1 then find the value of {tex}y\left( {\frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex}
question_text: If y(x) is a solution of {tex}\left( {\frac{{2 + \sin x}}{{1 + y}}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \cos x{/tex} and y(0) = 1 then find the value of {tex}y\left( {\frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex}
explanation: Given that, {tex}\left( {\frac{{2 + \sin x}}{{1 + y}}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \cos x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{1 + y}} = - \frac{{\cos x}}{{2 + \ sinx }}dx{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac{1}{1+y}dy= - \int \frac{cos x}{2 + \sin x}dx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left( {1 + y} \right) = - \log \left( {2 + \sin x} \right) + \log C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left( {1 + y} \right) + \log \left( {2 + \sin x} \right) = \log C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log \left( {1 + y} \right)\left( {2 + \sin x} \right) = \log C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( {1 + y} \right)\left( {2 + \sin x} \right) = C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1 + y = \frac{C}{{2 + \sin x}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y = \frac{C}{{2 + \sin x}} - 1{/tex} ..(i) When x = 0, y = 1 then {tex}1 = \frac{C}{2} - 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow C = 4{/tex} On putting C = 4 in Eq. (i), we get {tex}y = \frac{4}{{2 + \sin x}} - 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore y\left( {\frac{\pi }{2}} \right) = \frac{4}{{2 + \sin \frac{\pi }{2}}} - 1 = \frac{4}{{2 + 1}} - 1{/tex} {tex}= \frac{4}{3} - 1 = \frac{1}{3}{/tex}
solution: Given that, {tex}\left( {\frac{{2 + \sin x}}{{1 + y}}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \cos x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{1 + y}} = - \frac{{\cos x}}{{2 + \ sinx }}dx{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac{1}{1+y}dy= - \int \frac{cos x}{2 + \sin x}dx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left( {1 + y} \right) = - \log \left( {2 + \sin x} \right) + \log C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left( {1 + y} \right) + \log \left( {2 + \sin x} \right) = \log C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log \left( {1 + y} \right)\left( {2 + \sin x} \right) = \log C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( {1 + y} \right)\left( {2 + \sin x} \right) = C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1 + y = \frac{C}{{2 + \sin x}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y = \frac{C}{{2 + \sin x}} - 1{/tex} ..(i) When x = 0, y = 1 then {tex}1 = \frac{C}{2} - 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow C = 4{/tex} On putting C = 4 in Eq. (i), we get {tex}y = \frac{4}{{2 + \sin x}} - 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore y\left( {\frac{\pi }{2}} \right) = \frac{4}{{2 + \sin \frac{\pi }{2}}} - 1 = \frac{4}{{2 + 1}} - 1{/tex} {tex}= \frac{4}{3} - 1 = \frac{1}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that, {tex}\left( {\frac{{2 + \sin x}}{{1 + y}}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \cos x{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{1 + y}} = - \frac{{\cos x}}{{2 + \ sinx }}dx{/tex}

On integrating both sides, we get

{tex}\int \frac{1}{1+y}dy= - \int \frac{cos x}{2 + \sin x}dx{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left( {1 + y} \right) = - \log \left( {2 + \sin x} \right) + \log C{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left( {1 + y} \right) + \log \left( {2 + \sin x} \right) = \log C{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \log \left( {1 + y} \right)\left( {2 + \sin x} \right) = \log C{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left( {1 + y} \right)\left( {2 + \sin x} \right) = C{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow 1 + y = \frac{C}{{2 + \sin x}}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow y = \frac{C}{{2 + \sin x}} - 1{/tex} ..(i)

When x = 0, y = 1 then

{tex}1 = \frac{C}{2} - 1{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow C = 4{/tex}

On putting C = 4 in Eq. (i), we get

{tex}y = \frac{4}{{2 + \sin x}} - 1{/tex}

{tex}\therefore y\left( {\frac{\pi }{2}} \right) = \frac{4}{{2 + \sin \frac{\pi }{2}}} - 1 = \frac{4}{{2 + 1}} - 1{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{4}{3} - 1 = \frac{1}{3}{/tex}

question_html: Find a particular solution of the differential equation {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y \tan x = \sin x{/tex}, given that y = 0, when {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex}.
question_text: Find a particular solution of the differential equation {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y \tan x = \sin x{/tex} , given that y = 0, when {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex} .
explanation: We have, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y \tan x = \sin x{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} . Here, P = 2 tanx and Q =sin x {tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { 2 \int \tan x d x } = e ^ { 2 \log | \sec x | }{/tex} {tex} = e ^ { \operatorname { logsec } ^ { 2 } x } \quad \left[ \because m \log n = \log n ^ { m } \mathbf { l }\right.{/tex} = sec 2 x [ {tex} \because{/tex} e logx = x] The general solution is given by {tex} y \times IF = \int ( Q \times I F ) d x + C{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \left( \sin x \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x \right) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \sin x \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \tan x \sec x d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sec ^ { 2 } x = \sec x + C{/tex} .............(ii) Also, given that y = 0, when {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex} . On putting y = 0 and {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex} in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}0 \times \sec ^ { 2 } \frac { \pi } { 3 } = \sec \frac { \pi } { 3 } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 0 = 2 + C \Rightarrow C = - 2{/tex} On putting the value of C in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}y \sec ^ { 2 } x = \sec x - 2{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y = \cos x - 2 \cos ^ { 2 } x{/tex} which is the required particular solution of the given differential equation
solution: We have, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y \tan x = \sin x{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} . Here, P = 2 tanx and Q =sin x {tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { 2 \int \tan x d x } = e ^ { 2 \log | \sec x | }{/tex} {tex} = e ^ { \operatorname { logsec } ^ { 2 } x } \quad \left[ \because m \log n = \log n ^ { m } \mathbf { l }\right.{/tex} = sec 2 x [ {tex} \because{/tex} e logx = x] The general solution is given by {tex} y \times IF = \int ( Q \times I F ) d x + C{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \left( \sin x \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x \right) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \sin x \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \tan x \sec x d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sec ^ { 2 } x = \sec x + C{/tex} .............(ii) Also, given that y = 0, when {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex} . On putting y = 0 and {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex} in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}0 \times \sec ^ { 2 } \frac { \pi } { 3 } = \sec \frac { \pi } { 3 } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 0 = 2 + C \Rightarrow C = - 2{/tex} On putting the value of C in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}y \sec ^ { 2 } x = \sec x - 2{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y = \cos x - 2 \cos ^ { 2 } x{/tex} which is the required particular solution of the given differential equation
solution_html:
We have,

{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y \tan x = \sin x{/tex}
which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex}.
Here, P = 2 tanx and Q =sin x
{tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { 2 \int \tan x d x } = e ^ { 2 \log | \sec x | }{/tex}
{tex} = e ^ { \operatorname { logsec } ^ { 2 } x } \quad \left[ \because m \log n = \log n ^ { m } \mathbf { l }\right.{/tex}
= sec2x [{tex} \because{/tex} elogx = x]
The general solution is given by
{tex} y \times IF = \int ( Q \times I F ) d x + C{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \left( \sin x \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x \right) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \sin x \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \tan x \sec x d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sec ^ { 2 } x = \sec x + C{/tex}.............(ii)
Also, given that y = 0, when {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex}.
On putting y = 0 and {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex} in Eq. (ii), we get
{tex}0 \times \sec ^ { 2 } \frac { \pi } { 3 } = \sec \frac { \pi } { 3 } + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 0 = 2 + C \Rightarrow C = - 2{/tex}
On putting the value of C in Eq. (ii), we get
{tex}y \sec ^ { 2 } x = \sec x - 2{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad y = \cos x - 2 \cos ^ { 2 } x{/tex}
which is the required particular solution of the given differential equation

question_html: Solve the differential equation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 1 + x + y + xy
question_text: Solve the differential equation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 1 + x + y + xy
explanation: We are given that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 1 + x + y + xy {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = (1 +x) + y (1 + x) {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = (1 + x) (1 + y) {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{1}{1+y}{/tex} dy = (1 + x) dx [separating variables] {tex}\Rightarrow\int \frac{1}{1+y} d y=\int{/tex} (1 + x) dx [Integrating both sides] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |1 + y| x + {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} + C, which is the general solution of the given differential equation.
solution: We are given that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 1 + x + y + xy {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = (1 +x) + y (1 + x) {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = (1 + x) (1 + y) {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{1}{1+y}{/tex} dy = (1 + x) dx [separating variables] {tex}\Rightarrow\int \frac{1}{1+y} d y=\int{/tex} (1 + x) dx [Integrating both sides] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |1 + y| x + {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} + C, which is the general solution of the given differential equation.
solution_html:
We are given that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 1 + x + y + xy
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = (1 +x) + y (1 + x)
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = (1 + x) (1 + y)
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{1}{1+y}{/tex} dy = (1 + x) dx [separating variables]
{tex}\Rightarrow\int \frac{1}{1+y} d y=\int{/tex} (1 + x) dx [Integrating both sides]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |1 + y| x + {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} + C, which is the general solution of the given differential equation.

question_html: Solve the differential equations: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x(2 \log x+1)}{\sin y+y \cos y}{/tex}
question_text: Solve the differential equations: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x(2 \log x+1)}{\sin y+y \cos y}{/tex}
explanation: We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x(2 \log x+1)}{\sin y+y \cos y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (sin y + y cos y) = x (2 log x + 1) dx Integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int{/tex} (sin y + y cos y) dy = {tex}\int{/tex} x (2 log x + 1) dx {tex}\Rightarrow \int \sin y d y+\int y \cos y d y=2 \int x \log x d x+\int x d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\cos y+\left[y \int \cos y d y-\int\left\{\frac{d}{d y}(y) \int \cos y d y\right\} d y\right]{/tex} {tex}=2\left[\log x \int x d x-\int\left\{\frac{d}{d x}(\log x) \int x d x\right\} d x\right]+\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\cos y+\left[y \sin y-\int \sin y d y\right]=2\left[\log x \times \frac{x^{2}}{2}-\int \frac{1}{x} \times \frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]+\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - cos y + y sin y + cos y = x 2 log x {tex}-\frac{x^{2}}{2}+\frac{x^{2}}{2}+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y sin y = x 2 log x + C Therefore ,y sin y = x 2 log x + C is the required solution of the given differential equation.
solution: We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x(2 \log x+1)}{\sin y+y \cos y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (sin y + y cos y) = x (2 log x + 1) dx Integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int{/tex} (sin y + y cos y) dy = {tex}\int{/tex} x (2 log x + 1) dx {tex}\Rightarrow \int \sin y d y+\int y \cos y d y=2 \int x \log x d x+\int x d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\cos y+\left[y \int \cos y d y-\int\left\{\frac{d}{d y}(y) \int \cos y d y\right\} d y\right]{/tex} {tex}=2\left[\log x \int x d x-\int\left\{\frac{d}{d x}(\log x) \int x d x\right\} d x\right]+\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\cos y+\left[y \sin y-\int \sin y d y\right]=2\left[\log x \times \frac{x^{2}}{2}-\int \frac{1}{x} \times \frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]+\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - cos y + y sin y + cos y = x 2 log x {tex}-\frac{x^{2}}{2}+\frac{x^{2}}{2}+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y sin y = x 2 log x + C Therefore ,y sin y = x 2 log x + C is the required solution of the given differential equation.
solution_html:
We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x(2 \log x+1)}{\sin y+y \cos y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (sin y + y cos y) = x (2 log x + 1) dx
Integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int{/tex} (sin y + y cos y) dy = {tex}\int{/tex} x (2 log x + 1) dx
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \sin y d y+\int y \cos y d y=2 \int x \log x d x+\int x d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-\cos y+\left[y \int \cos y d y-\int\left\{\frac{d}{d y}(y) \int \cos y d y\right\} d y\right]{/tex}{tex}=2\left[\log x \int x d x-\int\left\{\frac{d}{d x}(\log x) \int x d x\right\} d x\right]+\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-\cos y+\left[y \sin y-\int \sin y d y\right]=2\left[\log x \times \frac{x^{2}}{2}-\int \frac{1}{x} \times \frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]+\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - cos y + y sin y + cos y = x2 log x {tex}-\frac{x^{2}}{2}+\frac{x^{2}}{2}+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y sin y = x2 log x + C
Therefore ,y sin y = x2 log x + C is the required solution of the given differential equation.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the differential equation: cosec x log y{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + x2 y2 = 0
question_text: Solve the differential equation: cosec x log y {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + x 2 y 2 = 0
explanation: We have, cosec x log y {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + x 2 y 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosec x log y {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = -x 2 y 2 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{y^{2}} \log y d y=-\frac{x^{2}}{\ c osec x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{y^{2}}{/tex} log y dy = -x 2 sin x dx {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{y^{2}}{/tex} log y dy = {tex}-\int{/tex} x 2 sin x dx {tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{\log y}{y}+\int \frac{1}{y} \times \frac{1}{y}=-\left[-x^{2} \cos x+\int 2 x \cos x d x\right]+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{\log y}{y}-\frac{1}{y}=-\left[-x^{2} \cos x+2 x \sin x-2 \int \sin x d x\right]+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\left(\frac{1+\log y}{y}\right){/tex} = - [-x 2 cos x + 2x sin x + 2 cos x dx] + C {tex}\Rightarrow-\left(\frac{1+\log y}{y}\right){/tex} - x 2 cos x + 2 (x sin x + cos x) = C Hence It is the required solution.
solution: We have, cosec x log y {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + x 2 y 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosec x log y {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = -x 2 y 2 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{y^{2}} \log y d y=-\frac{x^{2}}{\ c osec x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{y^{2}}{/tex} log y dy = -x 2 sin x dx {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{y^{2}}{/tex} log y dy = {tex}-\int{/tex} x 2 sin x dx {tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{\log y}{y}+\int \frac{1}{y} \times \frac{1}{y}=-\left[-x^{2} \cos x+\int 2 x \cos x d x\right]+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{\log y}{y}-\frac{1}{y}=-\left[-x^{2} \cos x+2 x \sin x-2 \int \sin x d x\right]+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\left(\frac{1+\log y}{y}\right){/tex} = - [-x 2 cos x + 2x sin x + 2 cos x dx] + C {tex}\Rightarrow-\left(\frac{1+\log y}{y}\right){/tex} - x 2 cos x + 2 (x sin x + cos x) = C Hence It is the required solution.
solution_html:
We have,  cosec x log y{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + x2 y2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosec x log y{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = -x2 y2
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{y^{2}} \log y d y=-\frac{x^{2}}{\ c osec x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{y^{2}}{/tex} log y dy = -x2 sin x dx
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{y^{2}}{/tex} log y dy = {tex}-\int{/tex} x2 sin x dx
{tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{\log y}{y}+\int \frac{1}{y} \times \frac{1}{y}=-\left[-x^{2} \cos x+\int 2 x \cos x d x\right]+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{\log y}{y}-\frac{1}{y}=-\left[-x^{2} \cos x+2 x \sin x-2 \int \sin x d x\right]+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-\left(\frac{1+\log y}{y}\right){/tex} = - [-x2 cos x + 2x sin x + 2 cos x dx] + C
{tex}\Rightarrow-\left(\frac{1+\log y}{y}\right){/tex} - x2 cos x + 2 (x sin x + cos x) = C
Hence It is the required solution.

question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=x \cos x+\sin x{/tex}, given {tex}y\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=1{/tex}.
question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=x \cos x+\sin x{/tex} , given {tex}y\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=1{/tex} .
explanation: The given differential equation may be written as {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{x} \cdot y=\cos x+\frac{\sin x}{x}{/tex} ...(i) This is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} , where {tex}P=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} and {tex}Q=\cos x+\frac{\sin x}{x}{/tex} . Thus, the given differential equation is linear. {tex}\mathrm{IF}=e^{\int P \, d x}=e^{\int \frac{1}{x} d x}=e^{\log x}=x{/tex} So, its solution is given by {tex}y \times \mathrm{IF}=\int(Q \times \mathrm{IF}) d x+C{/tex} , i.e. {tex}y \times x=\int\left(\cos x+\frac{\sin x}{x}\right) x \, d x+C{/tex} {tex}=\int \underset{I}{x} \underset{II}{\cos} x d x+\int \sin x \, d x+C{/tex} {tex}=x \sin x-\int \sin x \, d x+\int \sin x \, d x+C{/tex} = x sin x + C. {tex}\therefore \quad y=\sin x+\frac{C}{x}{/tex} It is being given that when {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} , then y = 1. Put {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} and y = 1 in (ii), we get C = 0. Hence, y = sin x is the required solution.
solution: The given differential equation may be written as {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{x} \cdot y=\cos x+\frac{\sin x}{x}{/tex} ...(i) This is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} , where {tex}P=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} and {tex}Q=\cos x+\frac{\sin x}{x}{/tex} . Thus, the given differential equation is linear. {tex}\mathrm{IF}=e^{\int P \, d x}=e^{\int \frac{1}{x} d x}=e^{\log x}=x{/tex} So, its solution is given by {tex}y \times \mathrm{IF}=\int(Q \times \mathrm{IF}) d x+C{/tex} , i.e. {tex}y \times x=\int\left(\cos x+\frac{\sin x}{x}\right) x \, d x+C{/tex} {tex}=\int \underset{I}{x} \underset{II}{\cos} x d x+\int \sin x \, d x+C{/tex} {tex}=x \sin x-\int \sin x \, d x+\int \sin x \, d x+C{/tex} = x sin x + C. {tex}\therefore \quad y=\sin x+\frac{C}{x}{/tex} It is being given that when {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} , then y = 1. Put {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} and y = 1 in (ii), we get C = 0. Hence, y = sin x is the required solution.
solution_html:
The given differential equation may be written as
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{x} \cdot y=\cos x+\frac{\sin x}{x}{/tex} ...(i)
This is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex}, where {tex}P=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} and {tex}Q=\cos x+\frac{\sin x}{x}{/tex}.
Thus, the given differential equation is linear.
{tex}\mathrm{IF}=e^{\int P \, d x}=e^{\int \frac{1}{x} d x}=e^{\log x}=x{/tex}
So, its solution is given by
{tex}y \times \mathrm{IF}=\int(Q \times \mathrm{IF}) d x+C{/tex},
i.e. {tex}y \times x=\int\left(\cos x+\frac{\sin x}{x}\right) x \, d x+C{/tex}
{tex}=\int \underset{I}{x} \underset{II}{\cos} x d x+\int \sin x \, d x+C{/tex}
{tex}=x \sin x-\int \sin x \, d x+\int \sin x \, d x+C{/tex}
= x sin x + C.
{tex}\therefore \quad y=\sin x+\frac{C}{x}{/tex}
It is being given that when {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}, then y = 1.
Put  {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} and y = 1 in (ii), we get C = 0.
Hence, y = sin x is the required solution.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation (3xy + y2)dx + (x2 + xy)dy = 0, for x = 1 and y = 1.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation (3xy + y 2 )dx + (x 2 + xy)dy = 0, for x = 1 and y = 1.
explanation: Given differential equation is, (3xy + y 2 )dx + (x 2 + xy)dy = 0 The above Equation can be rewritten as, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = - \frac { 3 x y + y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } + x y } \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = - \frac { 3 \frac { y } { x } + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } } { 1 + \frac { y } { x } }{/tex} ...(i) which is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} . Therefore, on putting y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} in Eq.(i), we get, {tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = - \frac { 3 v + v ^ { 2 } } { 1 + v }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\ x \frac { d v } { d x } = - \frac { 3 v + v ^ { 2 } } { 1 + v }-v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = - \left( \frac { 3 v + v ^ { 2 } + v + v ^ { 2 } } { 1 + v } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = - \left( \frac { 2 v ^ { 2 } + 4 v } { 1 + v } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { ( 1 + v ) d v } { 2 \left( v ^ { 2 } + 2 v \right) } = - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} Therefore, on integrating both sides, we get, {tex}\int \frac { 1 + v } { 2 \left( v ^ { 2 } + 2 v \right) } d v = - \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} ...(ii) Again, putting, v 2 + 2v = z {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2v + 2)dv = dz {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( 1 + v ) d v = \frac { d z } { 2 }{/tex} Therefore, Eq. (ii) becomes, {tex}\int \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { d z } { 2 z } = - \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log | z | = - \log | x | + \log | C |{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 4 } [ \log | z | + 4 \log | x | ] = \log | C |{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log|zx 4 | = 4log|C| {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} zx 4 = C 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} zx 4 = C 1 , where C 1 = C 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 4 (v 2 + 2v) = C {tex}_1{/tex} [put z = v 2 + 2v] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x ^ { 4 } \left( \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 2 y } { x } \right) = C _ { 1 } \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} ...(iii) Also, given that y = 1 for x = 1. Therefore,on putting x = 1 and y = 1 in Eq. (iii), we get, {tex}1 \left( \frac { 1 } { 1 } + \frac { 2 } { 1 } \right) = C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C 1 = 3 So, on putting C 1 = 3 in Eq. (iii), we get {tex}x ^ { 4 } \left( \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 2 y } { x } \right) = 3{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} y 2 x 2 + 2yx 3 = 3
solution: Given differential equation is, (3xy + y 2 )dx + (x 2 + xy)dy = 0 The above Equation can be rewritten as, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = - \frac { 3 x y + y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } + x y } \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = - \frac { 3 \frac { y } { x } + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } } { 1 + \frac { y } { x } }{/tex} ...(i) which is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} . Therefore, on putting y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} in Eq.(i), we get, {tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = - \frac { 3 v + v ^ { 2 } } { 1 + v }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\ x \frac { d v } { d x } = - \frac { 3 v + v ^ { 2 } } { 1 + v }-v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = - \left( \frac { 3 v + v ^ { 2 } + v + v ^ { 2 } } { 1 + v } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = - \left( \frac { 2 v ^ { 2 } + 4 v } { 1 + v } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { ( 1 + v ) d v } { 2 \left( v ^ { 2 } + 2 v \right) } = - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} Therefore, on integrating both sides, we get, {tex}\int \frac { 1 + v } { 2 \left( v ^ { 2 } + 2 v \right) } d v = - \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} ...(ii) Again, putting, v 2 + 2v = z {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2v + 2)dv = dz {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( 1 + v ) d v = \frac { d z } { 2 }{/tex} Therefore, Eq. (ii) becomes, {tex}\int \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { d z } { 2 z } = - \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log | z | = - \log | x | + \log | C |{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 4 } [ \log | z | + 4 \log | x | ] = \log | C |{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log|zx 4 | = 4log|C| {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} zx 4 = C 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} zx 4 = C 1 , where C 1 = C 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 4 (v 2 + 2v) = C {tex}_1{/tex} [put z = v 2 + 2v] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x ^ { 4 } \left( \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 2 y } { x } \right) = C _ { 1 } \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} ...(iii) Also, given that y = 1 for x = 1. Therefore,on putting x = 1 and y = 1 in Eq. (iii), we get, {tex}1 \left( \frac { 1 } { 1 } + \frac { 2 } { 1 } \right) = C _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C 1 = 3 So, on putting C 1 = 3 in Eq. (iii), we get {tex}x ^ { 4 } \left( \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 2 y } { x } \right) = 3{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} y 2 x 2 + 2yx 3 = 3
solution_html:
Given differential equation is,
(3xy + y2)dx + (x2 + xy)dy = 0
The above Equation can be rewritten as,
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = - \frac { 3 x y + y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } + x y } \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = - \frac { 3 \frac { y } { x } + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } } { 1 + \frac { y } { x } }{/tex} ...(i)
which is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex}.
 Therefore, on putting y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} in Eq.(i), we get,
{tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = - \frac { 3 v + v ^ { 2 } } { 1 + v }{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow\ x \frac { d v } { d x } = - \frac { 3 v + v ^ { 2 } } { 1 + v }-v{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = - \left( \frac { 3 v + v ^ { 2 } + v + v ^ { 2 } } { 1 + v } \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = - \left( \frac { 2 v ^ { 2 } + 4 v } { 1 + v } \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { ( 1 + v ) d v } { 2 \left( v ^ { 2 } + 2 v \right) } = - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
Therefore, on integrating both sides, we get,
{tex}\int \frac { 1 + v } { 2 \left( v ^ { 2 } + 2 v \right) } d v = - \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} ...(ii)
Again, putting, v2 + 2v = z {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2v + 2)dv = dz
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( 1 + v ) d v = \frac { d z } { 2 }{/tex}
Therefore, Eq. (ii) becomes,
{tex}\int \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \frac { d z } { 2 z } = - \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 4 } \log | z | = - \log | x | + \log | C |{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 4 } [ \log | z | + 4 \log | x | ] = \log | C |{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log|zx4| = 4log|C|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} zx4 = C4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} zx4 = C1,
where C1 = C4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x4(v2 + 2v) = C{tex}_1{/tex} [put z = v2 + 2v]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x ^ { 4 } \left( \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 2 y } { x } \right) = C _ { 1 } \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} ...(iii)
Also, given that y = 1 for x = 1.
Therefore,on putting x = 1 and y = 1 in Eq. (iii), we get,
{tex}1 \left( \frac { 1 } { 1 } + \frac { 2 } { 1 } \right) = C _ { 1 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C1 = 3
So, on putting C1 = 3 in Eq. (iii), we get
{tex}x ^ { 4 } \left( \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 2 y } { x } \right) = 3{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y2x2 + 2yx3 = 3

question_html: Write the degree of the differential equation {tex}\left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 4 } + 3 x \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = 0{/tex}.
question_text: Write the degree of the differential equation {tex}\left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 4 } + 3 x \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = 0{/tex} .

question_html: Solve the following differential equation: {tex} x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) ( y d x + x d y ) = y \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) ( x d y - y d x ){/tex}.
question_text: Solve the following differential equation: {tex} x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) ( y d x + x d y ) = y \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) ( x d y - y d x ){/tex} .
explanation: According to the question, Given differential equation is, {tex} x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) ( y d x + x d y ) = y \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) ( x d y - y d x ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( x \cos \frac { y } { x } + y \sin \frac { y } { x } \right) \cdot y{/tex} {tex}- \left( y \sin \frac { y } { x } - x \cos \frac { y } { x } \right) \cdot x \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \left[ x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) + y \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \right] \cdot y } { \left( y \sin \frac { y } { x } - x \cos \frac { y } { x } \right) \cdot x }{/tex} ...(i) which is a homogeneous differential equation. on putting {tex} y = vx {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get {tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { ( x \cos v + v x \sin v ) \cdot v x } { ( v x \sin v - x \cos v ) \cdot x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v \cos v + v ^ { 2 } \sin v } { v \sin v - \cos v }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v \cos v + v ^ { 2 } \sin v } { v \sin v - \cos v } - v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v \cos v + v ^ { 2 } \sin v - v ^ { 2 } \sin v + v \cos v } { v \sin v - \cos v }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 2 v \cos v } { v \sin v - \cos v }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { v \sin v - \cos v } { v \cos v } d v = 2 \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac { v \sin v - \cos v } { v \cos v } d v = 2 \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \left( \frac { v \sin v } { v \cos v } - \frac { \cos v } { v \cos v } \right) d v = 2 \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \int \left( \tan v - \frac { 1 } { v } \right) d v = 2 \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | \sec v | - \log | v | = 2 \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \tan v d v = \log | \sec v | \text { and } \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x = \log | x | \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | \sec v | - \log | v | - 2 \log | x | = C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | \sec v | - \log | v | - \log | x | ^ { 2 } = C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because n \log m = \log m ^ { n } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | \sec v | - \log \left| v x ^ { 2 } \right| = C{/tex} {tex}[ \because \log m + \log n = \log m n ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| \frac { \sec v } { v x ^ { 2 } } \right| = C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log m - \log n = \log \left( \frac { m } { n } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| \frac { \sec \frac { y } { x } } { \frac { y } { x } \cdot x ^ { 2 } } \right| = C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \log \left| \frac { \sec \frac { y } { x } } { x y } \right| = C \Rightarrow \frac { \sec \frac { y } { x } } { x y } = e ^ { c }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sec \frac { y } { x } = A x y \quad \left[ \because e ^ { C } = A \right]{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution: According to the question, Given differential equation is, {tex} x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) ( y d x + x d y ) = y \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) ( x d y - y d x ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( x \cos \frac { y } { x } + y \sin \frac { y } { x } \right) \cdot y{/tex} {tex}- \left( y \sin \frac { y } { x } - x \cos \frac { y } { x } \right) \cdot x \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \left[ x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) + y \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \right] \cdot y } { \left( y \sin \frac { y } { x } - x \cos \frac { y } { x } \right) \cdot x }{/tex} ...(i) which is a homogeneous differential equation. on putting {tex} y = vx {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get {tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { ( x \cos v + v x \sin v ) \cdot v x } { ( v x \sin v - x \cos v ) \cdot x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v \cos v + v ^ { 2 } \sin v } { v \sin v - \cos v }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v \cos v + v ^ { 2 } \sin v } { v \sin v - \cos v } - v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v \cos v + v ^ { 2 } \sin v - v ^ { 2 } \sin v + v \cos v } { v \sin v - \cos v }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 2 v \cos v } { v \sin v - \cos v }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { v \sin v - \cos v } { v \cos v } d v = 2 \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac { v \sin v - \cos v } { v \cos v } d v = 2 \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \left( \frac { v \sin v } { v \cos v } - \frac { \cos v } { v \cos v } \right) d v = 2 \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \int \left( \tan v - \frac { 1 } { v } \right) d v = 2 \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | \sec v | - \log | v | = 2 \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \tan v d v = \log | \sec v | \text { and } \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x = \log | x | \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | \sec v | - \log | v | - 2 \log | x | = C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | \sec v | - \log | v | - \log | x | ^ { 2 } = C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because n \log m = \log m ^ { n } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | \sec v | - \log \left| v x ^ { 2 } \right| = C{/tex} {tex}[ \because \log m + \log n = \log m n ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| \frac { \sec v } { v x ^ { 2 } } \right| = C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log m - \log n = \log \left( \frac { m } { n } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| \frac { \sec \frac { y } { x } } { \frac { y } { x } \cdot x ^ { 2 } } \right| = C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \log \left| \frac { \sec \frac { y } { x } } { x y } \right| = C \Rightarrow \frac { \sec \frac { y } { x } } { x y } = e ^ { c }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sec \frac { y } { x } = A x y \quad \left[ \because e ^ { C } = A \right]{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution_html:
According to the question,
Given differential equation is,

{tex} x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) ( y d x + x d y ) = y \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) ( x d y - y d x ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left( x \cos \frac { y } { x } + y \sin \frac { y } { x } \right) \cdot y{/tex} {tex}- \left( y \sin \frac { y } { x } - x \cos \frac { y } { x } \right) \cdot x \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \left[ x \cos \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) + y \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \right] \cdot y } { \left( y \sin \frac { y } { x } - x \cos \frac { y } { x } \right) \cdot x }{/tex} ...(i)
which is a homogeneous differential equation.
on putting {tex} y = vx {/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { ( x \cos v + v x \sin v ) \cdot v x } { ( v x \sin v - x \cos v ) \cdot x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v \cos v + v ^ { 2 } \sin v } { v \sin v - \cos v }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v \cos v + v ^ { 2 } \sin v } { v \sin v - \cos v } - v{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v \cos v + v ^ { 2 } \sin v - v ^ { 2 } \sin v + v \cos v } { v \sin v - \cos v }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 2 v \cos v } { v \sin v - \cos v }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { v \sin v - \cos v } { v \cos v } d v = 2 \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int \frac { v \sin v - \cos v } { v \cos v } d v = 2 \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \left( \frac { v \sin v } { v \cos v } - \frac { \cos v } { v \cos v } \right) d v = 2 \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \int \left( \tan v - \frac { 1 } { v } \right) d v = 2 \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | \sec v | - \log | v | = 2 \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \tan v d v = \log | \sec v | \text { and } \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x = \log | x | \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | \sec v | - \log | v | - 2 \log | x | = C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | \sec v | - \log | v | - \log | x | ^ { 2 } = C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because n \log m = \log m ^ { n } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | \sec v | - \log \left| v x ^ { 2 } \right| = C{/tex} {tex}[ \because \log m + \log n = \log m n ]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| \frac { \sec v } { v x ^ { 2 } } \right| = C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log m - \log n = \log \left( \frac { m } { n } \right) \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| \frac { \sec \frac { y } { x } } { \frac { y } { x } \cdot x ^ { 2 } } \right| = C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \log \left| \frac { \sec \frac { y } { x } } { x y } \right| = C \Rightarrow \frac { \sec \frac { y } { x } } { x y } = e ^ { c }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sec \frac { y } { x } = A x y \quad \left[ \because e ^ { C } = A \right]{/tex}
which is the required solution.

question_html: Solve: 2y ex/y dx + (y - 2x ex/y) dy = 0
question_text: Solve: 2y e x/y dx + (y - 2x e x/y ) dy = 0
explanation: We have, 2y e x/y dx + (y - 2x e x/y ) dy = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{2 x e^{x / y}-y}{2 y e^{x / y}}{/tex} ...(i) Clearly, the given differential equation is a homogeneous differential equation. As the right hand side of (i) is expressible as a function of {tex}\frac{x}{y}{/tex} . So, we put x = vy and {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=v+y \frac{d v}{d y}{/tex} to get {tex}v+y \frac{d v}{d y}=\frac{2 v e^{v}-1}{2 e^{v}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \frac{d v}{d y}=\frac{2 v e^{v}-1}{2 e^{v}}-v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \frac{d v}{d y}=-\frac{1}{2 e^{v}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2ye v dv = -dy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2e v dv = {tex}-\frac{1}{y}{/tex} dy Integrating both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \int e^{v} d v=-\int \frac{1}{y} d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2e v = -log |y| + log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2e v = log {tex}\left|\frac{c}{y}\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2e x/y = log {tex}\left|\frac{c}{y}\right|{/tex} Hence, 2e x/y = log {tex}\left|\frac{c}{y}\right|{/tex} gives the general solution of the given differential equation.
solution: We have, 2y e x/y dx + (y - 2x e x/y ) dy = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{2 x e^{x / y}-y}{2 y e^{x / y}}{/tex} ...(i) Clearly, the given differential equation is a homogeneous differential equation. As the right hand side of (i) is expressible as a function of {tex}\frac{x}{y}{/tex} . So, we put x = vy and {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=v+y \frac{d v}{d y}{/tex} to get {tex}v+y \frac{d v}{d y}=\frac{2 v e^{v}-1}{2 e^{v}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \frac{d v}{d y}=\frac{2 v e^{v}-1}{2 e^{v}}-v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \frac{d v}{d y}=-\frac{1}{2 e^{v}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2ye v dv = -dy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2e v dv = {tex}-\frac{1}{y}{/tex} dy Integrating both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \int e^{v} d v=-\int \frac{1}{y} d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2e v = -log |y| + log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2e v = log {tex}\left|\frac{c}{y}\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2e x/y = log {tex}\left|\frac{c}{y}\right|{/tex} Hence, 2e x/y = log {tex}\left|\frac{c}{y}\right|{/tex} gives the general solution of the given differential equation.
solution_html:
We have, 2y ex/y dx + (y - 2x ex/y) dy = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{2 x e^{x / y}-y}{2 y e^{x / y}}{/tex} ...(i)
Clearly, the given differential equation is a homogeneous differential equation.
As the right hand side of (i) is expressible as a function of {tex}\frac{x}{y}{/tex}.
So, we put x = vy and {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=v+y \frac{d v}{d y}{/tex} to get
{tex}v+y \frac{d v}{d y}=\frac{2 v e^{v}-1}{2 e^{v}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \frac{d v}{d y}=\frac{2 v e^{v}-1}{2 e^{v}}-v{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \frac{d v}{d y}=-\frac{1}{2 e^{v}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2yev dv = -dy
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2ev dv = {tex}-\frac{1}{y}{/tex} dy
Integrating both sides,
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \int e^{v} d v=-\int \frac{1}{y} d y{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2ev = -log |y| + log C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2ev = log {tex}\left|\frac{c}{y}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2ex/y = log {tex}\left|\frac{c}{y}\right|{/tex}
Hence, 2ex/y = log {tex}\left|\frac{c}{y}\right|{/tex} gives the general solution of the given differential equation.

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex} \frac { d x } { d y }{/tex} + x cot y = 2y + y2cot y, (y {tex} \ne{/tex} 0), given that x =0, when y = {tex} \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex} \frac { d x } { d y }{/tex} + x cot y = 2y + y 2 cot y, (y {tex} \ne{/tex} 0), given that x =0, when y = {tex} \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question , Given differential equation is {tex} \frac { d x } { d y } + x cot y = 2y + y^2cot y{/tex} which is a linear differential equation in the form {tex} \frac { d x } { d y } +Px = Q{/tex} here {tex} P = cot y{/tex} and {tex}Q = 2y + y^2coty{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d y } = e ^ { \int \cot y d y }{/tex} {tex} = e ^ { \log | \sin y | } {/tex} = sin y The solution of the differential equation is given by {tex} x \times I F = \int Q \times I F d y + C{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad x \sin y = \int \left( 2 y + y ^ { 2 } \cot y \right) \sin y d y + C{/tex} {tex} = 2\int y \sin ydy + \int {\mathop {{y^2}}\limits_I } \mathop {\cos y}\limits_{II} dy + C{/tex} {tex} = 2 \int y \sin y d y + y ^ { 2 } \int \cos y d y{/tex} {tex} - \int \left[ \left( \frac { d } { d y } y ^ { 2 } \right) \int \cos y d y \right] d y{/tex} + C {tex}= 2 \int y \sin y d y + y ^ { 2 } \sin y - \int 2 y \sin y d y{/tex} {tex}= 2 \int y \sin y d y + y ^ { 2 } \sin y - 2 \int y \sin y d y + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x sin y = y^2sin y + C{/tex} Also, given that x = 0, when y = {tex} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} On putting x = 0 and y = {tex} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} in Eq.(i), we get {tex}0 = \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad C = - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} On putting the value of C in Eq.(i),we get {tex}x sin y = y^2sin y -\frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x = y ^ { 2 } - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }cosec \ y{/tex} which is the required solution of given differential equation.
solution: According to the question , Given differential equation is {tex} \frac { d x } { d y } + x cot y = 2y + y^2cot y{/tex} which is a linear differential equation in the form {tex} \frac { d x } { d y } +Px = Q{/tex} here {tex} P = cot y{/tex} and {tex}Q = 2y + y^2coty{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d y } = e ^ { \int \cot y d y }{/tex} {tex} = e ^ { \log | \sin y | } {/tex} = sin y The solution of the differential equation is given by {tex} x \times I F = \int Q \times I F d y + C{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad x \sin y = \int \left( 2 y + y ^ { 2 } \cot y \right) \sin y d y + C{/tex} {tex} = 2\int y \sin ydy + \int {\mathop {{y^2}}\limits_I } \mathop {\cos y}\limits_{II} dy + C{/tex} {tex} = 2 \int y \sin y d y + y ^ { 2 } \int \cos y d y{/tex} {tex} - \int \left[ \left( \frac { d } { d y } y ^ { 2 } \right) \int \cos y d y \right] d y{/tex} + C {tex}= 2 \int y \sin y d y + y ^ { 2 } \sin y - \int 2 y \sin y d y{/tex} {tex}= 2 \int y \sin y d y + y ^ { 2 } \sin y - 2 \int y \sin y d y + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x sin y = y^2sin y + C{/tex} Also, given that x = 0, when y = {tex} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} On putting x = 0 and y = {tex} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} in Eq.(i), we get {tex}0 = \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad C = - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} On putting the value of C in Eq.(i),we get {tex}x sin y = y^2sin y -\frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x = y ^ { 2 } - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }cosec \ y{/tex} which is the required solution of given differential equation.
solution_html:
According to the question ,

Given differential equation is 

{tex} \frac { d x } { d y }  + x cot y = 2y + y^2cot y{/tex}
which is a linear differential equation in the form {tex} \frac { d x } { d y } +Px = Q{/tex}
here{tex} P = cot y{/tex} and {tex}Q = 2y + y^2coty{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d y } = e ^ { \int \cot y d y }{/tex}
{tex} = e ^ { \log | \sin y | } {/tex}= sin y
The solution of the differential equation is given by 
{tex} x \times I F = \int Q \times I F d y + C{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad x \sin y = \int \left( 2 y + y ^ { 2 } \cot y \right) \sin y d y + C{/tex}
{tex} = 2\int y \sin ydy + \int {\mathop {{y^2}}\limits_I } \mathop {\cos y}\limits_{II} dy + C{/tex}
{tex} = 2 \int y \sin y d y + y ^ { 2 } \int \cos y d y{/tex}{tex} - \int \left[ \left( \frac { d } { d y } y ^ { 2 } \right) \int \cos y d y \right] d y{/tex} + C
{tex}= 2 \int y \sin y d y + y ^ { 2 } \sin y - \int 2 y \sin y d y{/tex}
{tex}= 2 \int y \sin y d y + y ^ { 2 } \sin y - 2 \int y \sin y d y + C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x sin y = y^2sin y + C{/tex} 
Also, given that x = 0, when y = {tex} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
On putting x = 0 and y = {tex} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} in Eq.(i), we get
{tex}0 = \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } + C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad C = - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex}
On putting the value of C in Eq.(i),we get
{tex}x sin y = y^2sin y -\frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x = y ^ { 2 } - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }cosec \ y{/tex}
which is the required solution of given differential equation.

question_html: Find the particular solution of the following differential equation  : (x2 + xy)dy = (x2 + y2)dx, given that y=0,when x=1.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the following differential equation : (x 2 + xy)dy = (x 2 + y 2 )dx, given that y=0,when x=1.
explanation: Given, (x 2 + xy)dy = (x 2 + y 2 )dx {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \left( \frac { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } + x y } \right){/tex} ...........(i) This is a homogeneous differential equation. On putting y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v \cdot 1 + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get, {tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \left( \frac { x ^ { 2 } + v ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } + x \cdot x v } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } { 1 + v } - v = \frac { 1 + v ^ { 2 } - v - v ^ { 2 } } { 1 + v } = \frac { 1 - v } { 1 + v }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \left( \frac { 1 + v } { 1 - v } \right) d v = \frac { 1 } { x } d x{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \left( \frac { 1 + v } { 1 - v } \right) d v = \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \int \left[ - 1 + \frac { 2 } { 1 - v } \right] d v = \log | x | + \log C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -v - 2 log (1 - v) = log | x | + log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -v = 2log(1 - v) + log | x | + log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -v = log(1 - v) 2 + log {C |x|} [ {tex}\because{/tex} log m + log n = log mn] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -v = log{C|x|(1-v) 2 } {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C | x | \left( 1 - \frac { y } { x } \right) ^ { 2 } = e ^ { - y / x } \left[ \because v = \frac { y } { x } \right]`{/tex} ...(ii) On putting x = 1 and y = 0 in Eq. (ii), we get C {tex}\cdot{/tex} 1(1 - 0) = e 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1 Thus, the required solution is {tex}| x | \left( 1 - \frac { y } { x } \right) ^ { 2 } = e ^ { - y / x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( x - y ) ^ { 2 } = | x | e ^ { - y / x }{/tex}
solution: Given, (x 2 + xy)dy = (x 2 + y 2 )dx {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \left( \frac { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } + x y } \right){/tex} ...........(i) This is a homogeneous differential equation. On putting y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v \cdot 1 + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get, {tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \left( \frac { x ^ { 2 } + v ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } + x \cdot x v } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } { 1 + v } - v = \frac { 1 + v ^ { 2 } - v - v ^ { 2 } } { 1 + v } = \frac { 1 - v } { 1 + v }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \left( \frac { 1 + v } { 1 - v } \right) d v = \frac { 1 } { x } d x{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \left( \frac { 1 + v } { 1 - v } \right) d v = \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \int \left[ - 1 + \frac { 2 } { 1 - v } \right] d v = \log | x | + \log C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -v - 2 log (1 - v) = log | x | + log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -v = 2log(1 - v) + log | x | + log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -v = log(1 - v) 2 + log {C |x|} [ {tex}\because{/tex} log m + log n = log mn] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -v = log{C|x|(1-v) 2 } {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C | x | \left( 1 - \frac { y } { x } \right) ^ { 2 } = e ^ { - y / x } \left[ \because v = \frac { y } { x } \right]`{/tex} ...(ii) On putting x = 1 and y = 0 in Eq. (ii), we get C {tex}\cdot{/tex} 1(1 - 0) = e 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1 Thus, the required solution is {tex}| x | \left( 1 - \frac { y } { x } \right) ^ { 2 } = e ^ { - y / x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( x - y ) ^ { 2 } = | x | e ^ { - y / x }{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, (x2 + xy)dy = (x2 + y2)dx

{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \left( \frac { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } + x y } \right){/tex}...........(i)
This is a homogeneous differential equation.
On putting  y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v \cdot 1 + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get,
{tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \left( \frac { x ^ { 2 } + v ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } + x \cdot x v } \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } { 1 + v } - v = \frac { 1 + v ^ { 2 } - v - v ^ { 2 } } { 1 + v } = \frac { 1 - v } { 1 + v }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \left( \frac { 1 + v } { 1 - v } \right) d v = \frac { 1 } { x } d x{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int \left( \frac { 1 + v } { 1 - v } \right) d v = \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \int \left[ - 1 + \frac { 2 } { 1 - v } \right] d v = \log | x | + \log C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -v - 2 log (1 - v) = log | x | + log C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -v = 2log(1 - v) + log | x | + log C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -v = log(1 - v)2 + log {C |x|} [{tex}\because{/tex} log m + log n = log mn]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -v = log{C|x|(1-v)2}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad C | x | \left( 1 - \frac { y } { x } \right) ^ { 2 } = e ^ { - y / x } \left[ \because v = \frac { y } { x } \right]`{/tex} ...(ii)
On putting x = 1 and y = 0 in Eq. (ii), we get
C{tex}\cdot{/tex}1(1 - 0) = e0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1
Thus, the required solution is
{tex}| x | \left( 1 - \frac { y } { x } \right) ^ { 2 } = e ^ { - y / x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( x - y ) ^ { 2 } = | x | e ^ { - y / x }{/tex}

question_html: Write the degree of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d^{3} x}{d t^{3}}+\frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}+\left(\frac{d x}{d t}\right)^{2}=e^{t}{/tex}.
question_text: Write the degree of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d^{3} x}{d t^{3}}+\frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}+\left(\frac{d x}{d t}\right)^{2}=e^{t}{/tex} .
explanation: The degree of a differential equation is the power of its highest derivative. {tex}\frac{d^{3} x}{d t^{3}}+\frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}+\left(\frac{d x}{d t}\right)^{2}=e^{t}{/tex} So, the degree of the given differential equation is 1.
solution: The degree of a differential equation is the power of its highest derivative. {tex}\frac{d^{3} x}{d t^{3}}+\frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}+\left(\frac{d x}{d t}\right)^{2}=e^{t}{/tex} So, the degree of the given differential equation is 1.
solution_html:
The degree of a differential equation is the power of its highest derivative.
{tex}\frac{d^{3} x}{d t^{3}}+\frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}+\left(\frac{d x}{d t}\right)^{2}=e^{t}{/tex}
So, the degree of the given differential equation is 1.

question_html: Write the degree of the differential equation {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}+\frac{1}{d y / d x}=2{/tex}.
question_text: Write the degree of the differential equation {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}+\frac{1}{d y / d x}=2{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}+\frac{1}{d y / d x}=2{/tex} Multiply {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} both sides, we get, {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}+1=2 \frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}-2 \frac{dy}{dx}+1=0{/tex} So, the degree of the given differential equation is 3.
solution: {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}+\frac{1}{d y / d x}=2{/tex} Multiply {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} both sides, we get, {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}+1=2 \frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}-2 \frac{dy}{dx}+1=0{/tex} So, the degree of the given differential equation is 3.
solution_html:
{tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}+\frac{1}{d y / d x}=2{/tex}
Multiply {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} both sides, we get,
{tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}+1=2 \frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}
{tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}-2 \frac{dy}{dx}+1=0{/tex}
So, the degree of the given differential equation is 3.

question_html: Solve the differential equation: x cos{tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} (y dx + x dy) = y sin{tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex}{tex}\cdot{/tex} (x dy - y dx)
question_text: Solve the differential equation: x cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} (y dx + x dy) = y sin {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} (x dy - y dx)
explanation: The given differential equation is, x cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} (y dx + x dy) = y sin {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} (x dy - y dx) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} dx + x 2 cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} dy = xy sin {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} dy - y 2 sin {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [xy cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} + y 2 sin {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} ] dx = [xy sin {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} - x 2 cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} ] dy {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x y \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+y^{2} \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{x y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-x^{2} \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{/tex} This is a homogeneous differential equation Putting y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} , we have, {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v x^{2} \cos v+v^{2} x^{2} \sin v}{v x^{2} \sin v-x^{2} \cos v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v \cos v+v^{2} \sin v}{v \sin v-\cos v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v \cos v+v^{2} \sin v}{v \sin v-\cos v}-v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v \cos v+v^{2} \sin v-v^{2} \sin v+v \cos v}{v \sin v-\cos v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2 v \cos v}{v \sin v-\cos v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{v \sin v-\cos v}{2 v \cos v} d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} Integrating both sides, we have, {tex}\int \frac{v \sin v-\cos v}{2 v \cos v} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} dx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{v \sin v-\cos v}{v \cos v} d v=2 \int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{v \sin v}{v \cos v} d v-\int \frac{\cos v}{v \cos v} d v=2 \int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \tan v d v-\int \frac{1}{v} d v=2 \int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |sec v| - log |v| = 2 log |x| + log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log {tex}\left|\frac{\sec v}{v}\right|{/tex} = log {tex}\left|C x^{2}\right|{/tex} Putting v = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} , we have, sec {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\frac{y}{x} \times C \times x^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sec {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = Cxy Hence, sec {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = Cxy is the required solution.
solution: The given differential equation is, x cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} (y dx + x dy) = y sin {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} (x dy - y dx) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} dx + x 2 cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} dy = xy sin {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} dy - y 2 sin {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [xy cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} + y 2 sin {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} ] dx = [xy sin {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} - x 2 cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} ] dy {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x y \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+y^{2} \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{x y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-x^{2} \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{/tex} This is a homogeneous differential equation Putting y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} , we have, {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v x^{2} \cos v+v^{2} x^{2} \sin v}{v x^{2} \sin v-x^{2} \cos v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v \cos v+v^{2} \sin v}{v \sin v-\cos v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v \cos v+v^{2} \sin v}{v \sin v-\cos v}-v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v \cos v+v^{2} \sin v-v^{2} \sin v+v \cos v}{v \sin v-\cos v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2 v \cos v}{v \sin v-\cos v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{v \sin v-\cos v}{2 v \cos v} d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} Integrating both sides, we have, {tex}\int \frac{v \sin v-\cos v}{2 v \cos v} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} dx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{v \sin v-\cos v}{v \cos v} d v=2 \int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{v \sin v}{v \cos v} d v-\int \frac{\cos v}{v \cos v} d v=2 \int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \tan v d v-\int \frac{1}{v} d v=2 \int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |sec v| - log |v| = 2 log |x| + log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log {tex}\left|\frac{\sec v}{v}\right|{/tex} = log {tex}\left|C x^{2}\right|{/tex} Putting v = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} , we have, sec {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\frac{y}{x} \times C \times x^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sec {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = Cxy Hence, sec {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = Cxy is the required solution.
solution_html:
The given differential equation is,
x cos{tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} (y dx + x dy) = y sin{tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex}{tex}\cdot{/tex} (x dy - y dx)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy cos{tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} dx + x2 cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex}dy = xy sin {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} dy - y2 sin{tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} dx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [xy cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} + y2 sin{tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex}] dx = [xy sin{tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex}- x2 cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex}] dy
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x y \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+y^{2} \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{x y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-x^{2} \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{/tex}
This is a homogeneous differential equation
Putting y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}, we have,
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v x^{2} \cos v+v^{2} x^{2} \sin v}{v x^{2} \sin v-x^{2} \cos v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v \cos v+v^{2} \sin v}{v \sin v-\cos v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v \cos v+v^{2} \sin v}{v \sin v-\cos v}-v{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v \cos v+v^{2} \sin v-v^{2} \sin v+v \cos v}{v \sin v-\cos v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2 v \cos v}{v \sin v-\cos v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{v \sin v-\cos v}{2 v \cos v} d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
Integrating both sides, we have,
{tex}\int \frac{v \sin v-\cos v}{2 v \cos v} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} dx{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{v \sin v-\cos v}{v \cos v} d v=2 \int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{v \sin v}{v \cos v} d v-\int \frac{\cos v}{v \cos v} d v=2 \int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \tan v d v-\int \frac{1}{v} d v=2 \int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |sec v| - log |v| = 2 log |x| + log C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log {tex}\left|\frac{\sec v}{v}\right|{/tex} = log {tex}\left|C x^{2}\right|{/tex}
Putting v = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex}, we have,
sec{tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\frac{y}{x} \times C \times x^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sec{tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = Cxy
Hence, sec{tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} = Cxy is the required solution.
