question_html: Solve the differential equation x2 dy + y(x + y) dx = 0, given that y = 1 when x = 1
question_text: Solve the differential equation x 2 dy + y(x + y) dx = 0, given that y = 1 when x = 1
explanation: We have, x 2 dy + y (x + y) dx = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 dy = -y (x + y) dx {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{y(x+y)}{x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d y}{d x}=-\left(\frac{x y+y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right){/tex} Since each of the functions xy + y 2 and x is a homogeneous function of degree 2. Therefore, equation (i) is a homogeneous differential equation. Putting y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} in (i), we have, {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\left(\frac{v x^{2}+v^{2} x^{2}}{x^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = -(v + v 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = -2v - v 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x dv = -(v 2 + 2v) dx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{v^{2}+2 v} d v=-\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} [By separating the variables] {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{v^{2}+2 v} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} [Integrating both sides] {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{v^{2}+2 v+1-1} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{(v+1)^{2}-1^{2}} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2 \times 1} \log \left|\frac{v+1-1}{v+1+1}\right|{/tex} = -log x + log C {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \log \left|\frac{v}{v+2}\right|{/tex} = -log x + log C {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left|\frac{v}{v+2}\right|{/tex} + 2 log x = 2 log C {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left|\frac{v}{v+2}\right|{/tex} + log x 2 = log k, where k = C 2 {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left|\frac{v x^{2}}{v+2}\right|{/tex} = log k {tex}\Rightarrow \left|\frac{v x^{2}}{v+2}\right|{/tex} = k {tex}\Rightarrow \left|\frac{\frac{y}{x} \times x^{2}}{\frac{y}{x}+2}\right|=k \Rightarrow\left|\frac{x^{2} y}{y+2 x}\right|{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \frac{y}{x}=v\right]{/tex} It is given that y = 1, when x = 1. Putting x = 1, y = 1 in (ii), Putting k = 1/ 3 in (ii), we have, {tex}\left|\frac{x^{2} y}{y+2 x}\right|=\frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow{/tex} 3x 2 y = {tex}\pm{/tex} (y + 2x) But, 3x 2 y = -(y + 2x) is not satisfied by y (1) = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} 3x 2 y = y + 2x {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} y = {tex}\frac{2 x}{3 x^{2}-1}{/tex} , which gives the required solution.
solution: We have, x 2 dy + y (x + y) dx = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 dy = -y (x + y) dx {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{y(x+y)}{x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d y}{d x}=-\left(\frac{x y+y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right){/tex} Since each of the functions xy + y 2 and x is a homogeneous function of degree 2. Therefore, equation (i) is a homogeneous differential equation. Putting y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} in (i), we have, {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\left(\frac{v x^{2}+v^{2} x^{2}}{x^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = -(v + v 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = -2v - v 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x dv = -(v 2 + 2v) dx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{v^{2}+2 v} d v=-\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} [By separating the variables] {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{v^{2}+2 v} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} [Integrating both sides] {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{v^{2}+2 v+1-1} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{(v+1)^{2}-1^{2}} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2 \times 1} \log \left|\frac{v+1-1}{v+1+1}\right|{/tex} = -log x + log C {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \log \left|\frac{v}{v+2}\right|{/tex} = -log x + log C {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left|\frac{v}{v+2}\right|{/tex} + 2 log x = 2 log C {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left|\frac{v}{v+2}\right|{/tex} + log x 2 = log k, where k = C 2 {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left|\frac{v x^{2}}{v+2}\right|{/tex} = log k {tex}\Rightarrow \left|\frac{v x^{2}}{v+2}\right|{/tex} = k {tex}\Rightarrow \left|\frac{\frac{y}{x} \times x^{2}}{\frac{y}{x}+2}\right|=k \Rightarrow\left|\frac{x^{2} y}{y+2 x}\right|{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \frac{y}{x}=v\right]{/tex} It is given that y = 1, when x = 1. Putting x = 1, y = 1 in (ii), Putting k = 1/ 3 in (ii), we have, {tex}\left|\frac{x^{2} y}{y+2 x}\right|=\frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow{/tex} 3x 2 y = {tex}\pm{/tex} (y + 2x) But, 3x 2 y = -(y + 2x) is not satisfied by y (1) = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} 3x 2 y = y + 2x {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} y = {tex}\frac{2 x}{3 x^{2}-1}{/tex} , which gives the required solution.
solution_html:
We have,

 x2 dy + y (x + y) dx = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 dy = -y (x + y) dx
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{y(x+y)}{x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d y}{d x}=-\left(\frac{x y+y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right){/tex}
Since each of the functions xy + y2 and x is a homogeneous function of degree 2. Therefore, equation (i) is a homogeneous differential equation.
Putting y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = v + x{tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} in (i), we have,
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\left(\frac{v x^{2}+v^{2} x^{2}}{x^{2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = -(v + v2)
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = -2v - v2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x dv = -(v2 + 2v) dx
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{v^{2}+2 v} d v=-\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} [By separating the variables]
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{v^{2}+2 v} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} [Integrating both sides]
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{v^{2}+2 v+1-1} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{(v+1)^{2}-1^{2}} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2 \times 1} \log \left|\frac{v+1-1}{v+1+1}\right|{/tex} = -log x + log C
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \log \left|\frac{v}{v+2}\right|{/tex} = -log x + log C
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left|\frac{v}{v+2}\right|{/tex} + 2 log x = 2 log C
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left|\frac{v}{v+2}\right|{/tex} + log x2 = log k, where k = C2
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left|\frac{v x^{2}}{v+2}\right|{/tex} = log k
{tex}\Rightarrow \left|\frac{v x^{2}}{v+2}\right|{/tex} = k
{tex}\Rightarrow \left|\frac{\frac{y}{x} \times x^{2}}{\frac{y}{x}+2}\right|=k \Rightarrow\left|\frac{x^{2} y}{y+2 x}\right|{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \frac{y}{x}=v\right]{/tex}
It is given that y = 1, when x = 1. Putting x = 1, y = 1 in (ii), Putting k = 1/ 3 in (ii), we have,
{tex}\left|\frac{x^{2} y}{y+2 x}\right|=\frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow{/tex} 3x2y = {tex}\pm{/tex} (y + 2x)
But, 3x2 y = -(y + 2x) is not satisfied by y (1) = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 3x2y = y + 2x {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex}y = {tex}\frac{2 x}{3 x^{2}-1}{/tex}, which gives the required solution.

question_html: Write the degree of the differential equation {tex}x\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)^{3}+y\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{4}+x^{3}=0{/tex}.
question_text: Write the degree of the differential equation {tex}x\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)^{3}+y\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{4}+x^{3}=0{/tex} .
explanation: Consider the given differential equation, {tex}x\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)^{3}+y\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{4}+x^{3}=0{/tex} Then the degree of a differential equation is the degree of the highest order derivative. In this given differential equation, the power of the highest order differential coefficient is 3 . Therefore the degree of this diff equation is 3.
solution: Consider the given differential equation, {tex}x\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)^{3}+y\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{4}+x^{3}=0{/tex} Then the degree of a differential equation is the degree of the highest order derivative. In this given differential equation, the power of the highest order differential coefficient is 3 . Therefore the degree of this diff equation is 3.
solution_html:
Consider the given differential equation, 
{tex}x\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)^{3}+y\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{4}+x^{3}=0{/tex}
Then the degree of a differential equation is the degree of the highest order derivative.
In this given differential equation, the power of the highest order differential coefficient is 3 .
Therefore the degree of this diff equation is 3.

explanation: In the given equation, the highest-order derivative is {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} and its power is 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} its order = 2 and degree = 2

question_html: Solve the following differential equation {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \sec x = \tan x , \left( 0 \leq x < \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} .
question_text: Solve the following differential equation {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \sec x = \tan x , \left( 0 \leq x < \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} .
explanation: We have, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \sec x = \tan x{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of first order and is of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} ...(i) Here, P = sec x and Q = tan x {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = \mathrm { e } ^ { \int P d x } = \mathrm { e } ^ { \int \sec x d x }{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { \log | \sec x + \tan x | }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \sec x d x = \log | \sec x + \tan x | \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} IF = sec x + tan x The general solution is {tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}y ( \sec x + \tan x ) = \int \tan x \cdot ( \sec x + \tan x ) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y ( \sec x + \tan x ) = \int \sec x \tan x d x + \int \tan ^ { 2 } x d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y ( \sec x + \tan x ) = \sec x + \int \left( \sec ^ { 2 } x - 1 \right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} y(sec x + tan x) = sec x + tan x - x + C {tex}\left[ \because \int \sec ^ { 2 } x d x = \tan x \right]{/tex} On dividing both sides by (sec x + tan x), we get {tex}y = 1 - \frac { x } { \sec x + \tan x } + \frac { C } { \sec x + \tan x }{/tex}
solution: We have, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \sec x = \tan x{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of first order and is of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} ...(i) Here, P = sec x and Q = tan x {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = \mathrm { e } ^ { \int P d x } = \mathrm { e } ^ { \int \sec x d x }{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { \log | \sec x + \tan x | }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \sec x d x = \log | \sec x + \tan x | \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} IF = sec x + tan x The general solution is {tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}y ( \sec x + \tan x ) = \int \tan x \cdot ( \sec x + \tan x ) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y ( \sec x + \tan x ) = \int \sec x \tan x d x + \int \tan ^ { 2 } x d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y ( \sec x + \tan x ) = \sec x + \int \left( \sec ^ { 2 } x - 1 \right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} y(sec x + tan x) = sec x + tan x - x + C {tex}\left[ \because \int \sec ^ { 2 } x d x = \tan x \right]{/tex} On dividing both sides by (sec x + tan x), we get {tex}y = 1 - \frac { x } { \sec x + \tan x } + \frac { C } { \sec x + \tan x }{/tex}
solution_html:
We have,
{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \sec x = \tan x{/tex}
which is a linear differential equation of first order and is of the form
{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} ...(i)
Here, P = sec x and Q = tan x
{tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = \mathrm { e } ^ { \int P d x } = \mathrm { e } ^ { \int \sec x d x }{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { \log | \sec x + \tan x | }{/tex}
{tex}\left[ \because \int \sec x d x = \log | \sec x + \tan x | \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} IF = sec x + tan x
The general solution is {tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}y ( \sec x + \tan x ) = \int \tan x \cdot ( \sec x + \tan x ) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y ( \sec x + \tan x ) = \int \sec x \tan x d x + \int \tan ^ { 2 } x d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y ( \sec x + \tan x ) = \sec x + \int \left( \sec ^ { 2 } x - 1 \right) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} y(sec x + tan x) = sec x + tan x - x + C {tex}\left[ \because \int \sec ^ { 2 } x d x = \tan x \right]{/tex}
On dividing both sides by (sec x + tan x), we get
{tex}y = 1 - \frac { x } { \sec x + \tan x } + \frac { C } { \sec x + \tan x }{/tex}

question_html: Find a particular solution of the differential equation {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \cot x = 4 x \; cosec \; x{/tex}, x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 given that y = 0, when {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}.
question_text: Find a particular solution of the differential equation {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \cot x = 4 x \; cosec \; x{/tex} , x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 given that y = 0, when {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} .
explanation: Given differential equation is {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + y \cot x = 4 x \; cosec \; x{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} , here P = cot x and Q = 4x cosec x {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { Pd } \mathrm { x } } = e ^ { \int \cot x d x }{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { \log | \sin x | } = \sin x \quad \left[ \because e ^ { \log | x | } = x \right]{/tex} The solution of linear differential equation is given by {tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \times \sin x = \int 4 x\ cosec x \cdot \sin x d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sin x = \int 4 x \cdot \frac { 1 } { \sin x } \cdot \sin x d x + c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sin x = \int 4 x d x + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \sin x = 2 x ^ { 2 } + C{/tex} ...(i) Also, given that y = 0, when {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} . On putting y = 0 and {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get {tex} 0 = 2 \times \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 } + C \Rightarrow C = \frac { - \pi ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} On putting {tex} C = - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get {tex} y \sin x = 2 x ^ { 2 } - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad y = 2 x ^ { 2 } \ cosec x - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \; cosec \; x{/tex} [dividing both sides by sin x] which is the required solution.
solution: Given differential equation is {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + y \cot x = 4 x \; cosec \; x{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} , here P = cot x and Q = 4x cosec x {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { Pd } \mathrm { x } } = e ^ { \int \cot x d x }{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { \log | \sin x | } = \sin x \quad \left[ \because e ^ { \log | x | } = x \right]{/tex} The solution of linear differential equation is given by {tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \times \sin x = \int 4 x\ cosec x \cdot \sin x d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sin x = \int 4 x \cdot \frac { 1 } { \sin x } \cdot \sin x d x + c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sin x = \int 4 x d x + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \sin x = 2 x ^ { 2 } + C{/tex} ...(i) Also, given that y = 0, when {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} . On putting y = 0 and {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get {tex} 0 = 2 \times \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 } + C \Rightarrow C = \frac { - \pi ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} On putting {tex} C = - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get {tex} y \sin x = 2 x ^ { 2 } - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad y = 2 x ^ { 2 } \ cosec x - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \; cosec \; x{/tex} [dividing both sides by sin x] which is the required solution.
solution_html:
Given differential equation is
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + y \cot x = 4 x \; cosec \; x{/tex}
which is a linear differential equation of the form
{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex}, here P = cot x and Q = 4x cosec x
{tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { Pd } \mathrm { x } } = e ^ { \int \cot x d x }{/tex}
{tex}= e ^ { \log | \sin x | } = \sin x \quad \left[ \because e ^ { \log | x | } = x \right]{/tex}
The solution of linear differential equation is given by
{tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \times \sin x = \int 4 x\ cosec x \cdot \sin x d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sin x = \int 4 x \cdot \frac { 1 } { \sin x } \cdot \sin x d x + c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \sin x = \int 4 x d x + C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \sin x = 2 x ^ { 2 } + C{/tex} ...(i)
Also, given that y = 0, when {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}.
On putting y = 0 and {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get
{tex} 0 = 2 \times \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 } + C \Rightarrow C = \frac { - \pi ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex}
On putting {tex} C = - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get
{tex} y \sin x = 2 x ^ { 2 } - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad y = 2 x ^ { 2 } \ cosec x - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \; cosec \; x{/tex} [dividing both sides by sin x]
which is the required solution.

question_html: Solve the following differential equation (1 + x2)dy + 2xy dx = cot x dx, where {tex} x \neq 0{/tex}.
question_text: Solve the following differential equation (1 + x 2 )dy + 2xy dx = cot x dx, where {tex} x \neq 0{/tex} .
explanation: Given the differential equation is - (1 + x 2 ) dy + 2xy dx = cot x dx {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} (1 + x 2 )dy = (cot x - 2xy)dx On dividing both sides by (1 + x 2 ), we get {tex} d y = \frac { \cot x - 2 x y } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } + \frac { 2 x y } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \cot x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of 1st order and is of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} Here, {tex} P = \frac { 2 x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} and {tex} Q = \frac { \cot x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int \frac { 2 x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } } = e ^ { \log \left| 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right| } = 1 + x ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \left[ \begin{array} { l } { \text { put } 1 + x ^ { 2 } = t \Rightarrow 2 x d x = d t , \text { then } } \\ { \int \frac { 2 x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x = \int \frac { d t } { t } = \log | t | = \log \left| 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right| } \end{array} \right]{/tex} The solution of linear differential equation is given by {tex} y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad y \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) = \int \frac { \cot x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \times \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) d x + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) = \int \cot x d x + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) = \log | \sin x | + C{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \int \cot x d x = \log | \sin x | \right]{/tex} On dividing both sides by (1 + x 2 ), we get {tex} y = \frac { \log | \sin x | } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { C } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution: Given the differential equation is - (1 + x 2 ) dy + 2xy dx = cot x dx {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} (1 + x 2 )dy = (cot x - 2xy)dx On dividing both sides by (1 + x 2 ), we get {tex} d y = \frac { \cot x - 2 x y } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } + \frac { 2 x y } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \cot x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of 1st order and is of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} Here, {tex} P = \frac { 2 x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} and {tex} Q = \frac { \cot x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int \frac { 2 x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } } = e ^ { \log \left| 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right| } = 1 + x ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \left[ \begin{array} { l } { \text { put } 1 + x ^ { 2 } = t \Rightarrow 2 x d x = d t , \text { then } } \\ { \int \frac { 2 x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x = \int \frac { d t } { t } = \log | t | = \log \left| 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right| } \end{array} \right]{/tex} The solution of linear differential equation is given by {tex} y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad y \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) = \int \frac { \cot x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \times \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) d x + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) = \int \cot x d x + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) = \log | \sin x | + C{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \int \cot x d x = \log | \sin x | \right]{/tex} On dividing both sides by (1 + x 2 ), we get {tex} y = \frac { \log | \sin x | } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { C } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution_html:
Given the differential equation is -

(1 + x2) dy + 2xy dx = cot x dx
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} (1 + x2)dy = (cot x - 2xy)dx
On dividing both sides by (1 + x2), we get
{tex} d y = \frac { \cot x - 2 x y } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } + \frac { 2 x y } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \cot x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
which is a linear differential equation of 1st order and is of the form
{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex}
Here, {tex} P = \frac { 2 x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} and {tex} Q = \frac { \cot x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int \frac { 2 x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } } = e ^ { \log \left| 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right| } = 1 + x ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} \left[ \begin{array} { l } { \text { put } 1 + x ^ { 2 } = t \Rightarrow 2 x d x = d t , \text { then } } \\ { \int \frac { 2 x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x = \int \frac { d t } { t } = \log | t | = \log \left| 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right| } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
The solution of linear differential equation is given by
{tex} y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad y \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) = \int \frac { \cot x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \times \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) d x + C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) = \int \cot x d x + C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) = \log | \sin x | + C{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \int \cot x d x = \log | \sin x | \right]{/tex}
On dividing both sides by (1 + x2), we get
{tex} y = \frac { \log | \sin x | } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { C } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
which is the required solution.

question_html: Find the particular solution of the following differential equation  {tex}x y \frac { d y } { d x } = ( x + 2 ) ( y + 2 ) ; y = - 1{/tex}when x = 1.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the following differential equation {tex}x y \frac { d y } { d x } = ( x + 2 ) ( y + 2 ) ; y = - 1{/tex} when x = 1.
explanation: We have, {tex}x y \frac { d y } { d x } = ( x + 2 ) ( y + 2 ){/tex} On separating the variables, we get {tex}\frac { y d y } { y + 2 } = \frac { x + 2 } { x } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( \frac { y + 2 - 2 } { y + 2 } \right) d y = \left( 1 + \frac { 2 } { x } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( 1 - \frac { 2 } { y + 2 } \right) d y = \left( 1 + \frac { 2 } { x } \right) d x{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \left( 1 - \frac { 2 } { y + 2 } \right) d y = \int \left( 1 + \frac { 2 } { x } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y - 2 \log | y + 2 | = x + 2 \log | x | + C{/tex} ...(i) Given that y = -1, when x = 1 On putting x = 1 and y = -1 in Eq. (i), we get -1 - 2log(1) = 1 + 2log|1| + C {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 1 = 1 + C \Rightarrow C = - 2{/tex} On putting C = -2 in Eq. (i), we get y - 2log|y + 2| = x + 2log|x| - 2 which is required particular solution.
solution: We have, {tex}x y \frac { d y } { d x } = ( x + 2 ) ( y + 2 ){/tex} On separating the variables, we get {tex}\frac { y d y } { y + 2 } = \frac { x + 2 } { x } d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( \frac { y + 2 - 2 } { y + 2 } \right) d y = \left( 1 + \frac { 2 } { x } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( 1 - \frac { 2 } { y + 2 } \right) d y = \left( 1 + \frac { 2 } { x } \right) d x{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \left( 1 - \frac { 2 } { y + 2 } \right) d y = \int \left( 1 + \frac { 2 } { x } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y - 2 \log | y + 2 | = x + 2 \log | x | + C{/tex} ...(i) Given that y = -1, when x = 1 On putting x = 1 and y = -1 in Eq. (i), we get -1 - 2log(1) = 1 + 2log|1| + C {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 1 = 1 + C \Rightarrow C = - 2{/tex} On putting C = -2 in Eq. (i), we get y - 2log|y + 2| = x + 2log|x| - 2 which is required particular solution.
solution_html:
We have, {tex}x y \frac { d y } { d x } = ( x + 2 ) ( y + 2 ){/tex}
On separating the variables, we get
{tex}\frac { y d y } { y + 2 } = \frac { x + 2 } { x } d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left( \frac { y + 2 - 2 } { y + 2 } \right) d y = \left( 1 + \frac { 2 } { x } \right) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left( 1 - \frac { 2 } { y + 2 } \right) d y = \left( 1 + \frac { 2 } { x } \right) d x{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int \left( 1 - \frac { 2 } { y + 2 } \right) d y = \int \left( 1 + \frac { 2 } { x } \right) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y - 2 \log | y + 2 | = x + 2 \log | x | + C{/tex} ...(i)
Given that y = -1, when x = 1
On putting x = 1 and y = -1 in Eq. (i), we get
-1 - 2log(1) = 1 + 2log|1| + C
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 1 = 1 + C \Rightarrow C = - 2{/tex}
On putting C = -2 in Eq. (i), we get
y - 2log|y + 2| = x + 2log|x| - 2
which is required particular solution.

question_html: Solve the following differential equation {tex}2 x ^ { 2 } \frac { d y } { d x } - 2 x y + y ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex}.
question_text: Solve the following differential equation {tex}2 x ^ { 2 } \frac { d y } { d x } - 2 x y + y ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question, Given differential equation is, {tex}2 x ^ { 2 } \frac { d y } { d x } - 2 x y + y ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } - \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { 2 x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} which is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} . put {tex}y=vx{/tex} , {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = v - \frac { v ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { - v ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \Rightarrow \frac { 2 d v } { v ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { 1 } { x } d x{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}2 \int v ^ { - 2 } d v = - \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 2 v ^ { - 1 } } { - 1 } = - \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { - 2 } { v } = - \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { - 2 x } { y } = - \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 2 x = y ( - \log | x | + C ){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y = \frac { - 2 x } { - \log | x | + C }{/tex} which is the required solution of differential equation.
solution: According to the question, Given differential equation is, {tex}2 x ^ { 2 } \frac { d y } { d x } - 2 x y + y ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } - \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { 2 x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} which is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} . put {tex}y=vx{/tex} , {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = v - \frac { v ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { - v ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \Rightarrow \frac { 2 d v } { v ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { 1 } { x } d x{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}2 \int v ^ { - 2 } d v = - \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 2 v ^ { - 1 } } { - 1 } = - \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { - 2 } { v } = - \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { - 2 x } { y } = - \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 2 x = y ( - \log | x | + C ){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y = \frac { - 2 x } { - \log | x | + C }{/tex} which is the required solution of differential equation.
solution_html:
According to the question,

Given differential equation is,
{tex}2 x ^ { 2 } \frac { d y } { d x } - 2 x y + y ^ { 2 } = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } - \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { 2 x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
which is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} .
put {tex}y=vx{/tex} ,

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = v - \frac { v ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { - v ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \Rightarrow \frac { 2 d v } { v ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { 1 } { x } d x{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}2 \int v ^ { - 2 } d v = - \log | x | + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 2 v ^ { - 1 } } { - 1 } = - \log | x | + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { - 2 } { v } = - \log | x | + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { - 2 x } { y } = - \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 2 x = y ( - \log | x | + C ){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad y = \frac { - 2 x } { - \log | x | + C }{/tex}
which is the required solution of differential equation.

question_html: Solve the following differential equation {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = 1 + x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex}, given that y = 1, when x = 0.
question_text: Solve the following differential equation {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = 1 + x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex} , given that y = 1, when x = 0.
explanation: According to the question , Given differential equation is , {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = 1 + x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = 1 \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) + y ^ { 2 } \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } = \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) d x{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac { d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } = \int \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { - 1 } y = x + \frac { x ^ { 3 } } { 3 } + C{/tex} ...(i) Given that y = 1, when x = 0. On putting x = 0 and y = 1 in Eq. (i), we get tan -1 1 = C {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { - 1 } ( \tan \pi / 4 ) = C \quad \left[ \because \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } = 1 \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = \frac {\pi} {4}{/tex} On putting the value of C in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } y = x + \frac { x ^ { 3 } } { 3 } + \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y = \tan \left( x + \frac { x ^ { 3 } } { 3 } + \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex} which is the required solution of differential equation.
solution: According to the question , Given differential equation is , {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = 1 + x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = 1 \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) + y ^ { 2 } \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } = \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) d x{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac { d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } = \int \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { - 1 } y = x + \frac { x ^ { 3 } } { 3 } + C{/tex} ...(i) Given that y = 1, when x = 0. On putting x = 0 and y = 1 in Eq. (i), we get tan -1 1 = C {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { - 1 } ( \tan \pi / 4 ) = C \quad \left[ \because \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } = 1 \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = \frac {\pi} {4}{/tex} On putting the value of C in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } y = x + \frac { x ^ { 3 } } { 3 } + \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y = \tan \left( x + \frac { x ^ { 3 } } { 3 } + \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex} which is the required solution of differential equation.
solution_html:
According to the question ,

Given differential equation is ,
{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = 1 + x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = 1 \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) + y ^ { 2 } \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } = \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) d x{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int \frac { d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } = \int \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { - 1 } y = x + \frac { x ^ { 3 } } { 3 } + C{/tex} ...(i)
Given that y = 1, when x = 0.
On putting x = 0 and y = 1 in Eq. (i), we get
tan-11 = C
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { - 1 } ( \tan \pi / 4 ) = C \quad \left[ \because \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } = 1 \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = \frac {\pi} {4}{/tex}
On putting the value of C in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } y = x + \frac { x ^ { 3 } } { 3 } + \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad y = \tan \left( x + \frac { x ^ { 3 } } { 3 } + \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right){/tex}
which is the required solution of differential equation.

question_html: Find a solution of {tex} x \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = 1 , {/tex} which satisfy the condition y = 0 when x = 2.
question_text: Find a solution of {tex} x \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = 1 , {/tex} which satisfy the condition y = 0 when x = 2.
explanation: We have differential equation, {tex} x \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { x \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because a ^ { 2 } - b ^ { 2 } = ( a - b ) ( a + b ) \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad d y = \frac { d x } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) }{/tex} Therefore, on integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int d y = \int \frac { d x } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = I + K{/tex} ...(i) where, {tex} I = \int \frac { d x } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) }{/tex} By using partial fraction method, let {tex} \frac { 1 } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) } = \frac { A } { x } + \frac { B } { x - 1 } + \frac { C } { x + 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = A(x - 1)(x + 1) + Bx(x + 1) + Cx(x - 1) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = A(x 2 - 1) + B(x 2 + x) + C(x 2 - x) Therefore, on comparing the coefficients of x 2 , x and constant terms from both sides, we get A + B + C = 0 B - C = 0 and -A = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = -1 Therefore, on solving above equations, we get A = -1, {tex}B = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} and {tex}C = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} , then {tex}\frac { 1 } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) } = \frac { - 1 } { x } + \frac { 1 / 2 } { x - 1 } + \frac { 1 / 2 } { x + 1 }{/tex} Therefore,on integrating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}I = \int \frac { 1 } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { - 1 } { x } d x + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { d x } { x - 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { d x } { x + 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = - \log | x | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x - 1 | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x + 1 |{/tex} Therefore,on putting the value of I in Eq. (i), we get {tex}y = - \log | x | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x - 1 | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x + 1 | + K{/tex} ....(ii) Also, we have, y = 0, when x = 2 Therefore,on putting y = 0 and x = 2 in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}0 = - \log 2 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 3 + K{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad K = \log 2 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad K = \log 2 - \log \sqrt { 3 } \quad \quad [ \because \log 1 = 0 ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad K = \log \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} Therefore,on putting the value of K in Eq. (i), we get {tex}y = - \log | x | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x - 1 | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x + 1 | + \log \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution: We have differential equation, {tex} x \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { x \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because a ^ { 2 } - b ^ { 2 } = ( a - b ) ( a + b ) \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad d y = \frac { d x } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) }{/tex} Therefore, on integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int d y = \int \frac { d x } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = I + K{/tex} ...(i) where, {tex} I = \int \frac { d x } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) }{/tex} By using partial fraction method, let {tex} \frac { 1 } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) } = \frac { A } { x } + \frac { B } { x - 1 } + \frac { C } { x + 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = A(x - 1)(x + 1) + Bx(x + 1) + Cx(x - 1) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = A(x 2 - 1) + B(x 2 + x) + C(x 2 - x) Therefore, on comparing the coefficients of x 2 , x and constant terms from both sides, we get A + B + C = 0 B - C = 0 and -A = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = -1 Therefore, on solving above equations, we get A = -1, {tex}B = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} and {tex}C = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} , then {tex}\frac { 1 } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) } = \frac { - 1 } { x } + \frac { 1 / 2 } { x - 1 } + \frac { 1 / 2 } { x + 1 }{/tex} Therefore,on integrating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}I = \int \frac { 1 } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { - 1 } { x } d x + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { d x } { x - 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { d x } { x + 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = - \log | x | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x - 1 | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x + 1 |{/tex} Therefore,on putting the value of I in Eq. (i), we get {tex}y = - \log | x | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x - 1 | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x + 1 | + K{/tex} ....(ii) Also, we have, y = 0, when x = 2 Therefore,on putting y = 0 and x = 2 in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}0 = - \log 2 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 3 + K{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad K = \log 2 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad K = \log 2 - \log \sqrt { 3 } \quad \quad [ \because \log 1 = 0 ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad K = \log \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} Therefore,on putting the value of K in Eq. (i), we get {tex}y = - \log | x | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x - 1 | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x + 1 | + \log \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution_html:
We have differential equation,
{tex} x \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = 1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { x \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because a ^ { 2 } - b ^ { 2 } = ( a - b ) ( a + b ) \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad d y = \frac { d x } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) }{/tex}
Therefore, on integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int d y = \int \frac { d x } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = I + K{/tex} ...(i)
where, {tex} I = \int \frac { d x } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) }{/tex}
By using partial fraction method,
let {tex} \frac { 1 } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) } = \frac { A } { x } + \frac { B } { x - 1 } + \frac { C } { x + 1 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = A(x - 1)(x + 1) + Bx(x + 1) + Cx(x - 1)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = A(x2 - 1) + B(x2 + x) + C(x2 - x)
Therefore, on comparing the coefficients of x2, x and constant terms from both sides, we get
A + B + C = 0
B - C = 0
and -A = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = -1
Therefore, on solving above equations, we get
A = -1, {tex}B = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} and {tex}C = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex},
then {tex}\frac { 1 } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) } = \frac { - 1 } { x } + \frac { 1 / 2 } { x - 1 } + \frac { 1 / 2 } { x + 1 }{/tex}
Therefore,on integrating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}I = \int \frac { 1 } { x ( x - 1 ) ( x + 1 ) } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { - 1 } { x } d x + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { d x } { x - 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { d x } { x + 1 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = - \log | x | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x - 1 | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x + 1 |{/tex}
Therefore,on putting the value of I in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}y = - \log | x | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x - 1 | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x + 1 | + K{/tex} ....(ii)
Also, we have,  y = 0, when x = 2
Therefore,on putting y = 0 and x = 2 in Eq. (ii), we get
{tex}0 = - \log 2 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 3 + K{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad K = \log 2 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log 3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad K = \log 2 - \log \sqrt { 3 } \quad \quad [ \because \log 1 = 0 ]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad K = \log \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex}
Therefore,on putting the value of K in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}y = - \log | x | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x - 1 | + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log | x + 1 | + \log \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex}
which is the required solution.

question_html: Find the particular solution of the following differential equation {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } - y + x \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = 0{/tex}, given that when x = 2, y = {tex} \pi{/tex}.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the following differential equation {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } - y + x \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = 0{/tex} , given that when x = 2, y = {tex} \pi{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question,we have to find the particular solution of the following differential equation {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } - y + x \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } - \frac { y } { x } + \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = 0{/tex} [dividing both sides by x] ...(i) This is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} . On putting y = vx {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get {tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } - v + \sin v = 0 \Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } + \sin v = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad cosec \ v \ d v + \frac { d x } { x } = 0{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex} \int cosec \; v\ d v + \int \frac { d x } { x } = \log C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \log | cosec \; v - \cot v | + \log x = \log C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x ( cosec \; v - \cot v ) = C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x \left[ cosec \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - \cot \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \right] = C{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} ...(ii) On putting x = 2 and {tex} y = \pi{/tex} in Eq. (ii) we get {tex} 2 \left[ cosec \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) - \cot \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) \right] = C \Rightarrow C = 2{/tex} On putting C = 2 in Eq. (ii), we get {tex} \therefore x \left[ cosec \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - \cot \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \right] = 2{/tex} which is the required particular solution.
solution: According to the question,we have to find the particular solution of the following differential equation {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } - y + x \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } - \frac { y } { x } + \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = 0{/tex} [dividing both sides by x] ...(i) This is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} . On putting y = vx {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get {tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } - v + \sin v = 0 \Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } + \sin v = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad cosec \ v \ d v + \frac { d x } { x } = 0{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex} \int cosec \; v\ d v + \int \frac { d x } { x } = \log C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \log | cosec \; v - \cot v | + \log x = \log C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x ( cosec \; v - \cot v ) = C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x \left[ cosec \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - \cot \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \right] = C{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} ...(ii) On putting x = 2 and {tex} y = \pi{/tex} in Eq. (ii) we get {tex} 2 \left[ cosec \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) - \cot \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) \right] = C \Rightarrow C = 2{/tex} On putting C = 2 in Eq. (ii), we get {tex} \therefore x \left[ cosec \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - \cot \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \right] = 2{/tex} which is the required particular solution.
solution_html:
According to the question,we have to find the particular solution of the following differential equation {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } - y + x \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } - \frac { y } { x } + \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = 0{/tex} [dividing both sides by x] ...(i)
This is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex}.
On putting y = vx {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get
{tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } - v + \sin v = 0 \Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } + \sin v = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad cosec \ v \ d v + \frac { d x } { x } = 0{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex} \int cosec \; v\ d v + \int \frac { d x } { x } = \log C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \log | cosec \; v - \cot v | + \log x = \log C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x ( cosec \; v - \cot v ) = C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x \left[ cosec \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - \cot \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \right] = C{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} ...(ii)
On putting x = 2 and {tex} y = \pi{/tex} in Eq. (ii) we get
{tex} 2 \left[ cosec \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) - \cot \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) \right] = C \Rightarrow C = 2{/tex}
On putting C = 2 in Eq. (ii), we get
{tex} \therefore x \left[ cosec \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - \cot \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) \right] = 2{/tex}
which is the required particular solution.

question_html: Solve the following differential equation {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } - y = \cos x{/tex}, if x = 0, y = 1.
question_text: Solve the following differential equation {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } - y = \cos x{/tex} , if x = 0, y = 1.
explanation: According to the question , {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } - y = \cos x{/tex} This is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q {/tex} , Where {tex} P = -1 \ and \ Q = cos x{/tex} we know that , {tex} \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { Pd } x } = e ^ { \int ( - 1 ) d x } = e ^ { - x }{/tex} The general solution is given by {tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( \mathrm { IF } \times Q ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { - x } = \int e ^ { - x } \cos x d x + C{/tex} ...(i) Now, {tex}\int {\mathop {{e^{ - x}}}\limits_I } \mathop {\cos }\limits_{II} xdx = {e^{ - x}}\sin x + \int {\mathop {{e^{ - x}}}\limits_I } \mathop {\sin }\limits_{II} xdx{/tex} by using integrating by parts for second integral , we get {tex}= e ^ { - x } \sin x - e ^ { - x } \cos x - \int e ^ { - x } \cos x d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \int e ^ { - x } \cos x d x = e ^ { - x } ( \sin x - \cos x ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int e ^ { - x } \cos x d x = \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { - x } ( \sin x - \cos x ){/tex} On substituting this value in Eq. (i), we get {tex}y \cdot e ^ { - x } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { - x } ( \sin x - \cos x ) + C{/tex} ...(ii) On putting x = 0, y = 1 in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}1 \cdot e ^ { - 0 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { - 0 } ( \sin 0 - \cos 0 ) + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( - 1 ) + C \Rightarrow C = \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex} On putting {tex}C = \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}y \cdot e ^ { - x } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { - x } ( \sin x - \cos x ) + \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( \sin x - \cos x ) + \frac { 3 } { 2 } e ^ { x }{/tex} which is the required particular solution to differential equation.
solution: According to the question , {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } - y = \cos x{/tex} This is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q {/tex} , Where {tex} P = -1 \ and \ Q = cos x{/tex} we know that , {tex} \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { Pd } x } = e ^ { \int ( - 1 ) d x } = e ^ { - x }{/tex} The general solution is given by {tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( \mathrm { IF } \times Q ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { - x } = \int e ^ { - x } \cos x d x + C{/tex} ...(i) Now, {tex}\int {\mathop {{e^{ - x}}}\limits_I } \mathop {\cos }\limits_{II} xdx = {e^{ - x}}\sin x + \int {\mathop {{e^{ - x}}}\limits_I } \mathop {\sin }\limits_{II} xdx{/tex} by using integrating by parts for second integral , we get {tex}= e ^ { - x } \sin x - e ^ { - x } \cos x - \int e ^ { - x } \cos x d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \int e ^ { - x } \cos x d x = e ^ { - x } ( \sin x - \cos x ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int e ^ { - x } \cos x d x = \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { - x } ( \sin x - \cos x ){/tex} On substituting this value in Eq. (i), we get {tex}y \cdot e ^ { - x } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { - x } ( \sin x - \cos x ) + C{/tex} ...(ii) On putting x = 0, y = 1 in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}1 \cdot e ^ { - 0 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { - 0 } ( \sin 0 - \cos 0 ) + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( - 1 ) + C \Rightarrow C = \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex} On putting {tex}C = \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}y \cdot e ^ { - x } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { - x } ( \sin x - \cos x ) + \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( \sin x - \cos x ) + \frac { 3 } { 2 } e ^ { x }{/tex} which is the required particular solution to differential equation.
solution_html:
According to the question ,  {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } - y = \cos x{/tex}
This is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q {/tex} ,
Where {tex} P = -1 \ and \ Q = cos x{/tex}

we know that , 
{tex} \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { Pd } x } = e ^ { \int ( - 1 ) d x } = e ^ { - x }{/tex}
The general solution is given by
{tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( \mathrm { IF } \times Q ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { - x } = \int e ^ { - x } \cos x d x + C{/tex} ...(i)
Now, {tex}\int {\mathop {{e^{ - x}}}\limits_I } \mathop {\cos }\limits_{II} xdx = {e^{ - x}}\sin x + \int {\mathop {{e^{ - x}}}\limits_I } \mathop {\sin }\limits_{II} xdx{/tex} 

 by using integrating by parts for second integral , we get 
{tex}= e ^ { - x } \sin x - e ^ { - x } \cos x - \int e ^ { - x } \cos x d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \int e ^ { - x } \cos x d x = e ^ { - x } ( \sin x - \cos x ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int e ^ { - x } \cos x d x = \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { - x } ( \sin x - \cos x ){/tex}
On substituting this value in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}y \cdot e ^ { - x } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { - x } ( \sin x - \cos x ) + C{/tex} ...(ii)
On putting x = 0, y = 1 in Eq. (ii), we get
{tex}1 \cdot e ^ { - 0 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { - 0 } ( \sin 0 - \cos 0 ) + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( - 1 ) + C \Rightarrow C = \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex}
On putting {tex}C = \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (ii), we get
{tex}y \cdot e ^ { - x } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { - x } ( \sin x - \cos x ) + \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( \sin x - \cos x ) + \frac { 3 } { 2 } e ^ { x }{/tex}
which is the required particular solution to differential equation.

question_html: Find the particular solution of the following differential equation, given that x = 2, y = 1  {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y = x ^ { 2 } , ( x \neq 0 ){/tex}.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the following differential equation, given that x = 2, y = 1 {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y = x ^ { 2 } , ( x \neq 0 ){/tex} .
explanation: We have, {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y = x ^ { 2 } ( x \neq 0 ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } + \left( \frac { 2 } { x } \right) \cdot y = x{/tex} ...(i) This is linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} , here {tex} P = \frac { 2 } { x }{/tex} and Q = x. {tex} \therefore \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { Pd } x } = e ^ { \int ( 2 / x ) d x }{/tex} {tex} = e ^ { 2 \log x } = e ^ { \log x ^ { 2 } } = x ^ { 2 }{/tex} The general solution is given by {tex}y \cdot {I F } = \int ( \mathrm { IF } \times Q ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \cdot x ^ { 2 } = \int x ^ { 2 } \times x d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \cdot x ^ { 2 } = \int x ^ { 3 } d x + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y \cdot x ^ { 2 } = \frac { x ^ { 4 } } { 4 } + C{/tex} ...(ii) On putting x = 2, y = 1 in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}1 \cdot 2 ^ { 2 } = \frac { 2 ^ { 4 } } { 4 } + C \Rightarrow 4 = 4 + C \Rightarrow C = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y \cdot x ^ { 2 } = \frac { x ^ { 4 } } { 4 }{/tex} [from Eq. (ii)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} which is the required particular solution.
solution: We have, {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y = x ^ { 2 } ( x \neq 0 ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } + \left( \frac { 2 } { x } \right) \cdot y = x{/tex} ...(i) This is linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} , here {tex} P = \frac { 2 } { x }{/tex} and Q = x. {tex} \therefore \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { Pd } x } = e ^ { \int ( 2 / x ) d x }{/tex} {tex} = e ^ { 2 \log x } = e ^ { \log x ^ { 2 } } = x ^ { 2 }{/tex} The general solution is given by {tex}y \cdot {I F } = \int ( \mathrm { IF } \times Q ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \cdot x ^ { 2 } = \int x ^ { 2 } \times x d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \cdot x ^ { 2 } = \int x ^ { 3 } d x + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y \cdot x ^ { 2 } = \frac { x ^ { 4 } } { 4 } + C{/tex} ...(ii) On putting x = 2, y = 1 in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}1 \cdot 2 ^ { 2 } = \frac { 2 ^ { 4 } } { 4 } + C \Rightarrow 4 = 4 + C \Rightarrow C = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y \cdot x ^ { 2 } = \frac { x ^ { 4 } } { 4 }{/tex} [from Eq. (ii)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} which is the required particular solution.
solution_html:
We have, {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y = x ^ { 2 } ( x \neq 0 ){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } + \left( \frac { 2 } { x } \right) \cdot y = x{/tex} ...(i)
This is linear differential equation of the form
{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex}, here {tex} P = \frac { 2 } { x }{/tex} and Q = x.
{tex} \therefore \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { Pd } x } = e ^ { \int ( 2 / x ) d x }{/tex} {tex} = e ^ { 2 \log x } = e ^ { \log x ^ { 2 } } = x ^ { 2 }{/tex} 
The general solution is given by
{tex}y \cdot {I F } = \int ( \mathrm { IF } \times Q ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \cdot x ^ { 2 } = \int x ^ { 2 } \times x d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \cdot x ^ { 2 } = \int x ^ { 3 } d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad y \cdot x ^ { 2 } = \frac { x ^ { 4 } } { 4 } + C{/tex} ...(ii)
On putting x = 2, y = 1 in Eq. (ii), we get
{tex}1 \cdot 2 ^ { 2 } = \frac { 2 ^ { 4 } } { 4 } + C \Rightarrow 4 = 4 + C \Rightarrow C = 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad y \cdot x ^ { 2 } = \frac { x ^ { 4 } } { 4 }{/tex} [from Eq. (ii)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex}
which is the required particular solution.

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \cot x = 2 x + x ^ { 2 } \cot x {/tex} ({tex} x \neq 0{/tex}) given that y = 0, when {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \cot x = 2 x + x ^ { 2 } \cot x {/tex} ( {tex} x \neq 0{/tex} ) given that y = 0, when {tex} x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} .
explanation: We have, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \cot x = 2 x + x ^ { 2 } \cot x , ( x \neq 0 ){/tex} This is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} . Here, P = cot x and Q = 2x + x 2 cot x. {tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { Pdx } } = e ^ { \int \cot x d x }{/tex} {tex} = e ^ { \log | \sin x | } = \sin x{/tex} The general solution is given by {tex}y \cdot \mathrm { IF } = \int ( \mathrm { IF } \times Q ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sin x = \int \left( 2 x + x ^ { 2 } \cot x \right) \sin x d x + C{/tex} {tex}= 2 \int x \sin x d x + \int x ^ { 2 } \cos x d x + c{/tex} {tex}= 2 \int x \sin x d x + x ^ { 2 } \sin x - \int 2 x \sin x d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \cdot \sin x = x ^ { 2 } \sin x + C{/tex} ...(i) On putting {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} and y = 0 in Eq. (i), we get {tex}0 \cdot \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } = \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \cdot \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } + C \Rightarrow C = - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} On putting {tex} C = \frac { - \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get {tex}y \cdot \sin x = x ^ { 2 } \sin x - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y = x ^ { 2 } - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 } cosec x{/tex} [divding both sides by sin x]
solution: We have, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \cot x = 2 x + x ^ { 2 } \cot x , ( x \neq 0 ){/tex} This is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} . Here, P = cot x and Q = 2x + x 2 cot x. {tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { Pdx } } = e ^ { \int \cot x d x }{/tex} {tex} = e ^ { \log | \sin x | } = \sin x{/tex} The general solution is given by {tex}y \cdot \mathrm { IF } = \int ( \mathrm { IF } \times Q ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sin x = \int \left( 2 x + x ^ { 2 } \cot x \right) \sin x d x + C{/tex} {tex}= 2 \int x \sin x d x + \int x ^ { 2 } \cos x d x + c{/tex} {tex}= 2 \int x \sin x d x + x ^ { 2 } \sin x - \int 2 x \sin x d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \cdot \sin x = x ^ { 2 } \sin x + C{/tex} ...(i) On putting {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} and y = 0 in Eq. (i), we get {tex}0 \cdot \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } = \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \cdot \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } + C \Rightarrow C = - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} On putting {tex} C = \frac { - \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get {tex}y \cdot \sin x = x ^ { 2 } \sin x - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y = x ^ { 2 } - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 } cosec x{/tex} [divding both sides by sin x]
solution_html:
We have, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + y \cot x = 2 x + x ^ { 2 } \cot x , ( x \neq 0 ){/tex}
This is a linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex}.
Here, P = cot x and Q = 2x + x2 cot x.
{tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { Pdx } } = e ^ { \int \cot x d x }{/tex} {tex} = e ^ { \log | \sin x | } = \sin x{/tex}
The general solution is given by
{tex}y \cdot \mathrm { IF } = \int ( \mathrm { IF } \times Q ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sin x = \int \left( 2 x + x ^ { 2 } \cot x \right) \sin x d x + C{/tex}
{tex}= 2 \int x \sin x d x + \int x ^ { 2 } \cos x d x + c{/tex}
{tex}= 2 \int x \sin x d x + x ^ { 2 } \sin x - \int 2 x \sin x d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \cdot \sin x = x ^ { 2 } \sin x + C{/tex} ...(i)
On putting {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} and y = 0 in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}0 \cdot \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } = \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \cdot \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } + C \Rightarrow C = - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex}
On putting {tex} C = \frac { - \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}y \cdot \sin x = x ^ { 2 } \sin x - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad y = x ^ { 2 } - \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 } cosec x{/tex}
[divding both sides by sin x]

question_html: For the differential equation {tex}x y \frac{d y}{d x}=(x+2)(y+2){/tex}, find the solution curve passing through the point (1, -1).
question_text: For the differential equation {tex}x y \frac{d y}{d x}=(x+2)(y+2){/tex} , find the solution curve passing through the point (1, -1).
explanation: For this question, we need to find the particular solution at point(1, -1) for the given differential equation. Given differential equation is {tex}\Rightarrow x y \frac{d y}{d x}=(x+2)(y+2){/tex} Separating variables, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{y+2} d y=\frac{(x+2) d x}{x}{/tex} Or, {tex}\Rightarrow\left(1-\frac{2}{\mathrm{y}+2}\right) \mathrm{dy}=\left(1+\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} Integrating both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow \int\left(1-\frac{2}{y+2}\right) d y=\int\left(1+\frac{2}{x}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int d y-2 \int \frac{1}{y+2} d y=\int d x+2 \int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y-2 \log (y+2)=x+2 \log x+C{/tex} Now separating like terms on each side, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - x - c = 2 log x + 2 log (y + 2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - x - c = log x 2 + log(y + 2) 2 Or, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - x - c = log{x 2 (y + 2) 2 } .....(i) Now we are given that, the curve passes through (1, -1) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -1 - 1 - c = log{1(-1 + 2) 2 } {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -2 - c = log (1) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = -2 + 0 ( {tex}\because{/tex} log(1) = 0) So c = -2 Putting the value of c in (i) y - x - (-2) = log{x 2 (y + 2) 2 } y - x + 2 = log{x 2 (y + 2) 2 }
solution: For this question, we need to find the particular solution at point(1, -1) for the given differential equation. Given differential equation is {tex}\Rightarrow x y \frac{d y}{d x}=(x+2)(y+2){/tex} Separating variables, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{y+2} d y=\frac{(x+2) d x}{x}{/tex} Or, {tex}\Rightarrow\left(1-\frac{2}{\mathrm{y}+2}\right) \mathrm{dy}=\left(1+\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} Integrating both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow \int\left(1-\frac{2}{y+2}\right) d y=\int\left(1+\frac{2}{x}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int d y-2 \int \frac{1}{y+2} d y=\int d x+2 \int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y-2 \log (y+2)=x+2 \log x+C{/tex} Now separating like terms on each side, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - x - c = 2 log x + 2 log (y + 2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - x - c = log x 2 + log(y + 2) 2 Or, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - x - c = log{x 2 (y + 2) 2 } .....(i) Now we are given that, the curve passes through (1, -1) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -1 - 1 - c = log{1(-1 + 2) 2 } {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -2 - c = log (1) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = -2 + 0 ( {tex}\because{/tex} log(1) = 0) So c = -2 Putting the value of c in (i) y - x - (-2) = log{x 2 (y + 2) 2 } y - x + 2 = log{x 2 (y + 2) 2 }
solution_html:
For this question, we need to find the particular solution at point(1, -1) for the given differential equation.
Given differential equation is
{tex}\Rightarrow x y \frac{d y}{d x}=(x+2)(y+2){/tex} 
Separating variables,
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{y+2} d y=\frac{(x+2) d x}{x}{/tex} 
Or,
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(1-\frac{2}{\mathrm{y}+2}\right) \mathrm{dy}=\left(1+\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} 
Integrating both sides,
{tex}\Rightarrow \int\left(1-\frac{2}{y+2}\right) d y=\int\left(1+\frac{2}{x}\right) d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \int d y-2 \int \frac{1}{y+2} d y=\int d x+2 \int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow y-2 \log (y+2)=x+2 \log x+C{/tex} 
Now separating like terms on each side,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - x - c = 2 log x + 2 log (y + 2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - x - c = log x2 + log(y + 2)2
Or,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - x - c = log{x2(y + 2)2} .....(i)
Now we are given that, the curve passes through (1, -1)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}-1 - 1 - c = log{1(-1 + 2)2}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -2 - c = log (1)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = -2 + 0 ({tex}\because{/tex} log(1) = 0)
So c = -2
Putting the value of c in (i)
y - x - (-2) = log{x2(y + 2)2}
y - x + 2 = log{x2(y + 2)2}

question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-y=\cos x{/tex}
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-y=\cos x{/tex}
explanation: Given differential equation is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\mathrm{Py}=\mathrm{Q}{/tex} , where P = -1 and Q = cos x Therefore, {tex}\mathrm{I} \cdot \mathrm{F}=e^{\int-1 d x}=e^{-x}{/tex} Multiplying both sides of equation by I.F, we get {tex}e^{-x} \frac{d y}{d x}-e^{-x} y=e^{-x} \cos x{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d }{d x}\left(y e^{-x}\right)=e^{-x} \cos x{/tex} On integrating both sides with respect to x, we get {tex}y e^{-x}=\int e^{-x} \cos x d x+C{/tex} ......(i) Let {tex}I=\int e^{-x} \cos x d x{/tex} = {tex}\cos x\left(\frac{e^{-x}}{-1}\right)-\int(-\sin x)\left(-e^{-x}\right) d x{/tex} = {tex}-\cos x e^{-x}-\int \sin x e^{-x} d x{/tex} = {tex}-\cos x e^{-x}-\left[\sin x\left(-e^{-x}\right)-\int \cos x\left(-e^{-x}\right) d x\right]{/tex} = {tex}-\cos x e^{-x}+\sin x e^{-x}-\int \cos x e^{-x} d x{/tex} or {tex}I=-e^{-x} \cos x+\sin x e^{-x}-I{/tex} or {tex}2 \mathrm{I}=(\sin x-\cos x) e^{-x}{/tex} or {tex}I=\frac{(\sin x-\cos x) e^{-x}}{2}{/tex} Substituting the value of I in equation (i), we get {tex}y e^{-x}=\left(\frac{\sin x-\cos x}{2}\right) e^{-x}+C{/tex} or {tex}y=\left(\frac{\sin x-\cos x}{2}\right)+\mathrm{C} e^{x}{/tex} which is the general solution of the given differential equation.
solution: Given differential equation is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\mathrm{Py}=\mathrm{Q}{/tex} , where P = -1 and Q = cos x Therefore, {tex}\mathrm{I} \cdot \mathrm{F}=e^{\int-1 d x}=e^{-x}{/tex} Multiplying both sides of equation by I.F, we get {tex}e^{-x} \frac{d y}{d x}-e^{-x} y=e^{-x} \cos x{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d }{d x}\left(y e^{-x}\right)=e^{-x} \cos x{/tex} On integrating both sides with respect to x, we get {tex}y e^{-x}=\int e^{-x} \cos x d x+C{/tex} ......(i) Let {tex}I=\int e^{-x} \cos x d x{/tex} = {tex}\cos x\left(\frac{e^{-x}}{-1}\right)-\int(-\sin x)\left(-e^{-x}\right) d x{/tex} = {tex}-\cos x e^{-x}-\int \sin x e^{-x} d x{/tex} = {tex}-\cos x e^{-x}-\left[\sin x\left(-e^{-x}\right)-\int \cos x\left(-e^{-x}\right) d x\right]{/tex} = {tex}-\cos x e^{-x}+\sin x e^{-x}-\int \cos x e^{-x} d x{/tex} or {tex}I=-e^{-x} \cos x+\sin x e^{-x}-I{/tex} or {tex}2 \mathrm{I}=(\sin x-\cos x) e^{-x}{/tex} or {tex}I=\frac{(\sin x-\cos x) e^{-x}}{2}{/tex} Substituting the value of I in equation (i), we get {tex}y e^{-x}=\left(\frac{\sin x-\cos x}{2}\right) e^{-x}+C{/tex} or {tex}y=\left(\frac{\sin x-\cos x}{2}\right)+\mathrm{C} e^{x}{/tex} which is the general solution of the given differential equation.
solution_html:
Given differential equation is of the form
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\mathrm{Py}=\mathrm{Q}{/tex}, where P = -1 and Q = cos x
Therefore, {tex}\mathrm{I} \cdot \mathrm{F}=e^{\int-1 d x}=e^{-x}{/tex} 
Multiplying both sides of equation by I.F, we get
{tex}e^{-x} \frac{d y}{d x}-e^{-x} y=e^{-x} \cos x{/tex} 
or {tex}\frac{d }{d x}\left(y e^{-x}\right)=e^{-x} \cos x{/tex} 
On integrating both sides with respect to x, we get
{tex}y e^{-x}=\int e^{-x} \cos x d x+C{/tex} ......(i)
Let {tex}I=\int e^{-x} \cos x d x{/tex} 
= {tex}\cos x\left(\frac{e^{-x}}{-1}\right)-\int(-\sin x)\left(-e^{-x}\right) d x{/tex} 
= {tex}-\cos x e^{-x}-\int \sin x e^{-x} d x{/tex} 
= {tex}-\cos x e^{-x}-\left[\sin x\left(-e^{-x}\right)-\int \cos x\left(-e^{-x}\right) d x\right]{/tex} 
= {tex}-\cos x e^{-x}+\sin x e^{-x}-\int \cos x e^{-x} d x{/tex} 
or {tex}I=-e^{-x} \cos x+\sin x e^{-x}-I{/tex} 
or {tex}2 \mathrm{I}=(\sin x-\cos x) e^{-x}{/tex} 
or {tex}I=\frac{(\sin x-\cos x) e^{-x}}{2}{/tex} 
Substituting the value of I in equation (i), we get
{tex}y e^{-x}=\left(\frac{\sin x-\cos x}{2}\right) e^{-x}+C{/tex} 
or {tex}y=\left(\frac{\sin x-\cos x}{2}\right)+\mathrm{C} e^{x}{/tex} 
which is the general solution of the given differential equation.
