question_html: Solve the initial value problem: x(x2 - 1) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 1; y(2) = 0
question_text: Solve the initial value problem: x(x 2 - 1) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 1; y(2) = 0
explanation: We have, x(x 2 - 1) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{x\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow d y=\left\{\frac{1}{x\left(x^{2}-1\right)}\right\} d x{/tex} (Separating the variables) Integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int d y=\int\left\{\frac{1}{x\left(x^{2}-1\right)}\right\} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\int\left\{\frac{1}{x\left(x^{2}-1\right)}\right\} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\int \frac{1}{x(x-1)(x+1)} d x{/tex} Let {tex}\frac{1}{x(x-1)(x+1)}=\frac{A}{x}+\frac{B}{x-1}+\frac{C}{x+1}{/tex} ( using partial fractions) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = A (x 2 - 1) + B (x 2 + x) + C (x 2 - x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = (A + B + C) x 2 + (B - C) x - A Equating the coefficients of various powers x on both sides we get, A + B + C = 0 ...(i) B - C = 0 ...(ii) A = -1 ...(iii) Solving (i), (ii) and (iii), we get A = -1 B = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore y=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{x-1} d x-\int \frac{1}{x} d x+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{x+1} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |x - 1| - log |x| + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |x + 1| + C = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |x - 1| + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |x + 1| - log |x| + C It is given that y(2) = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} 0 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |2 - 1| + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |2 + 1| - log |2| + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = log |2| - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |3| Substituting the value of C, we get y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |x - 1| + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |x + 1| - log |x| + log |2| - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |3| {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} 2y = log |x - 1| + log |x + 1| - 2 log |x| + 2 log|2| - log |3| {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y = log |x - 1| + log |x + 1| - log |x 2 | + log 4 - log 3 {tex}\Rightarrow 2 y=\log \frac{4(x-1)(x+1)}{3 x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{2} \log \frac{4\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{3 x^{2}}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{2} \log \frac{4\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{3 x^{2}}{/tex} is the solution to the given differential equation.
solution: We have, x(x 2 - 1) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{x\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow d y=\left\{\frac{1}{x\left(x^{2}-1\right)}\right\} d x{/tex} (Separating the variables) Integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int d y=\int\left\{\frac{1}{x\left(x^{2}-1\right)}\right\} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\int\left\{\frac{1}{x\left(x^{2}-1\right)}\right\} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\int \frac{1}{x(x-1)(x+1)} d x{/tex} Let {tex}\frac{1}{x(x-1)(x+1)}=\frac{A}{x}+\frac{B}{x-1}+\frac{C}{x+1}{/tex} ( using partial fractions) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = A (x 2 - 1) + B (x 2 + x) + C (x 2 - x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = (A + B + C) x 2 + (B - C) x - A Equating the coefficients of various powers x on both sides we get, A + B + C = 0 ...(i) B - C = 0 ...(ii) A = -1 ...(iii) Solving (i), (ii) and (iii), we get A = -1 B = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore y=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{x-1} d x-\int \frac{1}{x} d x+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{x+1} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |x - 1| - log |x| + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |x + 1| + C = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |x - 1| + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |x + 1| - log |x| + C It is given that y(2) = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} 0 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |2 - 1| + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |2 + 1| - log |2| + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = log |2| - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |3| Substituting the value of C, we get y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |x - 1| + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |x + 1| - log |x| + log |2| - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |3| {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} 2y = log |x - 1| + log |x + 1| - 2 log |x| + 2 log|2| - log |3| {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y = log |x - 1| + log |x + 1| - log |x 2 | + log 4 - log 3 {tex}\Rightarrow 2 y=\log \frac{4(x-1)(x+1)}{3 x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{2} \log \frac{4\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{3 x^{2}}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{2} \log \frac{4\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{3 x^{2}}{/tex} is the solution to the given differential equation.
solution_html:
We have, x(x2 - 1) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{x\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow d y=\left\{\frac{1}{x\left(x^{2}-1\right)}\right\} d x{/tex} (Separating the variables)
Integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int d y=\int\left\{\frac{1}{x\left(x^{2}-1\right)}\right\} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=\int\left\{\frac{1}{x\left(x^{2}-1\right)}\right\} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=\int \frac{1}{x(x-1)(x+1)} d x{/tex}
Let {tex}\frac{1}{x(x-1)(x+1)}=\frac{A}{x}+\frac{B}{x-1}+\frac{C}{x+1}{/tex}   ( using partial fractions)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = A (x2 - 1) + B (x2 + x) + C (x2 - x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = (A + B + C) x2 + (B - C) x - A
Equating the coefficients of various powers x on both sides we get,
A + B + C = 0 ...(i)
B - C = 0 ...(ii)
A = -1 ...(iii)
Solving (i), (ii) and (iii), we get
A = -1
B = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
C = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore y=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{x-1} d x-\int \frac{1}{x} d x+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{x+1} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |x - 1| - log |x| + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |x + 1| + C
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |x - 1| + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |x + 1| - log |x| + C
It is given that y(2) = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |2 - 1| + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |2 + 1| - log |2| + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = log |2| - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |3|
Substituting the value of C, we get
y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |x - 1| + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |x + 1| - log |x| + log |2| - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |3|
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} 2y = log |x - 1| + log |x + 1| - 2 log |x| + 2 log|2| - log |3|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y = log |x - 1| + log |x + 1| - log |x2| + log 4 - log 3
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 y=\log \frac{4(x-1)(x+1)}{3 x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{2} \log \frac{4\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{3 x^{2}}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{2} \log \frac{4\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{3 x^{2}}{/tex} is the solution to the given differential equation.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the initial value problem: (x+1) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 2e-y -1, y(0) = 0
question_text: Solve the initial value problem: (x+1) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 2e -y -1, y(0) = 0
explanation: We have, (x+1) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 2e -y -1, y(0) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 1) dy = (2e -y - 1) dx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{x+1} d x=\frac{1}{2 e^{-y}-1} d y{/tex} [On separating the variables] Integrating both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{x+1} d x=\int \frac{1}{2 e^{-y}-1} d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{x+1} d x=\int \frac{e^{y}}{2-e^{y}} d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{x+1} d x=-\int \frac{e^{y}}{e^{y}-2} d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |x + 1| = - log |e y - 2| + log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |x + 1| + log |e y - 2| + log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |(x + 1) (e y - 2)| = log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |(x + 1) (e y - 2) | = C ...(i) It is given that y (0) = 0 i.e. y = 0. Putting x = 0 and y = 0 in (i), we get |(0 + 1) (1 - 2)| = C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1 Putting C = 1 in (i), we get |(x + 1) (e y - 2) = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 1) (e y - 2) = {tex}\pm 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{y}-2=-\frac{1}{x+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{y}=\left(2-\frac{1}{x+1}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\log \left(2-\frac{1}{x+1}\right){/tex} , which is required solution.
solution: We have, (x+1) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 2e -y -1, y(0) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 1) dy = (2e -y - 1) dx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{x+1} d x=\frac{1}{2 e^{-y}-1} d y{/tex} [On separating the variables] Integrating both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{x+1} d x=\int \frac{1}{2 e^{-y}-1} d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{x+1} d x=\int \frac{e^{y}}{2-e^{y}} d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{x+1} d x=-\int \frac{e^{y}}{e^{y}-2} d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |x + 1| = - log |e y - 2| + log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |x + 1| + log |e y - 2| + log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |(x + 1) (e y - 2)| = log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |(x + 1) (e y - 2) | = C ...(i) It is given that y (0) = 0 i.e. y = 0. Putting x = 0 and y = 0 in (i), we get |(0 + 1) (1 - 2)| = C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1 Putting C = 1 in (i), we get |(x + 1) (e y - 2) = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 1) (e y - 2) = {tex}\pm 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{y}-2=-\frac{1}{x+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{y}=\left(2-\frac{1}{x+1}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\log \left(2-\frac{1}{x+1}\right){/tex} , which is required solution.
solution_html:
We have, (x+1) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 2e-y -1, y(0) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 1) dy = (2e-y - 1) dx
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{x+1} d x=\frac{1}{2 e^{-y}-1} d y{/tex} [On separating the variables]
Integrating both sides,
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{x+1} d x=\int \frac{1}{2 e^{-y}-1} d y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{x+1} d x=\int \frac{e^{y}}{2-e^{y}} d y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{x+1} d x=-\int \frac{e^{y}}{e^{y}-2} d y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |x + 1| = - log |ey - 2| + log C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |x + 1| + log |ey - 2| + log C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |(x + 1) (ey - 2)| = log C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |(x + 1) (ey - 2) | = C ...(i)
It is given that y (0) = 0 i.e. y = 0. Putting x = 0 and y = 0 in (i), we get
|(0 + 1) (1 - 2)| = C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1
Putting C = 1 in (i), we get
|(x + 1) (ey - 2) = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 1) (ey - 2) = {tex}\pm 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow e^{y}-2=-\frac{1}{x+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow e^{y}=\left(2-\frac{1}{x+1}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=\log \left(2-\frac{1}{x+1}\right){/tex}, which is required solution.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the initial value problem: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 1 + x2  + y2 + x2 y2, y(0) = 1
question_text: Solve the initial value problem: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 1 + x 2 + y 2 + x 2 y 2 , y(0) = 1
explanation: We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 1 + x 2 + y 2 + x 2 y 2 , y(0) = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = (1 + x 2 ) (1 + y 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{\left(1+y^{2}\right)}{/tex} = (1 + x 2 ) dx Integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac{d y}{\left(1+y^{2}\right)}=\int{/tex} (1 + x 2 ) dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan- 1 y = x + {tex}\frac{x^{3}}{3}{/tex} + C ...equation(i) We know that at x = 0, y = 1 Substituting the values of x and y in equation(i), we get {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} = 0 + 0 + C {tex}\Rightarrow C=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} Substituting the value of C in equation(i), we get {tex}\tan -^{1} y=x+\frac{x^{3}}{3}+\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\tan -^{1} y=x+\frac{x^{3}}{3}+\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} is the required solution.
solution: We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 1 + x 2 + y 2 + x 2 y 2 , y(0) = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = (1 + x 2 ) (1 + y 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{\left(1+y^{2}\right)}{/tex} = (1 + x 2 ) dx Integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac{d y}{\left(1+y^{2}\right)}=\int{/tex} (1 + x 2 ) dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan- 1 y = x + {tex}\frac{x^{3}}{3}{/tex} + C ...equation(i) We know that at x = 0, y = 1 Substituting the values of x and y in equation(i), we get {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} = 0 + 0 + C {tex}\Rightarrow C=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} Substituting the value of C in equation(i), we get {tex}\tan -^{1} y=x+\frac{x^{3}}{3}+\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\tan -^{1} y=x+\frac{x^{3}}{3}+\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} is the required solution.
solution_html:
We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 1 + x2  + y2 + x2 y2, y(0) = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = (1 + x2) (1 + y2)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{\left(1+y^{2}\right)}{/tex} = (1 + x2) dx
Integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int \frac{d y}{\left(1+y^{2}\right)}=\int{/tex} (1 + x2) dx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan-1y = x + {tex}\frac{x^{3}}{3}{/tex} + C ...equation(i)
We know that at x = 0, y = 1
Substituting the values of x and y in equation(i), we get
{tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} = 0 + 0 + C
{tex}\Rightarrow C=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
Substituting the value of C in equation(i), we get
{tex}\tan -^{1} y=x+\frac{x^{3}}{3}+\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\tan -^{1} y=x+\frac{x^{3}}{3}+\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} is the required solution.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the initial value problem: {tex}x y \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = (x + 2) (y + 2), y(1) = -1
question_text: Solve the initial value problem: {tex}x y \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = (x + 2) (y + 2), y(1) = -1
explanation: Given that, {tex}x y \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = (x + 2) (y + 2), y(1) = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{y+2} d y=\frac{x+2}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y+2-2}{y+2} d y=\frac{x+2}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(1-\frac{2}{y+2}\right) d y=\left(1+\frac{2}{x}\right) d x{/tex} Integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int\left(1-\frac{2}{y+2}\right) d y=\int\left(1+\frac{2}{x}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - 2 log |y + 2| = x + 2 log |x| + C ...(i) Given that y(1) = -1, i,e, at x = 1, y = -1 Substituting the values of x and y in (i), we get -1 - 2 log |1| = 1 + 2 log |1| + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - 1 = 1 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = -2 Substituting the value of C in (i), we get y - 2 log |y + 2| = x + 2 log |x| - 2 Hence, y - 2 log |y + 2| = x + 2 log |x| - 2 is the required solution.
solution: Given that, {tex}x y \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = (x + 2) (y + 2), y(1) = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{y+2} d y=\frac{x+2}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y+2-2}{y+2} d y=\frac{x+2}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(1-\frac{2}{y+2}\right) d y=\left(1+\frac{2}{x}\right) d x{/tex} Integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int\left(1-\frac{2}{y+2}\right) d y=\int\left(1+\frac{2}{x}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - 2 log |y + 2| = x + 2 log |x| + C ...(i) Given that y(1) = -1, i,e, at x = 1, y = -1 Substituting the values of x and y in (i), we get -1 - 2 log |1| = 1 + 2 log |1| + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - 1 = 1 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = -2 Substituting the value of C in (i), we get y - 2 log |y + 2| = x + 2 log |x| - 2 Hence, y - 2 log |y + 2| = x + 2 log |x| - 2 is the required solution.
solution_html:
Given that, 
{tex}x y \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = (x + 2) (y + 2), y(1) = -1
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{y+2} d y=\frac{x+2}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y+2-2}{y+2} d y=\frac{x+2}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(1-\frac{2}{y+2}\right) d y=\left(1+\frac{2}{x}\right) d x{/tex}
Integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int\left(1-\frac{2}{y+2}\right) d y=\int\left(1+\frac{2}{x}\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - 2 log |y + 2| = x + 2 log |x| + C ...(i)
Given that y(1) = -1, i,e, at x = 1, y = -1
Substituting the values of x and y in (i), we get
-1 - 2 log |1| = 1 + 2 log |1| + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - 1 = 1 + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = -2
Substituting the value of C in (i), we get
y - 2 log |y + 2| = x + 2 log |x| - 2
Hence, y - 2 log |y + 2| = x + 2 log |x| - 2 is the required solution.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the initial value problem: {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x} \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+x-y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=0, y(1)=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
question_text: Solve the initial value problem: {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x} \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+x-y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=0, y(1)=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
explanation: Given that, {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x} \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+x-y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{x-y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{x \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{/tex} This is a homogeneous differential equation. Putting y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} , given equation reduces to {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{1-v \sin v}{\sin v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{1-v \sin v}{\sin v}-v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{1}{\sin v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin v dv = {tex}-\frac{1}{x}{/tex} dx, if x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 Integrating both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow \int{/tex} sin v dv = {tex}-\int \frac{1}{x}{/tex} dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - cos v = - log |x| + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} + log |x| = C ...(i) It is given that y (1) = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} i.e., when x = 1, y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} . Putting x = 1 and y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} in (i), we get {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -cos {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} + log 1 = C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 0 Putting C = 0 in (i), we get -cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} + log |x| = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |x| = cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} Hence, log |x| = cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} , is the required solution.
solution: Given that, {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x} \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+x-y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{x-y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{x \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{/tex} This is a homogeneous differential equation. Putting y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} , given equation reduces to {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{1-v \sin v}{\sin v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{1-v \sin v}{\sin v}-v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{1}{\sin v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin v dv = {tex}-\frac{1}{x}{/tex} dx, if x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 Integrating both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow \int{/tex} sin v dv = {tex}-\int \frac{1}{x}{/tex} dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - cos v = - log |x| + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} + log |x| = C ...(i) It is given that y (1) = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} i.e., when x = 1, y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} . Putting x = 1 and y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} in (i), we get {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -cos {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} + log 1 = C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 0 Putting C = 0 in (i), we get -cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} + log |x| = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |x| = cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} Hence, log |x| = cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} , is the required solution.
solution_html:
Given that, {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x} \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+x-y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{x-y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{x \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{/tex}
This is a homogeneous differential equation.
Putting y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}, given equation reduces to
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{1-v \sin v}{\sin v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{1-v \sin v}{\sin v}-v{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{1}{\sin v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin v dv  = {tex}-\frac{1}{x}{/tex} dx, if x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0
Integrating both sides,
{tex}\Rightarrow \int{/tex} sin v dv = {tex}-\int \frac{1}{x}{/tex} dx 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - cos v = - log |x| + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} + log |x| = C  ...(i)
It is given that y (1) = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} i.e., when x = 1, y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}.
Putting x = 1 and y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} in (i), we get
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -cos {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} + log 1 = C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 0
Putting C = 0 in (i), we get
-cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} + log |x| = 0 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |x| = cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex}
Hence, log |x| = cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex}, is the required solution.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the initial value problem: 2x2 {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - 2xy + y2 = 0, y(e) = e
question_text: Solve the initial value problem: 2x 2 {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - 2xy + y 2 = 0, y(e) = e
explanation: We have, 2x 2 {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - 2xy + y 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 x y-y^{2}}{2 x^{2}}{/tex} This is a homogeneous differential equation Putting y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} , it reduces to {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2 v-v^{2}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = -v 2 {tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{2}{v^{2}} d v=\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{-2}{v^{2}} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} [On integrating] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{v}{/tex} = log |x| + C {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 x}{y}{/tex} = log |x| + C ...(i) It is given that y (e) = e i.e. x = e when y = e Putting x = e and y = e in (i), we get 2 = 1 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1 Putting C = 1 in (i), we get {tex}\frac{2 x}{y}{/tex} = log |x| + 1 {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{2 x}{1+\log |x|}{/tex} Hence, {tex}y=\frac{2 x}{1+\log |x|}{/tex} gives the required solution to differential equation.
solution: We have, 2x 2 {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - 2xy + y 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 x y-y^{2}}{2 x^{2}}{/tex} This is a homogeneous differential equation Putting y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} , it reduces to {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2 v-v^{2}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = -v 2 {tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{2}{v^{2}} d v=\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{-2}{v^{2}} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} [On integrating] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{v}{/tex} = log |x| + C {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 x}{y}{/tex} = log |x| + C ...(i) It is given that y (e) = e i.e. x = e when y = e Putting x = e and y = e in (i), we get 2 = 1 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1 Putting C = 1 in (i), we get {tex}\frac{2 x}{y}{/tex} = log |x| + 1 {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{2 x}{1+\log |x|}{/tex} Hence, {tex}y=\frac{2 x}{1+\log |x|}{/tex} gives the required solution to differential equation.
solution_html:
We have, 2x2 {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - 2xy + y2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 x y-y^{2}}{2 x^{2}}{/tex}
This is a homogeneous differential equation
Putting y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}, it reduces to
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2 v-v^{2}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = -v2
{tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{2}{v^{2}} d v=\frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{-2}{v^{2}} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} [On integrating]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{v}{/tex} = log |x| + C
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 x}{y}{/tex} = log |x| + C ...(i)
It is given that y (e) = e i.e. x = e when y = e
Putting x = e and y = e in (i), we get
2 = 1 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1
Putting C = 1 in (i), we get
{tex}\frac{2 x}{y}{/tex} = log |x| + 1 {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{2 x}{1+\log |x|}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}y=\frac{2 x}{1+\log |x|}{/tex} gives the required solution to differential equation.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the initial value problem: (xey/x + y) dx = x dy, y(1) = 1
question_text: Solve the initial value problem: (xe y/x + y) dx = x dy, y(1) = 1
explanation: We have, (xe y/x + y) dx = x dy {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{y / x}+\frac{y}{x}{/tex} This is a homogeneous differential equation. Putting y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} it reduces to {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = e v + v {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = e v {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} e -v dv = {tex}\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} , if x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 Integrating both side with respect to x {tex}\Rightarrow \int e^{-v} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -e -v = log |x| + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -e -y/x = log |x| + C ...(i) It is given that y (1) = 1 i.e. when x = 1, y = 1. Putting x = 1, y = 1 in (i), we get: -e -1 = C Putting C = {tex}-\frac{1}{e}{/tex} in (i), we get -e -y/x = log |x| {tex}-\frac{1}{e}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e -y/x = {tex}\frac{1}{e}{/tex} - log |x| {tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = log (1 - e log |x|) = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x - x log (1 - e log |x|) Hence, y = x - x log (1 - e log |x|), is the solution of the given equation.
solution: We have, (xe y/x + y) dx = x dy {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{y / x}+\frac{y}{x}{/tex} This is a homogeneous differential equation. Putting y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} it reduces to {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = e v + v {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = e v {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} e -v dv = {tex}\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} , if x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 Integrating both side with respect to x {tex}\Rightarrow \int e^{-v} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -e -v = log |x| + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -e -y/x = log |x| + C ...(i) It is given that y (1) = 1 i.e. when x = 1, y = 1. Putting x = 1, y = 1 in (i), we get: -e -1 = C Putting C = {tex}-\frac{1}{e}{/tex} in (i), we get -e -y/x = log |x| {tex}-\frac{1}{e}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e -y/x = {tex}\frac{1}{e}{/tex} - log |x| {tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = log (1 - e log |x|) = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x - x log (1 - e log |x|) Hence, y = x - x log (1 - e log |x|), is the solution of the given equation.
solution_html:
We have, (xey/x + y) dx = x dy
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{y / x}+\frac{y}{x}{/tex}
This is a homogeneous differential equation. Putting y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} it reduces to
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = ev + v
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = ev
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} e-v dv = {tex}\frac{d x}{x}{/tex}, if x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0
Integrating both side with respect to x
{tex}\Rightarrow \int e^{-v} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -e-v = log |x| + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -e-y/x = log |x| + C ...(i)
It is given that y (1) = 1 i.e. when x = 1, y = 1. Putting x = 1, y = 1 in (i), we get: -e-1 = C
Putting C = {tex}-\frac{1}{e}{/tex} in (i), we get
-e-y/x = log |x| {tex}-\frac{1}{e}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e-y/x = {tex}\frac{1}{e}{/tex} - log |x| {tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = log (1 - e log |x|) = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x - x log (1 - e log |x|)
Hence, y = x - x log (1 - e log |x|), is the solution of the given equation.

question_html: Solve the initial value problem: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y cot x = 4x  cosec x, y{tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} = 0
question_text: Solve the initial value problem: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y cot x = 4x cosec x, y {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} = 0
explanation: Here, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y cot x = 4x cosec x, y {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} = 0 It is a linear differential equation. Comparing it with, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q P = cot x, Q = 4x cosec x I.F. {tex}=e^{\int p d x}{/tex} {tex}=e^{\int \cot x d x}{/tex} = e log sin x = sin x Solution of the equation is given by, y {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) = {tex}\int{/tex} Q {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) dx + c y (sin x) = {tex}\int{/tex} 4x cosec x {tex}\times{/tex} (sin x) dx + c = {tex}\int{/tex} 4x dx + c y sin x = {tex}4 \frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} + c = 2x 2 + c Put y = 0, x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} 0 = {tex}\frac{\pi^{2}}{2}{/tex} + c c = {tex}-\frac{\pi^{2}}{2}{/tex} Now, y sin x = 2x 2 {tex}-\frac{\pi^{2}}{2}{/tex}
solution: Here, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y cot x = 4x cosec x, y {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} = 0 It is a linear differential equation. Comparing it with, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q P = cot x, Q = 4x cosec x I.F. {tex}=e^{\int p d x}{/tex} {tex}=e^{\int \cot x d x}{/tex} = e log sin x = sin x Solution of the equation is given by, y {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) = {tex}\int{/tex} Q {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) dx + c y (sin x) = {tex}\int{/tex} 4x cosec x {tex}\times{/tex} (sin x) dx + c = {tex}\int{/tex} 4x dx + c y sin x = {tex}4 \frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} + c = 2x 2 + c Put y = 0, x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} 0 = {tex}\frac{\pi^{2}}{2}{/tex} + c c = {tex}-\frac{\pi^{2}}{2}{/tex} Now, y sin x = 2x 2 {tex}-\frac{\pi^{2}}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y cot x = 4x  cosec x, y{tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} = 0
It is a linear differential equation. Comparing it with,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q
P = cot x, Q = 4x cosec x
I.F. {tex}=e^{\int p d x}{/tex}
{tex}=e^{\int \cot x d x}{/tex}
= elog sin x
= sin x  
Solution of the equation is given by,
y {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) = {tex}\int{/tex} Q {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) dx + c
y (sin x) = {tex}\int{/tex} 4x cosec x {tex}\times{/tex} (sin x) dx + c
= {tex}\int{/tex} 4x dx + c
y sin x = {tex}4 \frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} + c
= 2x2 + c
Put y = 0, x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
0 = {tex}\frac{\pi^{2}}{2}{/tex} + c
c = {tex}-\frac{\pi^{2}}{2}{/tex}
Now, y sin x = 2x2 {tex}-\frac{\pi^{2}}{2}{/tex}

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}x\left(x^{2}-1\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=1{/tex}, y = 0 when x = 2
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}x\left(x^{2}-1\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=1{/tex} , y = 0 when x = 2
explanation: Given, {tex}\mathrm{x}\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}-1\right) \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{x(x-1)(x+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{dy}=\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1)(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} As a result can be directly obtained with direct integration. So, Integrating both the sides with the help of partial fractions- {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{x(x-1)(x+1)}=\frac{-1}{x}+\frac{1}{2(x-1)}+\frac{1}{2(x+1)}{/tex} Now applying the integration of both sides- {tex}\int \mathrm{dy}=-\int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}-1}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}+1}{/tex} y = -log x + 0.5 log (x – 1) + 0.5 log (x – 1) + C y = -log x + log {tex}\sqrt{(x-1)(x+1)}{/tex} y = log {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{(x-1)(x+1)}}{x}\right){/tex} + C {using log properties} given that: for y = 0, x = 2 0 = log {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{(2-1)(2+1)}}{2}\right){/tex} + C 0 = log {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right){/tex} + C {tex}\therefore{/tex} C = -log {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = log {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{(x-1)(x+1)}}{x}\right){/tex} - log {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = log {tex}\left(\frac{2}{x} \sqrt{\frac{\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{3}}\right){/tex} is the required particular solution.
solution: Given, {tex}\mathrm{x}\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}-1\right) \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{x(x-1)(x+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{dy}=\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1)(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} As a result can be directly obtained with direct integration. So, Integrating both the sides with the help of partial fractions- {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{x(x-1)(x+1)}=\frac{-1}{x}+\frac{1}{2(x-1)}+\frac{1}{2(x+1)}{/tex} Now applying the integration of both sides- {tex}\int \mathrm{dy}=-\int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}-1}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}+1}{/tex} y = -log x + 0.5 log (x – 1) + 0.5 log (x – 1) + C y = -log x + log {tex}\sqrt{(x-1)(x+1)}{/tex} y = log {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{(x-1)(x+1)}}{x}\right){/tex} + C {using log properties} given that: for y = 0, x = 2 0 = log {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{(2-1)(2+1)}}{2}\right){/tex} + C 0 = log {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right){/tex} + C {tex}\therefore{/tex} C = -log {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = log {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{(x-1)(x+1)}}{x}\right){/tex} - log {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = log {tex}\left(\frac{2}{x} \sqrt{\frac{\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{3}}\right){/tex} is the required particular solution.
solution_html:
Given, {tex}\mathrm{x}\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}-1\right) \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{x(x-1)(x+1)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{dy}=\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1)(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex}
As a result can be directly obtained with direct integration. So, Integrating both the sides with the help of partial fractions-
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{x(x-1)(x+1)}=\frac{-1}{x}+\frac{1}{2(x-1)}+\frac{1}{2(x+1)}{/tex}
Now applying the integration of both sides-
{tex}\int \mathrm{dy}=-\int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}-1}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}+1}{/tex}
y = -log x + 0.5 log (x – 1) + 0.5 log (x – 1) + C
y = -log x + log {tex}\sqrt{(x-1)(x+1)}{/tex}
y = log {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{(x-1)(x+1)}}{x}\right){/tex} + C {using log properties}
given that: for y = 0, x = 2
0 = log {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{(2-1)(2+1)}}{2}\right){/tex} + C
0 = log {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right){/tex} + C
{tex}\therefore{/tex} C = -log {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y = log {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{(x-1)(x+1)}}{x}\right){/tex} - log {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y = log {tex}\left(\frac{2}{x} \sqrt{\frac{\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{3}}\right){/tex} is the required particular solution.

question_html: Find the particular solution of the following differential equation; {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} x}=1+x^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}+x^{2} \mathrm{y}^{2}{/tex}, given that {tex}\mathrm{y}=1{/tex} when x = 0
question_text: Find the particular solution of the following differential equation; {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} x}=1+x^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}+x^{2} \mathrm{y}^{2}{/tex} , given that {tex}\mathrm{y}=1{/tex} when x = 0
explanation: Given Differential Equation is: {tex}dy\over dx{/tex} = 1 + x 2 + y 2 + x 2 y 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}dy\over dx{/tex} = 1(1 + y 2 ) + x 2 (1 + y 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}dy\over dx{/tex} = (1 + y 2 )(1 + x 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}dy\over 1 + y^2{/tex} + y 2 = (1 + x 2 ).dx ...(By Separation of Variables) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan -1 y = x + {tex}x^3\over3{/tex} + c When y = 1 and x = 0, we get c = {tex}\pi\over4 {/tex} Hence, the particular solution is given as, tan -1 y = x + {tex}x^3\over3{/tex} + {tex} {tex}\pi{/tex} \over4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\tan \left(x+{x^{3} \over 3}+{\pi \over 4}\right){/tex}
solution: Given Differential Equation is: {tex}dy\over dx{/tex} = 1 + x 2 + y 2 + x 2 y 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}dy\over dx{/tex} = 1(1 + y 2 ) + x 2 (1 + y 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}dy\over dx{/tex} = (1 + y 2 )(1 + x 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}dy\over 1 + y^2{/tex} + y 2 = (1 + x 2 ).dx ...(By Separation of Variables) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan -1 y = x + {tex}x^3\over3{/tex} + c When y = 1 and x = 0, we get c = {tex}\pi\over4 {/tex} Hence, the particular solution is given as, tan -1 y = x + {tex}x^3\over3{/tex} + {tex} {tex}\pi{/tex} \over4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\tan \left(x+{x^{3} \over 3}+{\pi \over 4}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Given Differential Equation is:
{tex}dy\over dx{/tex} = 1 + x2 + y2 + x2y2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}dy\over dx{/tex} = 1(1 + y2) + x2(1 + y2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}dy\over dx{/tex} = (1 + y2)(1 + x2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}dy\over 1 + y^2{/tex} + y2 = (1 + x2).dx   ...(By Separation of Variables)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan-1y = x + {tex}x^3\over3{/tex} + c
When y = 1 and x = 0, we get
c = {tex}\pi\over4 {/tex}
Hence, the particular solution is given as,
tan-1y = x + {tex}x^3\over3{/tex} + {tex}{tex}\pi{/tex}\over4{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=\tan \left(x+{x^{3} \over 3}+{\pi \over 4}\right){/tex}

question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=2 x^{3}, x>0{/tex}
question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=2 x^{3}, x>0{/tex}
explanation: The given differential equation may be written as {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{-1}{x}\right) y=2 x^{2}{/tex} ...(i) This is of the form, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} , where {tex}P=\frac{-1}{x}{/tex} and Q = 2x 2 . Thus, the given differential equation is linear. {tex}=e^{\int P \, d x}=e^{\int-\frac{1}{x} d x}=e^{-\log x}=e^{\log \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)}=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} . So, the required solution is given by {tex}y \times \mathrm{IF}=\int\{Q \times \mathrm{IF}\} d x+C {/tex} , where C is an arbitrary constant, i.e. {tex}y \times \frac{1}{x}=\int\left(2 x^{2} \times \frac{1}{x}\right) d x+C=2 \int x \, d x+C=x^{2}+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} y = x 3 + Cx. Hence, the required solution is y = x 3 + Cx.
solution: The given differential equation may be written as {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{-1}{x}\right) y=2 x^{2}{/tex} ...(i) This is of the form, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} , where {tex}P=\frac{-1}{x}{/tex} and Q = 2x 2 . Thus, the given differential equation is linear. {tex}=e^{\int P \, d x}=e^{\int-\frac{1}{x} d x}=e^{-\log x}=e^{\log \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)}=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} . So, the required solution is given by {tex}y \times \mathrm{IF}=\int\{Q \times \mathrm{IF}\} d x+C {/tex} , where C is an arbitrary constant, i.e. {tex}y \times \frac{1}{x}=\int\left(2 x^{2} \times \frac{1}{x}\right) d x+C=2 \int x \, d x+C=x^{2}+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} y = x 3 + Cx. Hence, the required solution is y = x 3 + Cx.
solution_html:
The given differential equation may be written as
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{-1}{x}\right) y=2 x^{2}{/tex} ...(i)
This is of the form, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex}, where {tex}P=\frac{-1}{x}{/tex} and Q = 2x2.
Thus, the given differential equation is linear.
{tex}=e^{\int P \, d x}=e^{\int-\frac{1}{x} d x}=e^{-\log x}=e^{\log \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)}=\frac{1}{x}{/tex}.
So, the required solution is given by
{tex}y \times \mathrm{IF}=\int\{Q \times \mathrm{IF}\} d x+C {/tex}, where C is an arbitrary constant,
i.e. {tex}y \times \frac{1}{x}=\int\left(2 x^{2} \times \frac{1}{x}\right) d x+C=2 \int x \, d x+C=x^{2}+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} y = x3 + Cx.
Hence, the required solution is y = x3 + Cx.

question_html: Show that the differential equation {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} is homogeneous and solve it.
question_text: Show that the differential equation {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} is homogeneous and solve it.
explanation: The given differential equation may be written as {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y+\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}}{x}{/tex} ...(i) On dividing the Nr and Dr of RHS of (i) by x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\left\{\frac{y}{x}+\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}}\right\}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} Therefore,the given differential equation is a homogeneous Putting v = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} in (i), we get v + {tex}x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v x+\sqrt{x^{2}+v^{2} x^{2}}}{x}=v+\sqrt{1+v^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac{d v}{d x}=\sqrt{1+v^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{\sqrt{1+v^{2}}}=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d v}{\left(1+v^{2}\right)}=\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log|v + {tex}\sqrt{1+v^{2}}{/tex} | = log |x| + log |C 1 | where C 1 is an arbitrary constant {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left|\frac{v+\sqrt{1+v^{2}}}{x}\right|{/tex} = log |C 1 | {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{v+\sqrt{1+v^{2}}}{x}=\pm C_{1}{/tex} = C(say) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad v+\sqrt{1+v^{2}}=C x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y+\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} = Cx 2 , which is the required solution [ {tex}\because{/tex} v = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} ]
solution: The given differential equation may be written as {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y+\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}}{x}{/tex} ...(i) On dividing the Nr and Dr of RHS of (i) by x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\left\{\frac{y}{x}+\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}}\right\}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} Therefore,the given differential equation is a homogeneous Putting v = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} in (i), we get v + {tex}x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v x+\sqrt{x^{2}+v^{2} x^{2}}}{x}=v+\sqrt{1+v^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac{d v}{d x}=\sqrt{1+v^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{\sqrt{1+v^{2}}}=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d v}{\left(1+v^{2}\right)}=\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log|v + {tex}\sqrt{1+v^{2}}{/tex} | = log |x| + log |C 1 | where C 1 is an arbitrary constant {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left|\frac{v+\sqrt{1+v^{2}}}{x}\right|{/tex} = log |C 1 | {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{v+\sqrt{1+v^{2}}}{x}=\pm C_{1}{/tex} = C(say) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad v+\sqrt{1+v^{2}}=C x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y+\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} = Cx 2 , which is the required solution [ {tex}\because{/tex} v = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} ]
solution_html:
The given differential equation may be written as
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y+\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}}{x}{/tex} ...(i)
On dividing the Nr and Dr of RHS of (i) by x, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\left\{\frac{y}{x}+\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}}\right\}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex}

Therefore,the given differential equation is a homogeneous 
Putting v = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} in (i), we get
v + {tex}x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v x+\sqrt{x^{2}+v^{2} x^{2}}}{x}=v+\sqrt{1+v^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac{d v}{d x}=\sqrt{1+v^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{\sqrt{1+v^{2}}}=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d v}{\left(1+v^{2}\right)}=\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log|v + {tex}\sqrt{1+v^{2}}{/tex}| = log |x| + log |C1| where C1 is an arbitrary constant
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left|\frac{v+\sqrt{1+v^{2}}}{x}\right|{/tex} = log |C1|
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{v+\sqrt{1+v^{2}}}{x}=\pm C_{1}{/tex} = C(say)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad v+\sqrt{1+v^{2}}=C x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y+\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} = Cx2, which is the required solution [{tex}\because{/tex} v = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex}]

question_html: Solve the differential equation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = 2x2
question_text: Solve the differential equation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = 2x 2
explanation: We are given that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = 2x 2 ...(a) Clearly, it is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q, where P = {tex}-\frac{1}{x}{/tex} and Q = 2x 2 Now, I.F. = {tex}=e^{\int P d x}=e^{\int-\frac{1}{x} d x}=e^{-\log x}=e^{\log x^{-1}}=x^{-1}=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} Multiplying both sides of (a) by I.F. = {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} , we get {tex}\frac{1}{x} \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{1}{x^{2}}{/tex} y = 2x Integrating both sides with respect to x, we get {tex}y\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)=\int{/tex} 2x dx + C [Using: y (I. F.) = {tex}\int{/tex} Q (I.F.) dx + C] {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = x 2 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x 3 + Cx, which is the required solution of the given differential equation.
solution: We are given that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = 2x 2 ...(a) Clearly, it is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q, where P = {tex}-\frac{1}{x}{/tex} and Q = 2x 2 Now, I.F. = {tex}=e^{\int P d x}=e^{\int-\frac{1}{x} d x}=e^{-\log x}=e^{\log x^{-1}}=x^{-1}=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} Multiplying both sides of (a) by I.F. = {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} , we get {tex}\frac{1}{x} \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{1}{x^{2}}{/tex} y = 2x Integrating both sides with respect to x, we get {tex}y\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)=\int{/tex} 2x dx + C [Using: y (I. F.) = {tex}\int{/tex} Q (I.F.) dx + C] {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = x 2 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x 3 + Cx, which is the required solution of the given differential equation.
solution_html:
We are given that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = 2x2...(a)
Clearly, it is a linear differential equation of the form
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q, where P = {tex}-\frac{1}{x}{/tex} and Q = 2x2
Now, I.F. = {tex}=e^{\int P d x}=e^{\int-\frac{1}{x} d x}=e^{-\log x}=e^{\log x^{-1}}=x^{-1}=\frac{1}{x}{/tex}
Multiplying both sides of (a) by I.F. = {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex}, we get
{tex}\frac{1}{x} \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{1}{x^{2}}{/tex}y = 2x
Integrating both sides with respect to x, we get
{tex}y\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)=\int{/tex} 2x dx + C [Using: y (I. F.) = {tex}\int{/tex} Q (I.F.) dx + C]
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = x2 + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x3 + Cx, which is the required solution of the given differential equation.

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation (1 + e2x)dy + (1 + y2)ex dx = 0, given that y = 1, when x = 0.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation (1 + e 2x )dy + (1 + y 2 )e x dx = 0, given that y = 1, when x = 0.
explanation: Given differential equation is, (1 + e 2x )dy + (1 + y 2 )e x dx = 0 Above equation may be written as {tex}\frac { d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } = \frac { - e ^ { x } } { 1 + e ^ { 2 x } } d x{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac { d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } = - \int \frac { e ^ { x } } { 1 + e ^ { 2 x } } d x{/tex} On putting e x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e x dx = dt in RHS, we get {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } y = - \int \frac { 1 } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { - 1 } y = - \tan ^ { - 1 } t + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { - 1 } y = - \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( e ^ { x } \right) + C{/tex} ...(i) [put t = e x ] Also, given that y = 1, when x = 0. On putting above values in Eq. (i), we get tan -1 1 = -tan -1 (e 0 ) + C {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { - 1 }1 = - \tan ^ { - 1 } 1 + C \quad \left[ \because e ^ { 0 } = 1 \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \tan ^ { - 1 } 1 = C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) = C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = 2 \times \frac { \pi } { 4 } = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} On putting {tex}C = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } y = - \tan ^ { - 1 } e ^ { x } + \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \tan \left[ \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( e ^ { x } \right) \right] = \cot \left[ \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( e ^ { x } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}= \cot \left[ \cot ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 1 } { e ^ { x } } \right) \right] \left[ \because \tan ^ { - 1 } x = \cot ^ { - 1 } \frac { 1 } { x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y = \frac { 1 } { e ^ { x } }{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution: Given differential equation is, (1 + e 2x )dy + (1 + y 2 )e x dx = 0 Above equation may be written as {tex}\frac { d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } = \frac { - e ^ { x } } { 1 + e ^ { 2 x } } d x{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac { d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } = - \int \frac { e ^ { x } } { 1 + e ^ { 2 x } } d x{/tex} On putting e x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e x dx = dt in RHS, we get {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } y = - \int \frac { 1 } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { - 1 } y = - \tan ^ { - 1 } t + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { - 1 } y = - \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( e ^ { x } \right) + C{/tex} ...(i) [put t = e x ] Also, given that y = 1, when x = 0. On putting above values in Eq. (i), we get tan -1 1 = -tan -1 (e 0 ) + C {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { - 1 }1 = - \tan ^ { - 1 } 1 + C \quad \left[ \because e ^ { 0 } = 1 \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \tan ^ { - 1 } 1 = C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) = C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = 2 \times \frac { \pi } { 4 } = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} On putting {tex}C = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } y = - \tan ^ { - 1 } e ^ { x } + \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \tan \left[ \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( e ^ { x } \right) \right] = \cot \left[ \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( e ^ { x } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}= \cot \left[ \cot ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 1 } { e ^ { x } } \right) \right] \left[ \because \tan ^ { - 1 } x = \cot ^ { - 1 } \frac { 1 } { x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y = \frac { 1 } { e ^ { x } }{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution_html:
Given differential equation is,

(1 + e2x)dy + (1 + y2)ex dx = 0
Above equation may be written as
{tex}\frac { d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } = \frac { - e ^ { x } } { 1 + e ^ { 2 x } } d x{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int \frac { d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } = - \int \frac { e ^ { x } } { 1 + e ^ { 2 x } } d x{/tex}
On putting ex = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ex dx = dt in RHS, we get
{tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } y = - \int \frac { 1 } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } d t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { - 1 } y = - \tan ^ { - 1 } t + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { - 1 } y = - \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( e ^ { x } \right) + C{/tex} ...(i) [put t = ex]
Also, given that y = 1, when x = 0.
On putting above values in Eq. (i), we get
tan-11 = -tan-1(e0) + C
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { - 1 }1 = - \tan ^ { - 1 } 1 + C \quad \left[ \because e ^ { 0 } = 1 \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \tan ^ { - 1 } 1 = C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) = C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = 2 \times \frac { \pi } { 4 } = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}
On putting {tex}C = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } y = - \tan ^ { - 1 } e ^ { x } + \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \tan \left[ \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( e ^ { x } \right) \right] = \cot \left[ \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( e ^ { x } \right) \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \cot \left[ \cot ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 1 } { e ^ { x } } \right) \right] \left[ \because \tan ^ { - 1 } x = \cot ^ { - 1 } \frac { 1 } { x } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad y = \frac { 1 } { e ^ { x } }{/tex}
which is the required solution.

question_html:
Solve the following differential equation.
{tex} \cos ^ { 2 } x \frac { d y } { d x } + y = \tan x{/tex}
question_text: Solve the following differential equation. {tex} \cos ^ { 2 } x \frac { d y } { d x } + y = \tan x{/tex}
explanation: According to the question,we have to solve, {tex}\cos ^ { 2 } x \frac { d y } { d x } + y = \tan x{/tex} Therefore,on dividing both sides by cos 2 x, the given equation can be rewritten as, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + \frac { y } { \cos ^ { 2 } x } = \frac { \tan x } { \cos ^ { 2 } x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } + y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \tan x \sec ^ { 2 } x{/tex} which is the linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} Here, P = sec 2 x and Q = tanx sec 2 x {tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int \sec ^ { 2 } x d x } = e ^ { \tan x }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \sec ^ { 2 } x d x = \tan x + C \right]{/tex} The solution of linear differential equation is given by {tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y \times e ^ { \operatorname { tan } x } = \int \tan x \sec ^ { 2 } x \cdot e ^ { \operatorname { tan } x } d x + C{/tex} ...(i) Therefore,on putting tan x = t {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} sec 2 x dx = dt in Eq.(i), we get {tex} y{e^{ tan x}} = \int {\mathop t\limits_I } \mathop {{e^t}}\limits_{II} dt + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { \operatorname { tan } x } = t \int e ^ { t } d t - \int \left[ \frac { d } { d t } ( t ) \int e ^ { t } d t \right] d t + C{/tex} [using integration by parts] {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { \tan x } = t e ^ { t } - \int 1 \times e ^ { t } d t + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { \tan x } = t e ^ { t } - e ^ { t } + C{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad y e ^ { \tan x } = \tan x e ^ { \tan x } - e ^ { \tan x } + C [ \because t = \tan x ]{/tex} On dividing both sides by e tan x , we get y = tan x - 1 + Ce - tan x which is the required solution.
solution: According to the question,we have to solve, {tex}\cos ^ { 2 } x \frac { d y } { d x } + y = \tan x{/tex} Therefore,on dividing both sides by cos 2 x, the given equation can be rewritten as, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + \frac { y } { \cos ^ { 2 } x } = \frac { \tan x } { \cos ^ { 2 } x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } + y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \tan x \sec ^ { 2 } x{/tex} which is the linear differential equation of the form {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} Here, P = sec 2 x and Q = tanx sec 2 x {tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int \sec ^ { 2 } x d x } = e ^ { \tan x }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \sec ^ { 2 } x d x = \tan x + C \right]{/tex} The solution of linear differential equation is given by {tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y \times e ^ { \operatorname { tan } x } = \int \tan x \sec ^ { 2 } x \cdot e ^ { \operatorname { tan } x } d x + C{/tex} ...(i) Therefore,on putting tan x = t {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} sec 2 x dx = dt in Eq.(i), we get {tex} y{e^{ tan x}} = \int {\mathop t\limits_I } \mathop {{e^t}}\limits_{II} dt + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { \operatorname { tan } x } = t \int e ^ { t } d t - \int \left[ \frac { d } { d t } ( t ) \int e ^ { t } d t \right] d t + C{/tex} [using integration by parts] {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { \tan x } = t e ^ { t } - \int 1 \times e ^ { t } d t + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { \tan x } = t e ^ { t } - e ^ { t } + C{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad y e ^ { \tan x } = \tan x e ^ { \tan x } - e ^ { \tan x } + C [ \because t = \tan x ]{/tex} On dividing both sides by e tan x , we get y = tan x - 1 + Ce - tan x which is the required solution.
solution_html:
According to the question,we have to solve,
{tex}\cos ^ { 2 } x \frac { d y } { d x } + y = \tan x{/tex}
Therefore,on dividing both sides by cos2x, the given equation can be rewritten as,
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + \frac { y } { \cos ^ { 2 } x } = \frac { \tan x } { \cos ^ { 2 } x }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } + y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \tan x \sec ^ { 2 } x{/tex}
which is the linear differential equation of the form
{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex}
Here, P = sec2x and Q = tanx sec2x
{tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int \sec ^ { 2 } x d x } = e ^ { \tan x }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \int \sec ^ { 2 } x d x = \tan x + C \right]{/tex}
The solution of linear differential equation is given by
{tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad y \times e ^ { \operatorname { tan } x } = \int \tan x \sec ^ { 2 } x \cdot e ^ { \operatorname { tan } x } d x + C{/tex} ...(i)
Therefore,on putting tan x = t {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} sec2x dx = dt in Eq.(i),
we get
{tex} y{e^{ tan x}} = \int {\mathop t\limits_I } \mathop {{e^t}}\limits_{II} dt + C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { \operatorname { tan } x } = t \int e ^ { t } d t - \int \left[ \frac { d } { d t } ( t ) \int e ^ { t } d t \right] d t + C{/tex} [using integration by parts]
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { \tan x } = t e ^ { t } - \int 1 \times e ^ { t } d t + C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { \tan x } = t e ^ { t } - e ^ { t } + C{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad y e ^ { \tan x } = \tan x e ^ { \tan x } - e ^ { \tan x } + C [ \because t = \tan x ]{/tex}
On dividing both sides by etan x, we get
y = tan x - 1 + Ce-tan x
which is the required solution.

question_html: Show that the differential equation {tex}y d x + x \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| d y - 2 x d y = 0{/tex} is homogeneous and solve it.
question_text: Show that the differential equation {tex}y d x + x \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| d y - 2 x d y = 0{/tex} is homogeneous and solve it.
explanation: According to the question , Given differential equation is {tex}y d x + x \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| d y - 2 x d y = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y d x = \left[ 2 x - x \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| \right] d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { 2 x - x \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| }{/tex} Now, let {tex}F ( x , y ) = \frac { y } { 2 x - x \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| }{/tex} On replace x by {tex}\lambda x{/tex} and y by {tex}\lambda y{/tex} both sides, we get {tex}F ( \lambda x , \lambda y ) = \frac { \lambda y } { 2 \lambda x - \lambda x \log \left| \frac { \lambda y } { \lambda x } \right| }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \lambda y } { \lambda \left[ 2 x - x \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| \right] }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow F ( \lambda x , \lambda y ) = \lambda ^ { 0 } \frac { y } { 2 x - x \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| } = \lambda ^ { 0 } F ( x , y ){/tex} So, the given differential equation is homogeneous. On putting y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} in Eq. (i). we get {tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v x } { 2 x - x \log \left| \frac { v x } { x } \right| } = \frac { v } { 2 - \log | v | }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v } { 2 - \log | v | } - v = \frac { v - 2 v + v \log | v | } { 2 - \log | v | }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { - v + v \log | v | } { 2 - \log | v | }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 2 - \log | v | } { v \log | v | - v } d v = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac { 2 - \log | v | } { v ( \log | y | - 1 ) } d v = \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On putting log |v| = t {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { v } d v = d t{/tex} Then, {tex}\int \frac { 2 - t } { t - 1 } d t = \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \left( \frac { 1 } { t - 1 } - 1 \right) d t = \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | t - 1 | - t = \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | \log v - 1 | - \log v = \log | x | + C{/tex} [put t = log |v|] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| \frac { \log v - 1 } { v } \right| = \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log m - \log n = \log \left( \frac { m } { n } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left| \frac { \log v - 1 } { v } \right| - \log | x | = C \Rightarrow \log \left| \frac { \log v - 1 } { v x } \right| = C{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \log \left| \frac { \log \frac { y } { x } - 1 } { y } \right| = c \left[ \because y = v x \Rightarrow v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution: According to the question , Given differential equation is {tex}y d x + x \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| d y - 2 x d y = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y d x = \left[ 2 x - x \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| \right] d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { 2 x - x \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| }{/tex} Now, let {tex}F ( x , y ) = \frac { y } { 2 x - x \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| }{/tex} On replace x by {tex}\lambda x{/tex} and y by {tex}\lambda y{/tex} both sides, we get {tex}F ( \lambda x , \lambda y ) = \frac { \lambda y } { 2 \lambda x - \lambda x \log \left| \frac { \lambda y } { \lambda x } \right| }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \lambda y } { \lambda \left[ 2 x - x \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| \right] }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow F ( \lambda x , \lambda y ) = \lambda ^ { 0 } \frac { y } { 2 x - x \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| } = \lambda ^ { 0 } F ( x , y ){/tex} So, the given differential equation is homogeneous. On putting y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} in Eq. (i). we get {tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v x } { 2 x - x \log \left| \frac { v x } { x } \right| } = \frac { v } { 2 - \log | v | }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v } { 2 - \log | v | } - v = \frac { v - 2 v + v \log | v | } { 2 - \log | v | }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { - v + v \log | v | } { 2 - \log | v | }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 2 - \log | v | } { v \log | v | - v } d v = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac { 2 - \log | v | } { v ( \log | y | - 1 ) } d v = \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On putting log |v| = t {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { v } d v = d t{/tex} Then, {tex}\int \frac { 2 - t } { t - 1 } d t = \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \left( \frac { 1 } { t - 1 } - 1 \right) d t = \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | t - 1 | - t = \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | \log v - 1 | - \log v = \log | x | + C{/tex} [put t = log |v|] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| \frac { \log v - 1 } { v } \right| = \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log m - \log n = \log \left( \frac { m } { n } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left| \frac { \log v - 1 } { v } \right| - \log | x | = C \Rightarrow \log \left| \frac { \log v - 1 } { v x } \right| = C{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \log \left| \frac { \log \frac { y } { x } - 1 } { y } \right| = c \left[ \because y = v x \Rightarrow v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution_html:
According to the question ,
Given differential equation is
{tex}y d x + x \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| d y - 2 x d y = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y d x = \left[ 2 x - x \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| \right] d y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { 2 x - x \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| }{/tex}
Now, let{tex}F ( x , y ) = \frac { y } { 2 x - x \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| }{/tex}
On replace x by {tex}\lambda x{/tex} and y by {tex}\lambda y{/tex} both sides, we get
{tex}F ( \lambda x , \lambda y ) = \frac { \lambda y } { 2 \lambda x - \lambda x \log \left| \frac { \lambda y } { \lambda x } \right| }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \lambda y } { \lambda \left[ 2 x - x \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| \right] }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow F ( \lambda x , \lambda y ) = \lambda ^ { 0 } \frac { y } { 2 x - x \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| } = \lambda ^ { 0 } F ( x , y ){/tex}
So, the given differential equation is homogeneous.
On putting y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} in Eq. (i).
we get {tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v x } { 2 x - x \log \left| \frac { v x } { x } \right| } = \frac { v } { 2 - \log | v | }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v } { 2 - \log | v | } - v = \frac { v - 2 v + v \log | v | } { 2 - \log | v | }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { - v + v \log | v | } { 2 - \log | v | }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 2 - \log | v | } { v \log | v | - v } d v = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int \frac { 2 - \log | v | } { v ( \log | y | - 1 ) } d v = \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
On putting log |v| = t {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { v } d v = d t{/tex}
Then, {tex}\int \frac { 2 - t } { t - 1 } d t = \log | x | + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \left( \frac { 1 } { t - 1 } - 1 \right) d t = \log | x | + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | t - 1 | - t = \log | x | + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | \log v - 1 | - \log v = \log | x | + C{/tex} [put t = log |v|]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| \frac { \log v - 1 } { v } \right| = \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \log m - \log n = \log \left( \frac { m } { n } \right) \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left| \frac { \log v - 1 } { v } \right| - \log | x | = C \Rightarrow \log \left| \frac { \log v - 1 } { v x } \right| = C{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \log \left| \frac { \log \frac { y } { x } - 1 } { y } \right| = c \left[ \because y = v x \Rightarrow v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex}
which is the required solution.

question_html:
Solve the following differential equation.
{tex}\left[ x \sin ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - y \right] d x + x d y = 0{/tex}
question_text: Solve the following differential equation. {tex}\left[ x \sin ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - y \right] d x + x d y = 0{/tex}
explanation: According to given question , Given differential equation is, {tex}\left[ x \sin ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - y \right] d x + x d y = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } - \sin ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} which is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} . Put {tex}y=vx,{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} {tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = v - \sin ^ { 2 } v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad cosec ^ { 2 } v d v = - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int cosec ^ { 2 } v d v + \int \frac { d x } { x } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \cot v + \log | x | = C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \cot \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) + \log | x | = C\quad \left[ \because v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = x \cdot \cot ^ { - 1 } ( \log x - \mathrm { C } ){/tex} which is the required solution.
solution: According to given question , Given differential equation is, {tex}\left[ x \sin ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - y \right] d x + x d y = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } - \sin ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} which is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} . Put {tex}y=vx,{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} {tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = v - \sin ^ { 2 } v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad cosec ^ { 2 } v d v = - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int cosec ^ { 2 } v d v + \int \frac { d x } { x } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \cot v + \log | x | = C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \cot \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) + \log | x | = C\quad \left[ \because v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = x \cdot \cot ^ { - 1 } ( \log x - \mathrm { C } ){/tex} which is the required solution.
solution_html:
According to given question ,

Given differential equation is,
{tex}\left[ x \sin ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - y \right] d x + x d y = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } - \sin ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex}
which is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex}.
Put {tex}y=vx,{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex}
{tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = v - \sin ^ { 2 } v{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad cosec ^ { 2 } v d v = - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int cosec ^ { 2 } v d v + \int \frac { d x } { x } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \cot v + \log | x | = C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad - \cot \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) + \log | x | = C\quad \left[ \because v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y = x \cdot \cot ^ { - 1 } ( \log x - \mathrm { C } ){/tex}

which is the required solution.

question_html:
Solve the following differential equation.
(1 + y2)(1 + log |x|)dx + x dy = 0
question_text: Solve the following differential equation. (1 + y 2 )(1 + log |x|)dx + x dy = 0
explanation: According to given question, Given differential equation is, {tex}(1 + y^2)(1 + log |x|)dx + xdy = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 + \log | x | } { x } d x = \frac { - d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac { 1 + \log | x | } { x } d x = - \int \frac { d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x + \int \frac { \log | x | } { x } d x = - \int \frac { d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | x | + I _ { 1 } + K = - \tan ^ { - 1 } y{/tex} ...(i) where, {tex}I _ { 1 } = \int \frac { \log | x | } { x } d x{/tex} Put {tex}\log | x | = t \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { x } d x = d t{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I _ { 1 } = \int t d t = \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + C _ { 1 } = \frac { ( \log | x | ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + C _ { 1 }{/tex} On putting the value of I 1 in Eq.(i), we get {tex}\log | x | + \frac { ( \log | x | ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + C = - \tan ^ { - 1 } y{/tex} [where C = C 1 + K] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { - 1 } y = - \left[ \log | x | + \frac { ( \log | x | ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + C \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y = \tan \left[ - \log | x | - \frac { ( \log | x | ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - C \right]{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution: According to given question, Given differential equation is, {tex}(1 + y^2)(1 + log |x|)dx + xdy = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 + \log | x | } { x } d x = \frac { - d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac { 1 + \log | x | } { x } d x = - \int \frac { d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x + \int \frac { \log | x | } { x } d x = - \int \frac { d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | x | + I _ { 1 } + K = - \tan ^ { - 1 } y{/tex} ...(i) where, {tex}I _ { 1 } = \int \frac { \log | x | } { x } d x{/tex} Put {tex}\log | x | = t \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { x } d x = d t{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I _ { 1 } = \int t d t = \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + C _ { 1 } = \frac { ( \log | x | ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + C _ { 1 }{/tex} On putting the value of I 1 in Eq.(i), we get {tex}\log | x | + \frac { ( \log | x | ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + C = - \tan ^ { - 1 } y{/tex} [where C = C 1 + K] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { - 1 } y = - \left[ \log | x | + \frac { ( \log | x | ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + C \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y = \tan \left[ - \log | x | - \frac { ( \log | x | ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - C \right]{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution_html:
According to given question,

Given differential equation is,
{tex}(1 + y^2)(1 + log |x|)dx + xdy = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 + \log | x | } { x } d x = \frac { - d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int \frac { 1 + \log | x | } { x } d x = - \int \frac { d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x + \int \frac { \log | x | } { x } d x = - \int \frac { d y } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log | x | + I _ { 1 } + K = - \tan ^ { - 1 } y{/tex} ...(i)
where, {tex}I _ { 1 } = \int \frac { \log | x | } { x } d x{/tex}
Put {tex}\log | x | = t \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { x } d x = d t{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad I _ { 1 } = \int t d t = \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + C _ { 1 } = \frac { ( \log | x | ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + C _ { 1 }{/tex}
On putting the value of I1 in Eq.(i), we get
{tex}\log | x | + \frac { ( \log | x | ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + C = - \tan ^ { - 1 } y{/tex} [where C = C1 + K]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^ { - 1 } y = - \left[ \log | x | + \frac { ( \log | x | ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + C \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad y = \tan \left[ - \log | x | - \frac { ( \log | x | ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - C \right]{/tex}
which is the required solution.

question_html: Show that the differential equation of xdy - ydx {tex}= \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}, is homogeneous and solve it.
question_text: Show that the differential equation of xdy - ydx {tex}= \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} , is homogeneous and solve it.
explanation: Given differential equation is, {tex} x d y - y d x = \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left( y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } \right) d x = x d y{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } + \sqrt { 1 + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} which is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} put, y = vx {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} {tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = v + \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } \Rightarrow \frac { d v } { \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex} \int \frac { d v } { \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } } = \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \log \left| v + \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } \right| = \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex} \left[ {\because \int {\frac{{dx}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {x^2}} }}} = \log \left| {x + \sqrt {{x^2} + {a^2}} } \right|and\int {\frac{{dx}}{x}} = \log |x|} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log \left| \frac { y } { x } + \sqrt { 1 + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } } \right| = \log | x | + C \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \log \left| \frac { y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } } { x } \right| - \log | x | = C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \frac{{\left| {\frac{{y + \sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2}} }}{x}} \right|}}{x} = c{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \log m - \log n = \log \left( \frac { m } { n } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } } { x ^ { 2 } } = e ^ { C } \left[ \begin{array} { l } { \text { if } \log y = x } \ { \text { then } y = e ^ { x } } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } = x ^ { 2 } \cdot e ^ { C }{/tex} {tex} \therefore y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } = A x ^ { 2 }{/tex} , where A = e C which is the required solution.
solution: Given differential equation is, {tex} x d y - y d x = \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left( y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } \right) d x = x d y{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } + \sqrt { 1 + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} which is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} put, y = vx {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} {tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = v + \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } \Rightarrow \frac { d v } { \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex} \int \frac { d v } { \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } } = \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \log \left| v + \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } \right| = \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex} \left[ {\because \int {\frac{{dx}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {x^2}} }}} = \log \left| {x + \sqrt {{x^2} + {a^2}} } \right|and\int {\frac{{dx}}{x}} = \log |x|} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log \left| \frac { y } { x } + \sqrt { 1 + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } } \right| = \log | x | + C \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \log \left| \frac { y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } } { x } \right| - \log | x | = C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \frac{{\left| {\frac{{y + \sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2}} }}{x}} \right|}}{x} = c{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \log m - \log n = \log \left( \frac { m } { n } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } } { x ^ { 2 } } = e ^ { C } \left[ \begin{array} { l } { \text { if } \log y = x } \ { \text { then } y = e ^ { x } } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } = x ^ { 2 } \cdot e ^ { C }{/tex} {tex} \therefore y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } = A x ^ { 2 }{/tex} , where A = e C which is the required solution.
solution_html:
Given differential equation is,
{tex} x d y - y d x = \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left( y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } \right) d x = x d y{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } + \sqrt { 1 + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} 
which is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex}
put, y = vx
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex}
{tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = v + \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } \Rightarrow \frac { d v } { \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex} \int \frac { d v } { \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } } = \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \log \left| v + \sqrt { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } \right| = \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex} \left[ {\because \int {\frac{{dx}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {x^2}} }}} = \log \left| {x + \sqrt {{x^2} + {a^2}} } \right|and\int {\frac{{dx}}{x}} = \log |x|} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \log \left| \frac { y } { x } + \sqrt { 1 + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } } \right| = \log | x | + C \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \log \left| \frac { y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } } { x } \right| - \log | x | = C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \frac{{\left| {\frac{{y + \sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2}} }}{x}} \right|}}{x} = c{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \log m - \log n = \log \left( \frac { m } { n } \right) \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } } { x ^ { 2 } } = e ^ { C } \left[ \begin{array} { l } { \text { if } \log y = x } \ { \text { then } y = e ^ { x } } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } = x ^ { 2 } \cdot e ^ { C }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore y + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } = A x ^ { 2 }{/tex}, where A = eC
which is the required solution.

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Solve the following differential equation.
xdy - (y + 2x2)dx = 0
question_text: Solve the following differential equation. xdy - (y + 2x 2 )dx = 0
explanation: xdy - (y + 2x 2 )dx = 0 xdy = (y + 2x 2 )dx {tex}\boldsymbol{x} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = y + 2x 2 Divide throughout by x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y}{ x}{/tex} + 2x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{y}{ x}{/tex} = 2x Clearly this is a homogeneous linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q The integrating factor I.F is {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\int-\frac{1}{x} d x}{/tex} = e −logx = {tex}\frac{1}{ x}{/tex} The required solution is {tex}y e^{\int P d x}{/tex} = {tex}\int Q e^{\int P d x}{/tex} dx + c (i.e) y( {tex}\frac{1}{ x}{/tex} ) = {tex}\int 2 x\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) d x{/tex} + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{ x}{/tex} = 2x + c y - 2x 2 - cx = 0 is the required solution.
solution: xdy - (y + 2x 2 )dx = 0 xdy = (y + 2x 2 )dx {tex}\boldsymbol{x} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = y + 2x 2 Divide throughout by x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y}{ x}{/tex} + 2x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{y}{ x}{/tex} = 2x Clearly this is a homogeneous linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q The integrating factor I.F is {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\int-\frac{1}{x} d x}{/tex} = e −logx = {tex}\frac{1}{ x}{/tex} The required solution is {tex}y e^{\int P d x}{/tex} = {tex}\int Q e^{\int P d x}{/tex} dx + c (i.e) y( {tex}\frac{1}{ x}{/tex} ) = {tex}\int 2 x\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) d x{/tex} + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{ x}{/tex} = 2x + c y - 2x 2 - cx = 0 is the required solution.
solution_html:
xdy - (y + 2x2)dx = 0 
xdy = (y + 2x2)dx
{tex}\boldsymbol{x} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = y + 2x2
Divide throughout by x
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y}{ x}{/tex} + 2x
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{y}{ x}{/tex} = 2x
Clearly this is a homogeneous linear differential equation of the form
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q
The integrating factor I.F is {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\int-\frac{1}{x} d x}{/tex} = e−logx = {tex}\frac{1}{ x}{/tex}
The required solution is  {tex}y e^{\int P d x}{/tex} = {tex}\int Q e^{\int P d x}{/tex}dx + c
(i.e) y({tex}\frac{1}{ x}{/tex}) = {tex}\int 2 x\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) d x{/tex} + c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{ x}{/tex} = 2x + c
y - 2x2 - cx = 0 is the required solution.
