question_html: Show that the differential equation xy {tex}log(\frac{y}{x}){/tex}dx + {y2 - x2 log{tex}(\frac{y}{x}){/tex}} dy = 0 is homogeneous and solve it.
question_text: Show that the differential equation xy {tex}log(\frac{y}{x}){/tex} dx + {y 2 - x 2 log {tex}(\frac{y}{x}){/tex} } dy = 0 is homogeneous and solve it.
explanation: We can write the given differential equation as, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x y \log \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{\left\{x^{2} \log \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-y^{2}\right\}}{/tex} ...() On dividing the Nr and Dr of RHS of (i) by x 2 , we have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \log \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{\left\{\log \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}\right\}}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} Therefore, the given differential equation is homogeneous. Putting y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} in (i), we have, {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v x^{2} \log v}{x^{2}\left(\log v-v^{2}\right)}=\frac{v \log v}{\left(\log v-v^{2}\right)} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac{d v}{d x}=\left\{\frac{v \log v}{\left(\log v-v^{2}\right)}-v\right\}=\frac{v^{3}}{\left(\log v-v^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\left(\log v-v^{2}\right)}{v^{3}} d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int\left\{\frac{\log v}{v^{3}}-\frac{1}{v}\right\} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} [integrating both sides] {tex}\Rightarrow \int(\log v) v^{-3} d v-\int \frac{1}{v} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x+C_{1}{/tex} where C 1 is an arbitrary constant {tex}\Rightarrow(\log v) \cdot\left(\frac{v^{-2}}{-2}\right)-\int \frac{1}{v} \cdot\left(\frac{v^{-2}}{-2}\right){/tex} dv - log | v | = log |x| + C 1 [integrating by parts] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{-\log v}{2 v^{2}}+\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} v -3 dv - log |v| = log |x| + C 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{-\log v}{2 v^{2}}-\frac{1}{4 v^{2}}{/tex} - log |v| = log |x| + C 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\log v}{2 v^{2}}+\frac{1}{4 v^{2}}{/tex} + log |v| + log |x| = -C 1 = C(say) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\log \left|\frac{y}{x}\right|}{2\left(\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right)}+\frac{x^{2}}{4 y^{2}}{/tex} + log |y| = C [ {tex}\because{/tex} v = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} 2x 2 log | {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} | + x 2 + 4 y 2 log |y| = 4Cy 2 {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}\left(2 \log \left|\frac{y}{x}\right|+1\right){/tex} + 4 y 2 log |y| = 4Cy 2 which is the required solution.
solution: We can write the given differential equation as, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x y \log \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{\left\{x^{2} \log \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-y^{2}\right\}}{/tex} ...() On dividing the Nr and Dr of RHS of (i) by x 2 , we have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \log \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{\left\{\log \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}\right\}}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} Therefore, the given differential equation is homogeneous. Putting y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} in (i), we have, {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v x^{2} \log v}{x^{2}\left(\log v-v^{2}\right)}=\frac{v \log v}{\left(\log v-v^{2}\right)} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac{d v}{d x}=\left\{\frac{v \log v}{\left(\log v-v^{2}\right)}-v\right\}=\frac{v^{3}}{\left(\log v-v^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\left(\log v-v^{2}\right)}{v^{3}} d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int\left\{\frac{\log v}{v^{3}}-\frac{1}{v}\right\} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} [integrating both sides] {tex}\Rightarrow \int(\log v) v^{-3} d v-\int \frac{1}{v} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x+C_{1}{/tex} where C 1 is an arbitrary constant {tex}\Rightarrow(\log v) \cdot\left(\frac{v^{-2}}{-2}\right)-\int \frac{1}{v} \cdot\left(\frac{v^{-2}}{-2}\right){/tex} dv - log | v | = log |x| + C 1 [integrating by parts] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{-\log v}{2 v^{2}}+\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} v -3 dv - log |v| = log |x| + C 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{-\log v}{2 v^{2}}-\frac{1}{4 v^{2}}{/tex} - log |v| = log |x| + C 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\log v}{2 v^{2}}+\frac{1}{4 v^{2}}{/tex} + log |v| + log |x| = -C 1 = C(say) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\log \left|\frac{y}{x}\right|}{2\left(\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right)}+\frac{x^{2}}{4 y^{2}}{/tex} + log |y| = C [ {tex}\because{/tex} v = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} 2x 2 log | {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} | + x 2 + 4 y 2 log |y| = 4Cy 2 {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}\left(2 \log \left|\frac{y}{x}\right|+1\right){/tex} + 4 y 2 log |y| = 4Cy 2 which is the required solution.
solution_html:
We can write the given differential equation as,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x y \log \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{\left\{x^{2} \log \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-y^{2}\right\}}{/tex} ...()
On dividing the Nr and Dr of RHS of (i) by x2, we have,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) \log \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{\left\{\log \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}\right\}}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex}
Therefore, the given differential equation is homogeneous.
Putting y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} in (i), we have,
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v x^{2} \log v}{x^{2}\left(\log v-v^{2}\right)}=\frac{v \log v}{\left(\log v-v^{2}\right)} {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac{d v}{d x}=\left\{\frac{v \log v}{\left(\log v-v^{2}\right)}-v\right\}=\frac{v^{3}}{\left(\log v-v^{2}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\left(\log v-v^{2}\right)}{v^{3}} d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int\left\{\frac{\log v}{v^{3}}-\frac{1}{v}\right\} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} [integrating both sides]
{tex}\Rightarrow \int(\log v) v^{-3} d v-\int \frac{1}{v} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x+C_{1}{/tex} where C1 is an arbitrary constant
{tex}\Rightarrow(\log v) \cdot\left(\frac{v^{-2}}{-2}\right)-\int \frac{1}{v} \cdot\left(\frac{v^{-2}}{-2}\right){/tex}dv - log | v | = log |x| + C1 [integrating by parts]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{-\log v}{2 v^{2}}+\frac{1}{2} \int{/tex} v-3 dv - log |v| = log |x| + C1
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{-\log v}{2 v^{2}}-\frac{1}{4 v^{2}}{/tex} - log |v| = log |x| + C1
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\log v}{2 v^{2}}+\frac{1}{4 v^{2}}{/tex} + log |v| + log |x| = -C1 = C(say)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\log \left|\frac{y}{x}\right|}{2\left(\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right)}+\frac{x^{2}}{4 y^{2}}{/tex} + log |y| = C [{tex}\because{/tex} v = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} 2x2 log |{tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex}| + x2 + 4 y2 log |y| = 4Cy2
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}\left(2 \log \left|\frac{y}{x}\right|+1\right){/tex} + 4 y2 log |y| = 4Cy2
which is the required solution.

question_html: In the differential equation show that it is homogeneous and solve it: x2dy + y(x + y) dx = 0.
question_text: In the differential equation show that it is homogeneous and solve it: x 2 dy + y(x + y) dx = 0.
explanation: The given differential equation is, x 2 dy + y(x + y) dx = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{y(x+y)}{x^{2}}=-\left(\frac{y}{x}+\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} the given differential equation is a homogenous equation. The solution of the given differential equation is: Put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\left(\frac{\mathrm{vx}}{\mathrm{x}}+\frac{(\mathrm{vx})^{2}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = -v-v 2 -v = -2v -v 2 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{2 \mathrm{v}+\mathrm{v}^{2}}=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} Integrating both the sides we get: {tex}\int \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{2 \mathrm{v}+\mathrm{v}^{2}}=-\int \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{log|c|}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{\mathrm{d} v}{1+2 v+v^{2}-1}{/tex} = -ln |x| + ln|c| {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{\mathrm{d} v}{(v+1)^{2}-1^{2}}{/tex} + ln|x| = ln|c| {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \ln \left|\frac{v+1-1}{v+1+1}\right|{/tex} + ln|x| = ln|c| {tex}\Rightarrow \ln \left|\frac{\mathrm{v}+1-1}{\mathrm{v}+1+1}\right|{/tex} + 2 ln|x| = 2 ln|c| Re-substituting the value of y = vx we get {tex}\Rightarrow \ln \left|\frac{\frac{y}{x}}{\frac{y}{x}+2}\right|{/tex} + lnx 2 = ln|c| 2 {tex}\Rightarrow \ln \left|\frac{y}{y+2 x}\right|{/tex} + Inx 2 = In|c| 2 {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} y=c^{2}(y+2 x){/tex} , which is the required solution.
solution: The given differential equation is, x 2 dy + y(x + y) dx = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{y(x+y)}{x^{2}}=-\left(\frac{y}{x}+\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} the given differential equation is a homogenous equation. The solution of the given differential equation is: Put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\left(\frac{\mathrm{vx}}{\mathrm{x}}+\frac{(\mathrm{vx})^{2}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = -v-v 2 -v = -2v -v 2 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{2 \mathrm{v}+\mathrm{v}^{2}}=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} Integrating both the sides we get: {tex}\int \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{2 \mathrm{v}+\mathrm{v}^{2}}=-\int \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{log|c|}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{\mathrm{d} v}{1+2 v+v^{2}-1}{/tex} = -ln |x| + ln|c| {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{\mathrm{d} v}{(v+1)^{2}-1^{2}}{/tex} + ln|x| = ln|c| {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \ln \left|\frac{v+1-1}{v+1+1}\right|{/tex} + ln|x| = ln|c| {tex}\Rightarrow \ln \left|\frac{\mathrm{v}+1-1}{\mathrm{v}+1+1}\right|{/tex} + 2 ln|x| = 2 ln|c| Re-substituting the value of y = vx we get {tex}\Rightarrow \ln \left|\frac{\frac{y}{x}}{\frac{y}{x}+2}\right|{/tex} + lnx 2 = ln|c| 2 {tex}\Rightarrow \ln \left|\frac{y}{y+2 x}\right|{/tex} + Inx 2 = In|c| 2 {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} y=c^{2}(y+2 x){/tex} , which is the required solution.
solution_html:
The given differential equation is,
x2dy + y(x + y) dx = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{y(x+y)}{x^{2}}=-\left(\frac{y}{x}+\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} the given differential equation is a homogenous equation.
The solution of the given differential equation is:
Put y = vx
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\left(\frac{\mathrm{vx}}{\mathrm{x}}+\frac{(\mathrm{vx})^{2}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex}= -v-v2-v = -2v -v2
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{2 \mathrm{v}+\mathrm{v}^{2}}=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}}{/tex}
Integrating both the sides we get:
{tex}\int \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{2 \mathrm{v}+\mathrm{v}^{2}}=-\int \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{log|c|}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{\mathrm{d} v}{1+2 v+v^{2}-1}{/tex} = -ln |x| + ln|c|
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{\mathrm{d} v}{(v+1)^{2}-1^{2}}{/tex} + ln|x| = ln|c|
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \ln \left|\frac{v+1-1}{v+1+1}\right|{/tex} + ln|x| = ln|c|
{tex}\Rightarrow \ln \left|\frac{\mathrm{v}+1-1}{\mathrm{v}+1+1}\right|{/tex} + 2 ln|x| = 2 ln|c|
Re-substituting the value of y = vx we get
{tex}\Rightarrow \ln \left|\frac{\frac{y}{x}}{\frac{y}{x}+2}\right|{/tex} + lnx2 = ln|c|2
{tex}\Rightarrow \ln \left|\frac{y}{y+2 x}\right|{/tex} + Inx2 = In|c|2
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} y=c^{2}(y+2 x){/tex}, which is the required solution.

question_html: Show that the function y = A cos 2x - B sin 2x is a solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+4 y=0{/tex}
question_text: Show that the function y = A cos 2x - B sin 2x is a solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+4 y=0{/tex}
explanation: We have, y = A cos 2x - B sin 2x ...(i) Differentiating both sides of (i) with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = -2 A sin 2x - 2 B cos 2x ...(ii) Differentiating both sides of (ii) with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -4 A cos 2x + 4 B sin 2x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -4 (A cos 2x - B sin 2x) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -4y ...[Using (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+4 y=0{/tex} Hence, the given function is the solution to the given differential equation.
solution: We have, y = A cos 2x - B sin 2x ...(i) Differentiating both sides of (i) with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = -2 A sin 2x - 2 B cos 2x ...(ii) Differentiating both sides of (ii) with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -4 A cos 2x + 4 B sin 2x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -4 (A cos 2x - B sin 2x) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -4y ...[Using (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+4 y=0{/tex} Hence, the given function is the solution to the given differential equation.
solution_html:
We have,
y = A cos 2x - B sin 2x ...(i)
Differentiating both sides of (i) with respect to x, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = -2 A sin 2x - 2 B cos 2x ...(ii)
Differentiating both sides of (ii) with respect to x, we get
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -4 A cos 2x + 4 B sin 2x
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -4 (A cos 2x - B sin 2x)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -4y ...[Using (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+4 y=0{/tex}
Hence, the given function is the solution to the given differential equation.

question_html: Form the differential equation representing the family of curves given by (x-a)2 + 2y2 = a2, where a is an arbitrary constant.
question_text: Form the differential equation representing the family of curves given by (x-a) 2 + 2y 2 = a 2 , where a is an arbitrary constant.
explanation: {tex}{(x - a)^2} +2 y^2 = {a^2}{/tex} ....(i) Differentiating both side w.r.t.x,we get, {tex}2(x - a) + 4y\,{y'} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(x - a) + 2y\,{y'} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(x - a) = - 2y\,{y'}{/tex} ........ (ii) {tex}\Rightarrow x + 2y\,{y'} = a{/tex} ......... (iii) Put the value of (x - a) and a in equation (i),we get, {tex}( - 2yy')^2 + 2{y^2} = {(x + 2yy')^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow4{y^2}{(y')^2} + 2{y^2} = {x^2} + 4{y^2}{(y')^2} + 4xyy'{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow2{y^2} = {x^2} + 4xy\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}{(x - a)^2} +2 y^2 = {a^2}{/tex} ....(i) Differentiating both side w.r.t.x,we get, {tex}2(x - a) + 4y\,{y'} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(x - a) + 2y\,{y'} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(x - a) = - 2y\,{y'}{/tex} ........ (ii) {tex}\Rightarrow x + 2y\,{y'} = a{/tex} ......... (iii) Put the value of (x - a) and a in equation (i),we get, {tex}( - 2yy')^2 + 2{y^2} = {(x + 2yy')^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow4{y^2}{(y')^2} + 2{y^2} = {x^2} + 4{y^2}{(y')^2} + 4xyy'{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow2{y^2} = {x^2} + 4xy\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}{(x - a)^2} +2 y^2 = {a^2}{/tex} ....(i)

Differentiating both side w.r.t.x,we get,

{tex}2(x - a) + 4y\,{y'} = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow(x - a) + 2y\,{y'} = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow(x - a) = - 2y\,{y'}{/tex}........ (ii)

{tex}\Rightarrow x + 2y\,{y'} = a{/tex}......... (iii)

Put the value of (x - a) and a in equation (i),we get,

{tex}( - 2yy')^2 + 2{y^2} = {(x + 2yy')^2}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow4{y^2}{(y')^2} + 2{y^2} = {x^2} + 4{y^2}{(y')^2} + 4xyy'{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow2{y^2} = {x^2} + 4xy\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex}

question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation: {tex}\sec x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=\sin x{/tex}
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation: {tex}\sec x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=\sin x{/tex}
explanation: According to the question,we have, {tex}\sec x \frac { d y } { d x } - y = \sin x{/tex} Therefore,on dividing both sides by sec x, we get {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } - \frac { y } { \sec x } = \frac { \sin x } { \sec x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } - y \cos x = \sin x \cos x{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} Here, P = - cos x and Q = sin x cos x {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { Pdx } } = e ^ { \int - \cos x d x } = e ^ { - \mathrm { sin } x }{/tex} The solution of linear differential equation is given by {tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y e ^ { - \sin x } = \int \sin x \cos x e ^ { - \sin x } d x + C{/tex} Therefore,on putting sinx = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x dx = dt {tex}\therefore \quad y{e^{ - \sin x}} = \int {\mathop t\limits_I } \mathop {{e^{ - t}}}\limits_{II} dt + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { - \sin x } = t \int e ^ { - t } d t - \int \left[ \frac { d } { d t } ( t ) \int e ^ { - t } d t \right] d t + C{/tex} [using integration by parts] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { - \sin x } = - t e ^ { - t } - \int 1 \times \left( - e ^ { - t } \right) d t + C{/tex} {tex}= - t e ^ { - t } + \int e ^ { - t } d t + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { - \sin x } = - t e ^ { - t } - e ^ { - t } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { - \sin x } = - \sin x e ^ { - \sin x } - e ^ { - \sin x } + C{/tex} [put sin x = t] {tex}\therefore \quad y = - \sin x - 1 + C e ^ { \sin x }{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution: According to the question,we have, {tex}\sec x \frac { d y } { d x } - y = \sin x{/tex} Therefore,on dividing both sides by sec x, we get {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } - \frac { y } { \sec x } = \frac { \sin x } { \sec x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } - y \cos x = \sin x \cos x{/tex} which is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} Here, P = - cos x and Q = sin x cos x {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { Pdx } } = e ^ { \int - \cos x d x } = e ^ { - \mathrm { sin } x }{/tex} The solution of linear differential equation is given by {tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y e ^ { - \sin x } = \int \sin x \cos x e ^ { - \sin x } d x + C{/tex} Therefore,on putting sinx = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x dx = dt {tex}\therefore \quad y{e^{ - \sin x}} = \int {\mathop t\limits_I } \mathop {{e^{ - t}}}\limits_{II} dt + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { - \sin x } = t \int e ^ { - t } d t - \int \left[ \frac { d } { d t } ( t ) \int e ^ { - t } d t \right] d t + C{/tex} [using integration by parts] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { - \sin x } = - t e ^ { - t } - \int 1 \times \left( - e ^ { - t } \right) d t + C{/tex} {tex}= - t e ^ { - t } + \int e ^ { - t } d t + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { - \sin x } = - t e ^ { - t } - e ^ { - t } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { - \sin x } = - \sin x e ^ { - \sin x } - e ^ { - \sin x } + C{/tex} [put sin x = t] {tex}\therefore \quad y = - \sin x - 1 + C e ^ { \sin x }{/tex} which is the required solution.
solution_html:
According to the question,we have,
{tex}\sec x \frac { d y } { d x } - y = \sin x{/tex}
Therefore,on dividing both sides by sec x, we get
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } - \frac { y } { \sec x } = \frac { \sin x } { \sec x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } - y \cos x = \sin x \cos x{/tex}
which is a linear differential equation of the form
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex}
Here, P = - cos x and Q = sin x cos x
{tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { Pdx } } = e ^ { \int - \cos x d x } = e ^ { - \mathrm { sin } x }{/tex}
The solution of linear differential equation is given by
{tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad y e ^ { - \sin x } = \int \sin x \cos x e ^ { - \sin x } d x + C{/tex}
Therefore,on putting sinx = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x dx = dt
{tex}\therefore \quad y{e^{ - \sin x}} = \int {\mathop t\limits_I } \mathop {{e^{ - t}}}\limits_{II} dt + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { - \sin x } = t \int e ^ { - t } d t - \int \left[ \frac { d } { d t } ( t ) \int e ^ { - t } d t \right] d t + C{/tex} [using integration by parts]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { - \sin x } = - t e ^ { - t } - \int 1 \times \left( - e ^ { - t } \right) d t + C{/tex}
{tex}= - t e ^ { - t } + \int e ^ { - t } d t + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { - \sin x } = - t e ^ { - t } - e ^ { - t } + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { - \sin x } = - \sin x e ^ { - \sin x } - e ^ { - \sin x } + C{/tex} [put sin x = t]
{tex}\therefore \quad y = - \sin x - 1 + C e ^ { \sin x }{/tex}
which is the required solution.

question_html: Verify that the function {tex}y=e^{x}(a \cos x+b \sin x){/tex} (implicit or explicit) is a solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-2 \frac{d y}{d x}+2 y=0{/tex}
question_text: Verify that the function {tex}y=e^{x}(a \cos x+b \sin x){/tex} (implicit or explicit) is a solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-2 \frac{d y}{d x}+2 y=0{/tex}
explanation: It is given that y = e x (a.cosx + bsinx) = a.e x cosx + b.e x sinx Now, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=a \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \cos x\right)+b \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \sin x\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=a\left(e^{x} \cos x-e^{x} \sin x\right)+b \cdot\left(e^{x} \sin x+e^{x} \cos x\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=(a+b) e^{x} \cos x+(b-a) e^{x} \sin x{/tex} Now, again differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=(a+b) \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \cos x\right)+(b-a) \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \sin x\right){/tex} = {tex}(a+b) \cdot\left[e^{x} \cos x-e^{x} \sin x\right]+(b-a)\left[e^{x} \sin x+e^{x} \cos x\right]{/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}[\mathrm{a.cosx}-\mathrm{a.sinx}+\mathrm{b.cosx}-\mathrm{b.sinx}+\mathrm{b.sinx}+\mathrm{b.cosx}-\mathrm{a.sinx}-\mathrm{a.cosx}]{/tex} = {tex}\left[2 \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}(\mathrm{b.cosx}-\mathrm{a.sinx})\right]{/tex} Now, Substituting the values of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} in the given differential equations, we get, LHS = {tex}=\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+2 \frac{d y}{d x}+2 y{/tex} = 2e x (b cos x - a sin x) -2e x [(a + b)cosx + (b - a ) sinx] + 2e x (a cos x + b sin x) =e x [(2b cos x - 2a sin x) - (2a cos x + 2b cosx) - (2b sinx - 2a sin x) + (2a cosx + 2b sin x)] = e x [(2b - 2a - 2b + 2a)cosx] + e x [(-2a - 2b + 2a + 2b) sinx] = 0 = RHS. Therefore, the given function is the solution of the corresponding differential equation.
solution: It is given that y = e x (a.cosx + bsinx) = a.e x cosx + b.e x sinx Now, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=a \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \cos x\right)+b \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \sin x\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=a\left(e^{x} \cos x-e^{x} \sin x\right)+b \cdot\left(e^{x} \sin x+e^{x} \cos x\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=(a+b) e^{x} \cos x+(b-a) e^{x} \sin x{/tex} Now, again differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=(a+b) \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \cos x\right)+(b-a) \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \sin x\right){/tex} = {tex}(a+b) \cdot\left[e^{x} \cos x-e^{x} \sin x\right]+(b-a)\left[e^{x} \sin x+e^{x} \cos x\right]{/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}[\mathrm{a.cosx}-\mathrm{a.sinx}+\mathrm{b.cosx}-\mathrm{b.sinx}+\mathrm{b.sinx}+\mathrm{b.cosx}-\mathrm{a.sinx}-\mathrm{a.cosx}]{/tex} = {tex}\left[2 \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}(\mathrm{b.cosx}-\mathrm{a.sinx})\right]{/tex} Now, Substituting the values of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} in the given differential equations, we get, LHS = {tex}=\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+2 \frac{d y}{d x}+2 y{/tex} = 2e x (b cos x - a sin x) -2e x [(a + b)cosx + (b - a ) sinx] + 2e x (a cos x + b sin x) =e x [(2b cos x - 2a sin x) - (2a cos x + 2b cosx) - (2b sinx - 2a sin x) + (2a cosx + 2b sin x)] = e x [(2b - 2a - 2b + 2a)cosx] + e x [(-2a - 2b + 2a + 2b) sinx] = 0 = RHS. Therefore, the given function is the solution of the corresponding differential equation.
solution_html:
It is given that y = ex(a.cosx + bsinx) = a.excosx + b.exsinx 
Now, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=a \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \cos x\right)+b \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \sin x\right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=a\left(e^{x} \cos x-e^{x} \sin x\right)+b \cdot\left(e^{x} \sin x+e^{x} \cos x\right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=(a+b) e^{x} \cos x+(b-a) e^{x} \sin x{/tex} 
Now, again differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get,
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=(a+b) \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \cos x\right)+(b-a) \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \sin x\right){/tex} 
= {tex}(a+b) \cdot\left[e^{x} \cos x-e^{x} \sin x\right]+(b-a)\left[e^{x} \sin x+e^{x} \cos x\right]{/tex} 
= {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}[\mathrm{a.cosx}-\mathrm{a.sinx}+\mathrm{b.cosx}-\mathrm{b.sinx}+\mathrm{b.sinx}+\mathrm{b.cosx}-\mathrm{a.sinx}-\mathrm{a.cosx}]{/tex} 
= {tex}\left[2 \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}(\mathrm{b.cosx}-\mathrm{a.sinx})\right]{/tex} 
Now, Substituting the values of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} in the given differential equations, we get,
LHS = {tex}=\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+2 \frac{d y}{d x}+2 y{/tex} 
= 2ex(b cos x - a sin x) -2ex[(a + b)cosx + (b - a ) sinx] + 2ex(a cos x + b sin x)
=ex[(2b cos x - 2a sin x) - (2a cos x + 2b cosx) - (2b sinx - 2a sin x) + (2a cosx + 2b sin x)]
= ex[(2b - 2a - 2b + 2a)cosx] + ex[(-2a - 2b + 2a + 2b) sinx]
= 0 = RHS.
Therefore, the given function is the solution of the corresponding differential equation.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the initial value problem: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}+\ c os ec \frac{y}{x}{/tex} = 0, y(1) = 0
question_text: Solve the initial value problem: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}+\ c os ec \frac{y}{x}{/tex} = 0, y(1) = 0
explanation: Here we have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}+\ c os ec \frac{y}{x}=0{/tex} , y (1) = 0 This is an homogenous equation, put y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} - v + cosec v = 0 {tex}x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = cosec v {tex}\frac{d v}{\ c osec v}=\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} sin v dv = {tex}\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} Integrating both sides with respect to x, we get {tex}\int{/tex} sin v dv = {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex} -cos v = log e x + c cos v + log e x = -c cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} + log e x = -c as y(1) = 0 cos {tex}\left(\frac{0}{1}\right){/tex} = 0 + log e 1 = -c 1 + 0 = -c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} + log e x = 1
solution: Here we have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}+\ c os ec \frac{y}{x}=0{/tex} , y (1) = 0 This is an homogenous equation, put y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} - v + cosec v = 0 {tex}x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = cosec v {tex}\frac{d v}{\ c osec v}=\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} sin v dv = {tex}\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} Integrating both sides with respect to x, we get {tex}\int{/tex} sin v dv = {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex} -cos v = log e x + c cos v + log e x = -c cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} + log e x = -c as y(1) = 0 cos {tex}\left(\frac{0}{1}\right){/tex} = 0 + log e 1 = -c 1 + 0 = -c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos {tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} + log e x = 1
solution_html:
Here we have,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}+\ c os ec \frac{y}{x}=0{/tex}, y (1) = 0
This is an homogenous equation,
put y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} - v + cosec v = 0
{tex}x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = cosec v
{tex}\frac{d v}{\ c osec v}=\frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
sin v dv = {tex}\frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
Integrating both sides with respect to x, we get
{tex}\int{/tex} sin v dv = {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex}
-cos v = loge x + c
cos v + loge x = -c
cos{tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} + loge x = -c
as y(1) = 0
cos{tex}\left(\frac{0}{1}\right){/tex} = 0 + loge 1 = -c
1 + 0 = -c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = -1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos{tex}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} + loge x = 1

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-y \tan x=e^{x} \sec x{/tex}
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-y \tan x=e^{x} \sec x{/tex}
explanation: Given:- {tex}(x \log x) \frac{d y}{d x}+y=2 \log x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{x \log x} y=\frac{2}{x}{/tex} This is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} Where {tex}P=\frac{1}{x \log x}{/tex} and {tex}Q=\frac{2}{x}{/tex} Thus the given differential equation is linear differential equation Now, {tex}I F=e^{\int P \, d x}=e^{\int \frac{d x}{x \log x}}=e^{\log (\ln g x)}=\log x{/tex} Now solution is {tex}y \cdot(I, F)=\int(I F) Q \, d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot(\log x)=\int \log x \cdot \frac{2}{x} d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(log x) = (log x) 2 + C.
solution: Given:- {tex}(x \log x) \frac{d y}{d x}+y=2 \log x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{x \log x} y=\frac{2}{x}{/tex} This is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex} Where {tex}P=\frac{1}{x \log x}{/tex} and {tex}Q=\frac{2}{x}{/tex} Thus the given differential equation is linear differential equation Now, {tex}I F=e^{\int P \, d x}=e^{\int \frac{d x}{x \log x}}=e^{\log (\ln g x)}=\log x{/tex} Now solution is {tex}y \cdot(I, F)=\int(I F) Q \, d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot(\log x)=\int \log x \cdot \frac{2}{x} d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(log x) = (log x) 2 + C.
solution_html:
Given:- {tex}(x \log x) \frac{d y}{d x}+y=2 \log x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{x \log x} y=\frac{2}{x}{/tex}
This is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p y=Q{/tex}
Where {tex}P=\frac{1}{x \log x}{/tex} and {tex}Q=\frac{2}{x}{/tex}
Thus the given differential equation is linear differential equation
Now, {tex}I F=e^{\int P \, d x}=e^{\int \frac{d x}{x \log x}}=e^{\log (\ln g x)}=\log x{/tex}
Now solution is {tex}y \cdot(I, F)=\int(I F) Q \, d x+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot(\log x)=\int \log x \cdot \frac{2}{x} d x+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(log x) = (log x)2 + C.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Show that the differential equation (y2 - x2)dy = 3xydx is homogeneous and solve it.
question_text: Show that the differential equation (y 2 - x 2 )dy = 3xydx is homogeneous and solve it.
explanation: The given differential equation may be written as {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{3 x y}{\left(y^{2}-x^{2}\right)}{/tex} ...(i) On dividing the Nr and Dr of RHS of (i) by x 2 , we have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{3\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{\left\{\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}-1\right\}}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} Therefore, the given differential equation is homogeneous. Putting y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} in (i), we have, {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{3 v}{\left(v^{2}-1\right)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac{d v}{d x}=\left\{\frac{3 v}{\left(v^{2}-1\right)}-v\right\}=\frac{\left(4 v-v^{3}\right)}{\left(v^{2}-1\right)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\left(v^{2}-1\right)}{\left(4 v-v^{3}\right)} d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{\left(v^{2}-1\right)}{v(2-v)(2+v)} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\text { Let } \frac{\left(v^{2}-1\right)}{v(2-v)(2+v)}=\frac{A}{v}+\frac{B}{(2-v)}+\frac{C}{(2+v)}{/tex} Therefore, (v 2 - 1) {tex}\equiv{/tex} A(2 - v)(2 + v) + Bv (2 + v) + Cv(2 - v) .. (ii) Putting v = 0 on each side of (iii), we get A = {tex}\frac{-1}{4}{/tex} Putting u = 2 on each side of (iii), we get B = {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex} Putting v = -2 on each side of (iii), we get C = {tex}\frac{-3}{8}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\left(v^{2}-1\right)}{v(2-v)(2+v)}=\frac{-1}{4 v}+\frac{3}{8(2-v)}-\frac{3}{8(2+v)}{/tex} ...(iv) Putting these values from (iv) in (ii), we have, {tex}-\frac{1}{4} \int \frac{d v}{v}+\frac{3}{8} \int \frac{d v}{(2-v)}-\frac{3}{8} \int \frac{d v}{(2+v)}=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{x} d x+\frac{1}{4} \int \frac{d v}{v}+\frac{3}{8} \int \frac{-d v}{(2-v)}+\frac{3}{8} \int \frac{d v}{(2+v)}{/tex} = log |C 1 | where C 1 is an arbitrary constant {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |x| + {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} log |v| + {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex} log |2 - v| + {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex} log |2 + v| = log |C 1 | {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8 log |x| + 2 log |v| + 3 log |2 - v| + 3 log |2 + v| = 8 log |C 1 | {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |x 8 v 2 (2 - v) 3 (2 + v) 3 | = log ( {tex}C_{1}^{8}{/tex} } {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |x 8 v 2 (2 - v) 3 (2 + v) 3 |= {tex}C_{1}^{8}{/tex} = C (say) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x^{6} {y^{2}}{{}}\left(2-\frac{y}{x}\right)^{3}\left(2+\frac{y}{x}\right)^{3}{/tex} = C This is the required solution of given differential equation.
solution: The given differential equation may be written as {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{3 x y}{\left(y^{2}-x^{2}\right)}{/tex} ...(i) On dividing the Nr and Dr of RHS of (i) by x 2 , we have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{3\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{\left\{\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}-1\right\}}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} Therefore, the given differential equation is homogeneous. Putting y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} in (i), we have, {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{3 v}{\left(v^{2}-1\right)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac{d v}{d x}=\left\{\frac{3 v}{\left(v^{2}-1\right)}-v\right\}=\frac{\left(4 v-v^{3}\right)}{\left(v^{2}-1\right)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\left(v^{2}-1\right)}{\left(4 v-v^{3}\right)} d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{\left(v^{2}-1\right)}{v(2-v)(2+v)} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\text { Let } \frac{\left(v^{2}-1\right)}{v(2-v)(2+v)}=\frac{A}{v}+\frac{B}{(2-v)}+\frac{C}{(2+v)}{/tex} Therefore, (v 2 - 1) {tex}\equiv{/tex} A(2 - v)(2 + v) + Bv (2 + v) + Cv(2 - v) .. (ii) Putting v = 0 on each side of (iii), we get A = {tex}\frac{-1}{4}{/tex} Putting u = 2 on each side of (iii), we get B = {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex} Putting v = -2 on each side of (iii), we get C = {tex}\frac{-3}{8}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\left(v^{2}-1\right)}{v(2-v)(2+v)}=\frac{-1}{4 v}+\frac{3}{8(2-v)}-\frac{3}{8(2+v)}{/tex} ...(iv) Putting these values from (iv) in (ii), we have, {tex}-\frac{1}{4} \int \frac{d v}{v}+\frac{3}{8} \int \frac{d v}{(2-v)}-\frac{3}{8} \int \frac{d v}{(2+v)}=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{x} d x+\frac{1}{4} \int \frac{d v}{v}+\frac{3}{8} \int \frac{-d v}{(2-v)}+\frac{3}{8} \int \frac{d v}{(2+v)}{/tex} = log |C 1 | where C 1 is an arbitrary constant {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |x| + {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} log |v| + {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex} log |2 - v| + {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex} log |2 + v| = log |C 1 | {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8 log |x| + 2 log |v| + 3 log |2 - v| + 3 log |2 + v| = 8 log |C 1 | {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |x 8 v 2 (2 - v) 3 (2 + v) 3 | = log ( {tex}C_{1}^{8}{/tex} } {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |x 8 v 2 (2 - v) 3 (2 + v) 3 |= {tex}C_{1}^{8}{/tex} = C (say) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x^{6} {y^{2}}{{}}\left(2-\frac{y}{x}\right)^{3}\left(2+\frac{y}{x}\right)^{3}{/tex} = C This is the required solution of given differential equation.
solution_html:
The given differential equation may be written as
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{3 x y}{\left(y^{2}-x^{2}\right)}{/tex} ...(i)
On dividing the Nr and Dr of RHS of (i) by x2, we have,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{3\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{\left\{\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^{2}-1\right\}}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex}
Therefore, the given differential equation is homogeneous.
Putting y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} in (i), we have,
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{3 v}{\left(v^{2}-1\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \frac{d v}{d x}=\left\{\frac{3 v}{\left(v^{2}-1\right)}-v\right\}=\frac{\left(4 v-v^{3}\right)}{\left(v^{2}-1\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\left(v^{2}-1\right)}{\left(4 v-v^{3}\right)} d v=\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{\left(v^{2}-1\right)}{v(2-v)(2+v)} d v=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}\text { Let } \frac{\left(v^{2}-1\right)}{v(2-v)(2+v)}=\frac{A}{v}+\frac{B}{(2-v)}+\frac{C}{(2+v)}{/tex}
Therefore, (v2 - 1) {tex}\equiv{/tex}  A(2 - v)(2 + v) + Bv (2 + v) + Cv(2 - v) .. (ii)
Putting v = 0 on each side of (iii), we get A = {tex}\frac{-1}{4}{/tex}
Putting u = 2 on each side of (iii), we get B = {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex}
Putting v = -2 on each side of (iii), we get C = {tex}\frac{-3}{8}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{\left(v^{2}-1\right)}{v(2-v)(2+v)}=\frac{-1}{4 v}+\frac{3}{8(2-v)}-\frac{3}{8(2+v)}{/tex} ...(iv)
Putting these values from (iv) in (ii), we have,
{tex}-\frac{1}{4} \int \frac{d v}{v}+\frac{3}{8} \int \frac{d v}{(2-v)}-\frac{3}{8} \int \frac{d v}{(2+v)}=\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{x} d x+\frac{1}{4} \int \frac{d v}{v}+\frac{3}{8} \int \frac{-d v}{(2-v)}+\frac{3}{8} \int \frac{d v}{(2+v)}{/tex} = log |C1| where C1 is an arbitrary constant
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |x| + {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} log |v| + {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex} log |2 - v| + {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex} log |2 + v| = log |C1|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8 log |x| + 2 log |v| + 3 log |2 - v| + 3 log |2 + v| = 8 log |C1|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |x8v2(2 - v)3(2 + v)3| = log ({tex}C_{1}^{8}{/tex}}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |x8v2(2 - v)3(2 + v)3|= {tex}C_{1}^{8}{/tex} = C (say)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x^{6} {y^{2}}{{}}\left(2-\frac{y}{x}\right)^{3}\left(2+\frac{y}{x}\right)^{3}{/tex} = C

This is the required solution of given differential equation.

question_html: Solve the differential equation (x2 - y2) dx + 2xy dy = 0; given that y = 1 when x = 1
question_text: Solve the differential equation (x 2 - y 2 ) dx + 2xy dy = 0; given that y = 1 when x = 1
explanation: The given differential equation is, (x 2 - y 2 ) dx + 2xy dy = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x 2 - y 2 ) dx - 2xy dy {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{x^{2}-y^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y^{2}-x^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex} ...(i) Since each of the functions y 2 - x 2 and 2xy is a homogeneous function of degree 2, the given differential equation is therefore homogeneous. Putting y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} in (i), we have, {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v^{2} x^{2}-x^{2}}{2 x \cdot v x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v^{2}-1}{2 v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v^{2}-1}{2 v}-v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v^{2}-1-2 v^{2}}{2 v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\left(\frac{v^{2}+1}{2 v}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 v}{v^{2}+1} d v=-\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} [By separating the variables] {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{2 v}{v^{2}+1} d v=-\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex} [Integrating both sides] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log (v 2 + 1) = - log |x| + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log (v 2 + 1) + log |x| = log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (v 2 + 1) |x| = C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {(y 2 /x 2 ) + 1} |x| = C [ {tex}\because{/tex} v = y/x] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x 2 + y 2 ) = C |x| ...(ii) It is given that y = 1 when x = 1. Therefore, putting x = 1, y = 1 in (ii), we get, C = 2 Putting C = 2 in (ii), we obtain x 2 + y 2 = 2 |x| {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 + y 2 = {tex}\pm{/tex} 2x But, x = 1 , y = 1 do not satisfy x 2 + y 2 = -2x. Hence, x 2 + y 2 = 2x is the required solution.
solution: The given differential equation is, (x 2 - y 2 ) dx + 2xy dy = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x 2 - y 2 ) dx - 2xy dy {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{x^{2}-y^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y^{2}-x^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex} ...(i) Since each of the functions y 2 - x 2 and 2xy is a homogeneous function of degree 2, the given differential equation is therefore homogeneous. Putting y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} in (i), we have, {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v^{2} x^{2}-x^{2}}{2 x \cdot v x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v^{2}-1}{2 v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v^{2}-1}{2 v}-v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v^{2}-1-2 v^{2}}{2 v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\left(\frac{v^{2}+1}{2 v}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 v}{v^{2}+1} d v=-\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} [By separating the variables] {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{2 v}{v^{2}+1} d v=-\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex} [Integrating both sides] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log (v 2 + 1) = - log |x| + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log (v 2 + 1) + log |x| = log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (v 2 + 1) |x| = C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {(y 2 /x 2 ) + 1} |x| = C [ {tex}\because{/tex} v = y/x] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x 2 + y 2 ) = C |x| ...(ii) It is given that y = 1 when x = 1. Therefore, putting x = 1, y = 1 in (ii), we get, C = 2 Putting C = 2 in (ii), we obtain x 2 + y 2 = 2 |x| {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 + y 2 = {tex}\pm{/tex} 2x But, x = 1 , y = 1 do not satisfy x 2 + y 2 = -2x. Hence, x 2 + y 2 = 2x is the required solution.
solution_html:
The given differential equation is,
 (x2 - y2) dx + 2xy dy = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x2 - y2) dx - 2xy dy
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{x^{2}-y^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y^{2}-x^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex} ...(i)
Since each of the functions y2 - x2 and 2xy is a homogeneous function of degree 2, the given differential equation is therefore homogeneous.
Putting y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} in (i), we have,
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v^{2} x^{2}-x^{2}}{2 x \cdot v x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v^{2}-1}{2 v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v^{2}-1}{2 v}-v{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v^{2}-1-2 v^{2}}{2 v}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\left(\frac{v^{2}+1}{2 v}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 v}{v^{2}+1} d v=-\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} [By separating the variables]
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{2 v}{v^{2}+1} d v=-\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex} [Integrating both sides]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log (v2 + 1) = - log |x| + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log (v2 + 1) + log |x| = log C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (v2 + 1) |x| = C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {(y2/x2) + 1} |x| = C [{tex}\because{/tex} v = y/x]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x2 + y2) = C |x| ...(ii)
It is given that y = 1 when x = 1. Therefore, putting x = 1, y = 1 in (ii), we get, C = 2
Putting C = 2 in (ii), we obtain x2 + y2 = 2 |x| {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 = {tex}\pm{/tex} 2x
But, x = 1 , y = 1 do not satisfy x2 + y2 = -2x. Hence, x2 + y2 = 2x is the required solution.

question_html: Solve the differential equation: 2xy dx + (x2 + 2y2) dy = 0
question_text: Solve the differential equation: 2xy dx + (x 2 + 2y 2 ) dy = 0
explanation: We have, 2xy dx + (x 2 + 2y 2 ) dy = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{2 x y}{x^{2}+2 y^{2}}{/tex} (Rearranging the terms) Using {tex}y=\lambda y\\x=\lambda x{/tex} and simplifying we get, {tex}\frac{\operatorname dy}{\operatorname dx}=-\lambda^0\;\frac{2xy}{x^2+2y^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} This is a homogeneous differential equation. Put y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} , we get {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{2 v x^{2}}{x^{2}+2 v^{2} x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{2 v}{1+2 v^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{2 v}{1+2 v^{2}}-v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-3 v-2 v^{3}}{1+2 v^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1+2 v^{2}}{3 v+2 v^{3}} d v=-\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} (separating the variables) Integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac{1+2 v^{2}}{3 v+2 v^{3}} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} Substituting 3v + 2v 3 = t, we get 3 (1 + 2v 2 ) dv = dt (differentiating both sides) {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{3} \int \frac{d t}{t} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{3}{/tex} log |t| = -log |x| + log C {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{3}{/tex} log |3v + 2v 3 | = -log |x| + log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |3v + 2v 3 | = -3 log |x| + 3 log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |(3v + 2v 3 ) {tex}\times{/tex} x 3 | = log C 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (3v + 2v 3 ) {tex}\times{/tex} x 3 = C 3 Putting v = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} , we get {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\left(3 \times \frac{y}{x}+2 \times \frac{y^{3}}{x^{3}}\right) \times x^{3}\right]{/tex} = C 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3yx 3 + 2y 3 = C 1, where C 1 = C 3 Hence, 3yx 2 + 2y 3 = C 1 is the required solution.
solution: We have, 2xy dx + (x 2 + 2y 2 ) dy = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{2 x y}{x^{2}+2 y^{2}}{/tex} (Rearranging the terms) Using {tex}y=\lambda y\\x=\lambda x{/tex} and simplifying we get, {tex}\frac{\operatorname dy}{\operatorname dx}=-\lambda^0\;\frac{2xy}{x^2+2y^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} This is a homogeneous differential equation. Put y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} , we get {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{2 v x^{2}}{x^{2}+2 v^{2} x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{2 v}{1+2 v^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{2 v}{1+2 v^{2}}-v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-3 v-2 v^{3}}{1+2 v^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1+2 v^{2}}{3 v+2 v^{3}} d v=-\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} (separating the variables) Integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac{1+2 v^{2}}{3 v+2 v^{3}} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} Substituting 3v + 2v 3 = t, we get 3 (1 + 2v 2 ) dv = dt (differentiating both sides) {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{3} \int \frac{d t}{t} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{3}{/tex} log |t| = -log |x| + log C {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{3}{/tex} log |3v + 2v 3 | = -log |x| + log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |3v + 2v 3 | = -3 log |x| + 3 log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |(3v + 2v 3 ) {tex}\times{/tex} x 3 | = log C 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (3v + 2v 3 ) {tex}\times{/tex} x 3 = C 3 Putting v = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} , we get {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\left(3 \times \frac{y}{x}+2 \times \frac{y^{3}}{x^{3}}\right) \times x^{3}\right]{/tex} = C 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3yx 3 + 2y 3 = C 1, where C 1 = C 3 Hence, 3yx 2 + 2y 3 = C 1 is the required solution.
solution_html:
We have, 2xy dx + (x2 + 2y2) dy = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{2 x y}{x^{2}+2 y^{2}}{/tex} (Rearranging the terms)
Using {tex}y=\lambda y\\x=\lambda x{/tex} and simplifying we get,

{tex}\frac{\operatorname dy}{\operatorname dx}=-\lambda^0\;\frac{2xy}{x^2+2y^2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex}This is a homogeneous differential equation.
Put y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = v + x{tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}, we get
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{2 v x^{2}}{x^{2}+2 v^{2} x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{2 v}{1+2 v^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{2 v}{1+2 v^{2}}-v{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-3 v-2 v^{3}}{1+2 v^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1+2 v^{2}}{3 v+2 v^{3}} d v=-\frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} (separating the variables)
Integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int \frac{1+2 v^{2}}{3 v+2 v^{3}} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
Substituting 3v + 2v3 = t, we get
3 (1 + 2v2) dv = dt (differentiating both sides)
{tex}\therefore \frac{1}{3} \int \frac{d t}{t} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{3}{/tex} log |t| = -log |x| + log C
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{3}{/tex} log |3v + 2v3| = -log |x| + log C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |3v + 2v3| = -3 log |x| + 3 log C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |(3v + 2v3) {tex}\times{/tex} x3| = log C3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (3v + 2v3) {tex}\times{/tex} x3 = C3
Putting v = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex}, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\left(3 \times \frac{y}{x}+2 \times \frac{y^{3}}{x^{3}}\right) \times x^{3}\right]{/tex} = C3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3yx3 + 2y3 = C1, where C1 =C3
Hence, 3yx2 + 2y3 = C1 is the required solution.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation: {tex}(1+x) \frac{d y}{d x}-y=e^{3 x}(1+x)^{2}{/tex}
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation: {tex}(1+x) \frac{d y}{d x}-y=e^{3 x}(1+x)^{2}{/tex}
explanation: The given differential equation is:- {tex}(1+x) \frac{d y}{d x}-y=e^{3 x}(1+x)^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{1}{1+x} y=e^{3 x}(1+x){/tex} This differential equation is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} Where {tex}P=-\frac{1}{1+x}{/tex} and Q = e 3x (1 + x) Now {tex}I F=e^{\int \frac{1}{1+x}}=e^{\int \frac{-d x}{1+x}}=e^{-\log (1+x)}=\frac{1}{1+x}{/tex} {tex}(I.F.) \cdot y=\int(I.F.) Q \, d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{1+x} \cdot y=\int \frac{1}{1+x} e^{3 x}(1+x) d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{1+x} \cdot y=\int e^{3 x} d x+C \Rightarrow \frac{y}{1+x}=\frac{1}{3} \cdot e^{3 x}+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{3} \cdot e^{3 x}(1+x)+C(1+x){/tex}
solution: The given differential equation is:- {tex}(1+x) \frac{d y}{d x}-y=e^{3 x}(1+x)^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{1}{1+x} y=e^{3 x}(1+x){/tex} This differential equation is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} Where {tex}P=-\frac{1}{1+x}{/tex} and Q = e 3x (1 + x) Now {tex}I F=e^{\int \frac{1}{1+x}}=e^{\int \frac{-d x}{1+x}}=e^{-\log (1+x)}=\frac{1}{1+x}{/tex} {tex}(I.F.) \cdot y=\int(I.F.) Q \, d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{1+x} \cdot y=\int \frac{1}{1+x} e^{3 x}(1+x) d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{1+x} \cdot y=\int e^{3 x} d x+C \Rightarrow \frac{y}{1+x}=\frac{1}{3} \cdot e^{3 x}+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{3} \cdot e^{3 x}(1+x)+C(1+x){/tex}
solution_html:
The given differential equation is:- {tex}(1+x) \frac{d y}{d x}-y=e^{3 x}(1+x)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{1}{1+x} y=e^{3 x}(1+x){/tex}
This differential equation is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex}
Where {tex}P=-\frac{1}{1+x}{/tex} and Q = e3x(1 + x)
Now {tex}I F=e^{\int \frac{1}{1+x}}=e^{\int \frac{-d x}{1+x}}=e^{-\log (1+x)}=\frac{1}{1+x}{/tex}
{tex}(I.F.) \cdot y=\int(I.F.) Q \, d x+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{1+x} \cdot y=\int \frac{1}{1+x} e^{3 x}(1+x) d x+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{1+x} \cdot y=\int e^{3 x} d x+C \Rightarrow \frac{y}{1+x}=\frac{1}{3} \cdot e^{3 x}+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{3} \cdot e^{3 x}(1+x)+C(1+x){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y cot x = sin 2x
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y cot x = sin 2x
explanation: This is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} , where P = cot x, Q = sin 2x {tex}\text { I. } F=e^{\int \cot x \, d x}=e^{\log x | \sin x}=\sin x{/tex} So, the required solution is given by, {tex}y \times \sin x=\int \sin 2 x \sin x \, d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \sin x=2 \int \sin x \cos x \sin x \, d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \sin x=2 \int \sin ^{2} x \cos x d x{/tex} Put sin x = t {tex}\Rightarrow \cos x=\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x dx = dt {tex}\Rightarrow y \sin x=2 \int t^{2} d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \sin x=\frac{2 t^{3}}{3}+c{/tex} {tex}\therefore y \sin x=\frac{2}{3} \sin ^{3}(x)+c{/tex} .
solution: This is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} , where P = cot x, Q = sin 2x {tex}\text { I. } F=e^{\int \cot x \, d x}=e^{\log x | \sin x}=\sin x{/tex} So, the required solution is given by, {tex}y \times \sin x=\int \sin 2 x \sin x \, d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \sin x=2 \int \sin x \cos x \sin x \, d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \sin x=2 \int \sin ^{2} x \cos x d x{/tex} Put sin x = t {tex}\Rightarrow \cos x=\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x dx = dt {tex}\Rightarrow y \sin x=2 \int t^{2} d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \sin x=\frac{2 t^{3}}{3}+c{/tex} {tex}\therefore y \sin x=\frac{2}{3} \sin ^{3}(x)+c{/tex} .
solution_html:
This is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex}, where P = cot x, Q = sin 2x
{tex}\text { I. } F=e^{\int \cot x \, d x}=e^{\log x | \sin x}=\sin x{/tex}
So, the required solution is given by, {tex}y \times \sin x=\int \sin 2 x \sin x \, d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \sin x=2 \int \sin x \cos x \sin x \, d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \sin x=2 \int \sin ^{2} x \cos x d x{/tex}
Put sin x = t
{tex}\Rightarrow \cos x=\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x dx = dt
{tex}\Rightarrow y \sin x=2 \int t^{2} d t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \sin x=\frac{2 t^{3}}{3}+c{/tex}
{tex}\therefore y \sin x=\frac{2}{3} \sin ^{3}(x)+c{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 2y tan x= sin x
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 2y tan x= sin x
explanation: The given differential equation is: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 2y tan x= sin x This is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 2y tan x= sin x This is of the from {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} , where P = 2 tan x and Q = sinx Thus the given differential equation is linear Now {tex}IF=e^{\int P dx}=e^{\int 2 \tan x \, d x}=e^{2 \log (\sec x)}=\sec ^{2} x{/tex} Now the solution is {tex}(IF) \cdot y=\int(IF) Q \, d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sec ^{2} x \cdot y=\int \sec ^{2} x \cdot \sin x d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sec ^{2} x y=\int \sec x \tan x \, d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sec 2 x.y = sec x + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = Cos x + Cos 2 x
solution: The given differential equation is: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 2y tan x= sin x This is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 2y tan x= sin x This is of the from {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} , where P = 2 tan x and Q = sinx Thus the given differential equation is linear Now {tex}IF=e^{\int P dx}=e^{\int 2 \tan x \, d x}=e^{2 \log (\sec x)}=\sec ^{2} x{/tex} Now the solution is {tex}(IF) \cdot y=\int(IF) Q \, d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sec ^{2} x \cdot y=\int \sec ^{2} x \cdot \sin x d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sec ^{2} x y=\int \sec x \tan x \, d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sec 2 x.y = sec x + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = Cos x + Cos 2 x
solution_html:
The given differential equation is: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 2y tan x= sin x
This is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 2y tan x= sin x
This is of the from {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex}, where P = 2 tan x and Q = sinx
Thus the given differential equation is linear
Now {tex}IF=e^{\int P dx}=e^{\int 2 \tan x \, d x}=e^{2 \log (\sec x)}=\sec ^{2} x{/tex}
Now the solution is {tex}(IF) \cdot y=\int(IF) Q \, d x+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sec ^{2} x \cdot y=\int \sec ^{2} x \cdot \sin x d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sec ^{2} x y=\int \sec x \tan x \, d x+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sec2x.y = sec x + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = Cos x + Cos2x

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation: {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=x \log x{/tex}
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation: {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=x \log x{/tex}
explanation: The given differential equation is, {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=x \log x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{x} y=\log x{/tex} This is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} where {tex}P=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} and q = log x Thus the given differential equation is linear Now, {tex}I F=e^{\int P \, d x}=e^{\int \frac{1}{x} d x}=e^{\log x}=x{/tex} {tex}y \cdot(I F)=\int(I F) Q \, d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x y=\log x \int x \, d x-\int\left\{\frac{d}{d x}(\log x) d x\right\} d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x y=\frac{x^{2}}{2} \log x-\int \frac{1}{x} \cdot \frac{x^{2}}{2} d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x y=\frac{x^{2}}{2} \cdot \operatorname{log} x-\frac{x^{2}}{4}+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4xy = 2x 2 log x - x 2 + {tex}C_1{/tex} ,where {tex}C_1{/tex} =4C
solution: The given differential equation is, {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=x \log x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{x} y=\log x{/tex} This is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} where {tex}P=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} and q = log x Thus the given differential equation is linear Now, {tex}I F=e^{\int P \, d x}=e^{\int \frac{1}{x} d x}=e^{\log x}=x{/tex} {tex}y \cdot(I F)=\int(I F) Q \, d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x y=\log x \int x \, d x-\int\left\{\frac{d}{d x}(\log x) d x\right\} d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x y=\frac{x^{2}}{2} \log x-\int \frac{1}{x} \cdot \frac{x^{2}}{2} d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x y=\frac{x^{2}}{2} \cdot \operatorname{log} x-\frac{x^{2}}{4}+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4xy = 2x 2 log x - x 2 + {tex}C_1{/tex} ,where {tex}C_1{/tex} =4C
solution_html:
The given differential equation is,

{tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=x \log x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{x} y=\log x{/tex}
This is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex}
where {tex}P=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} and q = log x
Thus the given differential equation is linear
Now, {tex}I F=e^{\int P \, d x}=e^{\int \frac{1}{x} d x}=e^{\log x}=x{/tex}
{tex}y \cdot(I F)=\int(I F) Q \, d x+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x y=\log x \int x \, d x-\int\left\{\frac{d}{d x}(\log x) d x\right\} d x+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x y=\frac{x^{2}}{2} \log x-\int \frac{1}{x} \cdot \frac{x^{2}}{2} d x+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x y=\frac{x^{2}}{2} \cdot \operatorname{log} x-\frac{x^{2}}{4}+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4xy = 2x2 log x - x2 + {tex}C_1{/tex} ,where {tex}C_1{/tex}=4C

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the differential equation: (1 + x2) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - x = 2 tan-1 x
question_text: Solve the differential equation: (1 + x 2 ) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - x = 2 tan -1 x
explanation: The given differential equation is, (1 + x 2 ) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - x = 2 tan -1 x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 + x 2 ) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = x + 2 tan -1 x {tex}\Rightarrow d y=\left\{\frac{x}{1+x^{2}}+\left(\frac{2}{1+x^{2}}\right) \tan ^{-1} x\right\} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow d y=\left\{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}+\left(\frac{2}{1+x^{2}}\right) \tan ^{-1} x\right\} d x{/tex} Integrating both sides, we get, {tex}\int d y=\int\left\{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}+\left(\frac{2}{1+x^{2}}\right) \tan ^{-1} x\right\} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}} d x+2 \int\left[\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} \tan ^{-1} x\right] d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{2} \log \left|1+x^{2}\right|+2 \int\left[\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} \tan ^{-1} x\right] d x{/tex} Putting tan -1 x = t {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{1+x^{2}} d x=d t{/tex} {tex}\therefore y=\frac{1}{2} \log \left|1+x^{2}\right|+2 \int t d t{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |1 + x 2 | + t 2 + C = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |1 + x 2 | + (tan -1 x) 2 + C Hence, y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |1 + x 2 | + (tan -1 x) 2 + C is the solution to the given differential equation.
solution: The given differential equation is, (1 + x 2 ) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - x = 2 tan -1 x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 + x 2 ) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = x + 2 tan -1 x {tex}\Rightarrow d y=\left\{\frac{x}{1+x^{2}}+\left(\frac{2}{1+x^{2}}\right) \tan ^{-1} x\right\} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow d y=\left\{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}+\left(\frac{2}{1+x^{2}}\right) \tan ^{-1} x\right\} d x{/tex} Integrating both sides, we get, {tex}\int d y=\int\left\{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}+\left(\frac{2}{1+x^{2}}\right) \tan ^{-1} x\right\} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}} d x+2 \int\left[\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} \tan ^{-1} x\right] d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{2} \log \left|1+x^{2}\right|+2 \int\left[\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} \tan ^{-1} x\right] d x{/tex} Putting tan -1 x = t {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{1+x^{2}} d x=d t{/tex} {tex}\therefore y=\frac{1}{2} \log \left|1+x^{2}\right|+2 \int t d t{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |1 + x 2 | + t 2 + C = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |1 + x 2 | + (tan -1 x) 2 + C Hence, y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |1 + x 2 | + (tan -1 x) 2 + C is the solution to the given differential equation.
solution_html:
The given differential equation is,
(1 + x2) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - x = 2 tan-1 x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 + x2) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = x + 2 tan-1 x
{tex}\Rightarrow d y=\left\{\frac{x}{1+x^{2}}+\left(\frac{2}{1+x^{2}}\right) \tan ^{-1} x\right\} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow d y=\left\{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}+\left(\frac{2}{1+x^{2}}\right) \tan ^{-1} x\right\} d x{/tex}
Integrating both sides, we get,
{tex}\int d y=\int\left\{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}+\left(\frac{2}{1+x^{2}}\right) \tan ^{-1} x\right\} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}} d x+2 \int\left[\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} \tan ^{-1} x\right] d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{2} \log \left|1+x^{2}\right|+2 \int\left[\frac{1}{1+x^{2}} \tan ^{-1} x\right] d x{/tex}
Putting tan-1 x = t
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{1+x^{2}} d x=d t{/tex}
{tex}\therefore y=\frac{1}{2} \log \left|1+x^{2}\right|+2 \int t d t{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |1 + x2| + t2 + C
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |1 + x2| + (tan-1 x)2 + C
Hence, y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |1 + x2| + (tan-1 x)2 + C is the solution to the given differential equation.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the differential equation: x cos y = (xex log x + ex) dx
question_text: Solve the differential equation: x cos y = (xe x log x + e x ) dx
explanation: We are given that x cos y dy = (xe x log x + e x ) dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos y dy = {tex}\left(e^{x} \log x+\frac{1}{x} e^{x}\right) d x{/tex} Integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \cos y d y=\int\left(e^{x} \log x+\frac{1}{x} e^{x}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin y = log x {tex}\int e^{x} d x-\int \frac{1}{x} e^{x} d x+\int \frac{1}{x} e^{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin y = e x log x + C Hence, sin y = e x log x + C is the required solution.
solution: We are given that x cos y dy = (xe x log x + e x ) dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos y dy = {tex}\left(e^{x} \log x+\frac{1}{x} e^{x}\right) d x{/tex} Integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \cos y d y=\int\left(e^{x} \log x+\frac{1}{x} e^{x}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin y = log x {tex}\int e^{x} d x-\int \frac{1}{x} e^{x} d x+\int \frac{1}{x} e^{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin y = e x log x + C Hence, sin y = e x log x + C is the required solution.
solution_html:
We are given that x cos y dy = (xex log x + ex) dx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos y dy = {tex}\left(e^{x} \log x+\frac{1}{x} e^{x}\right) d x{/tex}
Integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int \cos y d y=\int\left(e^{x} \log x+\frac{1}{x} e^{x}\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin y = log x {tex}\int e^{x} d x-\int \frac{1}{x} e^{x} d x+\int \frac{1}{x} e^{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin y = ex log x + C
Hence, sin y = ex log x + C is the required solution.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the differential equation: y (1 - x2) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = x (1 + y2)
question_text: Solve the differential equation: y (1 - x 2 ) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = x (1 + y 2 )
explanation: The given differential equation is, y (1 - x 2 ) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = x (1 + y 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{1+y^{2}} d y=\frac{x}{1-x^{2}} d x{/tex} Integrating both sides, {tex}\int \frac{y}{1+y^{2}} d y=\int \frac{x}{1-x^{2}} d x{/tex} Substituting 1 + y 2 = t and 1 - x 2 = u 2y dy = dt and -2x dx = du {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{t} d t=\frac{-1}{2} \int \frac{1}{u} d u{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |t| = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |u| + log C {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |1 + y 2 | = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |1 - x 2 | + log C {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}{/tex} log (|1 + y 2 | |1 - x 2 |) = 2 log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 + y 2 ) (1 - x 2 ) = C 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 + y 2 ) (1 - x 2 ) = C 1 ...(where C 1 = C 2 ) Hence, (1 + y 2 ) (1 - x 2 ) = C 1 is the required solution.
solution: The given differential equation is, y (1 - x 2 ) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = x (1 + y 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{1+y^{2}} d y=\frac{x}{1-x^{2}} d x{/tex} Integrating both sides, {tex}\int \frac{y}{1+y^{2}} d y=\int \frac{x}{1-x^{2}} d x{/tex} Substituting 1 + y 2 = t and 1 - x 2 = u 2y dy = dt and -2x dx = du {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{t} d t=\frac{-1}{2} \int \frac{1}{u} d u{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |t| = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |u| + log C {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |1 + y 2 | = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |1 - x 2 | + log C {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}{/tex} log (|1 + y 2 | |1 - x 2 |) = 2 log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 + y 2 ) (1 - x 2 ) = C 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 + y 2 ) (1 - x 2 ) = C 1 ...(where C 1 = C 2 ) Hence, (1 + y 2 ) (1 - x 2 ) = C 1 is the required solution.
solution_html:
The given differential equation is,
y (1 - x2) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = x (1 + y2)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{1+y^{2}} d y=\frac{x}{1-x^{2}} d x{/tex}
Integrating both sides,
{tex}\int \frac{y}{1+y^{2}} d y=\int \frac{x}{1-x^{2}} d x{/tex}
Substituting 1 + y2 = t and 1 - x2 = u
2y dy = dt and -2x dx = du
{tex}\therefore \frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{t} d t=\frac{-1}{2} \int \frac{1}{u} d u{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |t| = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |u| + log C
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |1 + y2| = {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log |1 - x2| + log C
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}{/tex} log (|1 + y2| |1 - x2|) = 2 log C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 + y2) (1 - x2) = C2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 + y2) (1 - x2) = C1 ...(where C1 = C2)
Hence, (1 + y2) (1 - x2) = C1 is the required solution.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Solve the differential equation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 2y = 6ex
question_text: Solve the differential equation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 2y = 6e x
explanation: Here, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 2y = 6e x It is a linear differential equation, comparing it with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q P = 2, Q = 6e x I.F. {tex}=e^{\int p d x}{/tex} {tex}=e^{\int 2 d x}{/tex} = e 2x Solution of the equation is given by, y {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) = {tex}\int{/tex} Q {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) dx + c y {tex}\times{/tex} (e 2x ) = {tex}\int{/tex} 6e x {tex}\times{/tex} e 2x dx + c ye 2x = {tex}\int{/tex} 6e 3x dx + c ye 2x = {tex}\frac{6}{3}{/tex} e 3x + c ye 2x = 2e 3x + c y = 2e x + ce -2x
solution: Here, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 2y = 6e x It is a linear differential equation, comparing it with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q P = 2, Q = 6e x I.F. {tex}=e^{\int p d x}{/tex} {tex}=e^{\int 2 d x}{/tex} = e 2x Solution of the equation is given by, y {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) = {tex}\int{/tex} Q {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) dx + c y {tex}\times{/tex} (e 2x ) = {tex}\int{/tex} 6e x {tex}\times{/tex} e 2x dx + c ye 2x = {tex}\int{/tex} 6e 3x dx + c ye 2x = {tex}\frac{6}{3}{/tex} e 3x + c ye 2x = 2e 3x + c y = 2e x + ce -2x
solution_html:
Here, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 2y = 6ex
It is a linear differential equation, comparing it with
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q
P = 2, Q = 6ex
I.F. {tex}=e^{\int p d x}{/tex}
{tex}=e^{\int 2 d x}{/tex}
= e2x
Solution of the equation is given by,
y {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) = {tex}\int{/tex} Q {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) dx + c
y {tex}\times{/tex} (e2x) = {tex}\int{/tex} 6ex {tex}\times{/tex} e2x dx + c
ye2x = {tex}\int{/tex} 6e3x dx + c
ye2x = {tex}\frac{6}{3}{/tex} e3x + c
ye2x = 2e3x + c
y = 2ex + ce-2x

question_html: Solve the differential equation: {tex}4 \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 8y = 5e-3x
question_text: Solve the differential equation: {tex}4 \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 8y = 5e -3x
explanation: {tex}4 \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 8y = 5e -3x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 2y = {tex}\frac{5}{4}{/tex} e -3x This is a linear differential equation, comparing it with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q P = 2, Q = {tex}\frac{5}{4}{/tex} e -3x I.F. = {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex} {tex}=e^{\int 2 d x}{/tex} = e 2x Solution of the differential equation is given by y {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F) = {tex}\int{/tex} Q {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) dx + c ye 2x = {tex}\int \frac{5}{4}{/tex} e -3x {tex}\times{/tex} e 2x dx + c ye 2x = {tex}\int \frac{5}{4}{/tex} e -x dx + c ye 2x = {tex}\frac{-5}{4}{/tex} e -x + c y = {tex}\frac{-5}{4}{/tex} e -3x + ce -2x
solution: {tex}4 \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 8y = 5e -3x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 2y = {tex}\frac{5}{4}{/tex} e -3x This is a linear differential equation, comparing it with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q P = 2, Q = {tex}\frac{5}{4}{/tex} e -3x I.F. = {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex} {tex}=e^{\int 2 d x}{/tex} = e 2x Solution of the differential equation is given by y {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F) = {tex}\int{/tex} Q {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) dx + c ye 2x = {tex}\int \frac{5}{4}{/tex} e -3x {tex}\times{/tex} e 2x dx + c ye 2x = {tex}\int \frac{5}{4}{/tex} e -x dx + c ye 2x = {tex}\frac{-5}{4}{/tex} e -x + c y = {tex}\frac{-5}{4}{/tex} e -3x + ce -2x
solution_html:
{tex}4 \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 8y = 5e-3x
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 2y = {tex}\frac{5}{4}{/tex} e-3x
This is a linear differential equation, comparing it with
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q
P = 2, Q = {tex}\frac{5}{4}{/tex} e-3x
I.F. = {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex}
{tex}=e^{\int 2 d x}{/tex}
= e2x
Solution of the differential equation is given by
y {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F) = {tex}\int{/tex} Q {tex}\times{/tex} (I.F.) dx + c
ye2x = {tex}\int \frac{5}{4}{/tex} e-3x {tex}\times{/tex} e2x dx + c
ye2x = {tex}\int \frac{5}{4}{/tex} e-x dx + c
ye2x = {tex}\frac{-5}{4}{/tex} e-x + c
y = {tex}\frac{-5}{4}{/tex} e-3x + ce-2x
